Quick Pro/Cons:
Has speakers, but no volume control.
Has multiple video inputs, but no rotation control.
Came with HDMI and power cable, monitor is sleek, stand is elegant and sturdy.
Standard shipping was very fast.

My implementation is dual-monitors, suspended from the ceiling. (Upside-down). Lack of rotation control means: No ROKU, No 'Casting'. PC is required for video; output must be capable of 180° rotation video signal output.

Very happy with with build quality and packaging. Impeccable quality control.

Highly recommended for standard installation with external volume control. Perfect for a dedicated and standalone PC monitor.

-Nathan
Awesome Monitor. Arrived quickly and was even better than what it looked like online. My son LOVES it!

My MacBook Pro detected it had speakers (and made it the default output device), but no sound came out. I called support and they verified that the monitor does not have speakers. Other than that, it is a great monitor. If it weren't for the bad item description, I would have given it 5 stars (because I was expecting built-in speakers, especially when they were detected).

I purchased this display direct from Monoprice. Overall I'm very happy with it so far. It's really a beautiful display, with crisp image and bright colors. There is some discoloration when viewed off-angle, but I sit in front of my display, so not an issue for me. It is not glossy, which is a huge plus. The stand is metal, and very sturdy.

When I received the display, it had a single stuck white pixel. I returned the display, and they shipped me a replacement, and the replacement has a single dead pixel. So much for "pixel perfect". However, I think a dead pixel is much less noticeable than a stuck pixel, so am leaving it be.

If you plan to use a DVI cable, note that the screw-holders for their DVI connector seem abnormally short, so it is an extreme pain to get a DVI cable to screw into it. Also be sure to use a dual-link cable.

The picture quality on this monitor is alright. Lines and text are sharp, though the brightness isn't spectacular and I've found that even indoor lighting can fight with the backlight. What really irks me about this product is the incredibly poor design of the case/stand. Other portable monitors I have owned have a way to anchor the case to the stand where as this Asus product has you folding two separate flaps to then balance the monitor in a small gap between them. It simply does not work! I spend the first fifteen minutes at a customer site folding and refolding the flaps to try and make the poor quality velcro stick and, even then, I can expect the monitor to slide out of it's gap, roll right over the back flap, and fall flat onto the desk.

A simple kick stand built onto the back would work perfectly, every time, and I can't fathom why that isn't on the product. Would it really have eaten into the cost margin on this device that much? So in order to use this product and not lose my mind I had to purchase a separate tablet stand. Though cheap, I'm unhappy that I have to carry more gear than necessary to accommodate what is simply a poorly designed product.

No, this isn't quite 4K but at 1440p it's still quite nice and better than a standard 1080p monitor. The screen real estate is huge on this thing! A whole 32 inches! And there's almost no bezel to distract or take up space. Colors are fabulous, I don't notice any screen-tearing problems with my Nvidia GPU handling things, and I appreciate the DisplayPort connection as an option.

My only complaint (and not worthy of docking a star) is that the built-in speaker is on the weak side. That only stands to reason considering how incredibly thin this monitor is. There's barely any room for powerful speakers. Just the fact that it even has speakers in the first place is amazing. Consider them functional for really basic things like operating system prompts and low-quality video playback, but if you want to play games or listen to music you'll want some better speakers.

Bottom line: As long as you're getting this looking for high resolution, big screen, and low profile, you'll be happy and it's a five-star product. If you're looking for something to replace a television, speakers and all, you'll probably find the speakers underwhelming despite the lovely picture. Either way, it's a truly nice monitor that makes me look back at the days when I rocked a 12" CGA CRT and laugh at how far we've come. A definite five stars from me.

I was slightly disappointed at first. I went from a 24in Asus 1080p 144hz monitor and I did not notice that big of a difference. When I started gaming with the GSync on though, I knew why it was rated really well. i have had it for about 2 months and never had any problems with it. I REALLY like all the USB ports. I have my keyboard hooked up to it so when I turn the monitor off, the keyboard turns off. Very useful when you have a two year old that will randomly bang on the keyboard.
Nice screen for office use.  Has multiple hookups for different cords and works well.

Productivity-wise, this monitor is a GAMECHANGER. You can use the built-in USB 3.0 hub as a KVM switch, controlling 2 computers with a single keyboard and mouse. You assign the USB to a video source, and the system switches when you switch. Also, the resolution makes working with certain programs really easy, as you can spread a window out over the equivalent of 3 monitors, or run 3 windows side by side. I have a keyboard, mouse, and a 10 port USB 3.0 hub plugged in.

Lots of backlight bleed in the corners, so I wouldn't recommend watching movies on it. Still, fairly decent for gaming if there aren't too many dark scenes, I managed to get it running at 75Hz just fine with Nvidia's control panel. The curve is gentle and makes just a subtle improvement in viewing angles. Getting this at close to half of the cost of the G-Sync Predator made it a good deal for me, and I would recommend it to all but the hardcore FPS player who needs 100Hz or higher.

Upfront I did have in issue with a missing cable, KYY support team reached out and offered to make things right. I wish more sellers had better customer service, and care about the customer after the sell is complete. My years on this planet has taught me one thing, it is how you treat the customer goes along way, Thank you KYY.

The monitor itself is awesome, it is well constructed of metal and not plastic. It has a matte display which on the positive doesn’t have annoying glare like glossy screens, yet the colors for the most part are still vibrant, but not as much pop as a glossy screen, but for me being a portable monitor and working under a lot of difficult lighting situations I would take the matte screen any day.

The port selection is terrific, it has two USB-C ports and one mini-hdmi port (there is no room for a full sized HDMI port on this device). I have yet to try the mini-hdmi port but the USB-C port has worked terrific. I have numerous laptops (MacBook Pro, Mac Book Air, Surface Pro 7 and HP Spectre 13) and all of them were able to power the display without using the power adapter.

I have tried the power brick, it is not that big, but would not be big enough to supply power to the monitor and laptop at the same time. I do wish it had folding prongs, but this would be difficult due to the nature that this device is sold globally and creating custom adapters for each region would drive up costs.

I do appreciate that is does include a case with the device. while it does have a couple of options for angle adjustment, with a step angle you are able to fold up the front flap onto itself to make the monitor more secure from sliding down flat. I do wish that the front flap was just a little thicker to help better protect the screen if placed into a backpack. But for a freebie in the box you can’t complain.

The controls on the monitor have a button and a rocker control that is also able to be pushed in to make selections. It had taken me a little bit to get use to the controls, but it has a lot of options for adjustment. Honestly for the price I am surprised by this. For people doing color correction work, you should be able to calibrate this display to your liking.

4K YouTube content on this monitor is nothing short of amazing for the price. Is there better displays with OLED, Mini-LED, and super fast refresh rates, obviously, but again in this price range it quality is above the asking price.

I received this monitor a few days ago, and since then I've been mostly really pleased with it. The screen is big and crisp (even if the resolution is a tiny bit low for today's standards), and the overall setup is very sleek. The adjustable arm thing on the back is a big bonus for me. It's easy to adjust, and steady.

The touchscreen itself is very responsive, as much so as your phone or tablet would be. I'm using it with Windows 7, which is not as optimized for touch. Because of this, and just because the screen itself is pretty big and sometimes I'm lazy, I still often switch between touch and using the mouse. The touch also doesn't respond great with some games, so you may still want to consider using one if you're into that.

One pretty big drawback is that, for me, this monitor arrived with 3 dead pixels in the upper left corner. I decided against sending it back to Amazon, because it's fairly small, and I didn't think it would be worth the trouble. I didn't want to wait another 3 weeks sending a huge package to the other side of the country (and back) just for this. I checked acer's website to see if it was covered by the warranty, but from what I gather it isn't. It's apparently considered a minor aesthetic defect, because it's in a corner and less than 4 pixels.

I would just like to take a moment to point out that, uh, it's a monitor. It shows pictures. Isn't aesthetics the entire purpose? Isn't that why people have 21" color monitors, and not 2" calculator screens?

But I digress. I was debating even taking a star off for the dead pixels, because I guess it's kind of a luck of the draw thing. I'm going to guess that you, whoever you are, will probably not have this problem. Even if so, it's small enough for me personally to ignore.

In any case, I am very pleased with this monitor. It is sleek and crisp, and wonderful to use. If you can find a good deal on it, go for it.

This monitor may work fine -- if you use VGA style inputs on your desktop. While it is a hi-def monitor, it has no HDMI input as required by most new laptops.

So, I originally left a bad review but after innocn reached out to me, and replaced my faulty monitor, and after rigorous testing to insure my unit was fully functional, I feel like I owe the company some more props. In terms of picture quality, this Monitor is the best for its price.

I feel like innocn is providing a truly unique product, as affordable oleds are practically non existence in the tv/Monitor space. Similarly priced displays will cost you hundreds of dollars of more money. After experiencing the difference between the lcd and oled Nintendo Switch, I craved an oled docked experience, and as you can see from my picture, this innocns 13 inch display delivers, with its native 1080p resolution, which at 13 inches, looks incredible sharp, even with games not running nativily at 1080p such as xenoblade chronicles 2.

It's small size also makes it a good Monitor for taking over to friends houses. My only real gripe, is the lack of a foldable magnetic cover to use as a stand. Instead, innocn chose to go with a ditatchable magnetic kick stand, which is serviceable, but can be flimsy if your not taking care to align the magnet with the circle on the back of the display.

Also, take note to use the proper charges. The monitor includes a USB type c and micro hdmi to full hdmi cable, but hidden underneath the cardboard that holds the display and video cables is a very important power brick and a cable specifically labeled for power. Be sure to use the correct cable and power brick for the best experience.

Great monitor on a budget. Freesync is great if you'll be gaming, top two thirds are crazy thin, software is easy to navigate if you can get over the button layout. I found I could overclock it (HDMI is needed for anything over 60hz) to 85, but it may just be hardware lottery there. Color accuracy is unmatched at this price point, no mainstream mounting but stand is decent and sturdy. Note that it does not come with HDMI so you'll need to pick one of those up if you don't want to use the ancient vga format.
What appears to be good price now I know the reason.

Overall a fantastic monitor for the purchase price of $800. I see the price fluctuating pretty frequently, so how much it costs you may be different.

Pros:
- 160hz for an ultrawide is excellent.
- Works great with gsync.
- Color quality is great.
- Works great over HDMI and DisplayPort with windows.
- Low motion blur.
- No noticeable input lag.
- The HDR color depth is awesome. Just disable the local dimming or it can get annoying.
- Decent backlight bleed and IPS glow for an IPS panel. Definitely less bleed than the other 3 IPS panels that I've owned, and the backlight bleed is fairly evenly distributed.

Neutral:
- Managing an OSD (on screen display) with just the nipple is passable. As I switch inputs pretty often, my Alienware monitor let me set up a shortcut to double tap a dedicated button on the monitor to switch between HDMI and Displayport. This monitor requires you to always navigate the OSD menus (even though it doesn't take too much to switch inputs).
- The curve is subtle and is just "fine". No complaints, nothing great about it either.

Cons:
- Does not work properly at 160hz at full resolution (1440p ultrawide) when connected to my Macbook over HDMI. The Macbook will only allow 50hz, meaning everything feels choppy. It's a borderline-acceptable framerate for text-only workflows or just browsing the web.
- Overall build quality is just "good", which kinda sucks for an $800+ monitor. The plastic on the back feel flimsy.
- Local dimming is TERRIBLE. I highly recommend disabling local dimming but enabling HDR for the extra color depth.

Conslusion:
An excellent monitor for productivity and gaming, with just a couple minor gripes that prevent it from being perfect IMO.

Took some tuning to get the color right. Apparently, you need Display Port to Display Port wire to make 240hz work, or in my case, USB-C to DP wire. With HDMI you only get 120hz. It's a pretty good monitor.

I just wanted a big monitor for office use. I like to split screen and with this monitor I feel no need for a second monitor like my previous setup. I do a fair amount of media watching, but not gaming.

The multi-function toggle button at bottom for power and settings is ingenious though it takes a little getting used to. How do I turn this thing off???? But after a few times it's easy.

I am extremely pleased with this monitor for my purposes. Happy as a lark.

I was in the market for an ips, 1440p, high refresh rate monitor for gaming. I initially bought an MSI mag274qrf‑qd. However, that monitor had a stuck pixel in the middle of the screen, distracting backlight bleed even during gaming, but ips glow wasn’t that bad. So I returned that monitor, and avoided it because of the quality control issues.

When I bought this asus monitor, I found a cluster of three stuck pixels towards the left side of the screen (two very tiny ones around a bigger one), ips glow was significantly more worse on this unit than the MSI one, but backlight bleed wasn’t that bad on this unit and was not distracting like the MSI one. So I returned this monitor.

I read other reviews. I have an ATI 4290 on-board graphics using the ATI catalyst software.

-'s - The ASUS Quick start documentation is weak. There is major room for improvement here.

Other thoughts -
If you have two video cables plugged in, you will have your computer thinking you have two displays. Windows will autodetect and configure accordingly.

After discovering the multicables issue, I completely removed my vga cable from the PC and rebooted.

I got the same "black border issue" described by other people. I also got an oversized view which meant the computer was giving me an image too big for the monitor.

I fixed the situation:
1 Open the ATI Catalyst Control Center software.
2. Click the 'Graphics' drop-down menu, then click on 'Desktops & Displays'.
3. Then click the small picture of a monitor at the bottom left of the window and click 'Configure'.
4. This will bring you to the 'DTV' options screen. From here you will see the 'Scaling Options' tab which is the 3rd tab from the left.
5. You can click 'Scaling Options' you will see the Underscan/Overscan slider to adjust/eliminate the black borders around the screen.
6. I set my slider to Zero.

I also needed to use the remote control's middle button in the 2nd row to resize the screen to a 16:9 aspect ratio HD picture. Many people are describing symptoms that are indicative of a default 4:3 aspect ratio. Use the remote to switch it for HDMI.

The Cocopar 17" portable display is the best of breed as of this review.  I travel with a Thinkpad and two external panels (one on USB-C and one on HDMI), and have been through many external panels.  At one time, in the FHD era, the ASUS MB-168 series was king of the roost, but in the UHD/4K world, Cocopar's 17" is the quality leader.  Not only does this panel offer mini-DP in addition to the usual USB-C and HDMI, the three cables packed with it are high quality.  I've had several USB-C cables that came with other panels fail ... not what you want when in a hotel room working under deadline.  The only improvement would be to include a single 9H tempered glass screen protectors rather than two plastic screen protectors... that would be a first among external panel makers (all of which either include no screen protector or plastic screen protector(s).  You'll want a larger folding stand, like the Humixx Tablet Stand, ASIN B07S73HVW5, for this beast.  The smaller folding stands topple too easily, and I must have the panel above the desk (if you've ever worked in a Japanese or Hong Kong business hotel room, you'll know why).  This review is not a "free gift for a review" one.

Early versions of this monitor had bad firmware which caused serious problems with it not waking up right. However, newer versions of the firmware are okay. To tell if you have the good firmware, turn the monitor off. Then, while holding down the 2nd and 4th soft buttons from the top, turn it back on. When all the soft buttons' LEDs light up, let go and then tap the top button. A Factory Menu will appear. Look at the number under "R" in the bottom left. If it is 1019R or later, then you have good firmware. If it's before 1019R, then you probably have bad firmware.

I once had a version of this monitor which, when it tried to wake up out of sleep, continuously flashed the "Going into power saving mode" notification box, almost as if it was in an infinite loop of trying to go in and out of power savings mode. That monitor also had backlight bleeding in the left corners which was too bright to be acceptable. So I returned it for another one, which was still revision A00, manufactured in December 2013, but which has the 1019R firmware.

Dell unfortunately has not been willing to let customers upgrade firmware on their own, and has also not been willing to send back the same monitors to customers who send them in for warranty service, choosing instead to send them back refurbished monitors, which might have other defects or wear.

So I recommend that you buy this monitor at a brick and mortar store which matches Amazon's prices (many big name ones do), so that you can verify the monitor's firmware revision in the store itself before you buy it, and so that you can easily exchange the monitor if it doesn't work. Amazon has good return policies, but when there's a good chance you are going to get a bad product, it's best to stick to brick and mortar stores.

For Windows users who have problems with this monitor waking up badly, here's how I fixed it. Note: This only fixes it if the monitor goes to sleep; you still might get windows scrunched up in a 640x480 window, if you remove the monitor cable or power the monitor off completely, and then turn it back on or plug in back in:

Make a system restore in case something goes wrong and you need to restore previous settings. Go to Control Panel / System / System protection and click "Create".

Run regedit.exe.

Go to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\GraphicsDrivers\Configuration.

Look for the Dell monitor configuration (DEL4093...). There might be more than one, but that's okay. In my case, nothing but the key names and SetIds were different between the DEL4093 entries.

Right-click on one of the DEL4093 entries, and select Export. Save the .reg file somewhere (I usually choose Desktop).

While keeping regedit open, use your favorite editor (or Notepad) to edit the .reg file.

Look for the SIMULATED_* entry next to the DEL4093 entry in the registry. Copy its key name by right-clicking on it and "Copy Key Name".

In the editor, change all occurrences of the DEL4093* name to this full SIMULATED_* key name. This will be in three key paths (...\DEL4093*, ...\DEL4093*\00, ...\DEL4093*\00\00), and in the SetId.

Save the .reg file, and then in regedit, Import the file (File / Import). (Or just click on the .reg file from Explorer.)

Reboot your PC.

What we are doing is telling SIMULATED_* to exactly match the high-res Dell monitor settings, so that if the PC loses the EDID signal from the UP3214Q when it goes to sleep, then it will not change resolution from the Dell settings to the default 640x480.

I feel bad for the people who got bad firmware, and for the way Dell treated them, but if you can get one with a 1019R or newer firmware, and work out a few software issues with Windows, then you will get a very good monitor for the price. With 99% Adobe RGB and a much lower cost than the ASUS, it's worth going through a little bit of software hassles, as long as you get one with the newer firmware.

EDIT: If you plug both a DisplayPort and an HDMI cable between your monitor and the video card, and tell Windows that the screens are duplicated, you can avoid the reversion to 640x480 when it's asleep in most cases, because HDMI seems to still receive EDID even when DisplayPort doesn't. Occasionally you might need to power cycle the monitor with its power switch when you wake it out of sleep, but this is much less hassle than having to unplug the power cord or video cable, or experiencing resolution issues or split screens.

Great monitor, i was afraid G-Sync would not work well based on what i have heard online, but it works wonderfully. It works particularly well in games like assassins creed (ubisoft games) that are limited to 60 fps but actually display 61-62 frames. those extra 2 frames create tearing on regular monitors and if you use v-sync then stuttering becomes a problem. G-Sync fixes this perfectly, i have actually been going back to replay games like black flag and far cry for the stabilized frame rate.
For the 144 fps i have been playing Witcher 3, at ultra settings i get about 120 fps (prefer higher quality) but i have changed it to see the full 144 frames in witcher and it is amazing. I know the whole argument about fps (cant see over 60 fps) but i have tested this in a couple games with fps caps set by anther person and i could easily tell you the difference between 60 and 144 frames, a little harder is 120 to 144 but it is still noticeable.

Pro's-
--Easy to build
--144 frames makes all the difference
--G-Sync works perfectly
--Looks good/stand looks nice
Con's-
--Color is a little washed (TN panel)
--Hard to calibrate black (always a little gray, use nvidia control panel color settings)
--Has a fairly long warm up (takes 8 min till color and blacks look right upon wake up)
--Menu settings are a little scares, needs more settings
--Stand is a little too wide
--Speakers are laughably weak, as in you should buy some desktop speakers or headphones

Conclusion- for the price, i was also eyeing a ips 1080p 144 frame monitor without G-Sync....but honestly going to 144 frames for the first time was made so much better by G-Sync. Usually when your setting up a game you have to fiddle around with v-sync and triple buffering and even going into nvidia control panel to set more particular settings like adaptive refresh rate.......with G-Sync you just set your global v-sync to off and your done. The only thing i truly did not like about this monitor was the speakers. They are tiny 2w speakers that preform worse than you could imagine. it's not a huge problem as long as you know about it before you buy, i use headphones most of the time so i was fine. Beside that, i would recommend this monitor to any gamer with the rig to run these frames. If you can afford it, i would also recommend getting the ips version of this monitor for better visuals.

I was recently in the market for a monitor to hook up to a new gaming tower that I had purchased (my last few computers had been all-in-ones), so I took a trip to an electronics chain to see what was available. When I passed by the display in the store that had these LG ultra wide screen monitors I was hooked. I then went home to do research before I purchased. When I did so, I noticed that LG also makes the monitor in a 29". After looking at specs, I was leaning more towards the 29" for the main reason that it had speakers which were 7 watts each, rather than the 3 watts on the 25 inch. I didn't want to have to buy external speakers, so I figured these would sound much better. I got the width measurements of both monitors to make sure they would fit on my desk, and I went to the store to pick up the 29 inch. When I got to the store and saw the monitors again, the 29 inch looked amazing, but it's really, really wide. It was at that time I decided on going with this 25 inch instead. I bought the monitor, got it home, opened it and hooked it up, and I was really impressed.

One of my concerns with this monitor was that I might have an issue getting the wide resolution to work with my computer correctly. It runs Windows 8.1, and when hooked up I had no issues whatsoever. It automatically detected and changed the resolution. I was a pretty happy camper. Now, if you're hooking this machine up to a Mac (I have it hooked up to a MacBook Air AND my Windows 8 PC, since it has multiple inputs), and to get the full resolution out of a MacBook you must use a Mini Displayport to Displayport cable (you can get these for about ten bucks here on Amazon). This will allow the ultra wide resolution on a Mac. I read that you can use HDMI and get the resolution if you buy an application that changes the resolution, but that software was $20, so getting a new cord is cheaper - and easier.

As far as the display itself, if you've never had an IPS display in a monitor, you'll be amazed at the difference in the picture quality, brightness and color. It's VERY vivid. I found myself just staring at my rotating desktop wallpaper images as if I were looking at them in person. They were just gorgeous. My previous all-in-one was a top-of-the-line Dell with an ultra-something display, and seeing them side-by-side there was just no comparison between my old screen and this new one.

My second worry about buying this 25 inch monitor was the speakers, since they were the main reason I was leaning towards the larger one. When I first fired up iTunes and played music, it sounded a little flat. I know I've read in reviews that monitors with built-in speakers are hardly ever very impressive, so I knew this was normal. That's when I went into my equalizer in iTunes, played around with settings and eventually set it to "Treble Booster." When I did the sound really came alive. I was pretty impressed with the speakers built into the base of my previous all-in-one, but these speakers absolutely blew that one away. My all-in-one had speakers that pointed upward, and the ones in this LG monitor point downward, which I think makes a lot of difference, since the sound reverberates off of the desktop. To say I was extremely surprised at the sounds of these speakers is a huge understatement. If you're not impressed when you hook up this monitor, just fiddle with your sound settings (either in iTunes or in control panel); I assure you that you can make them sound amazing.

The only other thing that I was concerned about was the screen height. My previous all-in-one had a 23" regular-shaped screen, and this one, while much wider, is about an inch shorter. I worried that this might make it seem scrunched, but having it so wide completely makes up for it. I barely even realized it when I started using only the new screen. It's just so neat with the ultra wide shape that I didn't even realize it was shorter. In a way it's a little nicer, because when I'm doing things like internet surfing you can see more of the wallpaper on the edges, so it's prettier.

I'm not much of a gamer, but this monitor is pretty awesome with games. My main games are SimCity (the new version) and The Sims 3 (until The Sims 4 comes out). Both of them had no issue whatsoever working with this resolution (you just have to change the resolution in the game settings), and it's so nice to be able to play games like this with the extra width. One thing to keep in mind, though, is that if you have a computer with a struggling video card on games like this, having to output the images to this larger screen will make it a tad bit slower (it takes more power from the graphics card to output this wider screen image). When I first had this monitor hooked up as a second screen to my previous all-in-one (before my new tower arrived in the mail), it was pretty noticeable that it was dragging a bit more. But on my new computer (which has a pretty fancy schmancy graphics card...well, compared to my previous computer) it's not an issue. So that's definitely something to keep in mind!

Overall, I'm super happy with this monitor. I had a few reservations, but, once purchased, didn't have a single issue with anything at all. There are many monitors that sell for more than this price and are made by less popular brands, and LG is very well known for their screens, so this is a great deal for this price. If you are hesitating on buying this one, don't! Go do it...right now...this second...seriously. You'll be super glad you did.

This monitor came in a dented, poorly taped box, but inside that was the real box containing the monitor. I was a little nervous based on the shipping box. When I turned this monitor on after plugging it into an Amazon Basics display port adapter I thought the monitor was shot. The screen kept blinking off and streaks randomly appeared on the screen. I replaced that cable with the display port cable that came with the monitor and it has worked perfectly ever since.

I want to note that my monitor came with a standard USA plug, unlike what some reviewers have reported.

Physically, the monitor is sleek and attractive. The joystick control is wonderful after spending years trying to control select/1/2 buttons on the side of my old monitors. There is one poor design choice here, the power on light is part of the joystick which is located under the monitor so if you need to verify the monitor is on you have to cup your hand under the joystick to see if it is lit.

My initial impression was how bright the screen was, I needed sunglasses to avoid retina burn. The screen is perfectly sharp, and after using it for a few days before going back to work I found the fancy monitors at work seems really fuzzy in comparison. Images are particular sharp and vibrant. 4k resolution is definitely an improvement. With this resolution comes some quirks. I had to scale windows 10 up to 150% or everything was just too small. Even after doing that, the chrome browser tabs are hard to read...not the monitors fault but Googles for not allow font changes on the tabs.

The software that came with the monitor is a complete letdown. I was looking forward to using PIP to monitor a second computer while working on the main machine, but I guess I didn't understand the propaganda because the PIP feature only puts another window in its little box. Essentially, making it just the same (or almost the same) as a corner drag in windows 10. The other features of the software weren't worthy of keeping it on my machine. Now, the PBP feature of the monitor works but merely splits the screen in half so neither side is terribly usable because of the reduced font sizes. The hope of a useful displaying of two sources fell short and is disappointing.

As for drivers, I already have an AMD graphics card and when I tried to install a downloaded windows 10 driver from LG is said successful but took all of 1 seconds to run. I assume this means my driver was current.

General work, programming, word processing is phenomenal but gaming is a disappointment.

Minecraft: Moving around caused the screen to resemble old 8mm video where there would be flashes between frames. When I don't move the display looked great, but moving gives me a headache. Switching to a lower resolution doesn't seem to fix this. I'm experimenting with optifine options but nothing has worked well yet.

Civilization 5: Moving around the map was super sluggish and I had to lower the resolution to make it playable. In the reduced resolution the screen is a little blurry.

Galactic Civilization III: Again, moving around was sluggish. In this case I was able to select low-res textures and that solve the problem with no visible differences. To date, this game handles the 4k resolution the best.

Guild Wars 2: When the character selection screen popped up I said a big WOW. The details of my characters was amazing. I couldn't wait to jump into the game. Unfortunately, the game was slower, not so much sluggish but my characters moved just slower enough to notice. The big disappointment was the building and distant images. Everything was washed out and missing details. When I reduced resolution the details reappeared, but this was a letdown for sure.

Now with all the games, I'm considering the issue to be more with my graphics card (XFX AMD Radeon HD 7750 1GB GDDR5 ) rather than the monitor. I'm certainly asking the card to handle a heck of a lot more pixels per frame.

The monitor is great, but my expectations were diminished by the useless PIP feature and game play issues. At the time of this writing, the monitor is also very expensive so you'll have to weigh the price to awesomeness yourself. If your graphics card is up to the task, I can't see where you would have any issues using this amazing monitor for gaming.

UPDATE: My monitor broke by itself within 10 months! Actually it could have been earlier but I disregarded the issues as bugs. Firstly the Thunderbolt never worked right. It was extremely finicky and would sometimes work when plugged into one laptop and not the other laptop. Other times the reverse would happen. I figured it was bad drivers of the laptops. Then the Thunderbolt cable completely died and wouldn't work with anything. I got a new aftermarket cable and it worked but still finicky. Recently the monitor would shut off randomly on Thunderbolt and flicker on and off repeatedly. Again I thought it was a compatibility with one laptop. Then, the monitor would do the same thing on Displayport with my desktop computer. I tried every displayport on my high end GPU and the problem persists. I even tried it on my motherboard displayport integrated GPU bypassing my PCIE GPU and I would get the same problem. Sometimes I would be working for hours and then it would suddenly ficker on and off, other times as soon as I turn it on, it would flicker on and off. The only way to fix it would to hard power cycle the monitor with the switch in the back. This is a $1000 monitor. It should be built better.

I purchased two Asus PA329C monitors and returned them both. The PA329C is the cheaper version of this monitor also at 32 inches and 4k AdobeRGB but uses CFL backlight instead of LED and is much dimmer. At first I liked the PA329C because it included a SD card reader and extra USB ports on the side that the PA32UC did not have. However both monitors I purchased had several dead pixels and lots of trapped fibers under the anti-glare film. Dead pixels I can understand but filaments under the anti glare film seems like poor manufacturing.

Hesitantly I decided to spend a little more for the PA32UC thinking that this is a more premium monitor and if Asus fails again I'm just going to give up on Asus for monitors altogether. Finally they delivered. The monitor is big, bright, and well constructed. Best of all, there were no quality issues for once. The screen is magnficent and bright. The colors are very rich and I am very happy with the HDMI, Displayport, and the thunderbolt ports. The thunderbolt ports especially adds a lot of versatility to my work and allows me to plug in my laptops for the big screen experience and charging at the same time.

The good:
The screen quality. The colors are very good and 4k on a 32 inch screen is downright beautiful. Text and graphics are super crisp and sharp.

The brightness: The monitor is super bright and in a dark/dim room whites can be blinding. I have to play around with the brightness settings to a reasonable comfortable rate.

The stand:
The stand is great, it offers so much versatility for height, tilt, and and rotation. The stand is very high quality, metal and sturdy. It's a super attractive stand for any table.

The bad:
The thunderbolt 3 port only delivers a maximum of 60 watts to a laptop. While good for a slim ultrabook, a powerful laptop will loose power while in use even while "charging."

As a few others have pointed out, the rear joystick is very flimsy and wobbly. It doesn't feel sturdy at all and they jiggle when you touch them, longevity may be a problem. The rear buttons as well need seem like a cheap afterthought and difficult to use. The overall joystick and button placements are not ergonomically placed for optimal use.

The local dimming can take some getting used to. While it's awesome to have lots of local dimming zones, the "glow" of light items on a dark background is very distracting. While photo editing just dragging my mouse cursor around a dark patch creates a halo around everything. Additionally the local dimming is noticeably SLOW, when the scene changes quickly the local dimming can't catch up and there's a second where light areas are dark because the LEDs behind it aren't on. Although the local dimming does produce some excellent blacks, I wish there were better local dimming performance.

It takes a significant time for the monitor to "warm up" or turn on. With desktops for example, if you wanted to get into bios, you need to hit "delete" and "f1" during the post screen. The problem is you can never see the post screen because it takes a long time to turn on. By the time the monitor turned on I was waiting at the windows login screen. I've tried turning the power saving settings to low and this still isn't solved.

Overall I am very happy with the monitor. Some improvements would be better rear buttons and joystick, and I'm sorry that the SD card reader from the previous version was left out. However the Thunderbolt C port somewhat makes up for it.

Update June 2021:

I purchased two of these displays in October. As stated in the original review, one of them has failed completely and will not display an image. It was promptly replaced by Monoprice support, but unfortunately that was not the end of my problems and the replacement display also developed a defect.

I bought this 20" Dell E2014H to replace an old 17" Dell monitor that stopped working. Initially, I was considering the smaller 19" E1914H until I learned that model is only 1366x768 resolution, which was inadequate for my needs and, on a screen that size, is only 85 pixels per inch (PPI). This means the individual pixels that make up the image are larger, which translates into a coarse, pixelated image, kind of like looking through a screen door (a fact pointed out by several reviewers who bought that model). The 20" E2014H model I bought sports a 1600x900 resolution and 92 PPI, which is much better. However, beware that as you increase the PPI, this also makes on-screen text and icons smaller, which may pose readability issues for some people. I personally consider 90 PPI to be the lowest usable pixel density for a desktop monitor before image quality begins to suffer.

The E2014H is by no means a top-of-the-line monitor but for the price, it offers reasonably good performance for everyday use and was pretty much what I expected of a monitor in this price range. Photo buffs and graphics designers will probably be disappointed to know that the E2014H is not an IPS monitor but rather utilizes the more-common TN panel technology. That said, I found the image quality to be quite good (much better and brighter than my old monitor); photos looked sharp and vivid and, thanks to the better contrast ratio, black text appeared richer and kind of "popped," a quality that made for a better reading experience. However, like most TN monitors, viewing angles are somewhat limited (especially in the vertical direction) and can make for unwanted color shifts when viewing the screen from extreme angles. Fortunately, the E2014H includes an adjustable stand which lets you tilt (but not raise or lower) the screen to an angle that works for you.

Like most all monitors made in the last 5 years or so, the E2014H features LED backlight technology, which is superior to the CCFL backlight technology used in older monitors; LEDs are brighter, last longer, and use less power (14 watts for this 20-incher versus ~35 watts for my old 17" CCFL-backlit monitor). The E2014H sports a dual DVI/VGA input (but no HDMI) which should suit the majority of PC users. I had to use the VGA input, as my PC lacked DVI. As with most modern monitors, the E2014H utilizes a 16:9 widescreen format. Those switching from an older, squarish 4:3 or 5:4 screen may find that the wider 16:9 format takes a little getting used to but I didn't find the switch too difficult and I quickly adjusted to the new screen format.

The E2014H is housed in an all-black plastic case that feels reasonably solid although the stand feels somewhat flimsy and wobbly. A lighted power button is located at the lower-right corner of the monitor. When in low-power mode, the light slowly fades in and out almost like the monitor is "breathing," which is a cool effect. Above the power button you will find four tiny, unmarked control buttons; there are no symbols or icons to indicate their intended function which can make using them confusing.

Setup was fairly straightforward; simply assemble the stand, plug in the cables, and off you go. It took me all of 10 minutes to unbox the monitor and get everything put together. The only snag I ran into was getting my old PC to properly support the 16:9 screen resolution, an issue that was fixed easily enough with a video-driver update. I should also mention that the drivers supplied on the CD-ROM included with the monitor refused to install on my Windows XP SP2 system, although this is a minor issue as the monitor works just fine without the additional drivers. Included in the box you will find the monitor unit, a stand assembly, a 3-prong AC power cord, video cables for both the DVI and VGA inputs, a driver CD, and a basic setup guide.

Overall, the E2014H is a capable monitor and a good value for the money. It would make a good choice for those looking to upgrade or replace an old monitor on a tight budget. I give it 4.5 out of 5 stars.

This review is for the MB16AC -- the one with the USB-C port. I am using this with a 27" iMac, and it's simply plug-n-play.

KEY OBSERVATIONS
-------------------------------
- Sleek design and love the aesthetics.

- Most reviews I've read made a big deal about the dimness. At first I didn't think it would be a problem, but I do notice it the more I use it and wish it was brighter. I use it indoors though and use my iMac at about 60-70% brightness, so I can live with it.

- Many have issues with the glossy screen, but it matches the glossiness of my iMac and I like it.

- I initially thought the menu navigation wasn't too bad, but it is annoying. On the bright side, I won't be messing with the menu too much.

- Completely plug-n-play! I love USB-C.

WHY 3 STARS?
------------------------------
- There are 2 buttons on the lower left corner of the screen. The first button does not register presses very well. At this price point, you can get a full sized screen so I think we are paying a premium for the form factor and the convenience. So I think it's not too much to expect better quality assurance because I purchased this over the cheaper AOC expecting a higher quality.

- The left button not registering not annoying enough I requested an exchange. On the replacement, however, it was the right button that would not register. In both of the cases, I could feel that the buttons weren't situated correctly when I pressed it. I expect more for the price.

- The USB port is located on the left side of the monitor. On my iMAC, the USB-C port is on the right side of the monitor. I like to place my monitor to the left of me, but the cable that ASUS provided is too short. Which means I have to spend $ and purchase another cable.

This is "just ok".

I bought this monitor to mostly use for work and gaming. I do a lot of coding, so I have to look at a lot of lines of code, and the ghosting really bothered me when it came to scrolling through text, since you could very noticeably see a trail behind pretty much anything.
Even after tuning the monitor overdrive and other settings, I could not reduce the amount of inverse ghosting and black smearing that I saw. I might be overly sensitive to it, but at times, the ghosting made me feel motion sick. This was pretty much only noticeable when using the monitor for office use, and only sometimes noticeable when playing games.

Pros:
- Contrast ratio is amazing, so blacks are very black on this monitor.
- It's ultrawide, so you get a lot of screen real estate.
- It's amazing for playing games.
Cons:
- Black smearing was very noticeable to me.
- Ghosting was pretty noticeable on scrolling text.
- Response times didn't feel that good.

Conclusion:
If you aren't very sensitive to ghosting and black smearing, then this monitor is probably a really good buy, otherwise I would avoid this monitor.

Bought this monitor 2 years ago from Dell's website.

Pros:
- After realizing in 2008 that I could get any other 22" monitor from Newegg or Fry's for $180, I wondered why this Dell model was MSRP'd at $230 and on sale for $200. This monitor has an AMAZING STAND. This is often neglected as people look for "how many inches, how many pixels, how many lumens, what's the contrast ratio, does it have HDMI, how many watts...".
The base is very sturdy. My roommate is one of those "I got a good deal on Newegg" guys, and his monitor stand is tiny, and he has to be careful not to bump his monitor or it falls over. you get what you pay for. The fact that this monitor hasn't dropped in price in 2 years says something: it's a great monitor and it's in high demand. It can swivel on its base, which means you don't have to clean off your desk to swivel your screen. It can tilt back and forward. You can raise the monitor by up to 8 inches. The monitor can be quickly rotated to provide portrait viewing, helpful for reading digital books or viewing web pages. This stand is incredible.
- DVI output. Buy a HDMI-DVI cable off Amazon for $6 and you will forget that this monitor doesn't have HDMI.
- Good picture quality for a non-videophile.

Cons:
- The default color setting has overly-bright reds. The monitor settings can easily be changed, but I've read that sometimes monitor manufacturers will intentionally make colors too bright or dark if one of the colors is prone to premature dimming/dying. So far I haven't had any problems.
- This monitor was made back in the 16:10 days before manufacturers realized that TV shows and YouTube videos are filmed in 16:9. But it also means that a full-screen video does not compete with the taskbar.

- Product broke within 2 years.
- Terrible value when compared to better monitors like the Samsung CRG9
- Doesn’t work well with MacOS
- When you buy from Amazon, no one tells you but Amazon has registered the monitor to themselves and you have to submit a transfer ownership form. Dell uses this two years later to deny your warranty.
- Screen randomly cracked.
TL;DR: don’t buy this monitor at all, don’t buy a Dell, and certainly don’t buy a Dell from Amazon, you’re better off flushing your cash down a toilet.

I bought the 23.8-inch to use as the main monitor in a dual-montior setup. This is a very nice monitor especially for the price. I like Acer monitors and have bought them in the past for their price and quality. This was easy to put together with the stand, is light, and the colors are vibrant. I did have to do some small calibration to get the coloration to my liking, but it was very simple to adjust. I haven't seen any issues with backlight leaking or dead pixels as some others have.

The only con I can think of is that the monitor is only compatible with its packaged stand for mounting, so if you want a verticle mount, or to mount on some other apparatus, this probably isn't doable. It doesn't affect me because I don't plan to do either.

The product was delivered very quickly. But overall, it is not a monitor that I will keep. The monitor leans slightly forward and it cannot be adjusted. That may work if the monitor was elevated, but it's quite annoying and not comfortable to view while sitting on a desk. Please note, there is no audio on this monitor.
So far this monitor has been great. I'm glad I got this years ago and I actually don't plan on replacing this yet until 4k monitors come down in price or I can get a monitor with variable frequency.

---> Product Packaging - 5/5
The monitor came well packaged and protected. All cables and manuals were wrapped appropriately and the screen was held in sturdy foam.

---> Setup and Ease of Use - 5/5
The portable monitor is easy to setup and use. The instructions clearly explain how to get everything connected and ready to go. It can be a little tricky if you don't read the instructions, as I could see how some may not understand that depending on the connection you do or don't need the power adapter to be plugged in.

---> Design and Performance - 5/5
I'm impressed with the design. It is lighter and thinner than I expected. It is easy to carry this around, and I've incorporated this into my meetings where I am often having to both present and take notes concurrently. This lets me track and control my presentation on my main screen and take notes on this portable one or vice versa. I really value the versatility of having an extra monitor in the meetings I lead. The folding stand took a minute or two to figure it out, but once I got the hang of it, standing up the monitor is quick and easy.

The screen is both clear and bright, and as an IPS monitor, it has really good color. Other than meetings I really have been enjoying using this monitor to connect to my smartphone and sharing media with friends and family. I also totally missed that my phone goes into a desktop mode when connected to this screen. I connect a bluetooth mouse and keyboard and I've got myself another workstation. The Typc C and Mini HDMI provide good options to connect to various devices and I haven't had any issues. The audio speakers are decent. They aren't going to win any awards, but they suffice for basic needs. Also this monitor has a lot of menu options to adjust the picture and other aspects of the monitor.

---> Build Quality - 5/5
The monitor feels well made and durable. Nothing loose and no weird sounds. The buttons on the side have a good solid feel and the connections are tight.

---> Final Assessment
I recommend this monitor for anyone looking for a portable monitor. It has good picture quality for the price, is light enough to carry anywhere, and has a nice feel. Just be aware you will need to be tethered to the power adapter unless you have a Thunderbolt or other advanced connection that also serves up power in addition to the display signal.
Many different modes that are easily switched through, overall my favorite monitor. Great product for the price, would highly recommend!
This is a great travel monitor.  Perfect for a second monitor on the go.  Multiple connection and power over usb.

I 'm retroactively reviewing this. I've had it for years ( I have two).

used extensively for several years .

TLDR -- the simplest case i can make.
No: Coding/reading text: you want 4k these days at this size, things have improved as far as software support.
No: Gaming you need faster refresh. The resolution is good though. 2k is my ideal gaming monitor at this size
Yes: Color grading: static content this is a good choice. It 's also good for Movies, it' has real blacks. zero glow light bleed. You wouldn't know if it was on if you had nothing on a black background (it's a va right).

If i needed a Jack of all trades-- hard to recommend-- maybe ips 2-4k depending on GPU and frequency of given activity. I would honestly consider a 28 inch monitor if you don't have the fire power for 4k and you need to do a lot with text rather than a 32. I think you'll be happier and wont' give up as much as you think. That said i think 4k 32 is pretty much where things are going. It's better for productivity. If your not gaming it's' much more clear.

If you don't know what you're doing read up on monitor types. but you probably want an IPS, the color is better. I know that's controversial quality depends on monitor etc i'm telling you i look at a lot of monitors IPS is generally going to be preferred.

The rest:

Pros:

Build: (i mean frame) and fantastic stand, and buttons. I am 44 and have been into computers since the 80's this is without question by far the best built monitor i've ever used. I wish they build more things like this. Just from purely a construction standpoint. I know people want ultra-thin. This is not that. But i'm putting this first because if you want quality this monitor is special. They'll probably discontinue it eventually buy it while you can if you like quality. They should be commended for construction like this.

Stand. Best stand. I love that you can really dial it in esp important since it 's a va. it's great for work too. i won't buy a monitor with a bad stand. Even with my desk attachments i often go back to situations where these micro stands end up coming back into the picture. Hp, dell, apple, lg have various options with good stands. This one is nearly the best i've seen.

Colors: Surpurb. There is a level of color detail that's not present in most monitors. I've an IPS display too, a decent one (mid-range) it can't compete on details of color and banding. Example of what i mean: i can look at a bottle with hues printed on a label, and all the little details really come out. Both are 10 bit displays. The calibration is spot on as well. It's stunning and underrated in that way. This is truly a fantastic work monitor for photo stuff, technically speaking. That said its' also a little washed relatively t something newer. Do i enjoy it. It's ok.

Blacks are Pure. Being a AMVA (va panel) It's a solid va tech. You can easily compare blacks vs an IPS (technically i have a couple of these). it's just completely black no glow. Even good ips displays have this glow right. If you do a lot of working in the dark it's really something to consider.

Details : it's only a WQHD which at this size might as well be a 1080 monitor, but you just see details.

It is NOT a washed-out VA panel. So you don't need to worry about that. But it isn't an ips

@32 we are hitting the limit of a va not being curved. However this monitor is so good i don't even see dimming as i look to the corners.

Viewing angle for a VA is surprisingly good. I would say it's a bit atypical of the tech in that regard. It's not as vibrant as my IPS but it changes far less. usually IPS is the viewing angle champ. This monitor is that good.

Cons

Va: you probably want to be realistic this is not an IPS display. it's not going to quite have the richness that higher/mid end ips displays have. In my experience IPS has this rich glowly vibrant color ya know that's just fun. That's what apple is using by the way IPS. I frankly think IPS is better all around. Especially because viewing angles are more forgiving in general esp for really good ips. If you just want to study color have accuracy and pure blacks (say movies) this is solid. I just think for most people they will prefer IPS. I tend to myself for day-to-day usage. A very good IPs can minimize the black issue too. 300 range like this though i'm not aware of any.

Refresh rate: The refresh is too slow these days for gaming, and i would prefer higher. Refresh makes a huge difference 120 being kind of the sweet spot there.

MY PERSONAL MAIN ISSUE

Resolution: Ok so the monitor is huge @ 32 inches, After all this time i've decided WQHD just isn't what i'd choose in terms of PPI (given the improved support for 4k from when i bought this) . At the time i purchased this Windows, Linux was a bear working with 4k. Multimonitor support more so. I only started using mac in the last couple years so i can't speak to that, but now all platforms including mac, windows and linux have great 4k support. For working with a lot of text with this monitor, it's a drag. It my main issue since i code a lot. I use clear type/linux/mac etc with this. But i'm telling you next to a 4k monitor you'll definitely wonder how anyone can work with SpreadSheets or code with this as compared to that. It's the same with any of the 2k monitors. It's just not enough resolution for reading. I'm 44 and my eyes get fatigued a lot easier than they did in my 30;s. Every bit of Clarity even with good glasses helps. It' raises my enjoyment and frankly my ability to read and retain information. To give you some metrics to consider here is the PPI pixels per inch which is indicative Clarity and precision you'll get: Even with Aliasing you can only do so much, and this is what you have to work with.

32 inch monitor

1080p 69PPI
1440p 92 PPI
4k 138 PPI

Frankly the best reading experience where most people can't tell is over 200. This is why high end monitors shoot for about 220+ ergo ipad. Your phone is > 300. But gpus are holding us back. 8k is where a monitor of this size with proper software support so menus aren't small (like scaling well which most oses do now) would be ideal. That said >120 is considered good. As in you will really start seeing improvements between anything less than that and it diminishes after 120. With aliasing 120 can get pretty close to the quality you'd see with 220 for most people. So my case here is if your doing text stuff this isn't the right monitor as 32 and 2k just don't mix.

This monitor is basically a 24 inch 1080p monitor, but bigger. I hated 1080 monitors when they were a thing and this is basically that albeit with great quality. It is what it is. If you like that there you go, but for reading i don't. Is that a fault of the product, no, but it's something to consider when making a purchase.

here's the thing and why i wrote this. Picking a monitor really depends on what you're doing.
Perhaps this is more than a review of this monitor but it's some context. If you're buying a monitor carefully consider what you're doing. And if you only really do one thing buy a monitor for that. You'll be much happier i think. For what this is it 's exquisite even in 2021. Samsung this is how you build a monitor. I wish the stuff they were making now had this quality.

It's really kind of a shame they haven't updated this model to 4k. As i type this on my macbook pro i'm realizing just how much less detail there is in the color of some of these images as compared to this monitor. Especially when you look at dark-mid tones.

The Yiynova 19 is a decent alternative to the Wacom Cintiq line. I've been using it for a few days for my freelance artwork and it's certainly very fun to use and feels more natural than having to gaze at your screen while you move a pen, out of your line of sight, to control your mouse cursor.

The screen is large and detailed. The colors as well as the brightness, lose clarity as you change viewing angles. It's clearest when viewed head on. Although annoying, it still functions well enough at any angle.

The drivers on the included disk are out dated and conflict with any previously installed Wacom drivers on my computer. To solve this I had to not only visit a third party site for updated drivers, I also had to uninstall the drivers for me previous tablet. (The Wacom Bamboo MTE 450.) After a few minutes and a screen calibration, I had everything up and running.

I keep my Yiynova 19 on a table top and the included "VESA" stand holds the tablet without a worry of falling over. I like that it came installed on the back already rather than having to remove the previous stand that I'm used to seeing in review videos.

I use paint tool SAI for my work, and this tablet has increased my working speed dramatically. It's fun to use and productive. I'm happy with my purchase.

With a Display Port Cable, you can enable FreeSync which will bump it up to 75hz even with a GeForce card.

It does wobble some when I have my G29 attached to my desk and I'm doing some quick cornering, but not horribly so.

The colors are pretty rich, and the curvature is nice, but that's all I can speak positively about. The claim of 1ms response is criminally meaningless. I have a 6ms (non-gaming) monitor that I've fallen back on that exhibits far less persistence than this thing. 144/165 Hz means nothing if every frame bleeds into the next (and the next, and the next). I have two E22's and one F22(ultra thin bezel) and they are great displays. There is a price value on these though and that is 89.99-119.99+Shipping OR taxes. I would not pay more then 130(total) for any of these displays.

Audio is a huge con, so do not buy these if you want them for the speakers. You can DYI add on a custom audio setup if you want (12w RMS peak, so laptop speakers - but lots of choices here if you pull from Dell gaming laptops from Ebay) but cost eval that into the total cost of your purchase (Parts+Time).

The panels are pretty good (72% sRGB) IPS with good darks and lights, falls right in the price point. They range from 280nit to 320nit depending on the panel (my E22's both have different panels...), so plenty bright.

They all support Click DCC software, but the F22 does not work with the color sliders - only brightness and input selection from software.

Finally - These make good for a plug and play 'tv' setup where you want to hook up a console (Nintendo switch, PS4/PS5, Xbox) for a small room/media area setup. They are light weight, work on any monitor stand (VESA mounting!!), and are super thin to boot (while Bezel, not just the LCD area).

Usually when you run a monitor lower than it's native res, it looks really grainy. This one, while not as sharp as the full res, does not look all that grainy.
My big problem when I first plugged it in was that my mouse was extremely jumpy. I look all over for help and solutions and found none. Everyone said it was the mouse driver, which I knew wasn't true because when I lowered to 1280x1024, the mouse worked perfectly. Dropping down to lower widescreen resolutions didn't help though. So I spent some time messing around in all the various settings apps and discovered that nvidia, by default, was treating this as a television. Meaning refresh rate was 24hz! No wonder my mouse was so choppy. So I changed it from TV to monitor mode and it brought the refresh rate up to 30hz at 3440×1440 which was better, but still choppy. So I switched to 2560x1080 and that brought the refresh rate to 60hz. And now my mouse is perfect again.
So, I guess I have to get a new computer to take full advantage of this monitor. But Dell or nvidia or microsoft or somebody should change this monitor profile to default to monitor mode. Not TV mode.

For those concerned about the ghosting issue, it is real and it is no big deal. It goes away pretty quickly (30 seconds or so) and only shows up when you have a high contrast window/display and then you cover it with a dark one. You'll see a subtle outline where there were high contrast points. You get used to it and it's the price you pay for an otherwise awesome display at this price point.

The other notable downside is the button placement... on the back, not the bottom or front, of the screen. That makes it *REALLY* hard to get to the buttons, particularly when you've got stuff around the monitor. It makes it harder to use with multiple inputs and things like that.

But besides that, this is a real winner of a screen.

One note of warning: If you're using this with an older computer, make sure it can output 4K or have a plan of how you're going to upgrade it to get that.

Review from the tech geek in the immediate family:

For reference, comparisons are being made to the 4 year old Asus VG278 27in/144hz gaming monitor I've been using as my primary monitor.

The good: The increased refresh rate is noticeable, and the color depth is as good as a TN monitor can be. The monitor comes with an easy to use built-in menu which lets you quickly configure settings, including color palette, sharpness, and contrast, based on some configurable pre-made profiles. The curve makes the monitor feel a little bit bigger than a flat monitor, though it's right on the lower end of where a curve would actually help at all. It comes with a DisplayPort cable, and has HDMI ports as well for additional inputs.

The bad: The stand is, quite frankly, primitive. It's a flat tripod stand, and the only adjustments you can make are to the tilt of the screen--I had to put it on a small stack of textbooks and an old plastic dice box to get it stable at the right height for my desk setup. It could be fine for someone who's alright with looking downwards at a screen, but it takes external support for any sort of height adjustment. Compare this to the VG278, which has a swiveling, circular base as well as built in height and tilt adjustment. On top of that, it would take a very powerful machine to max out the monitor's 240hz refresh rate, making this a monitor best suited for enthusiasts building PCs in the $2000-2500 range--who probably have better options as far as monitors.

My overall rating is a 3/5: it's a good monitor on its own merits, but the curve is a bit gimmicky, the price is a bit high for what it offers, and the stand is uninspiring to say the least.

I bought this monitor for a development area extension of my desk, and received the monitor before I purchased the desk. It is meant to be an area where I can switch between my Surface Pro 3 and Macbook Pro (15" Retina, Mid 2014).

Macbook: Connecting to OS X works PERFECTLY. As soon as you plug it in, you will be delighted to see that it recognizes it as a Retina display and sets the resolution to "Looks like 1920x1080", making everything as clear and crisp as the built-in Retina display, at 60 hertz refresh rate, too. The scaling options available for the built-in display also apply to this, and you can select a few awkward numbered (but great-looking) resolutions, one of which includes "Looks like 2560 x 1440". The color reproduction is also noticeably superior to my other monitors. The included mDP to DP cable works perfectly for connecting from either Thunderbolt port to this display.

Surface Pro 3: Running the Windows 10 Insider Preview build 10240, I encounter just a few issues in connecting this monitor to the Surface. I knew going in that the Surface was NOT capable of 4K @ 60hz, only 4K @ 50hz, which isn't a big deal. Plugging the monitor in, it began running at 2560 x 1440, which was awkward, to say the least. It took about an hour of searching online to find out that 1. You must have Intel's graphics drivers installed and a custom resolution in Intel's utility set for 3840 x 2160 with a 50hz refresh rate and the last drop-down to "CVT-RB". 2. You must change the monitor's multi-stream settings to "Secondary". If you do step 2 without step 1, the monitor will run in 3840 x 2160 @ 30hz.

All in all, the only downside to this monitor is the anti-glare coating. Whites look greyish and textured compared to other monitors. It's extremely overbearing of an anti-glare coating. I was looking into removing it but didn't want to void the warranty or damage the monitor, so glued on it stays.

This is my second UP2414Q, I had to return the first one due to a dead DisplayPort input, and now I'm having to return a second one due to tiling/display issues. If you plan to use the full resolution on the DisplayPort at 60Hz refresh rate, I really do not recommend this monitor. Google around, this monitor is infamous for it's poor DisplayPort 1.2 support, you have to go into the menu to enable displayport 1.2, but you can't even go into the menu to enable it if you don't have the display functioning on a device. Some very poorly thought out design decisions. If you change resolutions often (going into full screen games, for example, or switching to a different computer) the display sometimes locks up and you must power it off and back on to have it functioning again. The tiling of the two internal displays sometimes doesn't work properly, so half the screen is wonky. All of these problems don't happen if you use displayport 1.1 or use HDMI. But, if you ask me... what is the point of this monitor in a lower resolution and lower refresh rate? It's like putting a bad engine into a sports car.

After a lot of research, I found this great IPS monitor that has all the essentials I was looking for: ultra wide screen, IPS, curved and USB-C connectivity. It immediately worked perfectly with my M1 Mac mini as well as my laptop from work (an HP EliteBook 845 G8 which stays charged through the USB-C plug, so no power cord is needed). The picture quality is beautiful...images don't appeared warped by the curvature as I was afraid they might. I don't use it for serious gaming, so I can't comment on that. The monitors stand is wide and takes up a lot of desk space, so you might want to consider a desk mounted monitor arm (the WALI Single Monitor Gas Spring Desk Mount works well for me).

I used this to replace my old LG 245WP monitor, a 24" IPS monitor, which had some ugly pressure marks on it.  I was expecting the same great color I had been used to, but when I plugged the ASUS in, I was blown away.  I use a Spyder3Pro colorimeter with ColorEyesPro software to calibrate all my displays (I do lots of photography), and I was amazed by the vibrancy I had been missing in my photos.  Sections of color that I though were clipped by oversaturation were revealed to have even more detail hiding.  Even certain colors on sites I visit regularly went from looking like normal to being quite vivid.  I'd say the biggest improvements were in green, red, and magenta. It was sort of like comparing something printed on regular printer paper vs looking at a magazine page.  A great monitor for an exceptional price, backed by an extensive warranty.

Recently I have acquired the New Samsung 27" SR75 monitor, and I must say, It was a great investment on my behalf, Not only is the Monitor relatively affordable, but the build quality possess a slick and distinguished design. Having the Ability to set up a 27" Monitor and not compromise any Desk space is a huge Benefit; especially for the average student, Employee, Home user, etc. The set up is very simple and straightforward, Adjusting the monitor settings is easy and simple to navigate through using the power toggle button; and If the monitor isn't needed, you can simply prop the screen at its highest point or bring the screen closer to the user for a more engaging experience. The quality of the 1440p display is a lot better than I had anticipated it to be when looking at the monitor from various angles the screen doesn't diminish like most LED monitors. The factory settings right out of the box are impressive, and do not require any adjusting really, with the exception of turning on the "Eye Saver Mode". Also, the picture quality doesn't lessen when the "Eye Saver Mode" is turned on, the refresh rate on the monitor could be a little better, however, this monitor is well suited for the office environment rather than for gaming use. The Stability of the Monitor could be a little less wobbly, I'm not much of an OCD Freak, however; I do have a cat that lays under the monitor and on occasion bumps into the SR75 making the screen wobble, not a big issue, but could use a little improvement. Another Benefit with this Monitor is the Cable Management, the little channel that is located on the back of the Monitor allows for the Y-cable to elegantly run down the spin of the monitor stand. The Y-cable design is an ingenious mechanism on Samsung's behalf, No one likes having excess cabling to clean up or zip tip together. Samsung took all the ache out of the cleanup portion and made the best Monitor option for Work, Play and space saving.

There are two bugs which I’m hoping will be fixed by a software update.

First is the screen will shift ever so slightly to one corner or another by a few pixels, it’s hard to explain.

Second is the volume will randomly go from 0 to 1 when not touching the remote.

My last gripe is the lack of common ports on the back.

Good monitor with sharp colors, good response for gaming, bright, etc. The ports are very good and the USB-Type C charges any laptop connected while acting as a display. The sound of the speakers could be a bit better but it is good enough.

One thing to note though, if you are using M1 Mac (not sure if this happens with other devices as well, haven't seen it happen on my PS5 or Lenovo laptop), there is flickering. But it might be caused by the variable refresh rate feature in Mac. It automatically sets your refresh rate on the monitor to 45-60Hz. I have started setting mine manually back to 60Hz and haven't seen the issue since. Be aware that the refresh rate goes back to 45-60Hz every time the monitor is reconnected.

************
Mac OS has since released an Update that has fixed the issue. It no longer flickers after connecting the monitor.

There's a lot of reviews on the Internet raving about this monitor which are true. Let me just point out a couple things I dislike about it.

First of all, LG markets this monitor to be MacPro compatible. (I'm on the New Mac Pro 10.9.2.)
LG only provided 2 cables for input (HDMI and Displayport). Displayport of course does not work with MacPro, HDMI only supports 50Hz.
I WANT 60HZ LIKE IT'S ADVERTISED! Why was a thunderbolt or minidisplay to display port NOT included? I had to salvage the cables from my Dell U3014 in order to use this at 60Hz. WHY LG?! Another cable that was not included was the USB uplink cable which add a few more USB ports to connect to the monitor. For $1000, why are these cables not included?

Other than the missing cables, this monitor is amazing. I will not think twice before buying this monitor again.

Ever since I first heard about GSYNC, I had always been half interested in acquiring a monitor that utilized it. However, initially most monitors were far too expensive resulting in a sore lack of budget options on the market. I hate screen tearing and I hate input lag so this truly seemed like something I needed. I had never enjoyed PC gaming before due to the inherent lag between my button presses and the actions on screen.

Finally, I found the Acer Predator XB241H and jumped on it with some blind optimism. There was only one 5-star review that vouched for its awesomeness. I took a risk and I'm happy to say that it totally paid off. This monitor is amazing! Coming from a 60 Hz gaming monitor to this is seriously night and day. I was absolutely floored when I switched my desktop to 144 Hz for the first time. Upon booting up my first game, I was even more ecstatic to realize that there was no input lag delaying my actions!

The actual build quality of the monitor is top-notch as well. I love the fact that the height can be adjusted and the menu buttons are located on the front.

In conclusion, do not hesitate to purchase this monitor. It has made me a believer in PC gaming again.

Please read. I purchased this monitor in NEW condition for roughly $1280.00. The monitor is awesome and amazing. The ultra wide screen, the build design, the RGB, everything is great. However, after using the device for a little over two weeks, I noticed that the bottom 2 corners had severe light bleed when the screen is solid black. Dell's staff was very helpful and polite. Unfortunately, using the service number labeled on the back of the monitor, they explained to me that device had been previously used and registered to a different user. Please be aware.
