Got this monitor as the perfect balance of picture quality (1440p > 1080p), gamer ready (155Hz with 1ms response time), and price, and it has been exactly that. Since I have a 2080ti, I can't imagine a better monitor for gaming, honestly. If I went 4k60, I lose the ability to be competitive. But after 150 fps, I don't notice a difference, so 1080p would be wasted power in my graphics card. A 2080ti can run anything I throw at it at 1440p max settings 110+fps (obviously better on simpler game like fortnite), so this monitor fits just right.
The number 1 problem for me was finding the right color presets. When initially setting it up for the best image, the RTS setting was best, but this caused colors to drag when it was set to 144 or 155Hz (the red pixels would update slightly before the blue). The only 2 presets that didn't do this were FPS (duh) and Cool, and Cool looks much better so I did that.
In hindsight, I kinda wish I got a 4k60 monitor too just to see the difference in resolution, but I would still use this monitor for gaming. And that moment of just saying "ahh, cool" isn't worth the price tag lol.
Bottom line, if you have a 2080ti, get this monitor.

as of 02/2016 this was the way to go for 4K for me.  I am doing photo editing so I need the extra resolution for pinpoint accuracy.  You get that. ONCE you can figure out how to set your video card, some smoke and mirrors, watch a few video tutorials, call ASUS who are about NO HELP at all!  Play around, study the internet and 7 hours later Whala! DisplayPort 3840 X 2160 @60 HZ.  (The obtaining the 60HZ was the hard part to get.)  ASUS Support on the internet gave me some clues but I found it not to be windows 10 friendly.  I called them and was on hold for over 45 minutes 3 times with no answer.  The first (Live Chat) Johnathan Ended the conversation soon as I typed out my problem.  Appilonia Only got me to the you need the Display port cable part and bailed out on me.  And after that They never answered the phone, Live chat or by e mail again.  I figured it out. (By accident so I can't even tell you how)  HOWEVER once it is set up.  What a pleasure to work Photo editing with.  The stand is incredible.  (As well as the picture and controls menu)  The stand allows you to lift the screen from 1.5 inches off the desk to almost 7.5 inches off the desk with ONE finger!  Once you get it up you can Rotate the screen from horizontal to vertical view! This articulation of the screen is a great feature and I might mention it tilts greatly in both vertical and horizontal. You can get the perfect height, angle and orientation with this display!  EASILY!  Paying over $400 i feel it was certainly worth the money, really wish ASUS was more supportive.  I actually always try to buy ASUS because I never have to deal with their support, and good thing.  Gamers you CAN get this to do 60HZ and this will be a great gaming display, but I hope you are better at figuring out how than I was.

I love this monitor. I have two of them on a monitor stand at home. They are big and bright and make computer tasks more efficient. I love the monitor, extremely crisp compared to my other 1080 Sceptre. But the wall mount plate was missing from the box so I couldn't install on my dual monitor mount until I was shipped that part. Oh boy, I hope you don't have to go through customer service because it took three emails across four days just to get them to finally send the part. Not an issue with the monitor itself, but missing parts are a problem nonetheless.

Double the resolution means by default everything in Windows is 1/2 the size. It was too small for me so I configured Windows 8.1 to display everything at 200%. Not everything scales well, but most things scaled better than I expected. Text is sharp and looks like high-quality print. And the number high resolution images available in the OS and online surprised me. True 4K video and images are stunning.

For gaming so far I've played Borderlands 2 and The Witcher 3. Graphics cards have a ways to go before they can render the details and view distance to truly take advantage of 4K, but it was still great. 60fps is a minimum for me, and the 60hz refresh and 5ms response time is enough. TN panels have better response time but there's no way I'd give up the advantages of IPS. The color reproduction is excellent, and stays true at any (normal) viewing angle.

This is the only 4K monitor I've had a chance to use for any significant amount of time, so it's possible there's better out there. This is much more than I've spent for monitors in the past, but so far it's worth it.

If you are looking to upgrade from a 60 hz monitor or considering getting a graphics card but sticking with a lower quality monitor, take this monitor into consideration. I say this because upgrading to a better monitor, whether it is for gaming, work, or general use, a monitor has a huge impact quality-wise. You will notice a difference without having to change any of your computer's internal hardware.

This monitor is a great upgrade for most gamers. You will notice a huge improvement in detail just by getting rid of that outdated one you are using with your current computer. If you do want to get this, I would highly recommend having a graphics card/motherboard that supports DisplayPort video out connections. This allows you to utilize the 144 hz refresh rate the monitor is built for.

I personally like this monitor so much, I bought a second one a year or two after I purchased the first (I multitask a lot). So far they have run well for years (countless hours of gaming, watching movies/tv shows via streaming services, and web browsing) without issues.

My recommendation is, if you do plan on getting this monitor, wait for a sale and get the best deal you can.

When I first got the monitor I thought it was a pretty good deal. Didnt have any issues for the first couple of months. Now whenever I am gaming at 144hz the screen will flicker black for about 5 seconds, and then come back. You can tell that there is something going on with the refresh rate because of how the picture looks around the edge of the screen. Almost shaky. If I turn my settings to have the monitor run at 60hz, there isn't any kind of problem. Was worried that there was something wrong with my graphics card, but tried the monitor on a different PC and had the same issue. This is also the only monitor that it does it to.

There are a few things I dislike about this monitor:
1-The on/off switch location is extremely bad for my situation, this is 2016 not 1990, the switch is located in the rear of the unit and it's extremely difficult to reach for me, and the unit doesn't come with a remote control (I know it's not a TV but if you're going to install the switch in the rear at least have the common sense to have a remote control on/off switch.
2-This monitor has both a regular Display Port and a Mini Display Port, my understanding is that Display Ports carry video and audio signals, on my monitor the Mini Display Port doesn't carry the audio, at least it doesn't work. The audio does work on the Display Port, just not on the Mini port.
3-Resolution, this monitor support 3840x2160 but doesn't support (True/Actual 4K resolution is beyond me), 4096x2160 is true 4K resolution (I mean what's another 256 pixels after you bothered with 8,294,400 pixels already), I don't know it's just weird.
4-The menu control button is also located in the rear and it's a joke at best, I'm sure there are better button systems out there that Philips should have used instead, although a minor complaint, they should still address this mickey mouse button because it's ridiculous.

Other than these minor complaints I like the monitor, it works great and the picture is awesome, but I want to send a message to Philips so they can make these fixes/corrections in the future, it doesn't make any sense what they did and why.

I would still rate this as a buy although with the warnings above.

I have had this monitor for about a month now and I don't know how I went so long without one of these. I have worked on a Macbook (4 years) and then a Macbook Air (next 4 years), and never had an external monitor. I decided recently I needed one to spare my neck and posture. I knew I wanted a monitor that had better than HDMI resolution, like the higher end iMacs. I watched for a while, and when I noticed this Acer at this price point, with this resolution, LED lighting, I bought it. The color, brightness, resolution - everything is gorgeous. No glare from the lighting.

If you want to see what your screen will look like with this monitor, go to Apple Store and look at the top-of-the-line iMac. Its display has the same pixel dimensions. Open up a few browser windows side by side and you won't believe how many windows you can have open and visible at the same time. The only difference is this monitor is 25" diagonal, and the Apple is 27", so everything is maybe 8% smaller than what you would see on the iMac, but still infinitely better than staring at a laptop screen.

Three minor issues cause me to drop my rating one star to four. First, I frequently have to disconnect my laptop from the monitor, and when I return I occasionally have trouble getting the macbook air to talk to the monitor again. Usually cycling power on the monitor fixes the problem. Second, the adjustment buttons are along the bottom right edge of the display Apple Store and look at the top-of-the-line iMac. Its display has the same pixel dimensions. Open up a few browser windows side by side and you won't believe how many windows you can have open and visible at the same time. The only difference is this monitor is 25" diagonalptop screen.

Three minor issues cause me to drop my rating from five to four stars. First, I frequently have to disconnect my laptop from the monitor to take it a meeting, and when I return and reconnect I occasionally have trouble getting the macbook air to talk to the monitor again. The monitor keeps trying to work, but flashes off, on, off, etc. Usually cycling power on the monitor fixes the problem. Second, the adjustment buttons are along the bottom right edge of the display (on the bottom, not facing forward), and unlabeled (except for power). While this makes the face of the display nice and clean, it makes the buttons hard to find and adjust. Third, when I'm connected to this monitor, my sound volume buttons on my macbook air are disabled. Practically useless UHD Monitor!
I did not buy from Amazon, but I always consult the product reviews there, which I consider the best. As consumers we should help each other by shearing experiences.
Researching for good extensive feedback I found only very limited info, mostly specs not giving any practical advice. After reading about display resolutions, ratios and so on I got a bit nervous, but decided so test it myself. Z27s is reasonably well built, as most pro monitors these days, nothing bad and nothing exceptional. Will not spend time describing gimmicks. The moment of truth came when I plugged and turned it on. My great expectations were quickly dispatched. No dead pixels, nice colors, but a major flow, which surprisingly came from the very essence of this monitor - its high resolution. According to the specs this display has 28 factory preset and 10 user modes, not to mention the many custom resolutions you could set through the NVIDIA control panel. All of them, which range below 1900 x 1080 are absolutely useless advertisement - who buys 4k display to run it at 640 x 480 or similar. Who will run 4k display even at 1920 x 1200 or 2048 x 1080, which has weird elongated (17:9) proportion, what is the point? The desired resolutions would be: 2048 x 1536, 2560 x 1440, 2560 x 1600, 3440 x 1440 and native 3840 x 2160, which have similar proportions, close to the physical proportions of the monitor. Circle will appear circle, or close to a circle at these resolution even of the image is stretched. Now, where is the problem? All of the above, except the native 3840 x 2160 are also close to useless, simply because images, interface, linear artwork, text or 3D wire models appear hazy and diluted. For untrained eye, especially without neck to neck comparison it might be a bit harder to see the problem. I put an EIZO FlexScan S2411W, which has max res of only 1920 x 1200 next to the HP and understood why the EIZO costed several years ago 2.5 times the current price of the HP. Now going back to the only resolution at which the Z27s performs relatively well. To maintain and work at this resolution especially in 3D modeling field you will need hell of a rig, particularly the video card. In enthusiast's world you could get away with one or two harnessed together gaming cards, $1000 each. In the pro world just one card could cost you 3-4K. How many people or even companies could or will be willing to spend the money?
For looking at high resolutions photographs or movies you could get by cheaper, but what is the point again when an image at the above EIZO S2411W at 2,304,000 total pixels looked better than the HP Z27s at 8,294,400 total pixels. And where will you get the HiRes content?
The problem does not end here. When running software, which interface was designed for 1920 x1200 pixels at 3840 x 2160 everything shrinks approximately four times and even with 20/20 vision you will need a microscope to read the text on the menus or distinguish the icons. Windows could help somewhat, to adjust text size and icons, but it has limited effect if any on other software, depending on integration. Scalable interface is the answer, but so far it is hard see it around. The solution in my eyes is dual 1920 x 1200 monitor setup. It gives enough surface to spread your tools, without being too demanding on resources.
This monitor should not exist, or it should be 60" to be justified, it appears before its time. It is my honest personal opinion, anyone has the right to differ.

When working on my MAC laptop or Surface pro 3, I find the need to have an extra monitor to fully do what I need to seamlessly. I didn't want to minimize other applications or be unable to do comparisons side-by-side. I spent a 1/2 day trying to get it to connect my Surface Pro 3 but it wouldn't stay on and kept showing a black screen. I did some research and found that people were having issues with the same thing and that it was a Windows 10 problem, update the drivers. I did that by going straight to Displaylink, website, using the August 15th latest release. Still didn't work. I even downgraded my Windows 10 to 8.1 - still didn't work. Then gadget geeks mentioned to buy the y - cable 3.0 usb which I have on order. I will update to see if this works when I receive my y cable. I emailed Asus customer service - no reply yet. I gave up and tried it on my MBP, installed the driver that came with the monitor and it worked! I was sooo happy! The screen resolution was sooo clear and it was beautiful. I bought an Anker (http://smile.amazon.com/Anker-Multi-Angle-E-readers-Smartphones-Compatible/dp/B00D856NOG/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1442509803&sr=8-1&keywords=anker+stand) to lift it up a little more off the table so I can use the space underneath for my papers. I didn't use the case that came with it but I did try it and it works fine. The case just bugs me because the flap was in the way. If you have an iMac, be in for a surprise, it is the exact same OS platform as my MBP, it will give you a black screen and mirror all the monitors instead of extending it. When you extend it, you will see everything but once you start working on it, the screen will start showing a black trail left behind from the mouse movements and typing. This is also a known issue that DisplayLink referenced to take it back to Apple. I would give it a 5 star but knocked off 1 because of the issue with Windows 10 Surface Pro 3. The product itself definitely has a classy look to it and it's ultra light, seemingly fragile but not cheap.

UPDATE:
Upgrade from 4 to 5 stars. I received my y cable usb 3.0 today from Cable Matters. The cable is short but it suits my needs. I plugged it into my Surface Pro 3 and it works. I was able to do this because I have the docking station with it. If you don't have a docking stations, you would need to buy a usb hub but keep in mind that the power will be drawn from the built in port of the usb and cause the Surface Pro 3 to get warm quicker. I am loving it.

One of the comments left mentioned the usb port that is on the SP3 power brick - totally forgot about it. (Original Review) Hands down the best feature set vs price available in gaming ultrawide monitors on the market..period! Yes its a $750 display but what you get on its own and especially compared to others in this price range is a really good value. At 1440p, 35" (not 34"), AMVA+ panel @ 100hz with G-Sync you check off all the right boxes. Add an 1800r curved screen and 4ms GtoG response time with overdrive and gaming profiles and you have one heck of a package. I've ever heard of many owners able to overclock this panel past 100hz successfully without frame skipping. The 120Hz OC is actually a feature in the monitors OSD. So whether that's an ACER guaranteed overclock or not I can't say but you would assume so. or For me 100hz is just perfect since I am using a GTX 1080 and 100hz on this monitor is about max for my rig anyway. I was about to pull the trigger on an LG 34" G-Sync ultrawide for just over $700 but when I found this bad boy it was an very easy choice. Higher resolution, better stand and 1" larger for only about $40 more.. no brainer. My overall experience with computer monitors is pretty deep. I have owned large 40" 4K productions displays all the way to 24" 144hz gaming monitors and many in between. The Acer Predator Z35P can literally do everything extremely well. Whether you are into video and photo editing or just a die hard gamer this is the only monitor you will ever need. My only issue with the Z35P is it takes quite a bit of calibrating to get good color and reduce the backlight bleed. Still has a little blb no matter how low the brightness is and colors aren't as vibrant as an IPS display. But im not taking off a star. Its just far too good of a gaming experience overall.

CPU - Core i5 6600K (Overclocked to 4.5Ghz)
GPU - GTX 1080 (Overclocked to 2088Mhz core & 5508Mhz memory
RAM - 32 Gig DDR4 3200

I've been blissfully enjoying this monitor for a few weeks now and it really is spectacular. I wanted to write a review so I could share a few things about this monitor that either I didn't know beforehand, or had a hard time finding out. First off this monitor is not the XR341CK. I know that might elicit a "Duh!" from some of you but when doing my research I found the old model number would be mixed in with my search results more often than not and it was easy to get spec's from that model mixed up with this one. This monitor's default Freesync range is 48hz - 75hz. It is possible to increase this range using (Custom Resolution Utility) down to 40hz - 75hz. I have noticed a mild intermittent flicker when the monitor crosses below that 48hz threshold but for me it's only noticeable in certain situations, like a wide open pale blue sky in Witcher 3 but it's probably best to keep it above the 48hz range if possible. It takes quite a bit of GPU to push this many pixels. I'm driving this monitor with an R9 Fury X and in most  titles I have to tweak the settings down just a bit from ultra to keep my FPS in that sweet spot. The panel overclocks to 110hz without issue but I have not been able to extend the upper portion of the Freesync range at all while overclocking. Freesync stops working with even the smallest overclock but this is probably something I'm not doing right in CRU. Besides, it's not like my poor Fury X could sustain such ludicrous numbers anyway. lol. My panel has very minor backlight bleed in the lower corners but it is really only visible staring at a blank dark screen with all of the lights out in the room, something I don't get around to doing very often.

I purchased this TV new from Amazon with the Shrek 3D bundle. With the bundle, it was a tremendous value and an excellent entry point into 3D without sacrificing much. They picture quality is excellent, and it performs very well in bright rooms in the "Dynamic" mode, which is important for my setup.

In general, the 2D to 3D on 3DTVs seems to get lackluster reviews. And the Samsung is no exception. The 2D to 3D sort of just makes the images crisper, but the actual 3D effect seems minimal for the most part.

I am experiencing the same issues others have noted with the 3D glasses cutting in and out, but much more so with one pair. I suspect it is a problem with the glasses themselves, and I will be attempting to return that pair to Samsung.

The biggest concern I have with the TV is likely a drawback of plasma TVs in general: image retention (and, potentially, "burn in.") Gaming or staying on a channel with a static, non-transparent logo or graphic (i.e. a cable news channel) will result in image retention rather quickly. While the issue has always been temporary up to this point, I would be cautious if you're purchasing this TV for heavy gaming. It seems it doesn't take long for image retention to occur, and I've read elsewhere manufacturers will NOT cover permanent "burn in" under warranty.

Obviously this TV doesn't have the apps or bells and whistles of other models, but this is somewhat irrelevant if you plan to use a higher-end BluRay or a gaming system with internet apps. Foregoing the built-in app features and opting for this model saves a good deal of money if you don't need them. Plus, connecting to the PC portal means instant computing on the big screen. And the USB portal is a nice feature if you have a lot of media files on an external hard drive.

I bought this monitor only for gaming. Decided it was time to upgrade from my 1080 samsung which I love, but felt like I was missing out on what the next gen consoles offers in terms of image quality. I play on a PS5 and Xbox series X and here's what I think:
-Set up is ridiculously simple (unless you want to mount on the wall), otherwise you just click the base on the back, and screw these 4 little screws to the foot of the base. And it comes with a screwdriver.
-Image quality is amazing, color is the most vibrant color I've seen on a monitor, tweak the setting to 75% sharpness and you got a fantastically detailed game (Guardians of the Galaxy looks so good!)
-Runs a ok 60fps when plugged into HDMI1.
Now here's what I dislike:
-2 out of the 3 HDMI ports are useless, on the description it says you get 4k at 60fps on HDMI1 and the same resolution on 30FPS with HDMI2 and 3, that's a LIE! Both the PS5 and Xbox Series X did not recognize the ports 2 and 3 as 4K, only 1080 for PS5 and 1440 for Xbox Series X. So that was misleading and frankly false advertisement. Now I had to buy a HDMI splitter to have both consoles plugged into the only one 4k port.
-Speakers are useless, I'm sitting at arms length to the monitor with volume at 100 and it's horribly low and frankly awful quality. However, that to me isn't a deal breaker since I always play with headphones.

Overall, I'm keeping it. I love the image quality, it is the main reason why I bought it. Had a downside of needing to buy extra HDMI splitter but it's an easy inexpensive fix. I mainly play RPGs, action and adventure games (Elden Ring, Nier, Horizon, Witcher) so 4k at 60fps does it ok for me. But if you play competitive online multiplayer games like Call of duty, Valorant etc. This might not be the monitor for you.

I've had a 34" ultrawide Dell monitor since 2015 and while it's an excellent monitor, I wanted something with high refresh rates and HDR, so I picked this up on black Friday when it was on-sale. First impression was really bad - the screen is way too curved. The entire point of curving the screen is that when you're sitting in front of it, it's so wide that it's distorted at the edges, so by doing a subtle curve, like Dell does, the screen actually appears to be flat, lines drawn basically seem to be straight all the way to the edge. It's a great optical illusion that makes ultrawide monitors great for production. However this is a stupid "gaming" monitor so they curved it way to much, so everything is literally distorted and curved - basically making it useless for any type of production work where doing things like having straight lines is required. I mean you could work on it, but it will likely screw with your sense of spacing and you'd need to check it on another monitor or phone, etc. It is probably something you could get used to, but it's literally completely unnecessary and adds absolutely nothing to the experience.

As for panel, it's VA - which means viewing angles aren't great - although they're not bad either. Comparing this directly to my 5-year Dell ultrawide, the viewing angles are terrible, but that's also a top of the line ultrasharp model which has a great IPS panel. If you're used to cheaper monitors it likely won't be a big deal, but if you're coming from a quality IPS, it's noticeable but not a deal breaker. On the positive sida VA gives you those high refresh rates at a low price. Also I find VA panels to be really colorful, normally in a good way. For games in particular they can look very good (and IPS is more subtle). Obviously compared to TN panels - which are ALL junk, VA and IPS are huge steps up in quality.

Now for this monitors features. First off is HDR! Or they should call it LOL - because it's not HDR and not even close. It's HDR compatible, that's the best thing you can say about it. The screen doesn't get anywhere close to bright enough to be a real HDR display, not even close. Still it gets bright compared to my 5-year old Dell, so it's got that going for it. However where HDR is useful is when watching HDR content you don't have to jump through hoops trying to convert it. It does, at times, look pretty good - but again, it's not real HDR and calling it HDR is a disgrace and false advertising. HDR compatible is a much more honest label.

Black levels are what you'd expect from a mid-range monitor - it's better than the cheapo stuff, but it's not good either. I will say it's a tiny bit better than the 5-year Dell, but that's typical of VA vs IPS panels. But in any case you won't confuse it with OLED or a decent LED TV with dimming zones. It will do, but that's all.

The star of the show and the only reason in my opinion to buy this is the high refresh rate - and there, everything works great. It is compatible with NVIDIA G-SYNC, it showed right up in NVIDIA control panel, no issues at all there. Just make sure you're using a display port cable - it will not work over HDMI. Thankfully they included a display port cable in the box since an old DP cable I had was throwing off a bunch of interference, but once I replaced it with what they provided, it worked perfectly. Also of note they provide a HDMI cable as well, which again is always nice to have.

The menu/joystick system works pretty well, the only issue is the stupid idea that you have to hold down the button for 5 seconds to turn it off. Why not 2 or 3 seconds? No one is going to accidentally hold down the button for 2 or 3 seconds. Just dumb engineering and really annoying since I generally turn off the screen when I'm going in and out of my office.

Overall for what I paid on Black Friday, which was 409, I think it's a very good value if you're mostly gaming or even for watching movies (watching proper anamorphic widescreen movies on a 21:9 monitor is fantastic). If you're doing photoshop work or desktop publishing, I don't know - this might be too "gamer" to be useful. I didn't color calibrate this, but I assume it's way out of whack and way too colorful - which I generally prefer so I don't mind.

I'll also say I have a fairly cheap 32" Dell 16:9 monitor that is also VA and I'd say the panel quality on both is about the same. So think of it that way, this is a fairly cheap VA panel (which isn't a terrible thing by any means) with 21:9 aspect ratio and a 144hz added on. HDR, as stated, is fake - don't buy this for it's HDR, but it is HDR compatible so it at least makes watching HDR content easier.

Overall I'm mixed on keeping it or sending it back. If they just didn't do the ridiculous and stupid "gamer" curve I could overlook any of the other issues, but the curve is absurd and literally serves no purpose they way they did it.

I've been eyeballing a few 1440p 144hz IPS monitors for a while now before finally settling on this over the similar 144hz IPS LG monitor that's been popular lately. According to reviews, this monitor is almost comparable to the LG, and after buying it myself I can say I'm pretty pleased with how it looks.

Here's what I love:
- Great colors for gaming, photo editing, and video production
- 1440p resolution lets you see and do more with an incredible amount of screen real estate compared to 1080p
- 144hz which is silky smooth for playing video games compared to 60hz
- Low response time so you can react quicker
- Compatible with NVidia Gsync so no need for v-sync and no screen tearing

What I don't like:
- It's an IPS panel, so expect at least some backlight bleeding. My particular unit seems to have a little more than I've noticed in other IPS panels, but at least in regular scenes it still looks great
- Monitor controls are on the back. This is annoying for me since I have to switch back and forth between my personal computer and work computer, and having to reach behind it to do so is frustrating.
- When switching between inputs, it seems to drop itself as a monitor on the input from my personal PC. This won't be an issue for single-screen users, but for triple-screen setups like mine, it can get annoying having to move things back around when Windows temporarily adjusts for two screens.

Overall, a great monitor for a good price, and worth it for people who play video games and also do production work.

The only reason I didn't give this monitor 5 stars ...It has but one flaw .The Xbox 360 runs to the monitor via HDMI , and when I try to watch any downloaded content , i.e. Marketplace TV shows and such , the monitor can't seem to get past the HDCP "handshake" protocol . The "opening" bar appears at the top , the audio kicks in on the reciever , but the screen just goes black . And I mean really black , as in no signal . If I hit the "B" button and drop back to the dashboard , the display pops right back up . I can get around this by being on DVI , pressing start , and then flip it back to HDMI once I hear the audio come on , the video is magically on at this point . A deal-breaker ? No , but inconvienent . Other than this one problem , the monitor is un-believably awesome ,especially at 1920x1080 .

But as I moved the monitor lower and higher on my stand up desk, it ended up catching the the HDMI cable and stress the HDMI socket connection at the monitor end. Particularly as per design if you have it right against the wall this would be a risk. You need to leave space in between to wall and the monitor.  Cable is not well supported and can slide out slightly from its cable run and this pulls the HDMI connection off its socket. result was the Monitor entry of the HDMI socket has been damaged; now there is no Signal and there is only one HDMI socket provided and there is no redundancy ! If you are planning to continuously use the adjustment of height feature I would not recommend unless you tie up the cable with some slack the stress free at the socket.  After damage I tried to use the mini-display port with an HDMI adaptor, even tried with a new HDMI cable, but I could never manage to get the signal back into the Monitor from my MacBook Pro; At the end I returned the monitor back and Amazon kindly accepted it.

Let's get the speaker issue out of the way. Some people say that there are speakers and I clearly don't hear any sound coming from the monitor. I wasn't really affected by this because I already had a speaker in hand to use so I wasn't too frustrated with this. However, I was at the moment I realized no sound was coming from the monitor and had to mount the speaker.
The picture quality is amazing ONLY if you tweek the settings. I can totally say that if you do not change the settings, the picture quality is pretty bad. The most important part is to lower the "Shadow Control" in "Game Settings." After trying this out, my entire screen looked much better. Changed contrast, blue light settings, etc. and it is amazing.
It works with my RTX2070 and it totally does 120 fps. Not sure if 144 because my pc can't do that (changing the cooling system). HOWEVER, in order to do high FPS you have to right click on your desktop screen and go to Nvidia Control Panel. Then go to "Set up G-Sync" (make sure to hit apply), and then go to "Change Resolution." My default settings were native 1080p with 60hz. Make sure scroll down to PC after Ultra HD, HD, SD and look for the PC 1080X1920. Click on it and then change your refresh rate to your liking (144hz). Click Apply. And Voila.
Now your monitor is everything they advertise it as.

I have this setup as the main display for my MacBook Pro. I have a Thunderbolt cable going to a Thunderbolt HDD, than Daisy Chaining to another Thunderbolt HDD, and finally a Mini DisplayPort to DisplayPort cable going to this monitor. The MacBook puts out at full 2560x1440 resolution and it looks amazing! I also have two DisplayLink (USB Graphics) adapters powering two 21" (1080P) monitors on each side of this one.

The MacBook Pro has no problem powering this one and I can extend my display across this monitor and the other two with no problem.

The color on this monitor looks amazing! I did not have to fine tune or do anything, after plugging it in it came up with a perfect picture and looked amazing!

The touch buttons are nice and makes this monitor really elegant.

The display is pretty big (it is a 27" after all), so I had to redo my desk to accommodate it. I actually put a small table behind my desk so I could sit this further away from me (being so large I need it a little further away to see it properly), plus I wanted it a little lower and this also put the feet under the desk, so I do not lose my desk space to the giant feet/stand from this monitor. I have the USB port plugged in (USB cable going from MacBook to 4 Port powered hub, and than going to monitor so I can use its hub). Its ports work great and they are powered so you can plug in high powered items (I am running one of the USB graphics off of it).

The monitor's refresh rate and picture are amazing and movies and games looks crystal clear on the monitor! The details are amazing and editing images are so much easier when you can see much more of the super HD photo when working on it without having to zoom into sections all the time. I looked over the monitor and did not see any dead pixels or anything! It looks crystal clear with no spots (put a white screen up to test).

I wish this monitor had Daisy Chaining for DisplayPort so I could get two monitor powered off of one Thunderbolt port, but it does not. However, that is the only thing I would change! This monitor is amazing! It also had HDMI with 3.5mm out for speakers if you are using HDMI (it does not have built in speakers). If you plan on using the 2560x1440 resolution you will need to use DisplayPort or Dual Link DVI, you will also want to check with your video card and see what it can put out.

For a super HD display this is a great choice! It is super large with a super high resolution, and looks amazing! This is a great choice for intense graphics applications and a great improvement to any setup! Highly recommend!!!

We purchased a 24" NEC monitor a few years ago, one that is more-or-less a predecessor of this one in terms of product development.  We liked it so well that we chose this one when we needed a new monitor for another computer.  This new monitor is even better than the old one.  The colors are accurate and "pop" even more, without being garish.  The base of the new monitor is more stable than that of the older one, and screen-adjustment is much easier on the new one.  Furthermore, the new screen is about 2" wider.  When we started shopping for the new monitor, we were thinking of getting one with more "real estate," that is, with a 27" screen, in order to be able to keep multiple windows open at the same time.  But we settled on this 24" one because of technical issues, and the resulting price issues.  We are not technical and had to do the best we could, but the following is what we think we determined.  A primary determinant of monitor quality is definition.  The levels of definition (if I remember correctly) are HD (high); UHD (ultra); and QHD (quad), also called 4k.  Evidently higher definition is not in itself better, because different levels of definition behave very differently on screens of different sizes.  HD is dated.  UHD is current and works for screens 24" and smaller, but produces poor picture quality on screens larger than 24".  One post said that on a 27" UHD screen you can "see the holes between the pixels."  Therefore a  good 27" screen requires QHD.  (Though there is an HP 27" UHD monitor that is said to be extremely good, as long as you are concerned more-or-less exclusively with text and need to keep multiple windows open at once.)  As for QHD, there are virtually no QHD screens 24" or smaller on the market, apparently because QHD doesn't work well on 24" screens.  Our conclusion was that for a 27" monitor to be as good as our older 24" one, it would have to have QHD.  However, that would require spending in the upper-mid level to lower-high level price range ($600-$900).  Our needs are merely those of fairly intensive home-users, so it seems excessive to spend that much for a monitor.  Therefore we settled on this 24" monitor with UHD, and we are delighted with our choice.

This monitor was purchased for a home office and is not used daily.  For this reason I did not want to invest a lot of money.  It was easy to mount to a dual monitor stand, but be aware the post at the bottom for a freestanding monitor does not come off.  The screen is a perfect size for my use.  The picture quality is ok, not as sharp as I would like, but definitely useable.  All things considered, a good value.

The good:
The colors look great. Especially compared to my Asus G15's screen (which is no slouch), the colors look bright and generally accurate. The screen gets sufficiently bright. In terms of response time, it also has fairly little ghosting compared to some other options in the $250 - $300 price bracket (the G27Q is so much worse in this regard, but the LG Nano IPS panels are better than the Acer for response time).

The bad:
The panel does have some backlight bleed. For mine, it happens around the top right corner. Furthermore, it does have poor black uniformity. There is some clouding in scenes that are nearly pitch black. Personally, this isn't as much of a deal breaker since I often use my monitor in a lit room. If you wanted deep and accurate blacks, get an OLED/VA panel, not an IPS one.

The ugly:
STUCK PIXELS. After playing some games on the monitor, I recognized the blue and green sub-pixels shown in the image. For me, this is not acceptable. Every time I see the stuck pixels, it's a reminder that the product I purchased is defective, making the panel unpleasant to use. Maybe it is just me, but I expect the products I purchase to work as described, and stuck/dead pixels are not in the description (for obvious reasons).

Overall:
I think the monitor is overall a fantastic panel for the price. However, I refuse to accept the QC issues. I am currently waiting for Amazon to get the monitor back in stock so I can replace it (I am assuming that is the reason why the replace option doesn't show up on my orders page even though I can return it). If it doesn't get back in stock within a couple of weeks, I will return this monitor and leave my review as is.

Honestly I was excited to see the CJ79 curved Thunderbolt 3 monitor as an option for my new MacBook Pro. I'm a long time Mac user and currently have two of the Thunderbolt 2 monitors on my desk. The option for a 1.5x wide monitor roughly the same height appealed to me despite less screen real estate as there would not be a bezel dividing the screens. I thought the curve would be fine as well. I got my new MBP and realized using my old monitors would require dongles to connect and would not offer charging so this seemed like the best option.

I ordered it and it arrived in a larger box somewhat well packed. No visible damage. Opened it up and it was indeed glorious at first sight. Very light weight as well. The problems started when I unpacked it and carefully removed the plastic protective film from the bezel - the film grabbed so well it removed some of the paint on the bezel. That sucks for a nearly $900 monitor and quite frankly it's unacceptable to have missing bezel paint on day one.

I could have lived with the missing paint perhaps but once connected to the MBP I realized that the image was a bit inferior. The font is pixelated and jagged. Seriously. I'm sure it's a Mac thing but the amount of adjustment and controls is very limited. I do image editing and CAD drawing and it was so noticeable I ended up buying a Thunderbolt 2 to Thunderbolt 3 dongle for $49 just to make sure it wasn't the output on my new MBP. It wasn't and honestly my old Mac Thunderbolt 2 monitors look much better than this Samsung.

Be warned this is all plastic and a little bit cheap looking. If you are a Mac user and do graphics or drawings please go see one in person before buying as it more than likely is a step backward as far as image quality especially compared to a Retina laptop screen.

I boxed it up for a return and started that process with Amazon. I will be using my old monitors and will need to purchase a dock with Thunderbolt 2 to 3 ports to make this work well. That sucks.

I was pretty excited when I got this and sort of overlooked the reviews claiming backlight bleeding. I figured they just got bad units. Well, mine had it, and it was terrible. What's worse is that it wasn't even uniform; it was almost all in the bottom left of the screen. Apparently this is a common problem. Yeah, I could replace it over and over until I got one without it, but why should someone have to do that? It's either poor packaging or poor quality control.

The other problem that wasn't as serious but actually bothers me more... was that the stand doesn't lay flat. It wobbles. I mean the part that sits on the desk isn't flat! How hard is it to test a stand to see if it lays flat? Another oversight.

I decided to send this back and I ordered an Asus PB278Q. It looks better and has none of the problems I described above. The backlight bleeding is minimal and the stand is great.

I haven't had a lot of luck with Dell stuff before, but I love the minimalist style of their monitors and enjoy the ones I have at work. I think this was the last straw for me. For the price, I can't recommend this at all. Get a PB278Q instead.

If you're in the market for a 1440p 144hz monitor, please look elsewhere. I wouldn't even buy thing thing if it dips below $200, and here's why:

- Probably the worst backlight bleed I've seen on a monitor. I'd cringe everytime a dark scene came on screen. It's more than just normal backlight bleed that you'd expect from a cheaper IPS panel. If you don't care about BLB, this might be fine, but I seriously considered returning this monitor on day one just for the BLB alone. Boy should I have listened to my gut...

- Horrible colors out of the box. Need to switch to 10bit color mode to get a decent image quality. Only problem is this limits you to 120hz, NOT the 144hz as advertised.

- Even though this is advertised as G-Sync "compatible", my GPU did not recognize it at all and I could never get it to function properly.

- When using a DisplayPort connection, I got constant red lines flickering on the monitor and an odd static sound coming from the port. At first this wasnt very noticable and only happened ever hour or so, but over the course of a month it got worse and worse, to the point of being a constant. Eventually this issue spread to the HDMI ports as well, which ended up sounding like a bomb was going off inside the monitor whenever I connected an HDMI cord.

- Acer customer service and their repair center are useless. Said it would be a ~2 month turnaround for repair. So what am I supposed to do in the meantime while I need a monitor for work?

I ended up returning this to Amazon and taking at $50 hit on the restocking fee, just so I wouldn't have to deal with Acer customer service and their repair center anymore. If you value your time and sanity, stay away from this thing. Only reason I gave this 2 stars is because when it worked, it was better than my 10 year old 1080p Dell.

4K video from YouTube looks fantastic on these monitors. Very clear and crisp picture. Almost looks like 3D. Monitors have good depth when viewing 4K signals.

This is a review of my experience with the BenQ ex27890q paired with a PS5.

I chose this monitor believing it was the perfect entry level monitor that supported everything you need for gaming on modern consoles. I was half right and I'm going to tell you why.

The BenQ ex2780q is the perfect size for gaming at a desk. 27 inches is just big enough that you can sit close and get right into the action and have a more intimate gaming experience. The picture quality is very impressive, though, it would be more impressive if the PS5 took full advantage of the monitor. More on that in a bit.

The colors out of the box are super vibrant in my humble unprofessional opinion, and the HDR makes the whole experience pop with bright highlights but somewhat unimpressive blacks. It's totally ok though because you'll bearly notice when playing games!

I've heard there are better HDR monitors on the market, but your looking at $200 more for those. Not to worry though, this monitor's implementation is great. Just don't use the hdri modes, if you want consistent bright highlights. game and cinema hdri modes both offer different image quality settings, but both modes adjust brightness depending on the ambient light in your room. Decrease brightness and you don't get the full HDR effect.

Now for the part about me being half right about pairing this particular monitor with the PS5. I'll start with resolution. The maximum resolution of this monitor is 1440p, which is perfect for gaming on consoles. Well, it would be if the PS5 supported native 1440p. That's something wildly known at this point and I went ahead and bought this monitor knowing that. I'm not taking points away for that. Not a big issue, you can get this monitor and future proof yourself for the imminent patch that enables native 1440p resolution.

The problem lies with the recent update to the PS5 that enables VRR. This monitor fully supports g-sync/freesync, so again, I won't hold that against the monitor. It's a Sony problem and hopefully will be patched so that the display will work with VRR. I just want it to be known to anyone in my position looking for a monitor that takes full advantage of the PS5, except resolution of course, but it's a necessary trade off if you don't want to murder the wallet. ;) I'll tell you why it's not a big deal next!

Other than that the display is perfect for the price and works really well. Now resolution is one of next gens biggest features. You'll notice a different from 1080p to 1440p and from 1440p to 4K, but the difference from 1440p to 4K isn't as noticable as 1080p to 4K.

With this monitor you're essentially future proofing yourself for 1440p support somewhere down the line. Most videogames won't render at a full native 4K and have 60fps. It's really taxing on the hardware, so most games will actually drop the resolution to 1440p to hit a nice stable 60fps, but some games will drop that resolution even further to 1080p to hit even higher fps targets, like, 120fps.

And that brings me to why I say this monitor is perfect for next gen gaming. 120fps works without a hitch and is very smooth paired with the 5ms response time of the display, and as I said, the colors are pretty amazing, especially paired with HDR! You'll get the best of both world with this monitor, high fps AND resolution! The difference between 1440p and 4K isn't that big a deal, especially when you can run your games at a smooth 60fps up to 120fps on console. Or all the way up to 144fps with a beefy PC.

All in all, this monitor is a great value and an excellent way to future proof yourself for next gen gaming without breaking the bank too hard.

This was a long one, but if you think I missed anything or you have any questions you'd like me to answer, I'd be more than happy to answer if I can.

Let me start this review by saying I am on the fence about whether or not to give this product a 5 start rating because I tend to hold products to a high standard, especially ones of this price range.

Now, this is a BEAUTIFUL monitor. I came from a triple monitor 5760x1080 setup to this and I am 100 percent sure I made the right choice. 3440x1440 is crisp and wide, yet you have slightly less of a performance hit when gaming than at 5760x1080. I am using a GTX 980TI and most games run over 60 fps nicely.

This monitor offers an abundance of input/output connections and is extremely adjustable with tilt, swivel, and up down motions. I love having the usb ports on this monitor since I can plug my mouse and keyboard into it and only have to run one usb cord down off the desk to where my rig is located. Also, the capacitive menu buttons are pleasant to use and the menu is equally easy to navigate.

This display goes in and out of sleep mode nicely so I don't have to worry about turning it off manually.

My one and only con which I almost knocked a star for is the backlight bleed. It is definitely there and it is definitely noticeable, but the majority of the time I do not notice it. If it was that big of a deal to me I would have knocked a star but I really think this monitor deserves 5 stars.

Price is also a little high but it's coming down to a more reasonable level.

hooking it up, - my laptop has USB 3.2, but when hooked to the monitor, the monitor said no signal, not sure why, probably a thunderbolt thing, the add does say you need 3.2 with thunderbolt. So many USB standards, it's hard to figure out what's what with USB now days. But it was not a problem, I just hooked it up with the HMDI cable and it started with no problem, Windows 10 seen it and set it up as soon as I plugged it in. A side note, my USB port on the Laptop did not have enough power to drive the monitor, I do use a powered USB 3.2 hub with the laptop, so I just plugged it in to the hub and the monitor worked great.
Color - took a bit to find the right setting, the default setting just did not seem as vibrant as my laptop monitor, cut me a break, I'm a programmer, not a graphics artist LOL, The monitor has a lot of custom settings to adjust the color, very easy to get lost if your not a graphics artist, since I don't know a lot about color settings, it took a bit, but I finally found the proper settings, now it looks as great as the monitor on my laptop.

This is my early review for the Acer XB270HU monitor. I have been using it for 1 week now and after a small amount of configuring I have it set up pretty nice. I am coming from and older set up so I don't have much to compare it to. I also bought a gtx 970 card to go along with this. You will need to configure this monitor because the settings out of the box are horrible. My confurations are as follows.

1. eColor mode - User
2. Brightness - 24
3. Contrast - 50
4. Gamma - 2.2
5. Colour Temp - User
6. RGB - 50, 45, 46
7. Gsync - on
8. Overdrive - Normal
9. 144hz

I had great results with these settings. Overdrive was causeing lots of issues on every mode but normal. I had to adjust a few settings in windows to see text in web pages and email better. I made my curser larger and font settings to custom 200% you can do this in the control panel then goto display. The only issue I had is with screen edge bleed, I don't think there should be any with the price of this. And my last issue is with the power switch, it is on the back near the center. You have to reach under and up to turn it on and off. Here is a small tip on putting the stand on the screen. Lay the screen face down then put the stand on. The 2 top prongs go in first then push down. All in all a very nice monitor, I hope it lasts for a long time.

While researching for a straight month about this monitor I heard the following statements over and over again:

1) Once you go ultra wide, you'll never go back.
2) G-Sync premium is worth it if you are already invested in the high-end economy.
3) 60Hz to 100Hz is a night and day difference.
4) The OSD (On Screen Display) is terrible.

After fiddling with the display for about 2 days I can say that:

1) I will never go back to 16:9.
2) I am awe struck of how much smoother the experience is with G-Sync.
3) I can't believe I though 60Hz was smooth now that I have tasted the 100Hz elixir.
4) The OSD is one of the worst I have used. Whoever designed it should be barred from designing future OSDs for monitors.

QC issues have plagued the early builds of this monitor and I was a bit worried when I found that mine was from a September batch. However I can safely say that I did a quick test for the largest concerns and then started to do that thing that was the entire point of the purchase...to enjoy it!

No Blue banding, No scan lines, No flickering, OC'd to 100Hz with no issue. No dead pixels, no wake from sleep bugs.

There is a minimum amount of back light bleed on the upper left and right corner but it is close to impossible to see unless I am strictly looking for it and am viewing an extremely dark picture. I have never noticed it when casually using the monitor.

The stand is very deep but very high quality and you feel secure that $1300 screen being held up is not going anywhere. I have an IKEA Fredde desk and was concerned that the stand would be too deep for the monitor raised shelf portion but I can safely say that it fits fine. The added handle on the stand makes for readjustments and first time setup procedures a breeze.

The major plays of this monitor as opposed to other Ultra wides are G-Sync and 100Hz. If those two features are a must have then I can recommended the Acer X34 Predator to you. However, if you simply want an Ultra wide at 3440x1440 with IPS technology then you may want to look for the cheaper alternatives. Keep in mind that for gaming purposes, the increased refresh rate and G-Sync capabilities are quite literally game changing.

*The OSD is horrendous but it is "Set it and forget it" once you tune it to your specifications, so I can't take away any stars for it.

There are two tiny flaws.

One flaw is that it sometimes doesn't wake up when the computer does and you have to do something to the little joystick control on the back to force it to wake.

Which leads to the second tiny flaw, the little joystick control on the back.

Other companies who make monitors have figured this out and have implemented a series of buttons, especially a dedicated and simple on/off button. Using the little joystick on the back to both turn the monitor on and off as well as navigate through the various menus is anti-intuitive and cumbersome, and the joystick is overly-sensitive to what direction you're pushing it. If you push it in even the slightest wrong direction you're suddenly selecting something you don't want. And sometimes, even when pushing it in the correct direction, you still somehow end up selecting an option you didn't want.

For me, the quality of the image and price outweigh these two tiny flaws.

And, I'm sure I'll get better at the joystick the more I use it.

I purchased the monitor for production work and it serves its purpose. Where this monitor falls short is when it's used for gaming.

60HZ vs 144HZ
Please be aware that the monitor is 60hz (at 3840x1080 resolution) most of the time despite it being advertised as a 144hz monitor. I myself have not seen any where in the settings and or the AMD utility that remotely mentions the monitor being capable of 144hz. In order to get a useable 120hz, you'll need to use the PBP setting (which basically splits the screen into two different input sources) and setting the resolution to 1920x1080. Only until then would you be able to set the monitor to 120hz. I have not used other 49" monitors, but to sell a product and claim its functionalities that are not usable by majority of people that just plug and play seems a bit dishonest.

RGB LED
That LED is basically useless, it 's not bright enough to provide back lighting, and it takes a dozen menu button pushes to change the color.

Before COVID, I spent a lot of time working way from a desk – at hotels, in coffee shops, outside on the porch, in bed – and I found it very difficult to have only one tiny laptop monitor. To supplement, I used a combination of an iPad and/or USB monitor clipped to my laptop (I used the Mountie+ by Ten One Design) or mounted on a stand. That worked well enough, but it was quite cumbersome to put together every time.

When Mobile Pixels came out with its first generation duel monitor, I considered getting it. I also considered the SideTrak. However, I decided to wait because brand new products tend to be a little temperamental. I have friends who were early adopters, and they mentioned issues with heat and inconsistent performance.

I am pleased to report that I haven’t identified any problems with the TRIO. The screen is crisp and clear. The casing is stable – I can move the screen to multiple angles, and even when my laptop screen is tilted back a bit, there are no issues with falling over. This was very easy to attach, and the magnet connection is strong (but doesn’t cause any problems for my computer or the peripherals I keep around my computer). My only complaint is that it is heavy. This definitely adds weight to your bag, so this will not be an EDC item for most people. However, for those who need to work on the go and can’t make do with just one laptop screen, I think this is the best product on the market. It is rare that I write a review on Amazon, but in the case of the Dell UltraSharp U2715H 27-Inch Screen LED-Lit Monitor I had to leave feedback.

The monitor is fine – but beware when you order of what comes or more importantly does not come in the box or on the display. If you have a very recent computer, you will probably have no issues, but if you don’t look out.

There is no VGA input, so you cannot just unplug your old monitor and plug in the U2715H. They do supply two cables: one is an HDMI to a miniDP, the other a DP to a USB. My computer does not have an HDMI, a miniDP or a DP. You can plug the DP into the monitor and then the USB into your computer. When I did that the computer knew I plugged something in, but the monitor went unrecognized. The monitor gave the message no DP plugged in.

Inserting the CD allowed me to run the Dell monitor manager, but it did not “see” the monitor.

The very limited directions are only pictures, and not really helpful ones.

Ultimately, I ended up purchasing an adapter and using an HDMI cable.

For MSI's first monitor , it's certainly an incredible piece of hardware. For its' specs and price it's unbeatable compared to any other monitor. It's received quite the negative feedback but that is based on factory defects (stuck pixels , etc.)  that can happen to any product. The problem I've seen the most is the backlight bleeding towards the top and bottom of the monitor. It might just be me but as of the 100+ hours I've already put into this on games ranging on all colors I have not noticed the backlight bleeding. Even on incredibly dark games with brightness,contrast, and gamma changes I have not noticed the bleeding. This monitor is well built and has a nice UI that allows for games to run on higher refresh rates with less motion blur. The only con yet is that the stand isn't the most adjustable but that can be makeshift fixed if needed be. The most it'll tilt upward leaves the viewing experience at a sharp 90 degrees so if you like your monitor usually a little more tilted up, you're going to need to find a different stand or something. All in all , the monitor is fantastic and I haven't had any problems with it yet. Will recommend !

*** EDIT ***

I contacted CS, which btw is EXCELLENT, and they did in fact tell me that the design of this particular monitor does in fact have that black ball all the way around. The reason we don't see it on the bottom part of the monitor is because the plastic is thicker thus covering the 1/4" black bar. That frame is in fact physical black and not dead pixels or an under scanned image. I am still debating if I want to send it back and get a different one, but after searching and for this price it's a pretty good deal. Also, the great customer support makes me want to stick with them.

However, once I actually started USING the monitor (you know, the thing you're supposed to do with a product) everything went downhill. The first monitor I received had multiple dead pixels  show up dead center in the middle of the screen after less than 24 hours, making it unbearable to use for almost any task. Therefore, I requested a replacement. After receiving the replacement, less than 3 hours after powering it on for the first time, the screen started to flash random subpixels on and off unless I set the refresh rate all the way down to 120hz. Personally, I'm not willing to accept that cut in performance for a 300+dollar monitor, but now Amazon refuses to return or replace this second monitor. How is it so hard to do any kind of QC at factory? This is absolutely unacceptable to first provide multiple defective units then refuse to take them back. I was really impressed at first with Acer but now I don't believe that I can purchase any of their items anymore in good conscience, and I HIGHLY recommend that anyone else looking for this monitor look elsewhere, as that's what I'm going to have to do now. I purchased three of these monitors for a Surround setup, currently in portrait. The included base is intuitive and stylish, allowing one to rotate from landscape to portrait by simply turning it. I originally had the three of them mounted onto a frame and displayed in landscape mode, but the ultra-wide 5780x1080 resolution was a bit jarring and far too unsupported. Presently, they're all set up in bezel-corrected 3950x1920, and it's quite the theatrical experience. Everything I play now is HUGE, equivalent to a 44 inch TV with 6 million pixels. The bezels are reasonably thick, but for the amount of screen area you get, aren't too bad. Keep in mind that running three of these in 144Hz mode requires at least one of them to be run through DisplayPort for it to function properly.

The 1ms response times and refresh rates are definitely noticeable and desirable over other panels, but the crowning feature of this monitor and other 144hz models are the LightBoost support. Check it out, as it'll change everything.

I'm removing a star for the color reproduction of this monitor. It's a TN panel, so the colors tend to be overly bright and washed out. Turning on LightBoost and strobing makes everything dimmer but much easier on the eyes. I've got the strobing feature turned on across all three panels. Still, I can't help but feel that the colors seem slightly off from what I'm used to. It's taken quite a bit of work to get to a setting that I can universally enjoy.

I purchased this particular monitor over the BENQ model in anticipation for Nvidia's G-SYNC technology. If that releases as a do-it-yourself module, I'll update this review with my impressions of that technology.

I have the monitor set up as my entertainment monitor while I do work or look things up on my laptop screen. It's working really well. The monitor can be several feet away because it's so huge. Even if I am using it for reading or working, I can see everything perfectly from a few feet away, so I can relax on my bed and type on the laptop without straining to read or see what's on the monitor. The resolution and clarity are great.

I've been watching Game of Thrones and other epic stuff that's too dark to see what's going on, and the black stabilization feature really seems to help me pick out what's happening. It just looks great.

You navigate the menu with a set of buttons behind the monitor that work like the buttons on an ATM, where a choice is displayed next to each button, and the choices change based on which menu you're in. There are always a few choices available so it's easy to screw up and change things you didn't mean to change, or exit a menu you had a hard time getting to, etc. I really wish it had come with a remote, or you could connect a PC/smartphone app to it as a remote. The menus would be easier to deal with, but also the screen is large, so you're likely to be sitting a few feet away. If you need to change any settings you have to keep getting up and messing with the menu. I don't like the way the menu works--there are too many options for such a basic, old-school menu nav system and it is tiresome to click through them. Once they're set up, you probably don't have to change them so much, but you'll still probably want to change color modes or change sources, that kind of thing. (This is actually getting really annoying now that I work from home and want to change the brightness to fit the lighting but it's such a pain in the neck.)

I'm playing audio through my laptop or standalone speakers, but you can run it through the monitor, too. I don't want to do that because there's no remote and the sound is poor/quiet, but it's nice to have the option.

Also, there's that DANG OLD MONITOR STAND! If you want to use this with a VESA monitor stand (and I suggest that you do!), you probably want to remove the built-in stand, and there are no directions whatsoever in the manual or on ViewSonic's site explaining how to do so. You have to unscrew the two screws that are on each side of the place that connects the stand to the monitor. Then the stand and the attached metal plate inside the monitor slide out downward.

Despite its few flaws I think it's excellent. I really love it.

As many of you can relate, working from home has been tough during the COVID pandemic. Ironically, my company had just switched lat year from desktop units to laptops with docking stations for use at our office. When I was forced to work from home, I no longer had two large monitors, but just a small laptop screen. So I began my search for a second monitor. I thought about buying a full-size monitor, but stumbled onto portable monitors. What a great idea! Doing some research on Amazon, Lepow monitors kept cropping up with great reviews. Looking through all the different Lepow monitors, I discovered they had a new 2020 model. I ordered one and was immediately pleased with the build quality. With a little color tweaking, I have been very happy with the display. What was really shocking was the sound quality of its speakers. I started to play a training video expecting to hear a tinny sound over my laptop, but was stunned to hear excellent sound quality from my new monitor.

I am now no longer stuck working from just my laptop screen, but can now expand to my new second monitor. In fact, I find myself working from its display more often than my laptop. Well worth the purchase!

Have not yet attempted to use the panel with my Hackintosh partition, I'll report back on how that goes another time.

Wow ... I just replaced 4 older 2K monitors (Dell 2408s) with two of these ASUS 4K monitors and I'm loving it. Several years ago I indulged myself in what I thought was an extravagance of 4 monitors. What I found was that my productivity and comfort was significantly enhanced. I've been programming since 1975 so I'm a reasonably good judge of these things. My Dell 2408s ran hot though, and since I've moved to a place which enjoys 115 degree summers it's become an increasing burden. I've been holding off until I could get a 4K monitor (I was originally planning on getting a single 40"). Finally one of my Dells fizzled and popped and I had to either replace it or upgrade the whole kit.

As I still don't see a good 60 hz 40" monitor I thought that I'd chance getting two of these ASUS 28" monitors. They're wonderful. They run cold, eliminating my heating problem. They're light, so my wall mounts no longer sag. And they installed on Windows 8.1 without a problem. I'm running them in side by side portrait mode, with one of them inverted so that the control buttons are all easily accessible on the outer edges. I can easily see 150 lines of code now which makes it much easier to read and I have the second monitor for my output and search logs. I increased my text size to 150%, which helped readability, selected the Extra Large mouse size, and enabled the Control button mouse finder -- 4320 x 3840 is after all a lot of screen and it's easy to lose the mouse.

It's only been a couple of days so I can't comment on longer term reliability but my initial impression is that these monitors are about as good as it gets.

This is probably the BEST multi-purpose monitor for coders, gamers, graphic artists, and video viewers. I have this connected to a MacBook Pro 16, a PS4, and a Nintendo Switch via Thunderbolt and HDMI for the PS4 and Nintendo. In comparison to my other monitor a Dell U3821DW, it beats it in everything other than maybe text clarity but the difference is very minor. For the size, the LG is also very light in comparison to the Dell. I suspect that is because Dell has the PSU inside the monitor while LG is a separate external PSU.

Initially, plugging in the MacBook Pro the screen felt washed out and a lot of the LG's settings were disabled. Took a little research to discover, if connected to an HDR-enabled monitor MacOS will automatically enable the HDR setting. You need to switch this off!! (System Preferences -> Displays -> uncheck High Dynamic Range). Windows has a similar configuration in the Settings->System section). Immediately, the monitor display is like night and day giving you that "WOW!" feeling. Videos are vibrant and text becomes sharp and clear. Additionally, ALL the LG monitor settings are now enabled.

For monitor speakers, the sound is very impressive; clear mid-range and OK bass. This will never replace a dedicated sound system but is good enough for watching YouTube videos. The only thing to beware of is the light sensor for the auto-brightness is at the top of the monitor so if you have a tall desk lamp switched on it causes a lot of confusion for the monitor. Given the price, the monitor stand could be of better quality - less plastic and requires to feel more solid like the Dell. Otherwise, it's hard to go wrong with this monitor which rightly deserves all the positive reviews.

I discovered you can only use a USB 3.0 port to drive this monitor. If you attempt to use a USB 2.0 port, it'll only work for a few minutes, and then go dark, while the system's USB recognition sound loops over, and over until you unplug the monitor. There's just not enough juice in 2.0 ports to drive the monitor.

I tried to use a PCIE adaptor card in the machine, but my video graphic's adaptor card is in the way, and there are no other PCIE slots. I'm going to try a PCIE USB 3.0 riser adaptor which might not get blocked by the graphics card. The graphics card is the kind which has a great big honkin' fan, and components on the wrong side.

The short time I was able to see the monitor working on a 2.0 port, It was interesting to see how the auto rotate feature worked. You can disable that feature, and set it manually in the applet that allows you to duplicate, or extend to monitors, ect.

So, if you don't want to go to the hassle, or just can't be bothered opening your machine to put in adaptors with USB 3.0 ports, you might want to look for a PC that already has USB 3.0 ports installed. If you are tech savvy, then consider a mother board that already has 3.0 ports.

Oh yeh, I was extremely disappointed with the short cord that came with the monitor. It should have been at least 6 feet, just in case your 3.0 ports are in back of the machine.

The included cover which doubles as a stand is interesting, but for better angle control, you might want a Fellows Book Lift, or Copy holder. It's very thin, and folds up to fit in a laptop case, along with this monitor.

Excellent and beautiful monitor.  I would have gotten a larger one, but the price difference is tremendous.  Anyway, this one does the job for me. PROS
- very easy to use
- super light weight and thin
- no power cable required
- you can use USB type c to connect to a Macbook pro laptop. Also it should work for other devices but I haven't tested it yet.
- comes with a screen cover that doubles as a kick stand

CONS
- the speaker on this thing is a joke but it really isn't needed, esp. If you'll be using this with a laptop.
- when the screen brightness (not volume) is anywhere between 10 and 90 - an annoying high pitched sound gets emmited from the screen. It's super weird especially if you like to work in silence...or if you step away from your desk/donw work for the day but you leave the screen plugged in, you can still hear it. I contacted seller support and they confirmed that I wasn't crazy, and this has something to do when the type of components used to manufacture the device.
- the kickstand that comes with the screen only has a limited range of angles that you can set it to. But this can be easily fixed by getting a stand for your needs.
- the location of the ports to connect the screen to an external device takes getting used to (just the look of it and the placement makes it a bit awkward to place the screen directly side by side with a laptop). The placement is the ports makes sense to keep the screen as thin as it is... but it would be better is the ports were maybe hidden on the back of the device.

The fact that I can have a second screen without the need to have a separate power cord AND it's highly portable so when I need to have a heavy work session away from my desk - makes this thing damn near perfect. The only 2 things I wish I could change about it are that weird sound related to the brightness and the placement of the ports.

I had wanted a 27" monitor for gamming and movie watching purposes for a long time. About a year ago I bought a bottom of the line 27" from Samsung (S27A550H, see my Amazon review). I was never really happy with it. It just didn't have the color accuracy based on my Asus 24" I use as my 2nd monitor. It might have been fine as a stand alone, but you could just constantly see it was not as vivid as the Asus. So this time I didn't fool around, go big or go home, so to speak, and I am NOT disappointed in the least with this product. Right out of the box it is easy to see that this is a superior product, even from the packaging alone.

Games are ultra smooth w/ the 120Hz refresh rate. I am not sure it is as breathtakingly noticeable as others report in other reviews of 120Hz monitors from around the web, but it certainly is noticeable even from just window movements on the screen. This coupled w/ the 2MS response time make this an incredibly fast monitor. Games/movies have no ghosting what so ever.

3D is, I will have to admit, much cooler than I thought it would be. I bought it as just an afterthought or an introduction into the tech, not really expecting to use it much. But am amazed at how well it works in games. It makes things pop on screen that I never would even look at, like the cross bar on the safe room doors in Left for Dead, for example. Now as good as they look, there is still an issue w/ FPS competitive Multiplayer games. The 3D effect kind of distorts your aim just a tad. You can correct it a bit by using the Convergence and 3D Depth controls for 3D Vision, but it still puts you at a disadvantage and you may find yourself shooting just slightly to the right or left of a target that is intent on killing you. Ok if you are playing against the computer, a total disadvantage playing against real people. So although BF3, for example, looks stunning in 3D, you just can't compete in the online games while in 3D mode. I also watched a 3D movie on this monitor as well, which looked amazing. (It was Avengers). The only issues you have to concern yourself with is not this monitor when watching movies, it will do the 3D no problem, but your Blu-Ray player may need a firmware upgrade or your program to watch movies may need to be upgraded (WinDVD, PowerDVD for example). That free version that came with your player may not play the 3D movie. The "Lightboost" tech that is inherent with 3D Vision 2 is supposed to help brighten the screen when playing 3D content. It seems to work, but I have nothing to really compare it to as I have never used the original 3D Vision. As with all Real 3D content, it is a bit darker due to the glasses, but it was not such that I would complain on this monitor, turn out the lights and look at it from straight ahead and the dark scenes are fine. So I am impressed w/the 3D and it will be used much more than I thought it ever would. (MAKE SURE YOU USE THE SUPPLIED DUAL LINK DVI CABLE FOR 3D VISION TO WORK PROPERLY) I can't stress that enough.

There are a few complaints here of dead/stuck pixels. My monitor did not have any. Also people complaining of light bleed from around the bottom or edges of the screen. The Stand is top notch, the controls are easy enough to use once you get to know where they are. This monitor is a bit on the heavy side for an LCD, but that is of no concern to me, once in place it will never move. The connections are a bit cumbersome to connect, but after reading some helpful reviews, I knew to make sure I did those connections before I put the monitor on the stand and stood it upright. Made it much easier to manage those tight fitting connections for the power cord and the DVI cable.

Overall, I would highly recommend this Monitor if you have the means. It looks amazing, both in 2D and 3D.
