I recently purchased two each of the following 27 inch 1440P IPS monitors to use in a 4 monitor array where I already owned two Acer K272HUL Dbmidpx L 27" IPS monitors. Ranked from favorite to least favorite below:

1) Acer Nitro XV272U Pbmiiprzx 27" WQHD - these are my new favorite 1440p monitors due to the fact that they seem to deliver the best image quality out of all of the monitors that I have tested. I use these for productivity and am not really a gamer. Great text readability, minimal back light bleed, nice sleek design, decent glare reduction without making images blurry (see notes on the Dell unit below). Great controls and menu except a little inconvenient if you have these mounted side by side as the controls are on the right rear of the monitors so might be difficult to access on the left monitor due to placement. You'll need to physically move the monitor to access the controls. Not a big deal to me though.
2) Asus ProArt Display PA278QV - these were my second favorite but I just simply preferred the image quality from the Acer Nitro XV272U a little better. I don't love the measuring lines on the front of the display but suspect they may be useful for video/photo editors? I also think the menu system and buttons can be improved a little. Overall, a nice monitor but didn't quite cut it for me.
3) Acer Nitro VG270U BMIIPX - great image quality and similar image quality like the other Acer's listed above, but the fatal flaw with these is they caused my computer to slow to a crawl whenever my PC would wake from sleep. I contacted Acer support and they believe it to be an issue with the firmware that's installed on the monitors. Returned them.
4) Dell U2719D - really nice sleek design with a great menu system controls and input options but the anti glare coating was too aggressive for my liking and the image quality was just "meh" to me when compared to the two Acer's listed above.

HOWEVER, people report that they just get another monitor that whines, so that's no help at all.

From Dell: "The displays team has not reported a systemic noise fault with the S3221QS line". So don't expect a fix for it. This casts doubt on ALL Dell monitors.

My wife purchased this monitor for me as a birthday present. She ordered it directly from the BenQ website, not through Amazon. I set the monitor up and was thrilled with both the video quality and the audio. But after about 30 minutes, a red vertical line appeared down the middle of the display. That's where the nightmare of dealing with BenQ customer service began.

On May 15 I contacted customer support via their website and informed them of the problem. (It's impossible to get through to tech support on the telephone.) I got no reply until May 20, and that was an email asking for more information.

On May 22 they sent me an email with a form to fill out requesting a replacement. Unfortunately I was traveling at the time and wasn't able to send the form back until June 7. One June 10 I got an email asking me to send them proof of purchase, which shouldn't have been necessary since it was purchased directly from them. I sent back the proof of purchase the same day. On June 13 I got an email saying my RMA had been processed. I also got a separate email stating that the new monitor was being shipped and that "The replacement will contain a prepaid label on the outside of the box to use to get the defective product returned to us." The RMA form said that I would have 10 days to return the defective unit or my credit card would be charged for it.

The replacement monitor arrived on June 17. It works great! I love this monitor! However, there was no prepaid shipping label on the outside, or the inside, of the box. I immediately emailed to ask how to ship back the old monitor. Yesterday I emailed again with the same question. And, having received no response, I emailed again this morning. So that 10 day clock is ticking towards a $160 charge on my credit card, with three days gone and I still don't have a shipping label.

Do the math... I've been dealing with this simple RMA since May 15, 35 days ago, and it has yet to be resolved.

The monitor is an outstanding product and deserves 5 stars, but I will NEVER buy anything from this company again!

If you're looking to get into 144hz gaming on a budget, then this is the monitor to get. Yes, like many others have stated, colors out-of-the-box are garbage. And even after extensive color calibration it won't look nearly as good as even a low-end IPS. Thankfully your eyes will get accustomed after a while and it won't "seem" that bad, although if you put an IPS monitor right next to it the difference is immediately noticeable. That being said, while gaming we tend not to notice the colors too much and the incredible smoothness of 144hz overall makes up for this shortcoming.

As far as eyestrain concerns, when I first got this monitor I fell into the PWM paranoia train and kept the brightness in OSD at 100%, while reducing it in Nvidia CP. I am prone to migranes and I thought the PWM was triggering my symptoms. But after turning the brightness down in OSD and using it for a while, I've realized it really was just paranoia. Either that or it just took a while for my eyes to adjust. Just make sure the overall brightness of the screen is not too high. This is definitely one bright monitor so spare those retinas! During night time turn down the brightness even more if necessary and put a lamp behind the screen or buy some LED backlight strips off Amazon.

If you've already bought this thing and are scratching your head trying to figure out how to make colors not suck, then do the following. First, activate the Asus ICC profile included in the CD, ignore the PCmonitors hype. Google instructions on how to properly activate ICC profiles. Next, open the OSD, switch to Standard Mode, set Brightness to 35, contrast to 50, leave the rest on default. I also set Color temp to 95/100/100 because it seemed too red to me. Forget about adjusting contrast or brightness in Nvidia CP because most games will ignore those settings and have their own contrast and gamma sliders anyway, so make sure to adjust those.

If you're an avid gamer, I would recommend this monitor for sure. Games like Overwatch and CS feel amazing with the 144hz. However, I would certainly not watch movies or do any color-sensitive work on this thing. If I didn't have a separate TV screen for movies, I would probably return it, since I watch TV shows and movies quite a lot.

Having never made a "specialty" monitor purchase before, I was a little wary of the reviews talking about dead pixels and backlight bleeding. However, after much debating and researching of several other ultrawide curved screen monitors, this seemed to be the be choice for the size. I am happy to report that the monitor I received does not appear to have any of these issues, and has been working like a dream.

I was a bit wary when the delivery person showed up with a VERY large box- understandable for the amount of protection this type of monitor likely needs for shipping. The box itself along with the foam packaging is very sturdy and a bit difficult to break down, but definitely provided excellent protection. The monitor itself seems a bit weighty, but most of the package weight appears to come from the monitor stand, which is an absolute monster.

The stand is very sturdy and cool-looking, however, I personally mounted the monitor on an adjustable arm for more desk space. Please be aware if you are using a different mount that has a snap-in mechanism to attach to the monitor, the angle in the center-back of the monitor makes it a bit difficult to get into place- not impossible, but takes a lot of finagling. While possible to mount with one person, I would recommend having two, so one person can hold the monitor while the other guides it into place.

Overall, it is a great alternative to having dual monitors for a work setup- I often use a split screen to work on accounting at home, and the curve actually makes it a bit easier to focus on. In gaming, it is also wonderful for a more immersive experience- Skyrim is absolutely gorgeous on this screen, and the wider field of view makes gameplay phenomenal.

I also love the customizable backlighting features on the monitor- not only does it look neat in general, it can also really help to set the mood for the game you are playing at the time (or even for whatever task you are trying to do in general on the computer). Overall, this is a lovely monitor and I am extremely happy with this purchase.

So for starters, this is a very nice entry to the gaming monitor world. Lets go over some pros and cons,

PROS:
~144hz + Grey to grey time is great. (When running blur busters, i had practically no ghosting.)
~The panel itself is very nice for VA. The contrast + viewing angles are near IPS standard (very good, much better than my TN), and the blacks are pretty dark.
~Being a 1ms monitor as well as a VA panel, This outperforms both IPS (slower) and TN (less vivid picture quality/ viewing angles) while staying within this price range.
~The pannel is also freesync, so that is a plus as well for AMD users. (Being equipped with only nvidia gpu's at the time, i am unable to give feedback on its performance.)

CONS:
~With a resolution of only 1920 x 1080, at 27" the pixels are a bit more noticeable than a 24", but definitely NOT a deal breaker. (keep reading for more info) This does make higher application antialiasing a little more important.
~Not having a fully adjustable stand is a bit annoying. (Its TILT up and down is adjustable... but nothing else.)
~Also not a VESA stand to fix the last issue. (it is not a bad stand, just not adjustable)
~I have a little backlight light bleed on the panel i received. However it is pretty much unnoticeable in day to day use.

Tips/ help:
~The older generation of this model (g27c) had some issues with having the panel display blue artifacting. I can confirm as of now, this issue seems to have been fixed with the upgrade to the new g27c2. As well as a faster response time, Gen 2 is much better than its predecessor. (dont worry too much about reviews pertaining to the older model)
~27" 1920x1080 is not bad at all. (Unlike what some might say.) Unless you are sitting closer than 2 feet-ish, the PPI is not really that noticable.
~A close competitor to this monitor would be the VIOTEK GN32C, while being close in appearance, You may think, "oh nice, this one is 5" larger." While this is true, In my experience, at 32" 1080p, The PPI (pixels per inch) become much more noticeable. To simulate how this feels on your current monitor, open a game and drop the resolution down one or two options. (assuming you have a 20"- 24" 1080p monitor) This might give you a better idea of what to expect at 32 in. (Not being 100% accurate) You can use higher antialiasing to counter some of the effect, but it only works so much to fix the jagged edges due to the pixels being more spaced apart.
~A LOT of people say in their reviews that Grey to Grey time doesn't matter at all. I disagree, while it is not a deal breaker, It does make a bit of difference. Blur trails or "ghosting" behind moving objects becomes slightly less noticable as the response time gets smaller. This is really only important for fast paced fps games.

In conclusion, would I recommend this monitor to the every day user/ gamer? Yes, I definitely would! I find that it falls between great performance for gaming and quality for everyday use, without stressing your pc. (In comparison to the more intensive 1440p) I have very few bad things to say about the monitor. Most of which can be looked over with no problem at all. With this monitor being so new, I understand proper reviews are scarce. so I hope my review covers any questions you have. If not, post a question and ill try to help out as best as I can.

I have been looking at this monitor for the last 6-12 months as a replacement to some decent Asus 23" monitors.  I am a photographer and use lightroom all the time.  I use a Datacolor Spyder 4 for Calibration.  The Asus monitors get about 95-98 percent of RGB and even though the models are the same and they were bought at the same time, they do not have the same brightness.  This Dell monitor was packed well, was easy to put together, came with the right cables (using the right cable is very important),  I ran calibration on this monitor and it was 100 percent of RGB and looking at the before/after, I can see that it pulled out a little red, but that was about it.  Dell monitors come with a calibration sheet and it was obvious that it was very close.  Due to the 4K size I had to adjust Windows 10 and Lightroom in a few ways.  I turned up the text size in Windows, and then went into Lightroom and turned up the font size by 200 percent.  I don't think I'm done playing with all the menu and font sizes, but it works well without super tiny text or icons.  The picture is totally stunning  on the Dell.  It is as-if I was looking through a dirty window on the other monitors.  This is clean/clear/accurate and my photos look fantastic here as well.  Now I need to buy a second one :)

This is a great monitor if your looking for one for your Xbox Series X. A few quick facts, the Series X is capable of doing 4K resolution at 120 FPS but there aren't a lot of games that do that right now but might in the future. You also need a monitor/TV with HDMI 2.1 to be able to output at 4K, 120 FPS. The Series X also does 1440p at 120 FPS and in my opinion, that is the sweet spot.

Before I bought this monitor, I played on a 4K TV and I was a little nervous going down to 2K (1440p) because I felt like I was spoiled with 4K. I did some research and learned that a 2K resolution with a 27 Inch monitor is about as sharp as 1080p at 24 inches. I also learned that 4K resolution is a waste on a monitor under 32 inches because of pixel count. I believe that finding a monitor that does 4K, HDR, and with a low response time is going to be pretty expensive. Then you have to worry about ghosting and other issues that I read about.

After having played on this monitor for a couple months I can tell you that I love gaming on this monitor. I play a lot of First Person Shooters (Warzone, Apex, Destiny 2) as well as RPG's ( Mass Effect, Fallout, Elder Scrolls, The Outer Worlds) and all these games look amazing on this monitor. I have my Series X set to 1440p, 120FPS and in the games that allow up to 120FPS, they are so smooth. As a console player who is use to 30FPS, going up to 60FPS and even 120FPS is awesome.

1440p on a 27 inch monitor looks AMAZING! The 1ms response time is a must for any serious FPS gamer and the curve is awesome. If you buy this for your Xbox Series X I don't think you will be disappointed. There's a lot of features and settings on the monitor itself that you can mess with. This monitor does not support HDR, but for me, this isn't a big deal.

I've been on the search for a good portable monitor for about the last 1-2 years to use as both a portable larger screen for gaming (Android Phone or Switch) as well as a portable monitor for working while on the road or away from my typical desk setup. I use Microsoft Surface products which have pretty great monitors so the bar was already set pretty high, since the portable monitor sits right next to the Surface device in my setup.

I had seen a couple of reviews praising this monitor from Uperfect but had to see it for myself. In a few short words I was blown away. I'll list a few of the great features:
- the monitor is very bright, especially when using the supplied AC adapter or other high wattage supply. One great feature, when the power being supplied is weaker, the monitor sets a lower brightness to allow it to function regardless.
- the colors are rich and vibrant, even when compared to the Surface device or my vibrant phone screen.
- after having used a few portable monitors and other devices needing micro-HDMI, mini-HDMI etc. it was an amazing convenience to just plug a standard HDMI into this monitor. A great feature I didn't know I wanted.
- the monitor settings control uses a series of buttons, NOT joysticks or rotary wheels that break and are delicate as seen on other brands. I have high confidence that these buttons will last and not be damaged when transporting the monitor (as seen on other brands). This is a PORTABLE monitor after all.
- the 13.3 inch size is surprisingly small and portable yet large enough to be very useable. I should mention that the overall monitor is also very light, making it even more portable.
- other little features that make this monitor flexible and convenient for any use: micro-usb passthrough for using wired keyboard/mice, 2X USB-C (one for signal, one for power), mini-display port (Mac) and the headphone jack if your device doesn't already have one (typical these days), the body is metal giving more confidence in the overall durability.
- when propping the monitor up for use, I find it easy to use the pencil method or if I need more angle variability I use my Moko stand (small and easy to transport). Since the monitor is so light (no battery) any light duty stand will do.

The only downvote and it's very minor is that the case is pretty cheap and not very useable. I'm working with Uperfect to remedy this.

Because the picture and overall quality are so high, I'm really looking forward to an eventual update of this monitor with a higher refresh rate (120Hz), and touch input, but keeping the same size, if they decide to go down that route.

Highly recommended for your PC, Phone, Nintendo Switch (with dock of course), Raspberry Pi, SFFPC and uses I haven't realized yet. It's so refreshing to see a brand finally get this right!

Overall, extremely satisfied with this monitor since I only paid $150 for it at Best Buy. If it's on sale below $200, I'd say go for it. It's great for watching YouTube, movies, and playing games on casually or semi-competitively. Coming from a 2009 IPS display, this blows it out of the water in terms of contrast and overall picture quality. If I had room on my desk, I'd be picking up another one.

Good:
- Color reproduction is amazing. Was pretty accurate out of the box.
- Good contrast levels and a built-in dynamic contrast setting if you're into that.
- High range of brightness levels. Gets bright enough that it's actually kind of irritating to use at max in a dark room. Lowest setting is dim, but still keeps a good quality picture.
- Buttons are on the bottom right side of the monitor. Good tactile feedback and easy to find and use.
- The stand is metal, about 7 inches deep/wide, and extremely sturdy. The display shakes very little even when jiggling the corner of the screen by hand. The stand never budges a bit.
- Semi-gloss coating on the display. Nowhere near as reflective as a full gloss display and doesn't have that washed out look of a matte coating. Reflections might be somewhat of a problem in an office environment, but at home, it's a non-issue.

Neutral:
- 2 HDMI and 1 VGA are the only connectors. Would have been nice to have DVI or DP instead of VGA. No idea why companies are still including it as an option. I can't even remember the last graphics card or laptop I saw that had VGA output on it.
- The panel has a 7ms response time, so if you're an extremely competitive gamer, I'd look somewhere else.
- "Borderless" display. The same gimmick that companies have been using the past few years where there's hardly any bezel, but about a 5mm black frame around the screen on the inside. Still looks nice though.

Bad:
- Very small amount of backlight bleeding on the bottom right half of the screen in one small spot that isn't apparent unless the entire screen is black. Not really a con, just throwing it out there for reference.
- There is a bit of ghosting when doing the UFO test on Blur Busters, but none noticeable in Dota 2, Battlefield 4, Diablo 3, or Witcher 3. There is a built-in blur reduction setting which seems to work a little bit, but if turned all the way up it causes overshooting.
Got this for my daughter who does graphic art and gaming. She now has the best of both worlds, but a bit of a challenge to configure.

El monitor está bien para el precio que se está pagando. No es que tiene los mejores colores pero, cumple su función para jugar o ver contenido multimedia.

Ahora bien, no se lo recomendaría a personas que vayan a usarlo para trabajar diseño o arquitectura

I know that this is a monitor made for businesses and people who sit at their screens all day, I do this, but have another computer for that. I'm at this monitor the rest of the evening and it's got a lot of great features built in, even a few I couldn't test because I didn't hook up the USB-C portion of it.

Build Quality:
Build quality is great. The stand is heavy enough to hold it without moving, but light enough that it rocks a little if your desk isn't perfectly flat. I'm coming from a slightly curved monitor so this one feels like the screen bulges the bezels a little, but I think it's just what I was used to looking at before.

Screen Quality:
It's an IPS screen that's nice and bright. The contrast is also great on it - my older monitor was a little washed out no matter what I set. With a few small tweaks I got this LG looking great very quickly. I know it's not an OLED, but the blacks are surprisingly black.

Refresh Rate:
This is where I take issue. 60hz refresh is fine. But that's where we lost a star. It's fine, but it's noticeable coming from anything that's not 60hz. I was on a 144hz before, with its own issues - but this just feels slower now. I can tell doing small things like scrolling a webpage - but it's less noticeable to me personally in the games that I'm playing on it.

Extra Features:
USB charging is really nice. Having HDMI, DisplayPort, AND USB-C to connect is excellent.
It comes with a built in webcam and mic - that I was pretty convinced didn't exist but at the very top of the monitor in the back - it slides up showing the camera and mic housing. I didn't test this out because I'm not connected via USB-C, if I decide to connect that, I'll take screenshots of the quality. I'd expect it to be on par with a laptop quality webcam and mic - since the sensor size is pretty small (about 1/3 the size of my Logitech webcam)

Overall
I really like this. I'm legitimately replacing a "better" monitor (27" instead of 24", curved, higher refresh rate) because this one is solid and for the non-gaming I do - this is hands down a better monitor. Thankfully for most of the games I play, this monitor is good enough, hence the 4/5 instead of 5/5.

This KUMK 15.6'' 4K IPS Travel Monitor measures 14.2" x 8.7" x 0.39" and weighs a pound and a half. This KUMK 15.6'' 4K IPS Travel Monitor has a 3840 by 2160 resolution with a 16:0 aspect ratio. This KUMK 15.6'' 4K IPS Travel Monitor comes with 100% sRGB color gamut and 400cd/m squared which is about 400 nits of brightness. For connectivity, this KUMK 15.6'' 4K IPS Travel Monitor comes equipped with 2-USB Type C ports, 1-mini HDMI port, and a 3.5mm headphone jack.

As a web development and design student, I am always looking for bright color accurate displays to use to complete assignments. This display is the best portable display in my library. The combination of its color accuracy, 15.6" size, and 400 cd/m squared brightness ensure I can create content that will print with the colors I intend. This KUMK 15.6'' 4K IPS Travel Monitor is also great for content consumption as well.

My mobile design setup includes an ASUS Zenbook Duo PC, this monitor, and a laptop portable power bank. I love that two USB C cables can connect this device to power and extend my laptop screen. I also appreciate this case coming with a cover that doubles as a stand for those times when I have forgotten to take this item with me on the go.

What I liked most about this KUMK 15.6'' 4K IPS Travel Monitor was:
- Bright, beautiful, color-accurate display
- Lightweight screen that ships with a faux leather magnetic cover that protects the screen in transit
- Comes with sufficient accessories to power the monitor and connect it to a source
- Connects to computers, tablets, iPads, phones, and more

What I liked least about this KUMK 15.6'' 4K IPS Travel Monitor was:
- I would have preferred a 16:10 aspect ratio to the 16:9 aspect ratio that comes with this monitor to get more content on the screen.
- Labels itself as a 'gaming monitor' but only has a 60Hz refresh rate.

Overall, if you are looking for a bright, color-accurate portable display to take along with you to complete design work then this KUMK 15.6'' 4K IPS Travel Monitor has to make your shortlist. At the time of this review this KUMK 15.6,'' 4K IPS Travel Monitor can be had for less than $250. This KUMK 15.6'' 4K IPS Travel Monitor has a 100% sRGB color gamut, 400 nits of brightness, and an acceptable 1000:1 contrast ratio. I found myself wishing this monitor had a 16:10 aspect ratio and I would have preferred a 90Hz refresh rate especially since the manufacturer labeled this device a 'gaming monitor.' That said, this KUMK 15.6'' 4K IPS Travel Monitor allows me to delve deep into design projects while I am mobile and I can trust my printed output will be consistent with what I see on my screen. For this reason, alone I give this KUMK 15.6'' 4K IPS Travel Monitor a confident recommendation. 4.5 out of 5.0

This is an excellent monitor. I actually bought it from Newegg when it was priced at $99.99 with free shipping, but I think the $119.99 current price here is a great value as well. I was afraid that 1600 x 900 might be a little smaller than I wanted, but it's turned out to be plenty of screen real estate. The 20" monitor is the perfect size for that resolution; text is readable at its default sizes, but the pixels aren't so big that images look blocky (as they might if the screen were larger but the resolution were the same). Pros: well put together, solid stand and base, adjustable by vertical, tilt or rotation; brilliant colors; various viewing modes for gaming, movies, etc.; runs Xbox X games well at 60hz; built in speakers; two HDMI 2 ports.

Cons: This monitor does not have true HDR but HDR-10. Setting the monitor to HDR will make some features (Brightness, Contrast, etc.) inaccessible. The HDR-10 for the Xbox Series X adds a lot of brightness, which washes the display out as if it were tinted. It is not a recommended feature to use in this case. If you can spend the money and want HDR, get a different monitor.

Another con is the internal speakers are weak. I bought a soundbar to solve this problem.

Sum: I think the manufacturer's choice to provide many features makes the whole less than it could be. It's better to exclude a few features in order to focus more on the priorities, like 120hz refresh rate.. It's worth the price as an entry into 4K gaming but it's lacking in some features.

After my 7 year old monitor died last week, I found this one by reading reviews on Amazon and elsewhere. It came this afternoon.

The hardest part of setting it up was getting it out of the box. I like to take things out gently, and store all the packing in the attic for future use. I appreciate the use of cardboard packing rather than a lot of molded foam (more environmentally friendly), but it's a very complicated 'origami,' and I finally tore up lots of tabs to get it out.

After that, however, installation was a snap. The start-up directions are simple, and this monitor (and the sound bar I also purchased) showed a real consideration of efficiency and elegance in the design. I really appreciate that.

I'm guessing that all the drivers are present in Windows 7 -- I didn't need to use the cd to get it going . . . everything worked perfectly.

The screen looks great, colors are terrific - I do lots of digital photography so that's really important for me. I've been picking monitors for working in photoshop for years -- but this is my first Dell. The aspect ratio is great for having all the tools available, but still have enough space for the image to be large. It's pretty amazing to have this wonderful screen (image and size) for working on photos for such a reasonable price.

The rotation is great! I love that I can turn it vertical (portrait) to look at documents.

Finally, I love that there are USB ports on the monitor -- I had that on my last monitor and it was a big factor in my selection.

So, while I've only had this monitor for about an hour, I love it.

[CONS] However, 'ghosts' of your UI/browser tabs/desktop wallpaper show up very fast, within 10 mins or less. Not ideal for long working sessions with the frame in one place, or a game with a static UI. Additionally, within the past few months, the backlight bleed has been getting past an acceptable level, from just the ambient zones of it to the very distinct corners of it pictured. [OVERALL] A shame this did not last longer, as I really liked this monitor overall. For the investment in a mid-high end monitor, I would want something that would get me 5-6 years at least.

The image on this display is stunning. Color is better than expected, and backlight is nice and uniform. Dual input for both audio and video, including DVI, is very handy. Switching between sources is just a button away. The easy to use controls at the upper right also give direct adjustment of brightness, contrast, and speaker volume. By pressing a 'Menu' button, you also get a host of other less needed adjustments. The display is very automatic, and only needed three adjustments after a day's use: I had to turn *down* the brightness, I needed to turn *up* the volume, and I had to tweek the phase adjustment. The phase adjustment wasn't required at all until I got to the "Shutdown" Windows screen that had dithered shading. All other screens, including line art, graphics, as well as high-res photos, was rock solid. The only flaw is the built-in 1W speakers which are very underpowered, and have no base response at all. The only advantage of having them in this unit is to keep your desk neat, but if you're going to listen to anything for an extended period, consider external speakers or another model display. Another alternative is to just plug in headphones in their handy jack at the bottom edge. For me, this worked just fine. In all, the trendy styling and great LCD have me very happy.

I owned this monitor for roughly two years and was happy with the feature set and display quality, until it developed some hotspots along the left side bezel.  I had a similar thing happen with an LED backlit television, so I knew there was an issue with the diffusion lenses for that portion of the LED array.  I contacted Asus and they were reasonably quick to respond, even providing me a prepaid Fedex label to return the unit for service.  So far, so good.  They suggest a 7-10 day turnaround for warranty repairs, which I felt quite reasonable - so I waited.  After two weeks or so, I get an email indicating the unit was not repairable and they proposed to send me a refurbished monitor that is completely different (wide gaming monitor 21:9 vs the 16:9 unit I sent) because parts were unavailable for my original monitor.  I balked initially, but was essentially told to either accept the replacement (which was a refurbished unit) or wait until who knows when for my parts to be available.  I suggested replacement with a new, similar monitor and they refused.  So I'm left with something completely different than what I bought, not well suited to the intended use (general purpose and photography editing) and with only the balance of the manufacturer's warranty on a refurb monitor.  I would NOT recommend Asus monitors as a result.

Amazing monitor for this price point! i've been using this for more than 9 months and didn't have any problems with it. It's very slim and lightweight.

I bought this monitor a few weeks ago to assist with the development of touchscreen software and I have been extremely happy with the purchase. I decided on this monitor for several reasons:

-Solid glass across the screen without a border. This allows me to use the entire touchscreen surface and all of Windows 8's features.

-10 point touch

-1920 x 1080 resolution

- Cost less than $500

- 100x100 VESA Mountable

I was a little reluctant to purchase at first due to some negative comments about connectivity / durability and a few people mentioned issues with receiving their unit DOA. The biggest complaint I saw was in regards to the USB cable. I want to mention that yes, the USB cable must be attached in order to use the touch feature. This is standard with all touchscreens. Some form of communication needs to be implemented in order for the computer to use the monitor as an input. This monitor uses a standard USB 3.0 A to B cable. It seems that many of the reviewers were upset because it does not use a typical USB 2.0 cable, but I prefer manufactures to keep up-to-date and use the latest and greatest technology. Please note that a USB 3.0 device can be plugged into a USB 2.0 port. You won't get the higher data transfer of 3.0 but after testing, I can say that you are able to use all 10 points when connected to a 2.0 port.

Several reviewers mentioned they thought the stand would break when making adjustments. I did not experience this. Yes, you hear a click and need to be a little less than delicate when moving the kickstand, but I do not believe it is worse or better than any other electronic part with plastic movable parts.

While specking out touchscreens, I noticed a considerable shift towards the design we find here. Manufactures are moving towards the kickstand design because that's what the market wants. Touchscreens used in reception areas, conference rooms, classrooms, and art space typically need a monitor that leans backwards to accommodate users that will usually be standing. This feature was not very use full when placing on my desk. I picked up a $40 VESA desk mount, removed the kickstand, and it looks amazing.

I am currently using the monitor for two computers, one running Windows 7 and one running Windows 8. I also have a 20” monitor (non-touchscreen) on a HDMI switcher. So far, no issues to report. Switching is seamless and Windows detects which monitor is connected while changing my resolutions, main display, and will enable extended desktop automatically.

The only thing I can think of that is a real draw back would be the high amount of reflection from the black on the screen. It will take a few lighting tricks to be able to use this monitor in a video, but it's doable.

The image quality is amazing, the price is right, and as long as you have space, I say go for it.

Its a good quality monitor for the price but i CANNOT for the life of me get the colors correct on it.  I have tried all of the settings for over an hour and just can't get it right.  The whites look dirty and its driving me crazy :(
It looks great. After 4 hours of use I tend to get headaches though. I'm a CAD Draftsman.

Not ready to make the jump from 1080p to 1440p yet.  Too many things I just couldn't get past on this one.  The super large base that sticks out over the keyboard off the front of my monitor stand.  The quirky and annoying button placement.  I could have gotten beyond this if the interface was more logical.  I am spoiled with Dell's fantastic consistent interface.  The shiny plastic is absurd on a more expensive device.  The product should not only feel but look premium.  I couldn't get the color balance to look right.  My first reaction, having used IPS panels for about 10 years was that this is not an IPS panel.  For some reason I couldn't get the panel to be crisp and unsaturated.  The blacks were way too dark for my taste.  It looks like I will be sticking to Dell for now.  There are some other offerings in the 24-27 inch range that are better right now.

Only buy this monitor if you plan on using it for a year, and then plan on dumping it into the garbage. I bought this monitor as my second screen for my laptop. It lasted just about a year when it started to automatically shutoff after 20-30 minutes of use. The screen only lights up, but does not turn back on. Don't bother calling LG for help either, they will tell you to reset it and then ship it to them (on your dollar) for "repairs". Save yourself the headache, and buy a Dell or HP. Not worth the reduced price.

This monitor turned out to be a big disappointment. I bought it because I thought Samsung was supposed to be known for good image quality, but thats where it all goes wrong with this one. To be fair, the build quality is very good and the size is perfect to be immersive in games.

However, the image quality problems started on day one. I noticed these weird horizontal lines that went across the whole monitor. Coming from a 4k monitor, I thought maybe it was just easier to see the pixels, but I compared it to a friends Ben Q monitor of the same size and resolution and it looked completely fine to me. I chalked it up to being from the "quantum leds" and eventually got used to it. I can still see it if I look for it, but its not as annoying anymore.

The second issue deals with weird horizontal lines again, but I'm not sure if they are related or not. Depending on how my open windows are positioned, if I move a non-fullscreened window up and down, there will be a weird shadow that follows it to the left and right. This shadow goes from both edges of the window I'm moving all the way to the edge of the monitor. I've never seen moving a window affect the rest of the monitor like this, but it is very distracting when it happens.

The third image quality issue is with these weird sploches the persist no matter what. I have two lines, one horizontal and one vertical, and they each span the entire monitor. It looks like it is made up of a bunch of circles overlapping each other, and it is a slightly different shade of whatever color should be underneath it. For games like Overwatch with lots going on, this is unnoticable. But for games like Diablo 2, where you have a static background image that pans across the screen, it is extremely noticable and makes me wish I had a different monitor.

My next issue with this deals with the controls on the back of the monitor. I work from home with a work laptop but I use the same desk and monitor for work and personal use. I have my desktop connected with display port and my laptop connected with HDMI. Often throughout the day I will switch between them both depending on what I am doing. You'd think it would be easy, but more often than not the monitor doesn't recognize my laptop and goes back to my desktop. For some reason Samsung does not let you pick which source to use, it cycles through them. You can suggest a source, but if it doesn't find an input it will keep on scrolling. I have no idea why this monitor can go from being on HDMI showing my laptop screen, I switch to display port and it shows me desktop, then 10 minutes later I try to switch back to HDMI but it doesn't let me, it auto cycles back to displayport. Nothing on my laptop has changed, and even worse, it doesn't always do this, so it makes finding the problem impossible. Samsung, just let me set which source to use, then show that. TVs have done this for decades, they don't automatically switch inputs, they let you be in charge and pick them.

To top all of this off, this monitor will randomly stop working on me for a bit. The screen goes completely black. Usually it comes back after a few seconds, but if I'm in the middle of a work meeting or a video game, I can't have the monitor cutting out on me. A few times this happened while gaming, it also crashed my audio and game, requiring me to restart my computer. Gotta love that while you're gaming with friends.

Overall I am very disappointed with this. Unfortunately I paid about $700 for it, which hurts a lot considering all my problems. Once I can find an affordable way to buy a better, non-Samsung, ultra wide, I'm definitely throwing this piece of garbage where it belongs.

Purchased 3 of these monitors in hopes to daisy chain them all and have a single USB-C connect to my laptop. So that was successful for the first and second monitors. The third monitor was stuck at 640x480 resolution 😭.

That was like the only resolution available too under “Lost Modes” too. I tried to make a custom resolution (using Intel’s Graphic Command Center) of 1920x1080 to match the other 2 monitors, and it kinda worked but it was hella fuzzy. Basically it’s active resolution was still 640x480 but stretched to 1920x1080. Weird, right?

Pros:
-Stand it comes with is really nice and tall, with good adjustment range. This is the only feature that really impressed me. It's 100mm VESA mount, so I'm putting this stand on my other monitor because it's the perfect height to place the monitor just above my 15" laptop screen.

-Decent viewing angles, but that's kind of cancelled out by the fact that the light bleed is even more annoying at an angle, even showing on lighter backgrounds.

Cons:
-It's a bit yellow no matter how I adjust the color settings unless I make it ridiculously blue.
-The backlight itself seems a bit yellow, judging by the bleed on a black screen.
-The backlight bleed is incredibly annoying, particularly when viewed at an angle.
-It has some built-in speakers which are unbelievably bad. Think free airplane over-ear headphones bad. I'm only using this with 5-6 year old laptops, and they all have better speakers.

-Stand could have been built to allow you to rotate the monitor vertical, in fact it looks from the outside like it should rotate, but you have to unscrew the monitor and turn it on the mount plate if you want that.

It's $90 more than this monitor if you want a brand new color perfect tempered glass QH2700 model with a scaler (so you can use lower resolutions if you want, or VGA connection if you can't do such high resolution over DVI) You might get a dark pixel or a scratch but trust me, that's way better. I purchased this monitor as someone who wanted to get back into the Ultrawide market - my first one decided during a move that it didn't want to work anymore. Tragic, really but it was a 3 year old X35 that ran at 2560x1080. I knew this would be expensive, but based on reviews by some of the different tech YouTubers I've seen, I opted to pull the trigger on this one over the Predator X35, as it was a couple hundred cheaper with the main tradeoff being HDR400 on this vice HDR1000 on the Predator - more on HDR later.

My overall impression - it's worth it if you've got the money to burn, but if not I'd suggest something at a lower price point. There's a 34" variant of this that's 3440x1440 with HDR and most of the same features that's about half the MSRP of this one.

Pros-
- Ultrawide, but a little taller. This is a 38" ultrawide monitor with a 24:10 aspect ratio as opposed to the traditional 21:9 or 32:9 of other ultrawides, which gives a bit more vertical screen real estate at 3840x1600. Normally, the 35" monitors that this competes with run at 3440x1440. It's not a massive difference, but it helps for productivity.
- This display is GORGEOUS. Bezels are unoffensive, the light ring on the back is pretty easy to tweak (or disable), and gets REALLY bright. HDR looks fantastic (provided you know what settings to use, more in the cons). The included stand is hefty but doesn't take up my whole space or have an overpowering gamer aesthetic. I have a monitor arm, so I don't use the included stand but I wouldn't mind using it if I couldn't use the arm.
- I/O is pretty standard - HDMI and Displayport with an integrated USB hub. You need to use the USB hub if you want to control the RGB software from your PC. There's some nifty features there and a couple of modes that aren't accessible through the OSD.
- Includes everything you need. All of the display input cables and USB are included, and the packaging isn't absolutely massive like other ultrawides.

Cons-
- The 24:10 aspect ratio can throw off some games that normally do support ultrawides and result in small black bars on the left and right of the screen. I don't notice in-game, but it's definitely there in some big titles like Call of Duty. This isn't LG's fault, but it's a consideration to take that this isn't a super common aspect ratio.
- Though the monitor officially supports 144hz and 175hz (on overclock), there's some pretty significant tradeoffs. Chroma subsampling and coronas can become a pretty serious problem if response time is set too high or the refresh rate is 175. This can also impact HDR performance. For me, the sweet spot is 120hz, Response time set to "fast", with HDR enabled (10bpc, YCbCr444). HDR Disabled I'm okay going up to 144, but that can be tough to achieve even with a 2080ti on modern AAA titles. Microsoft is terrible about HDR within Windows, so I generally disable it outside of games.
- HDR400 is a fair bit dimmer than HDR1000 on competing monitors. It still looks fantastic and I'm 100% down with it, but if you want the best possible HDR experience, this ain't it, chief.
- This display has a hefty price tag. I don't think its totally unreasonable given the feature set, but I'm of the mindset that they really need to do better on the marketing for this one to make sure people know what they're getting into with the different modes, HDR settings and so forth.
- It's 2020 and this monitor doesn't have an internal power supply. I get that it would have added weight to a monitor that's already pretty hefty, but come on, the power brick on this thing is a literal brick. I used some command strips to slap it onto the underside of my desk and forgot about it, but it's frustrating that I had to do that at all.

Was excited to try this monitor, but turned out to be a bust for me

Setup: Relatively easy, but more complex than most monitors (no snap-in mechanism for the monitor)

Issues:
1) Two 1.25 inch Vertical Stripes on the screen where the image is lighter than the surrounding screen. I tried changing the brightness / contrast settings and changed the background color in hopes I wouldn't really notice it, but given its location it's bothersome. I didn't see many other complaints about this, so maybe this is just a one off issue.

2) Upon turning on the monitor got a warning that if I leave the same image or screen up for longer than 30 minutes I risk "burning-in" the image to the screen. WHAT?! This is a computer monitor, there is almost ALWAYS something static on the screen (e.g menu bars, start menus, etc...). I'm not sure how they expect this to be used for web browsing, programming, or even watching the news given that warning. It seems pretty ridiculous to me, and should be displayed (prominently imo) on the description for this product (it's not).

To compensate the monitor has something called "Pixel Orbiting" which moves the screen a few pixels in a direction every so often. While this seems like an okay implementation, I still don't know how this is supposed to help completely. I would think even if you move the whole screen a few pixels in different directions there is always going to be some set of pixels that aren't changing colors.

3) The HDMI cable they included is wonky. For some reason no matter which laptop I plug it into, if touched slightly it will black out the screen and I have to re-adjust the HDMI cable.

Conclusion: Was hoping this monitor was going to be new go-to, but was disappointed in the lighter vertical stripes and 30 minute warning. Given the issues, rather than getting a replacement I'll get my usual Samsung from Amazon.

So, why only three stars? I admit that I am a bit picky when it comes to imaging tools. My most recent monitor lasted me about 10-years, and is still going strong, only it's a 19-inch, and I've longed for a wide screen for some time. My main computer use is image editing, and some video. For me, having consistent color is critical. Although my previous monitor was an older technology Active Matrix, the color and lighting was quite consistent across the panel - maybe the smaller size helped with this. There was some color shift at wider angles, but very minimal. The screen was also a matte finish, so reflection was minimal, but the blacks weren't quite black enough.

How does the HP 2509 compare to my old monitor? Here goes:

- Color: Perhaps it is because the panel is so large, but the color shift at angles is almost unbearable for photo image editing. You will find that you really have to pay attention to where your eyes are positioned, compared to the image, as even the slightest change in position affects the color;

- Contrast: The contrast is terrific, but... the fluorescent lamps back-lighting the screen at the top and bottom bezels bleed through the screen at the top and bottom. Not even worth consideration, unless you deal with black backgrounds, as do I;

- Screen finish: The glossy screen helps quite a lot with the contrast, compared to the light-scattering effects of matte screens. If you are not used to glossy screens, or if you have trouble focusing your vision, you may find the reflections difficult to handle. I work in a fairly dark room, with some side lights on to keep my eyes from dilating, and I recommend dark clothing and no windows behind you. I could probably live with the reflective screen;

- Sharpness: I'm OK with the sharpness. Others have negatively commented, but maybe I'm not as sensitive to this as they are;

- Resolution: Wonderful pixel resolution, and since I don't have to get too close to the screen with the larger monitor, I don't believe the dot-pitch is a problem. The joy of such a large screen is that one need not put his or her nose against the screen;

- HDMI: I have never used HDMI for a computer monitor, and only just discovered that my old video card [GT220] has HDMI out. Be aware, however, that audio though my HDMI out seems intermittent through the monitor. Not sure why. I have nice speakers plugged into my sound card, but, with the external speakers turned off, I heard Skype start-up sounds through the monitor. Weird, huh? I'll have to play around with that, but if you want more than system sounds, you probably plan to keep some nice speakers plugged in, anyway.

Verdict - I am likely returning my 2509 as the fluorescent bleed and the color shift is unacceptable for photo editing. If I kept this monitor, I would likely keep my old one next to it for color work, and only use the HP for composition. That may be a reasonable set up until I can afford one of those Apple Cinema Displays at close to $1k.

Thanks for reading my comments; I hope they help you decide.

Great monitor with G-sync features. One star off that you can't put this monitor to sleep, and that it sucks down energy even in deep sleep mode. Kinda strange that dell can't figure out how to get it right. Maybe newer ones don't have this issue, but I found a work around, you'll have to make a batch file, pretty easy to do (google it) and place it on your desktop, and when you want to go to bed or go for a walk or whatever and instead of turning the monitor off, just double click the batch file. Anyway, here is the code I have for windows 10.

echo off

C:\Windows\System32\powercfg.exe -Change -monitor-timeout-dc 1
C:\Windows\System32\powercfg.exe -Change -monitor-timeout-ac 1

START /MIN /WAIT C:\Windows\System32\scrnsave.scr -s

C:\Windows\System32\powercfg.exe -Change -monitor-timeout-dc 15
C:\Windows\System32\powercfg.exe -Change -monitor-timeout-ac 15

Basically what it does, is it puts it to sleep instantly, and when you move the mouse to take it back out of sleep it, I normally want my monitor to go to sleep in 15 mins, it reverts the change, but you can change this to whenever you want your monitor to sleep... So change 15 in the last two lines to whatever 30 mins, 45, 60 etc...etc... This pretty much solves this monitor's sleep issues for me.

Note to make a batch file,

1 Open a text editor like notepad (NOT word or wordpad)
2 Type or copy the code (6 lines above) paste into notepad
3 Save this as sleep.bat, make sure there is no .txt extension after the .bat.
4 Double-click the file icon.

Congrats you just made your first batch file!

OK. Have used this for three weeks now. I start by saying I was amazed one of these existed.

Pros:
-This is saving my eyes as others have mentioned. The pandemic has increased my screen time and I have computer vision syndrome. For that, I'm glad I bought it, since my eyes became so disfunctional, I couldn't work. So in that sense, it's worth the pricey sum of $1.3k. That the cost of being an early adopter.
-Nice form factor. Light. High res.
-Has bolt holes on the back if you want to mount to a stand. But the kickstand isn't bad. Sometimes I do prop it up a bit more.
-Definitely get the backlit version. If you eyes are already bad... My office is pretty bright, but the backlight helps a lot. Like the sepia best, since even easier on my eyes.
-Pretty responsive. You can adjust this - tradeoff between speed and how dark the pixels are. While some people may be turned off by all the adjustments and tradeoffs, I appreciate them. You can find your groove and then settings are permanent until you change them again.

Cons:
-Requires a lot of adjustement. Read the manual and play with these adjustments, as they were critical for getting this to work satisfactorily. Especially the contrast control if you are trying to "see" colors (e.g. highlighting in a document). So I tend to use one contrast setting for Word docs. One for email. One for pictures/videos, etc. It's not that bad to tweak these. But sometimes I just have to move something to the color monitor to work.
-Have to push "C" to clear the "snow quite" a bit

Neutral
-I first made teh Dasung my second monitor which caused the touchscreen to work on my primary monitor (Win 10, extended screen), making it useless. You have to set the Dasung to your "Main Display." Then this works.
-If you aren't using "dark mode" theme for your color monitor, it helps a lot too. I also use "Stylebot" etension in Chromium browsers which generally allows you make webpage dark mode. Make sure you put the "M" setting on "M3," which gives the most shades of grayscale. But sometimes higher contrast is beneficial. You will have to play with this setting to find the best for your applications.

Can't wait for a color version (have had 32 color e-paper watches for years, which I charge once/month). This would make the monitor more useful, as I expect it would require much less, if any, contrast adjustment.

The good news is that it looks GREAT, and operates seamlessly connected to the HDMI2 output from the Mac Mini. I didn't have any success trying to connect it via USB-C. As others have said, the directions suck, but if you've half a mind, it isn't hard to figure out how to put it together. Be aware, this thing is heavy and awkward to get to the back ports once you have it in place.

The curved screen took a bit to get use to - like the first day or so of getting a new pair of glasses. But now it feels fine. Another unexpected plus was that my iPhone panoramic pics look almost normal on this curved screen.

One thing that I became aware of was that because I was moving from a two monitor setup to a single ultra wide, I had to move my mouse more often to get to the top menu of a program (which was sitting top left of the screen like it should). On a two monitor setup, the menu options are replicated on both monitors (even if they're not mirroring), so if you have something on the right monitor, you can access it's menu options right above it. On the ultra wide, if you have a program sitting on the right side of the monitor, you have to go all the way to the top left of the screen to get to the menu options. It's a work flow thing, and certainly nothing to really complain about.

Bottom line - I love this monitor and am very glad that I added it to my rig and workflow.

Coming from an Asus VS247H-P, I decided to take the plunge and get something nice. Here's a summary of my thoughts so far:

- Packaging is very nice, which is a near-expected plus for something with a ~700$ pricetag.
- Asides from minor smudging on the screen that I was able to wipe off, there were no cosmetic issues anywhere on the monitor.
- No setup other than plugging in cords and adjusting placement on my desk. There's been no screen wobble, which is not the case for my other Asus monitor.
- I would say I was deathly scared of getting a bad panel, but it looks like Asus' QC is better now/I got lucky. I have yet to see any dead pixels, discoloration, or backlight bleed
- Coming from a productivity standpoint, I immediately noticed that any on-screen movement was clearer. The 1440p resolution also introduced a nice increase in detail. However, if you only plan on using this to write up documents or browse the internet, your money can be better spent elsewhere.
- Coming from a gaming standpoint, I love it! A few games of CS:GO felt oddly refreshing with 144hz 1440p. Slower-paced games that don't benefit as much from the high refresh rate, like Torchlight II, still looked and felt "new". I'm trying to avoid hyping this as much as I can, but it was very nice to experience tried-and-true games of mine on a much better monitor. Oh, and GSync makes lower fps games still feel fluid and smooth. Very cool stuff, especially since I'm running of a nVidia GTX 770 until those GTX 1080 prices settle down.
- If I had to complain about one thing so far, I would say the inability to change the color of the monitor's base. Performance wise, the colors on this monitor are very good (better than my other Asus), but it's still a TN panel. If you want more vibrant colors at the cost of response time, visit the Asus PG279Q instead.

I will update this review if anything changes or issues arise (fingers crossed they don't!).

I did a lot of research on monitors, learning the lingo which had changed since my last monitor purchase. I upgraded from a 21" Sony Trinitron Multiscan 500PS which I was very happy with, but Sony no longer makes monitors so I was back to square one. After reading many reviews on various brands I decided on a Samsung. Overall the reviews were very good, and Samsung had the best warranty (3 years). Some brands I researched folks said crapped out the minute the 1 year warranty expired.

After creating a chart of specifications and plugging about 15 Samsung models into it, and reading reviews on Amazon and several other websites, I decided on the Samsung 2693HM. First thing most folks wouldn't know is that Samsung has a BUNCH of different models, most aren't available in stores so go to their website and you can see a very detailed PDF file of the specs for each model.

I found a really good price here on Amazon but was afraid to buy online because of the few bad reviews. What if I hated the quality or it had bad pixels? Virtually all the manufacturers allow some bad pixels and won't take it back for that. I couldn't get a clear answer on how easy returns would be so I figured I'd go to a local store and look at it in person.

That didn't help. The picture clarity in the store was AWFUL. But my tech fella who went with me told me that the way they hook up monitors in stores to display them isn't going to show you the best it can offer. He said I could not trust what we were looking at.

Long story short I bought it locally, in the store with a really good return policy in case I hated it, their return policy allowed even for bad pixels, got it home and hooked it up to my MacPro1,1 (the original MacPro that came out in 2006). I paid $120 more for buying it locally to have the return policy peace of mind.

I do not have any addons to the MacPro, no extra graphics or game cards, just how it came off the shelf. It took a LOT of diddling to get it to my happy place, both in it's calibrations and using the Mac preferences. But once I got it to the happy place, the picture is excellent. No problems with the viewing angles or colors fading at the edges. No bad pixels that I can find. The picture is very clear. I had to change the fonts for everything, finally settling on Verdana 14 for most things. And turned font smoothing off for 9 and smaller. Originally the text was awful and hard to read until I made the changes. I had to make the text much bigger for this monitor and go with a heavier font.

Would I recommend this monitor? Yes, absolutely. Picture quality is excellent, this model has a lot of features including built in speakers, height adjustment, swivel, tilt, pivot, fast 5 ms response time.

Downside: Yup, as everybody said the buttons are awful. Not only do you need a flashlight, but the menu keeps turning itself off before you've finished making your changes so you have to bring it back up again. Of course once you've gotten to your happy place the buttons won't matter anymore so it's a minor downside. Also, don't make a bad judgement until you've done everything possible to maximize the picture quality and font quality. I spent hours getting it to its happy place. Especially the font (which you'll need to change the default in all your programs) because it initally looked awful, fuzzy and hard to read. If you have it too small some of the letters turn green. If you increase the size and choose heavier fonts the green goes away and the text becomes very clear.

What do I use it for? I create graphics for t-shirts and computer games, so image quality is important to me.

This is the monitor to consider if you want a little more of everything over the standard 24”, 1080p, 60hz monitor.
The monitor comes with a pretty stable stand. According to the manual you should attach the neck to the monitor first before attaching the base to the neck. The stand allows tilting the monitor 5 degrees forward to 22 degrees backward to allow for a wide range of angles for using it. The neck includes a handle that can be folded out to help carry the monitor, although you should verify the neck is still attached to the base before letting go as the friction between the neck and the base is fairly loose. VESA holes are also included if you want to use a different mounting solution.
The power plug and hdmi ports and 3.5mm audio in the back are tricky to access absent room to rotate the monitor sideways, but you only need to access them during setup. Not too hard to find the headphone jack by feel although I prefer having the headphone jack on the side instead. The intention of the rear jack is for you to connect a dedicated higher quality speaker rather than use a personal headphone.
At 9mm, the pixel-free bezel area on the monitor’s edge are reasonably sized in proportion to the overall size of the monitor, but it’s certainly not the smallest size bezel out there. Overall design of the monitor is nice without intruding on the screen. A small exception is the power button has a pretty bright white LED next to it whenever the monitor is on, which could affect your experience if you’re aiming to enjoy some content in darkness.
The menu is easy to navigate and pretty intuitive: you start by using the printed icons to initiate a function, and then the on screen UI will guide you if the buttons change function.
QHD/ 2K/ 1440p provides excellent detail for entertainment. It’s also an adequate amount of screen estate for most tasks and a step up in screen estate from 1080p, but I prefer having the screen space of 4K/2160p. If productivity is important to you, 27” 4K monitors can be had for just a little bit more money. Some people with eyesight issues may not be able to make use of higher pixel densities. My main monitor for the past year or so has been a 4K, 15.6” monitor (you read that right) and I am starting to have trouble using it from a distance. I was shocked at how much more accurately I was typing and clicking when I switched to this monitor from my small monitor. If you’re not a proficient touch typist and touch clicker then you too will benefit from switching to a larger monitor.
75hz is a hair higher than the standard 60hz, and in some situations the extra frame rate is worth it. Having used a gaming laptop with a 75hz screen for several years, I can’t say I’m a huge fan of this frame rate, as it causes issues with screen capture and editing, screen tearing etc since so many systems are designed around 60hz. If you have an AMD GPU, then the FreeSync feature of this monitor mitigates some problems caused by this uncommon frame rate by changing the monitor’s framerate to match the output of your computer. If you have an NVidia GPU like me, then you won’t benefit from FreeSync and should look for a monitor with G-Sync.
The monitor has no internal speaker but the quality of the 3.5mm audio output is good.
Power consumption at lowest brightness/ backlight intensity is measured at 12W. Maximum brightness results in 30W of power draw to really light up the screen when you need it. The high maximum brightness alongside the matte screen makes this monitor perform excellently to mitigate reflections in brightly lit environments such as those with lots of direct sunlight in an office – great for productivity.
With 30W of power draw, the thick desktop PC like power cable rated for 10A, 250V (that’s ~2500W) included in the box is overkill. Lenovo could’ve run this off USB-C and a small power brick if they wanted to. To be fair, nobody expects this kind of monitor to be at the forefront of technology, and I suppose a thick power cable will have better longevity. Most of the tech in this monitor has been around for a while, time tested and proven.
In terms of color and image quality, a matte screen is not going to look as good as a glossy screen but there are no major issues with it. The monitor arrives with the “reddish” profile turned on in the internal settings, which I switched to the neutral color profile. There’s an sRGB mode that adjusts brightness and color to some sort of industry standard for the most accurate color. There is also the low blue light color setting that is all the rage, for eye protection. This monitor does not support HDR.
I ran a simple dead pixel test and did not spot any dead pixels.
At a price point of $270 msrp presently discounted to $230, this monitor is of average value among monitors with similar specs. Lenovo did a good job with the usability and quality of the monitor, so if this particular combination of features is what you’re looking for, you can certainly pull the trigger with confidence.

What I liked:

+ Great response times: I don't think anyone is going to miss their 144hz TN panel after using this.
+ Near perfect gamma 2.2 tracking on default gamma 2 mode
+ Average Delta-E around 1.5: not bad
+ Amazing color gradient handling when set to 10bit mode
+ No BLB or dead pixels

What I disliked:

- Default whitepoint is too cool (measured 7200K) and none of the provided modes gave an actual 6500K. Luckily, with a colorimeter in-hand (Colormunki Display) I was able to calibrate to 6500K with minimal loss of contrast by adjusting the RGB gains.

- IPS glow isn't any better than my old IPS monitors. You'd think now that tablet and laptop IPS panels have mostly resolved this issue that monitor panels would do that too.

Other notes

- Contrast measured at 870:1, a bit low for an IPS panel although I cannot subjectively notice a difference in black levels compared to my other IPS monitor that measured 1150:1. If you're looking for deeper blacks you're better off with a 3000:1 VA.

- Gamma mode 2 is the only gamma mode that was calibrated at the factory. Changing the gamma mode to modes 1,3, or 4 resulted in a white point of 6300K, contrast ratio increased to 1060:1, however the gamma tracking is really bad on these modes.

- Gamma mode 1 results in an approx gamma of 2.0, mode 3 is approx gamma 2.5, mode 4 is approx gamma 2.3, but again the gamma curves on these modes are messed up and do not conform closely to these numbers so I ended up using gamma mode 2 and just correcting the white point.

I had been using a very similar & nearly the same vintage Asus Tuff Gaming 32 inch VA monitor prior to purchasing this one. It was good but had limitations. I didn't realize just how many until using this truly gaming configured Strix XG32VC.

First and foremost, this monitor has virtually no light bleed compared to the Tuff version and even that monitor was very good... but the XG32VC blows it away. I kid you not, if there was ever a gaming monitor that mimicks OLED visuals, it is THIS one. Uniformity is spectacular, clarity is perfect at an approx. viewing distance of 24 inches and in 165 HZ mode, this monitor simply does not flicker or hiccup whatsoever. I run an all Amd machine and the Freesync Premium Pro works like a charm, no matter what I play.

Asus clearly states that if you go into 170 HZ overdrive, along with other visual enhancement modes, you are apt to see some flickering and such. And I can confirm that it indeed happens. So I simply left overdrive turned off and I couldn't be more happy. What the 170 HZ rating tells me is that this monitor has 'margin' to work with and as a result, it can lock 165 HZ all day and all night.

I also own a SpyderX Pro and needed it to dial in my Asus Tuff Gaming counterpart... but not on this display!
While using the Asus Display Widget software, which I highly recommend installing btw, you can jump between all the different modes and see for yourself. In sRGB mode this monitor looks phenomenal and because of that, I've skipped calibrating it. Between my User settings for gaming and sRGB for content creation, this monitor completely fufills my needs. I couldn't be more pleased. As a side note, this monitor ships with a calibration sheet from the factory.

The 1800R curvature is the most I can tolerate... but that is a subjective matter. This panel is NOT OLED but that amount of curvature along with the stunning depth of black it achieves, makes for a very immersive experience.

This monitor is only HDR400 but it is the best one I've ever seen. Personally, I rarely use HDR mode in Windows but I'm happy to know that when I do want to switch to it - this monitor won't disappoint.
HDR600 or better requires an IPS panel and I'm not a fan of IPS glow.

Another thing I've noticed on other gaming monitors is delay/lag coming out of hardware sleep/standby.
The Asus Tuff monitor I had been using for a few years would take a long time coming out of its own imposed 'power down' mode. The XG32VC doesn't have this problem.

My only real gripe with this monitor is the lagginess of the Asus Display Widget. Once it is fully initialized, changing settings are instaneous... but the first 8 to 10 seconds upon opening it fresh (like cold start) has a period of no response. Predictively and consistent. And I'm fine with that. But once it is open and minimized in the Win task bar, it super quick and reactive as it needs to be.

Previous to this monitor I had a Lenovo L24Q-20 for about two years which is a 23.8-Inch QHD display. I love the monitor and have had absolutely no issues with it. At the time I purchased it, I was also considering a 27 inch monitor but ultimately thought it'd be too big for my desktop. Two years later I am now doing a lot of video and photography editing and the added screen real estate is more of a necessity than a luxury.

First off the minimalist bezel design is fantastic. There are absolutely no logos, buttons or any other distractions on the display itself. As a slight con the buttons controls are a bit awkward to use, being behind the display, but the monitor gives you visual indicators which buttons to press to access menu functions.

I use this display in my home office that gets a lot of natural light glare, and the matte finish of the monitor is very easy on my eyes. The high refresh 75Hz rate is very noticeable to me for less flicker particularly when I am editing 4K video. Text is crisp, color rendition is excellent right out of the box without any adjustment (default brightness is a bit high).

I don't game on my PC much but I have played a bit of Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 and it looks fantastic on this monitor.

The monitor offers multiple connections via DisplayPort or HDMI. Is packed very well in its shipping box. The design of the base is extremely sturdy. Even when I am mashing away at my keyboard the display doesn't shake or wobble at all. Unfortunately a minor nitpick is although the cool minimalist industrial design looks nice, there isn't very much tilt angle. It doesn't bother me because I use my display straight on, but if you are looking for something with a lot of angle adjustment this might not be the display for you.

I would say the only feature this monitor is lacking that it could use would be HDR. But the image quality is excellent so it is not a deal breaker.

This is the first LG product that I ever bought and I am quite impressed by the overall value it offers. For a selling price of $219 at COSTCO, this is a lower tier monitor because some of the high end ones sell at more than twice this amount. Despite the price tag, it delivers almost everything good models offer and more.

FUNCTIONALITY/ERGONOMICS:
I needed a monitor that could accommodate two windows at a very good resolution at the same time. I had a Dell 24 inch monitor, but it was not ideally suited for the job. Larger screen sizes come with height as well as width because of the traditional 16:9 aspect ratio. If you work long hours in front of a screen (which most people do these days) and are viewing two documents, it can give you neck/spondylitis issues if the monitor is at or higher than your eye level. Moving the neck up and down to view the entire document is not the best way to go about it. Ideally you should be looking 15 degrees or so below your eye level. The wider screen with 21:9 aspect ration layout lets you achieve that. You can open two windows at the same time that are easy to see and work with. Each window is 1280x1080 resolution- the highest you can get in this size. The screen only tilts to give you the best viewing angle. It does not move up and down. You will have to adjust your chair height if that is an issue. I found no need for this adjustment as long as your table height is correct for the chair. The monitor is only few inches above the table creating the perfect height level for me.

PICTURE/SOUND QUALITY:
This has a LED/IPS screen. Being LED, it is very thin and being IPS it has much better color accuracy and quality, as well as much wider viewing angle. Whether you can look down at it or across, there is no distortion in color or display. The screen is edge to edge with a very thin bezel at the bottom. You need to be EXTRA careful when opening the box and taking the screen out as you can damage the LED panel with the pressure from your finger or thumb. I have not compared this to other monitors in this class, but the overall picture quality is quite good. If you are using it for graphics design, this may not be for you. It also has built-in stereo speakers. The speaker quality is good, not great. If you want some real audio kick, you will need external speakers. It has outlets for both external speakers and headphones.

CONNECTIVITY:
It has plenty of input modes- HDMI (two) for connecting two laptops at the same time. It also has DVI-D and DP inputs with audio input for complete viewing and listening experience. No VGA, but that is not needed these days anyway. VGA output devices may not support this resolution anyway.

Overall, a great monitor for those who have a need to display two application windows at the same time. Single screen users may initially find this unnecessary waste of real estate and some money, but may end up finding this format more useful. After using this monitor for just few days, I like the idea of being able to see my desktop items while working on an application window. Highly recommend this product for the layout and format.

Since I am color blind, I am going to focus on the touch aspect of this monitor rather than the display. The monitor does seem nice and bright and sharp enough for my taste.

With Windows 8, the touch interface of this monitor doesn't perform as well as an all-in-one or a laptop that has built in touch function. There is a slight delay from when you touch the screen until it registers. Mostly, you don't notice it except that it makes it fairly difficult to activate the charm bar. You have to be more exact than you do on the all-in-ones or a laptop/tablet. It also affects the top swipe and the left side swipe in the same way. I believe that because of the slight delay for the screen to register the touch, your finger often moves past the bezel edge into the interior of the screen before registering the touchand doesn't bring up the charm bar. Also, if you play games like Wordament on it where it needs to register a quick swipe, it does miss some of the time. I tried 2 different monitors of this model and used both on 2 different PCs and saw the same behavior. I sent a video of the behavior to Acer support, and they confirmed that it was working properly. I hope that they can improve the performance of the monitor with some driver updates in the future.

Overall, the touch isn't terrible, but I bought the screen for my mother to use and it frustrated her too much to keep. Frankly, it should work better for the $500+ that you pay for it. It is a shame, because it has a lot of good features like a USB 3.0 hub built into it along with a nice selection on inputs. It even has speakers and can play audio through the HDMI connection, though they sound pretty bad.

I ended up buying an LG Touch-10 ET83. The touch is much more responsive and the charm bar displays easily. It still might not be as snappy as the all-in-ones, but the difference is negligible in my opinion.
