At this point I have only had this monitor for a day now, however I think I have a firm enough grasp to offer a good review of its capabilities.

If you are buying this monitor, chances are you are buying this for one main reason, gaming. As a gaming monitor, it is fantastic. There is one main reason why this monitor excels for gaming, 144Hz refresh rate. It is hard to put into words exactly how smooth motion is on this monitor and how fast it responds. There is practically zero input lag, and once you have played your video games at super high framerates on super high refresh rate monitors, there is no going back.

First person shooters is where this monitor really shines. When twitch gameplay and quick reflexes can make the difference, you really need a monitor that can keep up. I played a lot of BF4 today and really felt more connected to the game than before and just felt like I had that extra edge that really made my gameplay that much better.

A note to those of you that have heard our eyes can not see more than 30 fps, or 60 fps, or whatever you have heard, it simply is not true. Even though movies are shot at 24fps, they have motion blur to help with perceived motion. The fact is, you can ABSOLUTELY tell a difference between a regular old 60Hz monitor and this one.

Now, some areas where this monitor does not shine:
- Colors are really not great, especially out of the box, in fact they are quite bad. However, search around online for an ICC profile for this monitor and you can get them to improve dramatically. And while games do not necessarily take advantage of the ICC profiles, games do still look good. However, if you plan to do any photo editing or any sort of graphics work where colors are important, move along. Compared with my Asus PA248Q (which is made for photo editing) this monitor is not very good. Even when calibrated, the colors are not great, and the screen is not super uniform (The upper left portion of my screen is redder than the rest). This is also a TN panel, so viewing angles are not great, and colors tend to shift a bit as you move.
- Watching movies. Again because the colors are not great, watching movies or tv shows on this monitor is just ok. However, due to the super high refresh rate there is very little to no motion blur and practically no ghosting which is definitely a plus.

If gaming is not your primary purpose with this monitor, I do not think you should buy this monitor. If you primarily need a monitor for photo or multimedia editing, there are better options with much better color for around the same price. If you are planning to use this monitor primarily for surfing the web or doing simple office type tasks, there are much cheaper monitors you can buy.

However, for the price and what you get for a gaming monitor, this is simply great.

I should also add, that if it is something you are interested in, this monitor has support for NVIDIA 3D technology. I have not and will not personally use it, but if it is something you are interested in, then this monitor will fit the bill for that too.

Finally, I should talk a little about the stand. I find one of the items that separates really cheap monitors from more expensive monitors is the stand. To be honest, I find the stand a little bit ugly with a big "3D" printed on it, an d the little red ring around the circular base. However, the base does rotate smoothly and the stand offers up and down and angle adjustments as well as the option to turn the monitor vertical, something you do not often find on cheaper monitors.

When I first bought it and used it for the first time, I thought it was awesome since I could see how fluid 144hz gaming was. As I continued to see it used in different situations, I noticed the ghosting and corona effects. The ghosting is only apparent during 144 FPS gaming and only apparent in certain environments with certain color combinations. It's not apparent during other activities such as watching <60FPS videos. If you only play dark, grim, or achromatic games (e.g. Warframe, PUBG) that have minimal camera movement, then maybe this should be fine. If you play colorful and bright games (e.g. FFXIV) that have lots of 144 FPS cutscenes and lots of dynamic camera panning, then I don't recommend this monitor. Although the ghosting isn't visible for every object/background color combination, given the right conditions, it can make things look horrible.

I have a 60hz IPS monitor by Acer that had a pretty bad ghosting problem out of the box as well. However, with that monitor, the issue was due to a setting called "overdrive" which was set to extreme. Disabling it removed the ghosting. Apparently early versions of this monitor originally allowed you to disable overdrive to fix the ghosting, but Acer has removed the option for some unknown reason.

The only thing that kept me from returning this was the lightboost/strobelight hack. There was some guide that shows you how to trick Windows/Nvidia into thinking the monitor supports more features by changing some resolution parameters to some secret values. The hack essentially forces the monitor into a 120hz monitor and effectively removes noticeable ghosting in games (perhaps a 50% improvement over the default mode). The major downsides are that the picture quality diminishes (faint scanlines) and the color hues are screwed when its enabled. I've found that no amount of color calibration and RGB tweaking will prevent you from misinterpreting colors. In game, however, it looks good. However, if color accuracy is important to you, it's unacceptable.

It's been a good 6 months, but now I'm about ready to retire this monitor and replace it with a different 144hz monitor. Tired of the color/quality issues with the lightboost hack enabled and tired of the ghosting when its disabled. It's hard for me to recommend this monitor. Hopefully they fixed the issues described in some of the low ratings.

After some more research, it seems that monitor enthusiasts agree that this is the worst 144hz on the market. Apparently it's recommended to go with any 144hz that has either G-Sync or FreeSync (even if you won't use them) since those are more modern. It seems Acer has newer models offering 144hz and FreeSync for around the same price as this one, so I suggest looking into one of those instead.

For the attached pictures, the blurry image is 144hz and the clear image is 120hz with lightboost (my phone can only see up to 30FPS).
Good substitution of TV for PS4 players.

I've read numerous reviews on how to get it to work and there are claims that it does and others that it does not. I'm writing this to clear things up since I happen to have this display and is now using a mac and want to get this working. I have Mac with Mojave running, currently the latest version, 10.14.5. If you just want to know how to get it to work, skip to the "How to get it to work" section.

Power Issue and Display driver update
The main issue seems to be the lack of enough power from the Mac going to the display. There are reviews that says you have to update the driver from the DisplayLink website which you wouldn't have to do either. If you try, currently at the time of this review, it will tell you the driver on the mac is newer than on the DisplayLink website. So one less thing to worry about.

Mac adaptor will not work.
As the image shows, the mac adaptor does not work. My guess is it doesn't run the power to the accessories and only to the mac. The third parties ones do seem to all work (see images). I tried both a Totu and HooToo adaptor and both worked. Not sure if all will work but just FYI. To test if your adaptor works, if you plug the power and display to the adaptor, it should boot up, showing the ASUS logo (see image). If it does not do that, it will not work.

How to get it to work
First, make sure you are using a third party adaptor that also accepts power. Plug the display and the power to the adaptor and make sure the display boots up. Once you see that, you can then plug the adaptor to the mac port and the mac laptop will recognize the display. If you plug the adaptor in the mac port first, then the display, it will not recognize it.

This is just okay. It works well, it's well made. The real problem with it is that it's got all the limitations of a USB driven monitor. The one that bugs me the most is the loose/mushy mouse responsiveness and accuracy. DisplayPort has not figured this out, and perhaps they cannot. (DP is the software that allows Windows computers to work with this type of monitor.) I got this to function not as a temporary, traveling second monitory but to use as a regular second monitor. In particular, I wanted what this panel is: a high def, 15 inch sized, monitor. That is to match the size, aspect ratio, and resolution of the one built into my Thinkpad T560. It's pretty jarring in windows 10 to have monitors that do not match, and in this regard, this is exactly what I want. In fact, I mounted it (with velcro strips) to a monitor stand (and old Dell, where I took off the actual monitor itself which leaves a nice sized, square, reasonably flat surfce to do something like this).

Another limitation of USB monitors with windows if that you have no control over things like the gamma setting. If you use a windows computer and do not adjust gamma, let me make your day and computer better. Go to the Cortana search thing and type in "calibrate color display." Click through the settings. The only one that matters to me, and makes virtually any windows monitor on any computer I've seen look dramatically better, is the gamma setting. And it's the first setting that the routine walks you through. Just get to the gamma setting and pull that slider about 3/4 of the way down (so it's around the 25% position). Don't even bother doing anything else. I bet your monitor will looks amazingly better. I have many computers and I own a company with many more. The difference in how they look doing this is stunning. I just made an employee so happy yesterday showing her how to do this. It immediately makes most any LCD panel look less washed out, warmer, and seems to increase the overall contrast in a way not remotely possible with the contrast control. Why did I bother explaining all that? Because this is something you cannot do through Windows on an external display that is USB driven. Just not possible. And I don't think it's ever going to be.

So, overall, this ASUS monitor is really great. It's just that it has all the limitations of a USB monitor. I think there would be a huge market for a high quality, 15 inch LCD display that was driven by HDMI so that one would have all the adjustments that can be made to other monitors. With this type of model here, you can only change the brightness.

ASUS team, if you read this, consider making a version of this that can be driven by HDMI. Or, at least, add the ability to adjust such settings in a menu built into the monitor itself. Other monitors have both: the ability to adjust such things by the menu built in or through Windows. This device has neither. Having that ability built in to the monitor would at least solve the display issues (would do nothing, of course, for the mouse issues, but DisplayPort may eventually get that nailed.)

I was looking for a monitor for everyday use like gaming, coding, and internet with my Macbook Pro. Another major feature I was looking for was low power, as I'll be living for some time in a rural area powered by solar panels.

To be 100% honest, this is not a perfect monitor. After all it has an e-IPS panel, which is better than TN, but not as good a "real" IPS. Of course you can design and do video/photography work with it, but it's simply not appropriate for critical color accuracy.

That said, this is a wonderful monitor considering it's price and low power, probably the best monitor in it's range. It can even compete with monitors in the 600-700$ range. After manual calibration it has good color (although you can't control gamma curves), and after using a calibration device it gets even better. Honestly I don't think most people would notice the difference with a 3000$ monitor after calibration. Sure, nazi color freaks like me will notice that difference, but only monitors above (and beyond) 1500$ can possibly satisfy our uber exquisite taste.

As for the low power, I don't think it gets any better than this. There's even an indicator of how much energy it is using in the OSD menu.

All in all I'm very happy with it and I would recommend it to almost anyone, except pros in need of a more precise instrument. If you are indeed looking for a pro monitor at this price range there must be something wrong with your value scale. You wouldn't do pro photography work with a 300$ camera, would you? Same with your monitor.

Summary:

*) Now that I have the monitor adjusted, it is very nice. The text is sharp and the color
is quite good. It did take some effort to get it that way.

*) Very bright -- I'm still experimenting with what works best for me. I turn brightness
down to somewhere between 40% and 67%.

*) I could do without the wide shiny black bezel. It may look good in marketing stuff,
but it distracts from what is being shown on the screen, and its bulk is annoying in
my dual-head setup. I'd rather have a narrow flat black bezel.

*) Good overall quality -- no stuck or dead pixels, no edge bleed

Initially my monitor was blurry -- especially noticeable in text.
Samsung support was great -- they get 5/5 stars).
They spent about an hour on the phone with me trying to solve the problem, and
then emailed me a pre-paid UPS shipping label to return for service. Service found no defect.
I still saw same problem, so after some more troubleshooting, they were set up to exchange
the monitor.

Before the exchange could happen, I finally found the two problems myself,
and now everything is clear and sharp.

1) In the OSD, under Setup, if you scroll down there is a setting for PC/AV. Mine was set to AV.
It really must be set to PC if you are using the monitor with a PC.

2) My lighted area did not fill the glass. The problem turned out to be that my ATI video card was
set to 5 notches of underscan. Clearing that made the lighted area fill the glass, and also fixed
the remaining artifacts. It is important to neither overscan nor underscan -- otherwise there
is scaling and the software pixels are not 1:1 with the hardware pixels. The result is visual
artifacts, such as off-color and blurring.

I did have to take the time to adjust the colors. I used the program they supply, Natural Color Pro.
The result looked too washed out to me, so I turned on Magic Color. That made the colors pretty good.
Then I went back to Natural Color Pro and adjusted colors a little more. Colors now look good
(to my amateur eye) except that they seem just a little washed out in a few cases.
Based on my experience so far, I'll eventually get that adjusted better, too.

I notice that the color changes on text as it is moved -- either scrolled, or the window dragged. Samsung
support confirmed this is normal. Not really a problem to me, but makes me wonder whether moving
things like games will see anything like that -- if so, I would think it might be a problem.

Comments on some of the other reviews here:

> the old HP had better resolution and just looked better than the ... Samsung
Resolution is known (1920x1080), so I am guessing the author meant sharpness. My photos were
a little fuzzy, too, until I fixed the settings as described above. No longer a problem, once I did that.

> The colors seem ever so slightly off, particularly on the bottom.
I saw some of this, but in my case it was fixable. Some color adjustment helped. The other thing that helped was tilting the monitor to be more perpendicular to my line of sight.

That said, colors are off at the edges of the monitor, especially left and right -- just normal TFT effects.
If you are in the center of the monitor, then there is enough viewing angle on this big a monitor to see TFT
effects at the sides. Move your head so it is directly over the side of the monitor and the off-color goes away.

> there is a little fussing necessary to get it to work well
Definitely true in my experience -- for me, more than "a little"

> it thought my computer was a DVD player
I would expect that would be fixed by the PC/AV setting I mentioned above

> I hate the finger sensing buttons. It's extremely unpredictable.
I guess YMMV applies here -- the menu system, including the buttons, works fine for me.

> this monitor cycles through every input when you turn it on until it finds the right one
Mine does not. It does bring up the symbol for what I am using (HDMI), I suppose
to remind you which you are using in case you have more than one hooked up. No
hunting/cycling, though.

> You don't have to pay for the defective one to be sent back to you and they send you a replacement right away.
Samsung support also does it this way when needed. I was about to do just that when I discovered the
correct settings (as noted above).

Newcomer to 144hz monitors. This monitor is one of the best investments I've ever made. It takes a bit of time to get the color calibration down to my liking, but it's a trade im willing to take by how well this monitor performs. After fiddling with Nvidia's color calibration settings and monitor screen settings the colors dont look bad at all. There are some reviews on here saying this doesn't support 144hz but it does. First you NEED a display port for Freesyn/Gsync compatibility and you have to manually activate it in your Windows 10 Display Adapter properties and select 144hz, press the Input button below the Monitor and select "Display Port", exit then press the enter button on the Monitor and select "Set Up", then go to where it says "Freesync" and turn that On. If you're interested in my color set up press Enter on the Monitor and go to Picture Adjust, turn your contrast to 76 then go to Eco Mode and select Eco Mode 1, press Exit then go into Color Adjust settings, then select User Color and your Red and Green to 98, press exit then go to Image Adjust and set the Sharpness to 1, then set your Nvidia Color Settings as shown in the Picture i uploaded with this review and youre good to go! I have not had a single tear while i was gaming all day today. Its ef'n unreal. The only Con that i have is that it does not come with a Display Port. I have been looking for an external monitor since going into work from home mode and this monitor is pretty amazing. I have also included a picture on how you can use the monitor cover that is included as a stand. I have read all the reviews so far and is a very common question.

Here is my detailed review:

Pros:
+ Super easy to use, literally plug and play
+ Thin, lightweight, and portable
+ 4K picture quality
+ High quality feel and packaged very nicely
+ Comes with pretty much all the required cables: 2 usb c cables and a hdmi
+ Comes with a cover / stand! You normally have to buy this separately

Areas for improvement:
- Longer power cord would be nice but you can use any longer usb c cable to meet your needs
- On some devices, you cannot turn up brightness past 70 unless directly plugged into an outlet or stronger power source
- Sound system could use a boost
- Ability to go into portrait mode
- Allow for touch screen capability
- Having an external battery so you don't need a power source

None of these area of improvements detract from it being a 5 star product. These are merely suggestions for an even better product (which I assume would also come with a different price).

Bottom line: I highly recommend this product. This is really a great portable monitor and you will not regret getting. It is high quality, versatile, and so easy to use.

If you found my review helpful, please let me know!

This is my first 144hz curved screen gaming monitor. The main reasons that I purchased it were price, thin bezel, ADJUSTABLE height, curved screen, AOC quality, and 144hz panel. Be aware this monitor's screen is a VA panel. I found this monitor to be challenging to set up in order to make the screen look similarly as bright and colorful as an ISP panel. I spent a lot of time working with the NVIDA control panel to get the screen to look acceptable but I wasn't thrilled with it. I was about to settle for my adjustments when I saw another Amazon reviewer mention changing the GAMMA setting in the AOC monitor's settings to 3 and the panel color from warm to NORMAL. These 2 changes made a noticeable difference in picture color and brightness. Now I'm satisfied with the picture and it makes my games look great. The monitor housing and base feel very sturdy look very nice with red accents. The monitor's 144hz refresh rate make games looks sharp. The effect of the curved screen is not nearly as noticeable or immersive as I had hoped. It sort of stays out of the way while gaming but maybe that's what it's supposed to do. Overall, for the price this is a very nice Freesync equipped VA panel monitor. If you want to save some $$$ over a similar quality IPS monitor this is your monitor just be prepared to spend some time setting it up to enjoy its best picture. Also, the monitor's adjustable height feature was an important selling point for me.

I'm back to add a star to my review. After switching out my HDMI cable for a DP cable the picture and color are awesome. Also with the latest NVIDIA drivers I can use G-sync on this monitor. Win-win!

Went from a two 27" monitor setup to this was a great idea. Had an LG and an ASUS ProArt monitor and both were good but I wasn't really utilizing the two monitor setup so I decided to simplify and upgrade and glad I did! This is a really solid monitor for gaming and just about anything else IMHO.

I love the aesthetics of this monitor. Definitely one of the best looking 'white' monitors on the market rn. Right out of the box, it already held beautiful colors and brightness that I didn't really need to calibrate it. Bear in mind if you are going to use OD (overdrive: keeps moving objects sharp) option for this panel keep it between 2 and 3 especially if you are thinking of overclocking it at 170hz. This'll keep a minimal ghosting effect during game play. I never knew I needed G-Sync before but now I know that it is a game changer. Also, if you are thinking of getting a monitor with HDR... this is not it. Look for panels that have HDR10+, HDR1000, Dolby Vision, and etc for your High Dynamic Range media consumption. HDR400 is just a bear minimum excuse for HDR. HDR600 is ok but usable. So, the overall physical aesthetic (in my opinion) of this display is pleasing and beautiful! Picture quality is amazing but not out of this world like most screens I've used and/or seen. The HDR option looks muddy and you should just not turn that option on. So, if you're someone who loves Gundam (like me) and loves white monitors (like me) and just wants a better monitor that you previously had then look no further. This monitor is for you. But if you are someone who really cares about the best picture with HDR and you hate bottom lips on your display. You also want the best pixel count on your screen. The best crisp picture a monitor can provide then skip this monitor. This isn't for you. Happy gaming friends!

I am in the market for a new monitor for my home office and for some casual gaming. Everyone knows how much people swoon over refresh rate and this monitor is an excellent deal for a 27" curved screen with a 75Hz refresh rate. Some people are saying it's 60Hz, and I can confirm without a doubt that it is indeed 75Hz. I've looked at higher 144Hz monitors and even flat ones are considerably more and are typically only 24" flat screens for $250-300. So, for your dollar it's really hard to argue with this screen.

The screen quality isn't like "WOW", but the picture is very nice, although slightly washed out. It's nothing too bad and unless you're a real stickler I don't see anyone complaining about the image. It looks great while in games and I've not even tried to play with any of the settings yet as I'm sure the image can be improved from the factory settings.

The problems I had were that the model # on the box differs from the one on the website. I informed Amazon of this and they said that it is indeed the correct monitor. However, keep in mind the sticker on the back of it will read "C275W-1920R" not "C278W-1920R".

The other small problem I'm having is as I type the monitor shakes from the vibration and movement of my arms on my desk as I shift my hands around the keyboard or accidentally bump my desk. This issue is the feet on the underside of the base are not all even. The front footpad is shorter than the rest and it allows the screen to rock forward and back. It's a very simple issue, but still something very annoying that shouldn't be happening brand new out of the box.

I am debating on sending it back or seeing if Sceptre will send me out some new foot pads to mount on it. I think I may try out the 32" monitor, but I'm a little concerned that 1080p won't have enough pixel density on the 32" monitor. I'm also not sure if the 32" is 75Hz, so I'll have to look into that.

So yeah, I'm a gamer. It's why I bought this monitor; the high refresh rate. It's a crucial thing whenever you are online gaming on PC. Gameplay must be as smooth as possible when someone is trying to snipe you in Warzone.

But it is for sure a great monitor that produces a quality image. Some people have mentioned backlight bleed. I have had this monitor for over a year now and I haven't experienced that in the slightest. Sure, maybe I hit the panel lottery, but I doubt it.

The speakers on the monitor are not great, but hey, I can't fault Asus too hard for including them. They are at least handy in a pinch.

I paid $500 for this monitor, which is not exactly cheap. But you get what you pay for. This monitor is built like a tank and produces a high quality image perfect for gaming.

The only negative thing is that there is an option in the menu to put a red dot crosshair in the center of the screen. Once it is turned on and the realization sets in that you don't actually want this (who would?), it can be hard to figure out where in the menu deactivates it. You'd think it would be as simple as selecting the same option again, but no. Just imagine that, a single red pixel on your screen, driving you nuts. It's not just me, trust me, people have Googled it left and right. Never turn it on and you're golden.

That being said, that's an extremely minor con because its something that shouldn't be a recurring issue. Overall, this is a quality gaming monitor worth buying, if you can find it in stock.

If you're in need of a high refresh rate, 4K gaming monitor, consider this fine gem. After using it as my daily driver for almost a month as of this review, here are some of the goods and bads that I've experienced with this monitor

The Good
- Perfect size-to-resolution ratio, giving you a nice and sharp image at 4K 144Hz
- Has good color accuracy out of the box
- Very good brightness
- It's an IPS monitor, what more could you ask
- Absolutely no ghosting

The Bad
- You have to enable a setting within the monitor to get max brightness
- The screen turns off for a few seconds when you alt+tab out of a full screen application, but can be fixed
- The USB ports on the monitor have to be activated within the software on the monitor
- Has a rather large power brick compared to the other monitors I own

So while there are some kinks that come with the monitor, it doesn't avoid the fact that for about $600, you can get yourself a 4K monitor at a high refresh rate, which some other monitor might cost $800 and up.

I've had this for a week.

Pros:
Construction feels great. If you use the stand, the feet are metal and the stand is covered in a black shroud that is plastic but appears metal. The frame is metal (assuming aluminum). No glue spots on the edge of the screen.
Vesa mountable if you don't use the stand. The stand is highly adjustable and way better than most in the box stands I've used.
Bezels are almost not there. The sides have about 1 1/2 mm bezel and top has 1mm. About a 1cm bezel along the bottom, but expected as that's where the power switch is.
I dont have any dead or unresponsive pixels after running a pixel test. The picture is fantastic. Great colors out of the box but could use a slight more tweaking.
Gysnc was already enabled in the Nvidia control panel, and so far has played nice with any game I throw at it.
This is my first curved monitor, but I enjoy the curve quite a bit. It's adds a bit of immersive feeling to the games.
I sit. 3" from the screen and I can't see pixels or lines. Looks great from far away as well.

Cons
Typical VA panel cons. Black smearing and slight in low light scenarios. Bad viewing angels (a bit wider angles than I've experienced with other VA monitors). High pace/ fps games, stick with IPS or TN.
RGB. Rgb isn't a con for me, but the placement is. You won't see it at all no matter where you place the monitor, which defeats the entire purpose.

Other things to know
I can run AAA games at max settings with this monitor. I use a 2060s, and it can get taxing on my card at times. If you plan to run AAA games, at a minimum I'd have a 5600xt/2060s or to be safe I'd have a 2070s/5700xt.
Joystick feels great but if you want to use the appon screen app, make sure you have the USB connection plugged in as well as your display plug of choice. This was purchased at $399. Anything above $430, I think you'll find better options.

My son has been wanting a gaming monitor for higher refresh rates in his games for years now. Obviously we were excited to try this one out, and I have to tell you... I'm impressed.
I usually stick with name brands for my computer monitors, and for the retail price this is at, I wasn't expecting a whole lot. But the image quality is pretty sweet, the resolution at 2560 x 1440 is noticeably better than his old 1920x1080 one, and the 144hz refresh rate ain't bad either (a whooping jump over the 60hz his old one maxed out at, which is really what he wanted).
BUT WAIT, THERE'S MORE!

1) The monitor has SPEAKERS. He connected with the included display port cable and realized that a new audio device showed up on his desktop. I told him yeah, because DP and HDMI can carry audio so there's a 3.5mm jack on the back to plug in speakers. My LG computer monitors do that too, but they can't actually play sound. There's just a jack on the back to plug in speakers / headphones, and it just makes it easier to bring the plug closer than my desktop. But when he tried it, lo and behold, sound came out of the monitor like a TV! Holy cow, this could double as a media center for a fire stick or something because it has integrated sound! My son usually uses headphones for games and didn't like the look of speakers on his desk, so he's never had a way to play something out loud for another person to hear before. This is a VERY cool option to have!
2) There's an RGB strip on the rear of the monitor so it looks cool from behind! (see included photo).
3) There's a funky little projector circle that displays an image of a tiger head (seems to be the branding logo) on the wall behind the monitor. See included photo of wall to illustrate.

My son loved that projector thing and actually tried to find the logo online so he can make it his desktop background, but sadly the monitor doesn't seem to have any branding we can look up to find that logo, which is a shame. Otherwise, what an awesome product! More than I expected, so far we are very pleased with it!
fast delivery.  good clear picture.
Very nice bezels

The Good (Compared to my previous monitor, a TN monitor, the Asus VH236H):
- Above average viewing angles. Just slightly, a good IPS is better.
- Reduced color grading. Colors shift slightly depending on the position of your head, on a TN it's obvious if you look at a single image or color, on this monitor it's basically unnoticeable unless you get out of your chair and move up and down a lot.
- The out-of-the-box color settings are pretty good. There is enough saturation to avoid the washed out look that some bad TN monitors have.
- Above average contrast, on par with a good IPS. On most monitors blacks/dark areas aren't really dark, they're kind of grey, like a truly dark image that has been brightened. With this monitor there is a greater range between light and dark, which is most noticeable during dark scenes where details are preserved that other monitors would crush or brighten. This is the most impressive thing about this monitor in my opinion, it looks significantly better than your average TN or cheap IPS.
- There are no splotches, bright spots, bleed or moving bands that can often occur in cheap LED (edge-lit) monitors, especially cheap IPS.
- The anti-eye-strain features like low blue light and non-flicker seem to work, though it's not an obvious thing, to me anyways.
- The stand is very simple to install and remove, without screws. It's not overly wobbly either, you would have to shake your desk quite a bit to cause problems.

The Bad (I don't know how many of these problems are because of VA):
- Ghosting. BAD GHOSTING (I'm no expert but I think this is also referred to as motion overshoot and "long" image retention). For most colors it's not that bad compared to a low-tier TN, but on blacks it's very bad, and the trails/distortion are very obvious. It's hard to explain for people not familiar with it but imagine a completely white web page, with a single black bar on it. When you scroll the webpage up and down, the black bar leaves a dark trail across the white space. Another scenario where it is very obvious is mouse movement. Most monitors ghost, but in most cases the trail vanishes fast enough for it to be ignored, with this monitor the ghosting is impossible to ignore on high-contrast scenes/pages. Setting the monitor to "game" mode, which on some monitors changes internal hardware timings to reduce such effects, does nothing. Changing the response time setting to "advanced" cuts black ghosting by half, but also converts the ghosting into a kind of static grey bar that is just as obvious, it also increases ghosting and distortion on other colors. The "fastest" mode does the same, increasing distortion effects across the board even more, moving your mouse looks like turning on the "trails" effect in Windows. You're best best is leaving it on standard.
- Lighting angles are bad at extreme angles horizontally. The lighting is better than most cheap IPS or TN monitors, but if you move your head from side to side (horizontally), lighting gets weaker at the opposite edge. IE, move head to the extreme right, the left edges of the monitor get darker. Thankfully it's not very noticeable, but on uniformly dark screens it's more obvious.
- Physical buttons and the OSD they control are crappy, but that's pretty much standard outside of the high-end.
- The stand is tilt-only, but that's also standard at this price range.

Conclusion:
For me, the ghosting really kills it. I will continue to do comparisons on my own but it's bad enough during web-browsing and "normal desktop use", for gaming anyone who is even slightly picky would probably find it unacceptable. This monitor is best suited for image and video viewing, where the contrast gives the most noticeable benefit over other monitors at the same price (though this might be reduced with high-FPS video). The price has to be taken into consideration, this is a pretty cheap monitor, among the cheapest VA monitors you can get. Is it better than a cheap, crappy TN? yes, unless you value low response times and image retention above all, which you might if you're a gamer. Is it better than a cheap, crappy IPS? definitely I would say, IPS has enough inherit problems that it's not worth buying unless you're getting something decent, in the $200+ range at-least. In the end though, I would say for this price a good TN is the best balance of speed, color, and contrast. I like my old Asus better overall. The problem is decent to good TNs are hard to find, especially if you're on a budget. So I would say seek out a good TN, or save up for something better, be it VA or IPS. I don't see this monitor keeping it's position as my main, it's just not good enough. I'm going to try a nice Dell IPS or a high-grade gaming TN from BenQ next.

I bought this as my main workstation display to be accompanied by my 144hz 1080p gaming panel. I do graphic, web, 3D modeling and software design so I needed something with accurate colors. Cons:
Unfortunately this model doesn't come with any mini HDMI or mini Display port adapters or cables for some reason. Which means until I go buy one, I can't push 4k at 60hz because my Gtx 1070 only has IO for mini display, mini HDMI, or dvi. This would be an awesome addition for any future models. Even the Dell 4k I previously ordered contain minidisplay port cables. Other than that, the onscreen controls are just okay. Not good but not really as bad as some people on here are saying.

TLDR: if you're serious about accurate color and features, this monitor is amazing.

BenQs website and several reviews say the monitor is comes with Displayport 1.4 connection, but other reviews say its only Displayport 1.2, which cant handle the data needed for HDR. I can turn HDR on in Windows 10, but the selection to stream in HDR is grayed out and cannot be turned on. Update 2-10-19:

This is one of the finest 24" 4K monitor. I bought it because it offers an insane PPI given the 23.6" display and 4K pizels. If you are new to 4K monitors, this is a great, entry level 4K. But if you are one of those who likes big screens, I would suggest considering a 27" monitor which may have a slightly. Reasons to buy this monitor:

1. Insane PPI: I use this monitor to both code and see some occasional video (youtube) and gaming. This is a perfect monitor for all three and the pixel density make every other screen outdated. If I work for an hour on this monitor and look at my ipad pro, it just looks too blurry.

2. Small footprint and amazing swivel stand: Its 24" and doesn't take a lot of desk real estate. The stand is exceptional and you can adjust and rotate is as you please.

3. You like small fonts: There are some folks who like to work with small fonts and I am one of them. Although, now both ubuntu and windows offer good scaling support for 4K and you can increase the font size to human range. The default 4K on any 24" will be small and some prefer it.

Reasons to not get this monitor:

1. The only negative I have is the size. Some may wan't 27" even though it has a lower PPI especially if you want to see movies and enjoys a big screen. Personally, I knew what size I was getting and this was perfect.

2. Fresync only works between 40-60 FPS. So you need a fair bit of horsepower to drive this machine. I built my machine with Titan-X and Haswell and they makes everything run butter smooth!

I've uploaded some images but I recommend looking at youtube for a better view at how this monitor looks!

Absolutely beautiful monitor. My previous monitor was an old Viewsonic from 2006 that wasn't even 1080p, and when that one died several months ago, I knew this was the exact monitor I was going to get to finish my brand new gaming PC setup. Saved up for a couple months, and almost everything about it is perfect. Absolutely zero screen tearing with G-Sync (which also gets rid of stuttering from V Sync), resolution and colors are amazing, and the refresh rate is blisteringly fast. The option to push to 165hz is neat, but ultimately seems a bit superfluous. Until I either get another 980ti or a 1080, pushing to that kind of FPS on max settings and resolution for the newest games is possible, but too taxing even for my system. Even then, the difference between 144 and 165 is barely noticeable if at all.

There is one dead pixel that I've found, and it is close to the top-center, so that is unfortunate. However, it is only noticeable on a completely still, black or pixel-testing background, and it seems that it goes away after the monitor has warmed up, so it doesn't bother me at all. Not going to risk sending it back and getting another one that may have similar or worse issues. I have yet to really test every little detail, but I haven't noticed any other issues (i.e. backlight bleed, ghosting, etc) in games or other uses either.

The integrated speakers are also not great, but I primarily use head phones, and the speakers are acceptable for watching Youtube videos and such. I would never want to use them while gaming.

I also want to note that in my case, the bracket that facilitates the side to side pivoting of the monitor was screwed on so tight that I could not turn the screen and it was stuck angled to one side. I had to buy square screwdriver bits just to fix it.

If you want a monitor that has higher resolution and framerates, better colors, low response time, and G Sync, there is none better. Until technology progresses to the point where 4K IPS monitors with refresh rates higher than 60 are possible, this is best monitor you can get I think.

Purchased this monitor to replace my 7th Dell monitor...

Pros
- Build quality is good, base and arm are heavy and feels solid
- 32" IPS panel has no dead/stuck pixels at all
- Panel frame is thinner then my old Dell, not as thin as other brands but good enough
- Included side SD card reader and USB ports are very convenient
- KVM switch function saves me space on my desk when I have my work laptop connected at the same time and not have to have a separate keyboard/mouse
- "Puck" is great, easy to get to OSD menu and make changes
- Ambient sensor "Eye Protect" is really neat, screen brightness changes depending on light in the room so less strain during extended periods of usage
- Multiple screen modes with it's own color settings
- For UHD resolution 32" panel seems to be the best size without any scaling or eye squinting

Cons
- For a screen this size BenQ should use a thicker/heftier box, current box is too thin and could easily be damaged during shipping
- Touch sensitive controls not very responsive at times, maybe that's why they came up with the "puck" concept
- Wished the "puck" buttons were lit so I could tell which button is what since the arm, puck and base are all the same color
- USB3 hub doesn't allow my Mac to go into Sleep mode, only way to enter Sleep mode is to unplug the USB cable (I would take 1/2 star off for this). Hopefully it's a OS software fix, monitor is fairly new still so maybe it doesn't have the necessary parameters.
- Usual backlight bleed for a plat panel, no better or worse compared to other brands. All flat panels have backlight bleed issues, it's just a matter of how much and what is your personal tolerance level.
- 16:10 aspect ratio like a 30" panel would have been nicer instead of 16:9???

For this price point I think it's a great value for your money, no major issues for me and I will probably start buying BenQ from now on instead of paying a premium for other brands from now onwards.

I am a big fan of plasma TVs. I own a P42G10 from 2009. I love it, I didn't realize how much better the newer plasmas were until I set up my 50ST60. The picture is amazing. I bought this specifically to use with M1 Macbook Air via USB C.

Initial impressions: Easy to assemble, only one screw to hold the base on. Stand on back of monitor has a clip. Adjustments on stand are smooth. Cord does not have a box attached to it. It is just a cord, thankfully. Comes with USB C and HDMI cable. Instructions are horrible, but everything is easy to figure out.

Attaching to Macbook was easy. Mac puts the screen at 1440p and looks good. Stuck my wireless mouse receiver in the back of the monitor, works just fine. USB A ports are dated, not sure why they would put them back there. Other reviews have noted that the screen is dim and to disable Eco and EyeSaver mode to fix this, on my monitor they were already disabled. Screen does not get really bright but will work fine for my purposes, if you have a lot of outdoor light shining in directly at the monitor you may get a lot of glare. Enabling HDR makes the monitor unusably dim.

This monitor is great. For this price (approximately $150 at the time I bought it) you absolutely cannot get a better monitor of this size and of this resolution.

When I first bought the monitor (before receiving the actual unit), the pixel density concerned me. The pixel density is only 91 pixels per inch! I was somewhat worried. But, after receiving the item, this is not an issue. As long as the screen is 14 inches or more away from your eyes, the pixels aren't really discernible.

The screen size may be far too large for some, but it is perfect for me. There is plenty of room to multi-task and do work, as well as plenty of room to watch movies and play games.

The response time isn't great for gaming, only 60hz, but is completely acceptable as long as you're not trying to be a professional.

On the back of the monitor, the light bleeds through the bezel and looks kind of bad. This isn't an issue for me personally, because my monitor is against a wall, but for some people this may be an issue. (Others have not complained about this to my knowledge, so this could very well just be an issue with my unit and not others.)

The bezels are extraordinarily thin for a monitor of this price. They're only about 3/4 of a centimeter. They look even smaller when the display is off.

The controls for the monitor are somewhat finicky and awkward, but they work. From unboxing, the colors are very blue. Changing the color setting to "normal" makes the colors very accurate, and would be acceptable for graphic design and other such work. With some work, you could improve the colors even more. The contrast is very good.

I really do wish that this was VESA compatible, but it's not really an actual issue for me.

Two or three of these would make for a great multi-monitor setup, if you had the room.

All in all, a very good monitor. If you're on the fence, buy this thing. It's the best bang for your buck out there. Just beware that you get what you pay for with the quality of the materials.

I've had the time to test it out. Now for the review. I bought this monitor for $329 after tax and 1 day shipping. Not a bad price for 1440p 144hz with FreeSync.
Out of the box it comes with an HDMI cable, the power box / cable, a USB hub cable to power the 4 USB ports (very useful for a cluttered setup such as for a streamer), the monitor stand (not using since I have a quad monitor stand), and all of the bolts necessary to assemble it. One added touch was a Phillips screwdriver and a remote. Some companies (Asus looking at you) expect you to have a screwdriver already. As far as shipping goes, this thing is sealed tightly with form fitting Styrofoam ensuring that your monitor does not get damaged during delivery.

The setup was simple enough. The monitor OSD menu comes with 2 languages. An Eastern language (not sure which Apologies for not knowing) and English. By default, it's set to the Eastern Language. To switch the monitor menu to English, go to menu, go down to the 4th option, and then on the first option in that menu, switch it to English. After that, the menu becomes easy to understand. You have all of the basic options as well as some Overdriver options. I cannot test FreeSync as I have a GeForce GTX 1060 6gb. Apologies.

The monitor also has a slim bezzel. This was beautiful for the monitor. All buttons are located on the back of the monitor which was nice as well. (added a picture of the monitor next to my others for comparison. Ignore the mess. Had just installed it).
As far as gameplay goes, seeing an improvement against FPS games and RTS games is obvious when going from 60 up to 144hz.

The one con I found with this monitor was the lack of a Display Port cable. Shipping a 144hz monitor with an HDMI that can max scale to 75hz is a bit of a let down. The cost difference between HDMI and DP is almost negligible and will save the buyer and seller some hassle of figuring out issues.
Other than that small nitpick, I can 100% say, my money was well spent, and the monitor is as it is described on the packaging. If anyone has any questions about it, feel free to ask and I will answer on my free time.

Edit - July 7 2022:

The monitor has been great for me! I use it for work (sw development) as well as for gaming (AC: Odyssey, Halo: Infinite, etc). I got this for around $630 during a Black Friday sale & it is very worth the money. Not sure about MSRP though.

It's been ~5 months now. Here are some pros and cons:

Pros:
+ Due to the aggressive curve, games seem more immersive and work is a lot easier. Prior to this, I had 2 monitors (2 x 27" QHD). When working, I would have to move my head around to properly read everything as the monitors were flat and text toward the edge of the screen was harder to read than what was in front of me. This issue was worse for the monitor on the side. While it wasn't that bad, upgrading to the 343CQR made everything easier--now I just need to move my eyes to see/read everything. For the SW engineers/devs who are worried about how the text may appear on the screen, I find it better than flat monitors by a long shot. Of course, this is personal preference.

+ The colors are amazing. My main monitor used to be the Nixeus EDG27S-V2-- a great monitor for the money and probably the best within the price range. However, despite having an IPS panel, the colors on the 343CQR pop out far more due to the better contrast ratio. I can go back to the Nixeus monitor for gaming if I have to, but I wouldn't be very happy.

+ The mouse dongle can also act as a headphone jack dongle; it can be placed on either side of the monitor. I bought a 6-inch aux extension cable to make using the headphone jack easier too and it just hangs off this dongle ready for any headphone/set I want to plug in.

+ PIP is nice. I don't use it very often but it's there.

+ Menu is easier to navigate than most monitors. I wouldn't say it's the best UI but it's nice.

+ No speakers!--a pro if you're like me and don't like speakers on monitors.

+ 165Hz refresh rate is very nice, though not that much more noticeable from 144Hz.

Cons:
- I had to turn off vsync and gsync because the monitor would sometimes black out for a second in the middle of a gaming session. Not sure why this occurs

- Ghosting is somewhat noticeable to me. It's not enough for me to regret purchasing this monitor though.

- If you plan on using a monitor arm for this monitor, it is fairly heavy (~20 lbs, + or - 2 lbs)

- Some features seem like a gimmick (the scope vision thing, headphone stand on the side is flimsy)

- I kind of wish I had gone for the G9 mostly because the additional screen space would be amazing. Far from a deal-breaker but if you're the type that prefers no compromises, go for the G9

- Probably not the best monitor for competitive gaming/FPS (if you're a hardcore gamer)

- The DP cable that comes with the monitor is NOT sufficient enough to fully utilize the monitor. You will want to find a third-party DP cable that is VESA certified for DP 1.4.

All in all, I do really love this monitor. I think it's a great deal at the price I bought it at ($630 + tax)!

First let me tell you what got me started looking for a second monitor for my 15" Macbook Pro. I have a 46" basic LCD TV in the family room. I often kept my macbook and computer desk in the family room and hooked into that TV with an HDMI cord. This didn't lead to great resolution, as HDMI doesn't look as good as DVI and DVI wasn't available on that TV. Also, it is only 720p. Still, Having that huge second monitor was great for watching videos, surfing the web, working on photos, etc. It was really inconvenient to have the computer desk in the middle of the family room. I wanted a bedroom solution that could work as a better second monitor, and also replace my 22" tube television.

I checked out all of the current top monitor/TV combos as well as just monitors. I wanted something between 22" and 30" screen, full HD 1080p, and good color quality for photo editing. I was a little nervous from some reviews on this last point. Because of my need for high picture quality and a low price point, I hesitated on getting a hybrid with a TV tuner. I don't use the bedroom TV often enough to sacrifice monitor quality. I thought maybe I would just replace the TV with a monitor and stream Netflix and video at night. But finally, I gave in and went for what seemed to be the leading product across the board: the Samsung P2770HD.

Now, onto this product. My biggest concerns were picture quality for the computer and TV quality without a cable box. I couldn't be happier about both. The picture is fantastic. With a DVI cord into my Macbook (mid-2010), I get a crystal clear 1080p second monitor. And it is HUGE! Much bigger than I expected. I have been editing photos, reading online, Doing everything on this monitor. Streaming HD video looks unbelievable. I also hooked an XBox 360 into it. This was the best I've seen my games look, and I have started to play with that again. I don't think it will make it back out to the family room. For the TV, I couldn't have been happier to find out that there are now digital channels in between the basic cable channels that this TV picks up in full HD just through a standard cable connection. The picture is flawless. I haven't watched TV in the family room since.

All in all, this product has made me a recluse to my bedroom. It is the full package. I have almost no quarrels. The only things that are a minor inconvenience (although not enough to dock a star) are that you can't tilt it up or down and if you view at a steep angle, the color starts to look a little washed. It isn't bad though, I could sit on the floor and watch happily, but to edit photos I would want to look straight on. Lucky for me, this monitor sits eye-level at the desk and laying in bed, so the pivot isn't really needed.

Go for it, buy this monitor. If you are using a Mac, get the DVI adaptor and a DVI cord. You will not regret it.

I've been using Dell flat panel monitors for quite a while now. My first was a Dell 2005FPW, which I now use as a secondary monitor. I have Dell 2209's and 2210's at work, and have previously used 2207's. Each monitor has been incrementally better than the previous, but these IPS LED panels are more than an incremental improvement. The backlighting is remarkably even for this price point, colors are accurate and consistent without being overly (and unrealistically) vibrant, and the U2312HM has all the connectors you expect from a monitor these days.

Being a very picky, almost videophile user, I found the on-screen controls to be a bit lacking, but far beyond my Acer 24" LED (my one and only foray into Acer monitors because it was terrible). I used the Lagom calibration website (http://www.lagom.nl/lcd-test/) and the on-screen controls to get the monitor as close as I could to perfectly calibrated, then ran through the site again with the NVidia software to dial the screen the rest of the way in to perfect. It was far less time-consuming than when I did the same with my Acer, and was slightly faster than doing it with my old Dell 2005FPW (thanks to more robust on-screen controls making the second pass much quicker).

Once dialed in, this monitor is really beautiful. As I mentioned before, this monitor replaced a truly terrible Acer 24" LED and the difference when playing games is tremendous. Darker games like Diablo 3 don't wash out as the LED backlight is even and not excessively bright. More colorful games like Super Hexagon really pop, adding new dimension to some of my older games (Assassin's Creed never looked this good on my old Dell or the Acer). Ghosting doesn't appear to be much of an issue with this monitor, though I'm coming from a truly terrible monitor that ghosted like crazy, so I may not be the best source for that.

As I also use this monitor as my primary monitor when telecommuting, text quality is also very important to me. I use Visual Studio and Eclipse on a daily basis, and have fairly bright text on a black background in both IDEs. I've found that this reduces eye strain for me, and switching from the Acer, which blew out the black to a medium gray color, has further reduced eye strain. Black text on a white background, like most websites will probably use until we pry white backgrounds from their cold, dead hands, is more jarring but still crystal clear on this monitor. I don't personally use the monitor in portrait orientation, but many of my coworkers use theirs in that way. So good news for them, as this monitor's stand is actually tall enough to allow for that! I do use the rotation on the stand to easily plug in cables though, so it has utility beyond simply keeping the monitor in portrait orientation.

My only observed flaws are pretty minor. The input selection is a little goofy, as the monitor doesn't have a dedicated input selection button like my old Dell 2005. Instead, source selection is opened when pressing either of the two lower buttons (directly above the power button) if the active source has no signal. If you have a signal on the active source, then you have to go through the main menu to the Input Source sub-menu and change it there. Seems like a lot of clicks to eliminate a single physical button. It's also stupid to me that the on-screen menu isn't consistent with what is opened when pressing buttons. All things considered though, it's a minor annoyance.

Another minor annoyance to me is that the power button doesn't respond to quick presses. Every monitor I've ever owned does, so I've spent the last 15 years developing a habit of quickly tapping the power button on each monitor and my desktop when I walk away from my desk for the night. Unfortunately, that has led to this monitor being left in Sleep Mode all night. Again, it's a minor annoyance, but I guess having this feature is good for some as it prevents someone from accidentally turning off their monitor in the middle of something important.

tl;dr: Great monitor with even backlighting, excellent colors, adequate calibration capabilities built in, and plenty of connection types, all at a bargain price for its size. Given the price difference, I don't see the need to get those extra 120 lines for the 24" version. A must buy for anyone looking for a quality monitor at the $200 price range.

The problems I've ran into already:

-1 dead pixel on the middle-right side of the screen. I purchased a 4-year warranty so until this gets worse, I won't send it back because it really is hardly noticeable, even in solid color or black/white backgrounds.

-Why did MSI release this monitor without any proper and optimized support for their on screen display app?

-The "easy button" on the bottom left of the monitor is useless as the on screen display app MSI has isn't even supported for this brand new monitor, so when you press the button to open the application to quickly modify monitor settings, nothing happens.

-No RGB adjustments found or available at this time.

-No real step by step instructions to set this monitor up.

-It also came with wall mount screws and no wall mount bracket, why?

-This is an IPS display, so backlight bleed is literally guaranteed in some extent when viewing bright displays in a dark room. Truthfully, the backlight bleed on this monitor is pretty minimal overall.. When turning the background to all black background the bleed is minimal to moderate at its worst, but when viewing colors in the display, videos, playing games in a dark environment--there is virtually no backlight bleed except for maybe in still-screen-load screens, etc.

I think I'm more annoyed that MSI released this monitor and forgot to finish a few odds and ends. Seems like they rushed it out the door for something to work decent with windows 11 / 12-gen intel processors.
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Scoring:

5/5 for gaming in this "gaming era" with the specific technology out- 240hz at 1440, or 240hz at 1080p downscaled. And you know the rest from the details provided by MSI. The monitor for gaming for the price is pretty amazing.

4/5 for picture quality- the picture is not perfect, therefore does not deserve a 5/5. It is great, but it could be better. The lack of support for the on-screen display app to customize settings easily also contributed to this score.

3/5 for having a dead pixel and some backlight bleed in certain viewing angles.

With all that being said, I would still highly recommend this 1440p monitor over others on the market for gaming. Despite the areas of concern above, it is that much better in areas where this monitor shines.

I bought this monitor after trying it out on my brothers gaming PC, and while I can’t tell you whether it would be good or bad for gaming, I can say it’s awesome for doing multitasking— specifically for college. I hook this monitor up to my MacBook with a usb-c to hdmi adapter that also has an extra power slot on it too.

It works very well for having multiple documents up for writing papers, discussion boards, notes, zoom, etc. I honestly have a hard time using my laptop for schoolwork without it now, as it’s just very difficult to navigate without the added space the monitor gives me.

There’s a review saying the monitor sits at a terrible downward angle, this person probably doesn’t understand that the screen can be tilted. When it first comes out of the box, it is packaged and automatically at a very sharp downward angle fresh out of the box. Do not panic, just tilt the screen upwards as it is meant to be adjustable.

Due to the nature of widescreens, any video platform will not fill the screen up. This monitor is meant to be used as a dual monitor, it’s not made for theatrics. Many games I play do fill out the screen and give you that extra viewing angle (like stardew valley or minecraft,) and it looks extremely clean and amazing.

Also just to note, I haven’t really bothered finding the driver that helps resize windows automatically. With a MacBook, you have to manually resize every window and you can’t clip the window to the size and have it automatically resize like a windows user can. I believe there is a free driver on the LG website, it just doesn’t bug me enough to find it.

This also means my Macbook has to be placed to the left of the monitor in order for me to use the MacBook screen as a “second monitor.” If it is not placed to the left, I still have to drag and windows from my MacBook as if the laptop was on my left side. Just a note to keep in mind.

I got bit by the 144hz gaming bug by the AOC C24G1. I had to return that monitor, and following far too much research, I ordered this one. After using it for a little over a week, here are my thoughts:

Pros:
A 27" 2560x1440@144hz monitor is just about perfect when paired with a GTX 1070 or better.

Adaptive Sync is rock-solid. Much has been made over Nixeus's implementation: 30-144 fps with LFC and adaptive overdrive (which appears to work just fine). It seems like this monitor should be on Nvidia's G-Sync Compatible list already.

Color accuracy and viewing angles are terrific, as you would expect from an 8-bit AHVA panel.

Despite the following cons, this monitor performs better in dark scenes than the C24G1 did. While the contrast of a VA panel was nice, the smearing and blurriness was definitely knock against the AOC monitor.

Cons:
The IPS glow on my specific monitor is quite noticeable in dark scenes, especially in the bottom-right corner. It's enough for me to take a star off the rating and to make me miss the VA panel in the C24G1 every now and then. I am compensating with brightness set to 25 (50 is the default, and 25 is plenty bright in a dimly-lit room), sitting further away than I am used to, sitting eye level with the middle of the display, tilting the monitor slightly up, and turning on a light or two. In the end, adjusting to the IPS glow might actually be good for my eyes!

I think the ghosting and blurring that I can perceive is consistent with other IPS panels. I already dinged this panel for one weakness specific to IPS panels, so I won't fault it for this.

When taking photos in a completely dark room to evaluate the IPS glow, I also noticed just the tiniest amount of backlight bleed that is completely invisible to the naked eye, even in a dark room. Since it only shows itself in pictures, I won't fault Nixeus for this; overall, I'm very pleased with the build quality.

The stand is subpar, but that's part of why this is a <= $400 monitor. It does what it needs to do for me: it holds the monitor up and allows for a bit of tilt.

Other thoughts:
Make sure to start fresh with your graphics drivers. I noticed some flickering and color banding issues that were (hopefully) resolved after using DDU and installing the latest drivers from NVIDIA.

The monitor controls aren't as bad as some have suggested. I am used to them after a week.

Nixeus isn't a big name, and this monitor looks unassuming and lacks the outward appearance of a gaming monitor. But unless you need your monitor to look the part, this is a solid option to consider.

This is a review of the 34GP83A, LG's new 34-inch ultrawide with Nano IPS panel.

The color is fantastic. I don’t mean the oversaturated wide gamut. I tend to use the AMD setting that restrains it to sRGB just for design and photography uses. I realize the wide gamut is nice when you need it, but a lot of content doesn’t translate well to it. Having the option is nice, and it will offer me the chance to work in the DCI-P3 colour space in the future if that ever becomes a standard. Though I’m not sure whether that will be a thing considering how long sRGB has remained the standard even as technology has vastly improved. I also find it odd how restrained the color space for digital displays and photography has been when compared to television and video, but that’s another subject for another time. As for the colors here, once in sRGB-ish mode through the AMD software, it looks very accurate. I’ve not calibrated it this early on, that requires an additional calibration tool. I’d wanted to find “the” monitor first. I’d say it’s very close to matching my fairly perfect old monitor. I call it perfect as far as the colors matching my print workflow with photography. It’s pretty old though, so take this with a grain of salt. Perception is subjective, of course. I like the color temperature as well. It seems very close to 6500K using Gamer 1 mode with Gamma mode 2 and the default color temperature setting as “custom.” This is only slightly different from warm, the two nearly imperceptible from one another, but I prefer the slightly more neutral custom setting it started out with.

Brightness is intense. So I currently have it set to 30 with contrast at the default 70. That might go down to 65-60. I’ll see how my eyes handle the intensity. I don’t have a use for HDR right now. It’s not really HDR on this model anyway, but it’s nice to know I could at least try it in the future if Windows and other tech ever gets up to speed. Most content out there doesn’t use it, so I’m not worried. The color space options and improved brightness of the backlight are positives regardless. True, I have the brightness turned down, but it’s always better to have more headroom than none at all.

Responsiveness is on par with the LG 27GL850. Everything feels smooth and fluid. That’s another reason I wanted to upgrade. 75Hz on my old IPS was killing me. It often felt like stop-motion watching videos with how slow the pixel transitions were. I noticed this all the more on the monitor I’ve been using from work during the pandemic, as we work from home part of the time. That is a newer IPS with okay colours but terrible everything else. Watching content on it has been almost painful. So the LG is a sight for sore eyes, so to speak. Everyone can laugh now.

I know it’s strange for someone to purchase a “gaming” monitor for design and photography, as well as just general use purposes. Yet the fluidity of movement, even with just scrolling, is more than worth the upgrade in refresh rate. The vibrancy of the display in general is beyond anything I’ve ever seen, short of the aforementioned 27-inch nano-IPS display. I suppose there’s no point in trying to compare it to OLED as that’s a pipe dream likely never to come to mainstream displays. LG’s 48-inch OLED TV is way too expensive with some drawbacks otherwise. I’d considered it, but I could afford a new PC and monitor for what that thing costs. With the limited 1-year warranty of LG products, it wasn’t worth it to me. So here’s the monitor that finally hits the sweet spot and ticks off most of the boxes on my wish list.

Now, there are the flaws worth mentioning, as is always the case. There’s far more backlight bleed than I noticed on the 27GL850. I ended up having issues with that model I couldn't live with, sadly. This is in all four corners and really bleeds into viewing of dark scenes, or just working with dark content itself. I hate how it washes things out. This is worse than the IPS glow, though tends to blend with it to be almost mingled, if that makes any sense. If I were to only use the 16:9 area of the center of the monitor, it would be almost perfect. Blacks are very good in that regard, IPS glow isn’t an issue, and there’s little if any backlight bleed. So be aware that the curved ultrawide nano-IPS panel still isn’t perfect when it comes to these things. It’s probably a step up from the 34GK950F, but there’s definite BLB and IPS glow to contend with. Playing the panel lottery in hopes of getting a good one might be what buyers have to do. Roll the dice and hope what you get is minimal in this regard.

My experience has not been without some hiccups. On the second day of use, my PC had a sudden crash. This never happens. It’s virtually not happened in the time I’ve owned it. I’ll sometimes go weeks without rebooting. I just don’t have crashes. So that was strange. I understand my video card is nowhere near as powerful as what is needed for this kind of monitor (RX 560). It could never handle trying to game on it, so that will be a consideration in the future. For normal desktop use, the card I have is more than sufficient with this display. I only notice the GPU usage at max around 30-40% with a varied workload, say a few browser windows, a YouTube video playing in one, Discord open, etc. Just basic stuff. That’s with videos playing at 1080p or 1440p, whatever is available. So it’s more than capable of managing 144Hz 3440x1440p with general PC use. It also has DP 1.4 and HDMI 2.0. So that’s not an issue.

I was a bit worried in the beginning. I wasn’t sure what caused the crash as I wasn’t using the computer at the time. I came back from the other room just in time to witness the idle PC crashing and restarting. I tried updating AMD’s drivers, which is not something I do often unless there’s a good reason. I think people know that once you get drivers that work for you, updating them just “because” can sometimes cause more problems than it’s worth. At least this is definitely the case with AMD. The updated drivers made no difference. Not an hour later there was another strange occurrence. The display went all wonky, becoming garbled. So I tried unplugging the DP cable and plugging it back in. The 34GP83A popped up a message stating there was no input signal detected. So I had to force shut down the PC. At this point I was even more concerned. Once I was back in Windows, I was going through various settings trying to figure out what could be causing the issue/s, but nothing was coming to the surface that explained anything. Surely the DP input isn’t faulty, I thought. I was near giving up for the night, hoping not to lose anymore sleep, which is coincidentally when I noticed Windows try to put the monitor to sleep. It’s set in power saving features to do this after an hour. The signal was gone when I tried to wake it back up, and I had to force shut down for a second time. This has never occurred with my older monitor connected via DP. It's as if there's something the monitor is doing that's causing the system to destabilize. I considered it a possible issue with my video card, but trying it on another PC I have resulted in a similar outcome. This is highly unpredictable and just happens when it happens. Once the outcome hits the point where the monitor no longer detects a video signal or just plain causes the PC to crash, there's not a lot I can do.

Feeling fed up with the whole situation, I swapped out my old monitors for the night. I then spent two days using those as before, one via DVI and the other displayport. So I know that port is working on the video card. I ran through every test I could think of to see if I could get the same issue to crop up again, and it has not. This brought me to the conclusion there’s just something strange going on with the LG’s DP. I tried to submit a return request via Amazon to get a replacement, but for the first time I received a strange response to such a request. The site said that option was not available and asked if I wanted to speak with customer service. I entered a chat with someone who asked me redundant questions, obviously not tech savvy, and was told I’d have to be directed to speak with LG support. I was a bit baffled. My only other option was to return the monitor for a refund. The current cost is higher than I paid, so I don't want to do that. I don’t want a refund, I want the product. Yet I can either try to deal with LG support to troubleshoot or possibly go through an RMA, or I can return it for a refund through Amazon. If I reordered then, I’d have to pay the higher price. I spent the better part of my lunch break in chat with the associate, but he never was able to get me the LG support contact information. I had to head back to work before he did so, and I even asked him to email it to me. I never received anything.

I like Amazon and still hope to resolve this. I spent the next several days trying the LG monitor again, hoping there was just something inexplicable going on I could miraculously fix. I knew it was unlikely. I knew any moment my PC might crash again. I ended up trying another, higher quality DP cable form the one provided with the monitor itself. Perhaps the one that came with the monitor was faulty? It wasn’t the first thing I considered, but I gave it a try.

Meanwhile, it’s worth mentioning there were a few things missing from the retail product box. The USB hub cable and HDMI 2.0 cable were nowhere to be found. The quick start guide specifies everything included with the monitor, and the three cables were clearly supposed to be in the box. I would need the HDMI cable if I wanted to connect another device, and I can’t use the USB hub feature without their cable, with the warning in the quick start guide not to use any other cable than the one provided. I understand these aren’t incredibly expensive cables, but it’s nice for them to be included with a monitor in this price range. That’s especially the case since they’re supposed to come with it anyway. I think this may be an early adopter issue with a new model coming out. Someone clearly forgot to put them in the box. So it probably won’t happen to many if any others who buy this monitor.

In any case, I've had a couple more incidents since trying the better DP cable. So I've finally given up. I've read that sometimes DP cables have issues like this, but I've tried more than one. I've also tried more than one computer. That the symptoms match up seems to infer the only common denominator is the monitor. It appears to just have a glitchy port. I've watched HWMonitor to check system temps and power readings without any anomalies cropping up. So that's my conclusion. As I type this, I'm using the old IPS combo I've had for a long time now, one on DVI and the other displayport, without any issues.

I don’t want to deal with LG and their RMA process. I’ve heard nothing but horror stories about such things. It’s one gamble you end up making in buying something from LG. If this were a few months down the line, I’d have no choice. Right now though, I’ve had the monitor for only a few days. I should be able to return and get it replaced. So we’ll see how things progress. I’ll update this depending on what the outcome is.

Final verdict on the monitor itself: The display is fantastic, though you will most likely have backlight bleed in all four corners and some IPS glow. I doubt you can get away from this with a curved IPS ultrawide. The displayport issue is a headache. Let’s hope it’s not common with his model. If you need a good monitor for design or photography, videography, etc., as well as highly capable of gaming, I think this is a great option. Whether it’s worth the premium over VA options will come down to preference. I realize they’re half or less the cost, yet this model appears to be LG’s attempt to meet the competition in the middle with price. By all evidence available, it’s practically identical to the 34GN850. If I end up sending the monitor back without getting a replacement, I'm going to be very disappointed.

UPDATE 10-19-20:
I spoke with LG, and their support rep told me I should ask for a return and replacement through Amazon. So I went back to Amazon, and they finally agreed. At this point, however, I was more intent on a refund than a replacement. I've been a bit frustrated by this experience. I really liked the monitor, but the strange displayport glitch that no one seemed to have a solution for, not to mention the constant repetition of troubleshooting I'd already done, delaying things for more than a week, left me disillusioned. I understand Amazon wants to be sure there's a problem, but I'd not have sought to return the monitor otherwise. I explained in detail the problem and what I'd done to try to resolve it. Unfortunately, their customer support reps don't have a great understanding of the tech they sell. I understand training in every product sold through Amazon would be difficult, but this does create some hurdles in the return process.

I really was disappointed to have the rare defective unit. I realize how unusual this kind of issue with a displayport must be. As I otherwise enjoyed the monitor, when everything worked, I have amended the star rating to 4. Another thing worth mentioning is that the monitor worked fine via HDMI. Yet that limits you to 100Hz, so it's not an acceptable workaround. You should be able to use the displayport as it's the only connection that allows for the full refresh rate. I'm not sure why manufacturers only include on displayport while having two HDMI ports, especially when the latter are hardly useful for the type of customers buying these monitors. Just include two of each if you're going to do anything, or two displayports and one HDMI in case someone really needs it. Meanwhile, why HDMI 2.0 and not 2.1? It's virtually pointless.

Is the monitor good? Yes. Is the BLB and IPS glow there? Yes. Will you care? That's subjective. I found it tolerable, but it's not going to compare to OLED if you're used to LG's TVs. Color accuracy is good though, and pixel response is fantastic. That's my final summary.
