I took a leap of faith when I purchased this monitor while on sale for $299 -- an easier pill to swallow considering my firm belief in the notion that you get what you pay for. I figured that if something were to go sideways with regards to quality control, it wouldn't be as heartbreaking as it would be for a monitor twice or three times the price (and things do indeed happen across the price spectrum!). My worst fears were realized when the monitor stopped passing a video signal entirely -- no matter the cable (included ones and others) or connection (HDMI, DP, USB-C). A few days prior, one of the USB-A ports started dropping its signal too. I reached out to INNOCN customer support twice via email and did not receive a reply. Luckily I was also able to message them through Amazon, and I received a timely response. After a brief back-and-forth about possible troubleshooting, they issued a replacement as soon as I shipped back the defective unit in its original packaging with any included cables. I am due to receive it tomorrow, just 2 days after I dropped the defective unit off at UPS with the shipping label THEY provided me. Overall, I would give the product itself 3.5-4 stars. You certainly do get what you pay for. The build quality is relatively average, and I'm certain that alone can explain the issues I had with the USB port and the video signal. However, for $299, this monitor is an absolute steal for a 4K IPS screen with such a plethora of display and data connections. When it worked, it worked wonderfully. My experience with customer service honoring their warranty and making the process so easy pushes my rating to 5-stars, although I know I'd be eating my losses if this were outside of warranty.

I bought this kit to repair my Westinghouse LCM-22w3 LCD monitor. I ordered the kit on April 25th, was scheduled to arrive between April 29th and May 4th, and arrived on May 2nd through USPS. The LED came on, but the screen didn't come on. After some research, it seems as if this is a common problem with this monitor. After removing the power supply board from the monitor, most of the capacitors were bulged on the ends, indicating they were near failure or had already failed.

The kit came with the 9 new capacitors, a small coil of solder, a solder wick, 2 cotton swabs, and an alcohol wipe. Though, I didn't need the wick, nor the solder, I thought it was great that it came with these, just in case. It also came with a thank you note, as well as a link to some instructional videos they created, as they're not allowed to link them on Amazon. As a novice at soldering, I found them quite helpful.

After replacing them and putting the monitor back together, it turned right on and is now my second monitor. Seeing as I got this monitor for free from a friend who was going to throw it out, I got this monitor for the cost of this kit. The whole process of taking apart, de-soldering, soldering, and putting back together took about 2 hours.
Had to send back just wasnt what I wanted. Good product though.

I've ordered a matte-finish anti-glare filter that I hope will help, just be aware that if you plan to install this in a bright setting, you'll have a lot of glare and reflection on the screen.

Update: Laptop arrived, so I tried to reproduce the colors and contrast. This is what I changed
On the monitor menu; Contrast - 80, Color adjust - cool, sharpness, Image Adjust (sharpness) - 1
On nvidia control panel; Brightness - 50, contrast (Red - 60, Green - 55, Blue - 67), Gama - 0.98
My camera isn't that great but I hope you can make out the differences.

This monitor is absolutely great. There are a couple of minor issues I encountered, but for its quality and price this is a good buy.

Bad:

1.) 1 dead pixel for mine, although there isn't much to be done about that except lower yields and raise prices. The good thing is it is in the upper corner and isn't visible while sitting in the center.(I suppose due to refraction or something) It is quite small.

2.) Included calibrating software stopped working. The included software directly controls the monitor settings (although you can manually change the settings using the front buttons). It seems to be a good piece of software but it enables some hot buttons to pivot the image, and when I accidientally hit one while playing Starcraft, the software broke and uninstalling/reinstalling didn't help. I suspect some bad registry issue with the program. The monitor is still useful.

3.) If the contrast is set over about 72 or 73, shallow colors blend together. There is no point in pushing beyond that point. The precision of colors isn't what my old Triniton CRT was, but that is also expected from any LCD. If I make a picture in Paint and use a gray color with a value of 1(out of 255) I can't differentiate from black. Same with white and grey level 254 if that gives you an idea.

4.) Analog input isn't clean. I hooked my Powerbook to it with a VGA cable at 1600x1200 and there is some slight smearing when there is a big white-black transition. Slight meaning single pixel, nothing unworkable, just unattractive.

5.) The brightness control doesn't adjust black level (somewhat typical of LCDs)

Good: Even with contrast set to 73, it is fine. I turn brightness (It really adjusts the brightness, not black level!) down to 40 in my room because I don't even need the excessive brightness this thing produces. The DVI picture quality(what I use) is perfect. It has a 4 port USB 2.0 hub! It is beautiful, except for the big foot. Low resolution interpolation seems good and there is some adjustment for it in the menu. No fast movement problems like ghosting have been noticeable to me, unlike my old 15" Samsung monitor (old and bad).

I wrote a lot about the bad points up there, but they don't matter much to me. The bad pixel truely is small and is only visible from certain angles, fortunately not center. I don't use the PerfectSuite software to calibrate it, I rarely ever use the analog input, and I didn't need a higher contrast. I play games, program, and surf on it. I wanted a good 1600x1200 LCD panel and this is it! It has a USB hub and can pivot. This is a good price for that. DO NOT buy this if you want to manipulate photos. Viewsonic's best 19" model would probably be better for color reproduction or a more expensive professional LCD ($800+) like the Apples or Dell pros or even more expensive stuff, but you shouldn't be using any of the current LCDs anyway, use a CRT. If you must have super accurate blacks and color fidelity, you must wait until this new LED backlit LCD technology is developed. But, otherwise, this is a good buy.

I had previously used an OLED screen by another brand and it was actually purchased to be used primarly as Nintendo Switch portable display and it was for someone else, so it gave me the opportunity to test it out with my laptop which is an OLED 16:10 screen and while I did really like the OLED portable, I was worried about screen burn in as there was no software to built in to mitagate that; unlike my laptop. It also bugged me that my screen ratio was 16:10 and the OLED was 16:9.

So when I began searching for a screen for myself I had a few requirements: a 16:10 screen profile, 2K resolution, matte screen (OLED glossy was just too much glare even in low light), and USB-C connectivity, standard size HDMI (not micro).

Pretty easy requirements these days.

And this screen easily met those requirements. Out-of-the-box it was a little dark, but the menu screen is very easy to use. There are 2 USB-C ports and standard size HDMI port on one side and the controls are on the other, I wish there was a USB-C port on both sides to be able to get the screen closer to the side the of my laptop. My laptop thunderbolt port and Hdmi port are both on the left side, and the portable screen are on the right, so the cables are in the way and as such must sit a few inches further back from the laptop. I also wish the included folio stand did not require the flap in the front, but that is just a prefence.

Keeping with the design, if you need more height/angle of adjustment, the folio magnets are quite strong and it is possible to slide the back of the folio further down the screen to push the screen forward more...if it is needed. The first slot works for me.

I also like that it has two mounting points. I don't know if they sell a portable stand or not, but if i can find one that has the ability to adjust or allow the screen to sit directly on top of my laptop screen then that would be pretty cool too in spots where width of table is an issue.

The speakers of the screen are way better than I expected them to be. They are a little hollow sounding compared to my laptop speakers (defintitely more bass), but for the spoken word they would be just fine. I wouldn't recommend for music/movie audio.

Pictured: I was not currently using my OLED 16:10 laptop as it needed warranty work; the pictured laptop is standard FullHD 16:9 Matte Screen.

SAMSUNG 953BW - STAND REMOVAL

Read the last part of this post before you begin - it might be much easier than is described here, but I did not try it. Did it the hard way.

Here you will find how to take apart the base.

[...]

Here is how to take off the metal post that remains after removing the two plastic halves that cover it - when assembled, they make the vertical piece of the stand.

If you look at the bottom of the monitor frame, you will see that the metal post is attached to a metal plate, and that this plate is attached to another metal plate that is fixed to the monitor. Nevertheless, you will notice that the post can move a little with respect to the monitor.

As you will see in the picture of the rear of the monitor, in the link above: The post is held to the monitor by means of two rails, which hold the side edges of the metal plate attached to the post. And you will also see that there is a small rectangular hole in the plate, in which a retaining tab must go in to keep it from coming off.

I used the large blade of a regular swiss army knife and stuck it in between the two metal plates, in the center part of the space between them, and noticed that it would come to a stop a about the edge of the rectangular hole - it must have been hitting the retaining tab.
Then I hit the knife a couple of times with the heel of my hand, to see if it would go in any further, and: Bingo! It did.
Drove it almost all the way in, and then pulled on the post and it slid out. That's it !

LATER NOTICED: You don't even have to disassemble the vertical piece of the stand.
Just bend the monitor back as far as it would go, then remove the large piece of the base, and there you will see the edges of the two metal plates mentioned in the preceding paragraph where you have to introduce the blade

This is a good deal. I just received one today from Amazon. Literally setting it up now and test driving it. I did quite a bit of research to find a 27" IPS that had 2560 x 1440 resolution. You'll appreciate the extra pixels here versus the more common 1920 x 1080.

It came down to this Acer and a comparable model from Asus. The price was different, the Asus more expensive. I wasn't making the decision on price though, I would have paid more if I preferred one over the other.

I chose the Acer because of the inputs and outputs. The 2 HDMI in and the 1 Display Port were a key factor along with the Display port out so I could but a second one and daisy chain the two together. I'm running a laptop with only the Display Port out, so the ability to daisy chain two monitors was an important factor for me.

I also preferred if the monitor had a Vesa mount because I have an unused monitor arm that I'll put to use. Some of the brands are dropping the mount option in favor of a more high end design look.

It has two tiny speakers on the back, which frankly are fine for watching a quick cat video on YouTube or something. Nothing you're really gone to jam out with though. Desktop speakers or headphones are going to be far superior.

With about 15 minutes under my belt so far I love it and highly recommend it.

Don't forget to download the driver from Acer's website as you will likely need it to get the extended 2560 x 1440 resolution.

I don't normally write reviews, but I think there is a lot of uncertainty surrounding this product, so I thought I'd detail my experience as the Wacom tablets are outrageously priced (I also have an intuos tablet) and they need some competition, which they're getting from Yinova. Once you get it set up, the Yinova tablet works very well and is an excellent value.

First, the screen itself looks great. Some users have mentioned that the angle of viewing is more limited than a regular desktop LCD, and I think this is true, but this is not an issue for me with this screen as it's nicely set up to use as a tablet on my lap. Now onto addressing some issues I encountered, which have tripped up other purchasers as noted from their comments, and how they were resolved for me.

Do not use the drivers on the CD at all. Go straight to the thepandacity.com website and download the two drivers from there. I installed the CD drivers first, they did not work well and they conflicted with the updated drivers from the website (instead of replacing the older drivers, I ended up with 2 sets that were fighting each other and causing odd behavior like sudden horizontal lines while drawing).

Also my first pen required a lot of pressure to start registering as drawing, too much pressure to be easily usable. I sent Panda City an email about these issues and got a call back from Richard in about 2 hours. He told me about the driver conflict issue and also sent me a new pen for free, which arrived 2 days later. The new pen is much more sensitive and works very well, night and day vs. the first pen. If you're getting any odd behavior with this tablet, start off assuming uninstalling/reinstalling the drivers and contact Panda City to see about getting a replacement pen.

Everything is now working smoothly with the tablet and it's a huge upgrade over my old intuos for the same amount of money that the intuos cost me a few years ago.

The other major issue is the quality of the build. I still have a 1080p monitor going strong for multiple decades, yet this one failed within 3 years under the same usage. Half the screen became a mess of colors, and I know that the monitor is at fault because even the logo when it turns on is messed up. The only reason I replaced my previous monitor was that I physically broke it. I've never had a monitor go bad like this, so the fact that it did this in only 3 years is atrocious. I've only physically moved it into two different places. There simply isn't an acceptable reason for a monitor to do this. I've owned probably about 5-10 monitors in my time, and none of them have ever failed in such a short period of time.

I'm left sad and frustrated that I burned hundreds of dollars on this monitor, don't be me.

There are many pros, and a few cons, to this lovely piece of electronics. I'll start with the positives:
1)The screen is a matte LED, which means no glare! Of course, you don't want to set up with a light source behind you as you use it, but you will not be blinded by glare if you do. Only a bit of light bloom on your screen.
2) The resolution and picture quality is great, and the screen is massive! I watch HD movies and play video games on this thing all the time, and the detail is always crisp and clean with no issues.
3) Lightweight build, and borderline portable. I was a bit worried about stability when I ordered it, but the base is surprisingly sturdy. If you push at it or tug cords too hard, it can tip, but there have not been any problems with stability. The only thing stopping me from considering this a portable screen is the size.
4) There are lots of ports for whatever you need, all tucked neatly behind the bottom of the screen.
3) Sound is surprisingly great for a mono-speaker. I have yet to buy separate speakers, although I had planned to do so during purchase. The sound quality is that good. If you want surround, it won't be enough for you, but it's definitely good enough for everything else.
5) It has a power-saving sleep mode, which activates after a few seconds of no input and will deactivate once input resumes.

However, there are a few minor drawbacks that might make this a "do not buy" for some:
1) Since screen is matte and LED, meaning you cannot use cleaning solutions on it. A microfiber cloth must be used for cleaning and nothing else. MAYBE slightly dampened, but never wet or drippy.
2) There are no physical buttons on this monitor. Everything is touch-activated, and all the 'buttons' are located on the monitor's base, just behind the bottom of the screen, above the speaker. I am concerned that this may become an issue if the buttons malfunction, and sometimes it takes a few jabs of the finger to make the "on" button work.
3) The amount of ports is a little low, as there are only one of each kind. This means unplugging and replugging might become kind of a hassle, especially since ports (and therefore cords) are all behind the screen.

Display adjustments are viewed on the screen and changes are made with buttons on the lower right side of the screen frame. The main complaint I have is that it is hard to see the small buttons while you are making adjustments. There are two power switches. To be able to move the cursor over this large screen you will definitely need a cordless mouse. Still you will need a lot of space to move the mouse. I've installed 10 of these displays (one was a 65" version) and have had no issues getting the Supersign display working. As another reviewer stated there is a secret code to access the server menu.
Power on the display>press and hold the SETTINGS button (for about 5 sec) until a blank message window briefly pops up in the top right corner. (You may need to re-press and hold if you don't see it)
When you see the popup press zero 4 times (0000) and press OK. Some displays instead utilize the code 1105.
After you do that you should gain access to the secret menu. This is also where you can set up the Crestron control ID.
The packaging on the display is fairly good and we've only had one display broken (it appeared that someone ran a forklift into it, no protecting against that). The picture quality is outstanding and the Supersign software isn't too hard to deal with, especially if you're just displaying slideshows. Of course, only purchase this display for a commercial environment. It's not designed to be a residential display but I don't believe you would have any issues with it at all if you went that route. Out of the box the color isn't calibrated, I'll list below the correct color calibration for this monitor. First a few tips I'll share. First make sure the distance from the screen is two feet from you. Second make sure to set your video card to "Full" color output within Windows since this is a HDMI monitor. If you have a AMD video card the driver defaults to Full but with Nvidia they default to "Limited" (this is because it thinks it's a TV), for Nvidia users open the Nvidia control panel and on the left choose "Change Resolution", now on the right side and at the bottom change it from Limited to Full then click apply. RGB Full is what you want.

My Calibrated settings below (Spyder5).

Brightness: 12
Contrast: 60
Power Savings: Off

Picture Mode: Custom
SuperResoluton+: Off
Sharpness: 50
Black Level: High
Response Time: Off
Overscan: Off
DFC: Off
Black Stabilizer: 50

Gamma 1
Color Temp: Custom
RGB: 50,48,50

Secondly, I clicked on the Start Icon, and clicked on Settings;  I then clicked on "Ease of Access" and went to "Make Text Bigger" at the top. I've used dual monitors (with sizes ranging from 19" to 24") for over 15 years (when flat screens first starting becoming affordable for normal folks). Over time, I've really appreciated the flexibility of dual monitor displays. The ability to have two applications displaying in their full screen mode side-by-side is something that has consistently added to my productivity since adding a second display.

When offered the opportunity to review a 31.5" inch monitor, I had to consider its functionality both as a single large monitor (pretty much a no-brainer) AND as a replacement for two smaller displays in a dual screen set-up.

First, about the monitor.
This monitor has a brilliant, crisp display that works in all lighting conditions. The curved display (which may not have been a choice I would seek out) required no adaptation since I use this primarily while sitting and with it in the center of my field of vision. Overall, the curve is a neutral trait.

Some settings can be adjusted either through a typical OSD and button navigation, and others are controlled via Windows (Windows 10 in my case) display settings. The power button is on right rear of the monitor, and also serves as 4-way controller for the OSD menu options.

When first connected, it worked just fine with generic Windows drivers, and (when connected via the included HDMI cable). Windows and integrated Intel graphics on my mid-range desktop immediately recognized the change from a dual monitor to a single monitor configuration. As far as I can tell, AOPEN has yet to make available drivers for this monitor, but it worked fine using the Windows generic driver.

Great design choice: there is no "brick" for the AC power; just a standard power cord. Kudos to AOPEN for this decision.

About its utility as a replacement for two smaller displays.
Compared with the two 24" monitors this replaced, overall display area is increased, but the change is less dramatic than that long-ago shift from a single to dual displays. This adds about 4" in vertical display height while reducing the overall horizontal footprint by approximately 12". My crude alculations show the large display increased the overall display area from about 504 square inches to about 810 square inches. You get this extra space without the visual interference of the dual monitors' vertical frame/bezel in the center. For somebody with middle-aged eyes, this is an awesome change for working on spreadsheets without zooming the workbook size. And when your physical desktop is already near capacity (with lamps, speakers, and paperwork in the queue...and sometimes a cat) the returned horizontal space is a great benefit.

Sold as a gaming monitor, I don't use it for that. But my experience as a long-time PC user and sometime PC builder is that the bump is price that comes with the "gaming" appellation almost always comes with increased durability and performance.

This is a nice, big, clear monitor. Select with confidence.

After talking back and forth with the seller, I was able to figure out what my problem is and it may resolve what others have complained about where the second monitor does not work for them.

I have a Lenovo Yoga 6, running Windows 11. It comes with 2 USB-C ports and 2 USB-A ports. I do not have an HDMI port for the laptop itself which is why I purchased a hub for this capability. So if you use the 2 USB-C to USB-C ports directly to your laptop then it works perfectly fine and you now have 3 monitors. Done. However, you now just limited yourself and can no longer charge your laptop with the USB-C port. So now you have to be aware of your batter power. Use this feature if you have 100% battery life for a long extended time for work. You can also just use 2 monitors instead of 3 while the other USB-C port is charging your laptop. Monitors do not eat up your batter life quickly (at least not for me).

Now if you don't want to use up both USB-C ports here is what you do: Monitor #1 is USB-C to USB-C. Monitor #2 is USB-A (from laptop) to USB-C (to monitor) and then ALSO make sure to use the HDMI cord at the same time for Monitor #2. Yes 2 cords for 1 monitor.

However, since I don't have an HDMI port already in my laptop, I have to use a hub for that. But guess what? The hub requires a USB-C port from my laptop so then I have already used up both USB-C ports so what's the point? Again, be aware of your limitations and what you have available for your laptop.

Aside from this confusion and limitation issue, the monitors work perfectly fine. The presentation of how this all came shipped to me was incredible. Made me feel like this was a top of the line product. Beautifully presented and it even comes with a big black pouch to store the monitors for on-the-go. It is a bit clunky and heavy and I don't see myself putting this on my lap just because I would be scared it would fall over. I recommend always working on a table. However, even on a table you're limited on the angle you are working on. It doesn't let you push your main laptop monitor back. So I honestly started to use the box it came in to elevate the laptop to eyelevel. Or if you have a book or something to buffer the laptop up from the table so you can lean it back over the edge that works. The support bracket is sort of useless unless you're elevating it up on a book.

My production level has increased a lot by being able to split my screens in three different ways. This is especially helpful when video editing and using designer software like Adobe so it's helped me out a lot. As long as you aware of your limitations and what your laptop offers, then purchase this product if you can meet the qualifications. Hope this review helps others out there solve their problems.

UPDATE: Bought this in Nov 2021 and didn't use it for the month of January. I left it inside the black pouch that they provided with the USB-C cables and laid it flat on a table. Never touched it. It never fell and nothing ever touched it. Plugged it in a month later and suddenly I see the image that I have attached and I started freaking out. Not sure what caused it to crack. My only assumption is after a while the weight of the cable? I don't know because nothing else was inside the pouch. So my suggestion is NEVER put anything on top of this and keep it stored in something sturdier than the pouch with nothing touching the screens.

When the delivery man delivered this new monitor, I was so excited! I have a large monitor like this one at work and I love being able to work between my large monitor and my laptop screen so I can have several applications open and be able to see them all at a glance, but then when I come home from the office I'm stuck with my little laptop screen. I couldn't wait to be able to work from home with the same experience as in the office.

Let me begin by saying this monitor is absolutely beautiful and incredibly easy to assemble. You need a philips screwdriver to screw in the four screws that come with the monitor to attach it to its base. I love that the monitor easily hooks onto the base and sits there while it waits for you to screw them in. It doesn't balance or wobble or require a second person to hold it in place for you while you screw, so that's nice.

The monitor swivels nearly 180 degrees (not fully, but since I don't have handy protractor, I'm not exactly sure the degree) and also angles up and down a few degrees, which is nice if you're trying to avoid a glare or generally just need to angle it. And it has a third movement! The monitor also slides UP and DOWN about six or so inches. This is so awesome as I've never seen a monitor do this. When I set it up I like to have the monitor behind and above my laptop screen and the height of the monitor at its uppermost height was perfect. I could see someone who is shorter, like a child, lower the monitor down all the way, which puts it about 4" above the height of the desk or table.

In addition to the screws, the monitor comes with 3 cables: 1) a power cord, which for my purposes needs to be about 6-8" longer, but nothing an extension cord or rearranging of some things couldn't solve, 2) a video cable, and 3) a USB cable. I didn't need the USB cable and the video cable worked just fine for my laptop. The USB cable is leftover for me, but you may need it for your particular computer. Additionally, there is a "key" that comes with the monitor and I cannot figure out what this key is for. The lame picture instructions imply you can use it to lock/unlock the monitor in place, maybe so that it doesn't move in any of those three directions I mentioned previously, but there's no where to insert this key. The picture instructions have you to think there are holes along the back of the base, but there are not. There is only a rubbery backing. (Note, I also downloaded the instruction guide from ViewSonic's website and in the user guide it does NOT mention this key or how to use it.)

The last step in using your monitor, according to the included Quick Start Guide, is to insert the included CD labeled "User Guide and Installation Software" and is compatible with PC that has Intel Pentium processor or greater running on Windows Platform with 64MB or more of available RAM; and with Mac running Mac OS version 10.x Uh-oh! My Mac laptop doesn't have a drive to put in a CD. Quick thinker that I am, go to ViewSonic's website to see if I can download the software from their website. I locate the page and...only Windows drivers are available online for download. I start panicking. What if my laptop isn't compatible with this awesome monitor?! Slow down, there's a full User Guide to download as a PDF. Maybe the User Guide can clue me in.

The User Guide basically tells me that if my computer does not have a CD-ROM drive to please call Customer Support. So I call Customer Support. After only pressing a number on two different prompts, I'm quickly connected to technical support. He informs me that I shouldn't need to install ANY software and that once the monitor is plugged in, powered on, and connected to my laptop, the driver automatically installs on the computer. Well, I'm afraid that didn't happen. The monitor does turn on as for a second or two it flashes the ViewSonic and Intel inside logos, flashes a "No Signal" message, then goes dark. Tech support tells me that my computer or operating system just must not be compatible with this monitor. I suggested that rebooting my laptop could work, and tech support waited for me to reboot. Nada. I'm out of luck. Tech support didn't offer any additional assistance other than "your computer must just not be compatible."

UPDATE: Fortunately someone has commented on my review and has helped me on the road to beautiful monitor use. Thank you to that person since ViewSonic's support didn't offer any troubleshooting.

While I'm sure it'll work well for gaming, this display excels at productivity enhancement. To be honest, the best option in a 2FD (3840x1080) or 2QHD (5120x1440) display would be a curved screen -- if possible, one with a 1000R curvature. This display is *flat*. Flatter than the Iowa cornfields. That's fine if, like me, you bought it to use as a work from home primary display, a function it accomplishes with aplomb. This puppy gives you the equivalent of two 1920x1080 FHD displays in a single 44 inch (okay, 43.8", happy now?) unit. It's not lightweight, and it's terribly fragile, so PLEASE PLEASE PLEASE keep your original packaging and use that if you're planning to transport this display ANYWHERE outside your home. But as far as how it works, it's a winner. The IPS display is clear and bright, with good color reproduction and very little backlight bleed when viewing black background.

The best selling point of this monitor? Assuming Amazon hasn't raised the price since I bought mine, they're selling it for the ridiculously low price of $499.99. It MSRP's at $999.99 on Sceptre's own website, so take that for what it's worth.

Did I mention that it comes with two HDMI cables and a battery-powered remote? It does. Furthermore, it supports its full resolution at up to 120Hz via HDMI, DP1.4, and (believe it or not) USB-C. And it supports AMD FreeSync Premium. And it's VESA Certified for HDR600. And, oh yeah, if the resolution I mentioned above (3840x1080) hasn't clued you in, its aspect ratio is 32:9.

It's a quality monitor that works excellently as a productivity enhancer, and probably works well for gaming, at an absolute STEAL of a price. Buy one while they're still dirt cheap. You won't regret it.

Pros:
1. NO dead nor white pixels, no scratches, and no problems of any kind involving the screen.

I bought a display port cable anyways to be more future ready when I can afford a 4k monitor when the draconian prices finally stop.
5. Acer packaged this monitor well enough with, although not my preference, hard foam that was thick enough and is arrived without dents or chunks missing. Thus I can reuse the packaging and retail box whenever I need to move this monitor safely.

I purchased a top-of-the-line 15" MacBook Pro this year and struggled to use it for work since I heavily rely on dual monitors. This meant I had to run two USB-C to DisplayPort cables, a USB-C to USB-A hub, and another cable to power my MacBook. With this monitor, I run ONE Thunderbolt/USB-C cable to my MacBook and it works flawlessly. There are four USB-A ports on the monitor (2 in rear, two on the side), which I use to connect my wireless keyboard and mouse. Although these ports only run at USB 2.0 speeds when connected via USB-C, it doesn't bother me since I'm not using it for high speed data transfer.

Almost all other ultrawide monitors that have USB-C connectivity (besides the LG UltraFine) only supply a maximum of 60W of upstream power. Since a 15" MacBook Pro takes 85 watts to charge, you can end up relying on your battery to make up for the difference. The Dell U3818DW provides 100W of upstream power!

Now onto the display itself...

Picture Quality:
This thing is absolutely amazing when it comes to picture quality and text readability. I purchased an LG 34UC79G and hated using it for anything that required staring at text for any length of time. The Dell U3818DW runs at 3840 x 1600 (~111 PPI) and has roughly 40% more pixels than my old LG 34UC79G, which runs at 2560 x 1080 (~82 PPI). I can run Excel spreadsheets all day long on the Dell and my eyes don't feel fatigued, whereas the LG almost instantly gave me a headache. I occasionally play some Call of Duty and have to say it is pretty impressive on this monitor. Although it only has a 5ms response time and 60Hz refresh rate, it suits my needs for the small amount of gaming I use it for.

Build:
This thing is built like a tank! It arrived via UPS double-boxed with many "heavy" stickers slapped on it. The manufacturer's box is extremely thick with plenty of packaging to keep it safe during transport. The monitor alone weights about 27 lbs and feels very sturdy using the supplied stand. It can be VESA mounted if you want to keep the space underneath the monitor clear (which I may eventually do).

Connectivity:
1 x DisplayPort 1.2 (HDCP 2.2)
2 x HDMI 2.0 (HDCP 2.2)
2 x USB 3.0 Upstream port
2 x USB 3.0 Downstream port (side)
2 x USB 3.0 Downstream port (bottom)
1 x USB Type-C (Alternate mode with DP1.2, Power Delivery, and USB2.0)

The built-in speakers are decent but are definitely not going to give you the low-range thump that you'd get with an external set of computer speakers.

Overall, this monitor is everything I have been looking for to complement my 2017 15" MacBook Pro with a single-cable solution. Yes, it is a bit pricey, but you will be very happy once you see it in person.

UPDATE: After using this monitor for a few weeks, I had to take off one star for the following reasons:

1) No display manager software for Mac. This means you’re limited to snapping two windows side-by-side. If you want more windows, you have to manually resize and arrange them.

2) Built-in monitor speakers cannot be controlled by the keyboard volume controls. Again, a limitation due to lack of Mac software/driver.

3) Changing inputs is a bit cumbersome. The auto detect can get annoying and manual input selection requires too many button presses.

Although these are minor complaints, it’s not a lot to ask for Mac drivers & software when it’s a $1,000+ computer monitor with USB-C support.

Three months later, (at the tender age of 5 months post-manufacture) bright vertical lines shot up the right hand side. Dozens of them. I purchased one of these monitors a couple of months ago. Note that this is a glossy display (one of only a handful left, it seems), with all of the pertinent advantages and disadvantages. I like the extra saturation that glossy displays provide.

There are two panels used in this model: one is made by Samsung and other is made by LG (yes, the monitor is made by HP, but they buy the panel used inside of it from other companies). I have the LG panel. You can find out which you have by holding down the menu button when powering up the monitor and accessing a special "service" menu item. If the panel model starts with "LPL", you have the LG one.

I'm generally impressed by the quality of the display. The colors really "pop" and the contrast among whites is extremely precise. The contrast between very dark shades, on the other hand, leaves a bit to be desired; no amount of fiddling with the settings or calibration of the display will allow me to easily differentiate the two darkest shades of gray on a color chart with this monitor.

I saw no dead pixels on this display.

This monitor is BRIGHT. Too bright, in fact; I had to turn off the ambient light sensor and turn down the brightness to avoid eyestrain.

There is some backlight bleed when the display is blank, but it's unnoticeable when showing an image, so it doesn't bother me. I've heard that this is a common problem with the LG panel but not the Samsung.

Note that this is a TN panel, and the usual poor viewing angles apply. For a monitor of this size, this is actually a problem, as it's entirely possible to focus on the center of the screen and have the left side of the display appear kind of red out of the corner of your eye if you're not positioned perfectly. That takes a bit of getting used to.

Color fidelity: this is a wide gamut monitor with excellent coverage, including 100% of the sRGB color space! You NEED to use the included color profile with this monitor, or better yet, calibrate it with a colorimeter, if you plan on using this display to its full potential. The 6500K and 9300K settings are both kind of off, so you may want to fiddle with the individual RGB sliders until you get an acceptable white point. Again, a colorimeter is worth its weight in gold here. There is also an "sRGB" mode on this monitor, which is handy for previewing web work, but you'd be wasting the incredible color of this monitor if you set it permanently.

The colors seem to drift kind of quickly, too. I find myself noticing a color cast after about two weeks from calibration. Most displays can last about a month before needing recalibration.

I notice no ghosting whatsoever, not even in games. The response time is excellent.

Finally, there's a USB hub built into the monitor, which is very convenient. There are also built in speakers.

A few minor issues to comment on:

There is no DVI input; it only takes VGA and HDMI. You'll need to purchase a DVI to HDMI cable if you plan on using this with most video cards, but these are pretty cheap and there's no sacrifice of image quality.

The speakers are very poor quality. I wouldn't bother with them.

The display does have a "portrait mode", but it's not going to be usable if you put it on a desk or other surface directly: the stand is not tall enough to accommodate the width of this display, so when you try to rotate it, it will bang against the desk. It seems meant more for a wall-mount setup. You can still swivel it on three axes, though, which is kind of nice.

Overall, this is a solid display. Its wide color gamut in particular makes it suitable for print image editing, while its complete coverage of sRGB makes it suitable for web image editing. It is important to keep in mind that the colors may appear more saturated on the display than they will when printed, however. Movies will look excellent on it, so long as you can cram all of your viewers into the display's poor viewing angle. The included sRGB color mode is also handy in that it may be used to preview how others may see your images on the web. The white contrast is excellent. My primary complaints are the poor black contrast, the lack of speaker quality (I'd prefer if they just took $15 off of the cost and didn't include them), and the somewhat high price tag.

I really wanted to like this monitor (GNV34DBE) - on paper it checks nearly all the boxes I was looking for: ultrawide, 3440x1440, 1500R curve, 2 DP inputs, freesync, 144Hz, HDR, VESA mountable. Unfortunately, the black smearing common on VA panels and some freesync flickering issues were bad enough to make me return it.

Pros:
Ultrawide - I absolutely love ultrawide for games and productivity. You can have three windows open side by side by side for productivity, and gaming is significantly more immersive than a typical 16:9 aspect ratio.

High refresh rate - 144Hz is worth it, I would never go back to 60. Makes games so much more enjoyable, and even dragging the mouse around way better. This is about the cheapest you can get for a 144Hz 1440p ultrawide as well.

1500R curve - I thought this would be too aggressive, but I really like this level of curvature on an ultrawide monitor. I own a flat ultrawide and I hate looking at it after having the nice curve on this one. You have to swivel your head significantly less to see content on the edges of the screen. Not sure if it makes a difference ergonomically speaking, but it feels much more immersive, and looks super dope.

Neutrals:
HDR - Nice to have at this price point, but the monitor doesn't get bright enough to deliver a true HDR experience you would get from a nice TV. There's also a weird quirk where enabling HDR locks the max HDR brightness to your current brightness setting, so you have to remember to crank the brightness all the way up before you should turn HDR on.

OSD - The OSD is okay, nothing special. There are a few gaming oriented features that I never used. Aside from the one weird quirk with the HDR brightness, everything else is pretty straightforward. The button layout is slightly too far back and clunky for regular use, but at this price point, standard navigation buttons are expected. There are also volume controls, but there are no speakers built into this monitor.

VESA mount - The monitor is standard VESA compatible, but I believe it uses 75x75mm holes instead of 100x100mm, which is odd for a monitor this size. Additionally, the curve of the monitor makes it stick out quite a bit on a mount, which is something to consider due to the torque on your mount from the extension and weight of the monitor.

LED - There is a static red LED that you can turn on/off on the back of the monitor. Kind of cool, but I left it off as it didn't really add anything.

Cons:
Black / dark color smearing - This was by far the worst part of the monitor. I've heard of dark smearing on VA panels, but after reading the Tom's Hardware review for this monitor, I decided to bite the bullet anyways. The black level smearing for me is unbearable. I play a lot of FIFA, and you can see player's with black hair just smear across the screen when the camera moves. Even in GTA, you can see buildings in the distance smear when you rotate the camera. I tried to fix this through various overdrive settings, but all of them come with either too much smearing, or too much ghosting when you overdo it. Maybe I'm just used to IPS monitors with minimal smearing, but it was simply too much for me to overlook.

Freesync flickering - While this monitor is not Gsync compatible certified by Nvidia, you can still enable it in the Nvidia control panel settings. It works, but for some reason in certain games it would cause noticeable flickering. Many have suggested that this was due to a switch between low framerate compensation and freesync at lower framerates, but I was having this issue even between 100-120 fps. Disabling and re-enabling freesync in the monitor OSD would usually fix the issue, but it's a pain to constantly deal with just to play a game with freesync on.

TLDR; Good monitor, but black smearing and freesync flickering issues made me return it.

I am honestly beyond pleased with this monitor.  I picked it up as I was looking for an easy mobile set up to improve my work flow and this was exactly what I was looking for.  The fact I can power it through just a USB-C is so convenient too.  Highly recommend!

I spent a lot of time looking into which monitor to purchase. I have been incredibly impressed with this machine! The color reproduction is incredibly spot on for a monitor that costs under $1000. Exactly as described. Screen transitions between portrait and landscape fairly smoothly. Setup easy under Windows 10. Very pleased.

Pros
-Nice price
-Great display
-HDMI input
-VGA input (you can connect your desktop computer/laptop monitor to it)
-Firmware upgradeable, provided you have a desktop computer/laptop next to it, a USB cable, and an internet connection (or get the firmware from someone's computer with internet access)

Cons
-Limited options for wall mount. Uses non-standard 100mm x 200mm mount.
-Stand is somewhat difficult to remove. Screws are hard to fully remove.
-Remote is not a universal remote.
-TV is not compatible with nearly all current regular universal remotes. If you want to only use one remote for all your electronics, your easiest solution is to buy "learning" remote.
-Standby light is bright enough to be a night light. You may need to put tape over it if it is in your bedroom.
-No Over The Air tuner (STV or HDTV). You will need to buy a HDTV tuner (or use a cable box) to get HDTV stations. You will need to buy a VCR or antenna (or use a cable box) with composite output (red/white/yellow) with to get Standard TV stations.
-Remote has no separate input buttons. You must press "source" until you get the input you want.

Overall, even with the cons, this is a great tv if you want to break into the HDTV market, or want a second HDTV to compliment your other massive one someone else in your home. If you want a tv that's compatible with more of today's latest remotes, or you want a OTA tuner, there are higher end (or larger) Olevia models that you can shell out a few more dollars to get. I'd buy it again.
Great quality. Easy setup and take apart - I traveled with this item for a couple weeks and easy to disassemble. No installations necessary on the computer.  Nice image quality.

I have had this monitor for 3 years now and it still looks as amazing as the day I bought it.  I have no complaints over how it has performed over the years.  This is a great monitor to pick up and unless you were looking for a curved monitor, I’d highly recommend picking this up.

The screen can even be easily rotated to a vertical orientation; however, this makes the monitor extremely tall, and I find the monitor is (for my purposes at least) much better suited to a normal horizontal orientation.

I like the quality of the screen, however, the webcam does not produce a clear picture. The speakers are in the back of the monitor, making it hard to hear the person you are on a video call with.

Lives up to my expectations. I'm just isn't as a second display for work when I travel and it works great. Plenty bright. It comes with cables but I still needed to but an adapter to get from my mini displayport to mini hdmi. It works fine with my phone on USB C.
I love this monitor, lightweight and easy to install. I have a similar laptop at work which is twice the price. It’s as good as that one.

These are nice monitors at a good price. Use the built in monitor calibration in Windows for best results. Colors are bright, images and text are clear. They mount nicely on a dual monitor stand. No dead pixels.

My friend bought this monitor for me (from Fry's Electronics) as a Christmas present. It worked fine until the screen started going black for no reason. I would just switch it off and then back on to fix that problem. But later I heard a "poof" and it went black, and then there was a burnt smell coming from the monitor. Fast forward to about a week ago (mid sept) and on turning on the monitor the 2 computers connected just fine to it and was able to move the cursor onto the screen, but the actual monitor itself wouldn’t show an image. I would have gave them 4 stars the product came as if the item was partly used, some items already open and attached, the box looked like it had better days. LOL. Anywho, folks this product definitely impressed the crap out of me. It was a breeze to setup in my home office. It took me weeks to finally decide on this product; thanks to others reviews.

The setup was overall easy as this was my first installation. I knew right out of the box this was a quality system.
Caution: The arms will be wrapped with Velcro straps, as you remove the Velcro strap, the arms will begin to open, remember these have a spring, it will knock the heck out of someone to close, lol. Reminder, before mounting the arms, attach the cable management guides. The only problem I had was moving the monitor holder into the appropriate position, I couldn't turn it at all by hand. So it didn't make mounting the monitor as easy as I expected, but I made it work. Once you mounted the monitor I was able to turn with a breeze. Now the monitors might be high up, so here's a bit of advice as the directions wasn't immediately clear. To adjust the tension push the arm down so it's horizontal to the ground with one hand and hold it. With the other hand adjust the tension screw in the "-" direction. You will feel the arm not trying to rise eventually. You can do this before you mount the monitors as well if it's already in the rise/high position. You will thank me later. Next I started doing cable management. That was a chore as I have two computers (Laptop and a desktop on the floor) sharing the same monitors. Now here's a warning to those that have adjustable desks. If your PC/Desktop is on the floor, the standard 6ft monitor (VGA,HDMI, DVI, etc) cables will not be long enough. I would recommend getting 10ft cables. The 6ft monitor power cord aren't a problem. Now that everything was setup and configured, I was able to adjust the 24in monitors to my preferred viewing pleasure. Once all adjustments were made, the monitors stayed in their position. I could still move and adjust accordingly and no issues with the monitors moving. I can't wait until I get my 28in Monitors. This is definitely a perfect buy for me and I highly recommend. I can't tell you the weight of my monitors, and they are two different Dell models. Shipping was fast. Box has weight to it. Enjoy!

Update with recent macOS El Capitan and Sierra drivers: Perhaps due to Apple’s marketing of LG’s 5K display and closer partnering, out-of-box monitor support seems to have improved for the 31MU97 on my macbook, though I haven’t tried it with the Mac Pro again since I got a different monitor for it. The monitor is now automatically addressable at reasonable full-width resolutions at 50Hz over display port on my MacBook Pro 13” Early 2015. Finally, no black bars. The Apple support article at https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT206587 doesn’t even mention this 50Hz capability. Interestingly, there is no option to run it at 3840x2160 at 60fps like it used to. But I kind of want those 200 pixels more than the 10fps. The monitor was sitting in a closet for four months before I decided to try it again just to see if the LG drivers had improved. Color is great (I’m using sRGB mode) and I’m using it a bit farther away than before on a new desk so I don’t see the color aberrations for the viewing angles in the corners as much. Upping to 3 stars. What a crazy time it has been with 4K monitors.

Second year Update: Using it on the Mac Pro has been problematic as well, no matter which expensive cable I try it still generates black screens for some content (black text on white background is particularly bad) unless I use a 3rd party display tool to knock it down to 50fps refresh rate — and then I have to do that every reboot while the screen flickers on and off. Now it’s sitting on the floor in the closet until I update my Windows box to an nVidia 1080 card and see what luck I have with it there. It’s beautiful when it works.
Edit: I enjoyed many months of use of this monitor, occasionally switching between a MacBook Pro Retina 13" and my Windows games box using a Displayport to Mini-DP cable. Then I foolishly decided I wanted to upgrade to Windows 10. I ended up having to turn on "All Updates" not just "Required Updates" from Windows Update on Windows 8.1 (because otherwise the notification widget that allows you to upgrade doesn't get installed unless you do. Thanks MS.) The first boot after these updates to 8.1 were applied (before Windows 10 was installed!) suddenly the monitor would not be used except by the BIOS during boot, and Windows came up on my secondary monitor. After another few reboots Windows eventually decided it was a Generic P-n-P Monitor and only allowed screen sizes up to 1920 wide. I tried changing cables. I tried switching outputs on my nVidia card. I re-installed the drivers for the card. I've done everything but re-install Windows from scratch but it will no longer recognize it correctly. Meanwhile the monitor works just fine on my Macs and when my wife got a new Mac and plugged it in and it worked fine from the start (though, once in a while I've seen a screen blank for a few seconds then come back -- but it's not happening all the time and the Mac was upgrading some components during that boot.)

Other reviewers have had some similar "Generic Plug and Play Monitor" problems and sent theirs back for a new one, yet the fact that it connects and works perfectly on my Mac and its bad behavior started just as I did a Windows Update make me think it might be a software issue that doesn't happen with a different EDID/Serial number, or a hardware/firmware handshake problem that doesn't happen to manifest on Mac drivers (yet) but apparently does when you followed the upgrade steps I did.

In general, I'm pretty sure there are some hardware and firmware bugs in this monitor but there are no firmware updates on the site, so I'm pretty upset about this.

I've finally decided to give this monitor to my wife for use on her machine (which officially supports it at 60fps in single stream mode!) and looking for a different monitor to use with my MacBook Pro (which can't do 4096-wide modes as much as I've tried even with custom display parameters) and the gaming Windows box which is currently happily driving my wife's old iMac in target display mode as a 2560x1440 monitor. I'll be looking for one with a better input switching mechanism too, as while this monitor has tons of inputs it has a lot of steps through the menu to switch inputs.

Also, while setting it up with the new Mac I noticed in black screens there are some areas at the bottom of the screen that blossom slightly lighter than the surroundings (my wife turned the brightness up a lot higher than I usually used it). Didn't seem like image ghosting.

At this point I could only recommend this monitor for someone using one of the new cylinder Mac Pro, a Macbook Pro Retina 15" (the 13" cannot use all the pixels) and needs the true 4K Cinema aspect, which is still unusual to find on the market. Or someone who doesn't mind tinkering with Windows endlessly.

-- original review --

In general this monitor seems to be quite a good buy for its price point in the market at the time of this review (I'd hope to see it or its successor down in the just-sub-$1000 range in the next year) but it can get quite maddening for Mac users who are unwilling to spend some time with screen mode utilities to get the most out of it [see update below: your mileage may vary]. Cinema 4K may not be for you and a UHD 4K monitor is a better buy. For those looking for dual inputs, the PBP mode has some limitations especially for Mac users.

The target audience for the monitor is content creators and while color reproduction is very good, it definitely feels like it's still a "pro-sumer" class product. It features a factory calibration mode, sRGB and AdobeRGB modes which were good matches to my MacBook Pro profiles, though the factory mode seemed to bring out orange side of the spectrum a bit more. My only complaint so far is that the display has a tendency to take on a magenta cast as viewing angle gets more acute. You need to be at least 30 inches from the display to avoid the magenta tint at the edges being detectable when viewed on center. If you are looking for a monitor to use at closer range this may be troubling for you, but when seated normally it's only slightly noticeable. It's not much worse in viewing angle than my MacBook Pro Retina display but on that display you mostly lose contrast whereas on this display you get much more of a change in tint. Brightness range seems good but I'm not using it in a bright office. If you were looking to use this to display 4K cinema at a simulated viewing distance I can't see how you could go wrong. For editing photos up close and desktop publishing, I'd say it may not be the best but could be the best in its price point currently.

I have a few nits. Out of the box it is in "wide" aspect mode and will stretch 3840x2160 sources, terrible idea for a default since so many sources will be limited to this resolution and these are so much better viewed in Original or 1:1 mode, accessible through "Easy control" / "Ratio" option in the OSD menu. I'm planning on using it with my MacBook Pro retina laptop from work sometimes, and on a PC for CAD/design work and gaming. It's replacing a 30" Apple display that doesn't have good input choices for modern machines. Since my laptop uses a 3840x2160 monitor when at work I actually don't mind that it's not using the full width, the loss of ~2" of screen width is perhaps less annoying than the desktop moving stuff around.

The Picture-by-Picture mode is pretty useful on a display this size but sadly the built-in Mac Preferences Display scaling options (on Mavericks at least) doesn't have a HiDPI mode for the 2048x2160 pixel/1024x1080 point mode like it does for UHD modes. I haven't tried hacking it up with a utility to get that behavior, but it would be really nice since most web pages would still format okay at 1024 points. Using the HDMI output on the Mac is even worse, it's not able to understand the screen resolution for some reason, resulting in doubled vertical aspect ratios (everything is tall and thin). That is kind of annoying, though I believe it's probably another thing that can be tweaked with aftermarket utilities on the Mac.

Speaking of the Picture-by-Picture mode, be aware it is limited in that only the "master" side can be displaying a DisplayPort input (both DP and Mini-DP connectors are available) or HDMI, while the other side (the "sub") can only use one of the HDMI inputs! So no, you can't use both DP's on a MacBook Pro out of the box to emulate a 4096-wide display either, though that would be a waste of bandwidth anyway.

Update: I've been using the monitor for a few weeks now mostly on my new PC with a nVidia 970 series card and the included DP to mini-DP cable (though now the PC is the DP side and the monitor is the mini-DP side). It continues to be a winning combination of usable desktop space and picture quality like I have never used before, even though I do (now) see just a bit of irregular tint in the backlight right near the horizontal edges. Turning on the "Uniformity" option in the monitor OSM helps slightly and makes the whole display a little dimmer and more paper-like, but really it doesn't bother me and I prefer the brighter screen.

Like other reviewers have said, even on Windows you may have to take an extra step of pushing up the resolution to max in the control panel or create a "custom" resolution in the nVidia driver control panel to get the full width, but after that it's working fine. I've tried various Windows font scaling modes including a 160% custom scaling mode that looked okay (and made 1" on the monitor exactly 1" in the setup screen of the control panel) but since I have two monitors connected to the box, it was a bit weird moving back and forth at anything but 1:1 scaling. At 1:1 ratio regular text is pretty small but still readable from 25 inches away. I think at various times I've tried every cable combination on this monitor including a silly mishap with a DP cable that was not full bandwidth -- watch out, DP cables have more than one rating (the limited ones will say "1080p" and/or "RBR" for "Reduced Bit Rate" compliance, and usually have longer lengths) and basically the display just turns on and stays black with no indication of what's wrong (well, if you send it more than the cable can handle -- if you were outputting standard 1080p HD it would probably work, but I had already set it up the card for 4096x2160 on HDMI and swapped for the "better" cable to find out it wasn't a better cable at all). The HDMI inputs were surprisingly usable at 25 fps for everything but gaming, didn't have as much pointer lag as I feared.

But here's something interesting for Mac users: I switched to a different laptop and connected through a different third party mini-DP to DP cable instead of the mini-DP to mini-DP cable I was using before, and suddenly the Mac was immediately outputting at 4096x2160 full screen at 50 hz! I'm not sure what else changed, the new laptop is still on Mavericks, so unless the cable made a difference I can't explain it. Okay, there is not a whole lot of information about this monitor at the moment, so I thought I would convey a few important notes about this monitor. This is in no way a full review, but it does address some of the things I have been reading about this monitor that I disagree with.
Firstly, this monitor does not use a WVA or WVA+ display, in fact, I am fairly sure that there is no such thing. The only place someone could come to this conclusion is because on the box it says "32 inch WVA backlit monitor". Most people automatically assume that this means to describe the type of monitor it is. As others have said there is very little information when you Google the term WVA. Most techies however will tell you that this is simply an acronym for "Wide View/ing angle"

So many reviews from different sites are classifying this monitor wrong. The only thing they have in common is that this is indeed a VA monitor. Even one of the reviewers on here called up, and the people at HP(who don't specialize in every single product obviously) told him that it was a WVA monitor....

In the product manual that comes with the monitor (which no one seems to read) it clearly states that this uses a AMVA+ display! If you want, you can go ahead and call it a WVA AMVA+ monitor, but the actually technology at play is the newer AMVA+.

There honestly isn’t much information on this either, as it came out within the last couple years. However, if anyone is curious and isn’t familiar, here is a good website that explains about the older AMVA(not AMVA+ which is newer, and much improved).
<http://www.tftcentral.co.uk/articles/panel_technologies.htm>

Now a few things about this monitor. I have heard a lot of people comparing this to IPS monitors and how they color may not be as good. Also people saying that text may be a bit blurry because its 32 inches and only 2560x1440. I will start with the latter. I have noticed nothing remotely blurry when using this monitor. Most people probably have something set up wrong. Even from a few inches away (which I don’t recommend), no text ever comes out as blurry to me.

As for the IPS vs VA colors. Yes IPS will have more exact colors, however unless you are doing exact work where color accuracy is key, or are a professional photographer, you will NEVER know the difference. AMVA+ comes very close to the color accuracy of IPS monitors, and no doubt more accurate than many cheaper IPS monitors that you will find. What’s more is that unless you put this next to an IPS monitor and look at the exact same image; there is no way you would ever know that they colors are off in the least! People make way too much of a fuss over color accuracy, which is funny, as humans don't all see colors the exact same way in the first place!

In my opinion, this AMVA+ display is better than any IPS monitor for one reason. While IPS monitors may be more “true to color” than you will ever even be able to notice, VA monitors dominate when it comes to deep blacks. This monitor sports a static 3000:1 contrast ratio, which you will notice WAY MORE than any color differences.

Also, anyone complaining about this needing a higher resolution for the 32 inch screen is sitting WAY too close to their monitor!

Okay, when I rate a product, I rate it on how it should be if everything came as it should. There was also a noticeable scuff on the screen, which must have happened at the factory, as this monitor was the best packaged monitor I have ever had the privilege of opening.

(It is already peeling back ever so slightly) I don't know if that particular problem was a common one or I just received a bad apple. I would actually rate this 4 stars if I knew this to be a standard issue.

Sorry for all of the exclamation points…I had a lot of, well, points to make…..
Lastly, anyone who thinks that I am bias in any way should know that as much as I like this monitor, I am actually returning it. The monitor is simply too big for me.

But... These monitors have a built in passive USB hub. If you connect a USB device that asks for too much current it will forward that to your machine. Where Intel Macs have an SMC to prevent overcurrent conditions like this, the M1 Macs tried to incorporate that into the SoC, but it doesn't correctly prevent the overcurrent condition and blows the USB controller such that it can no longer power devices. This monitor blew the USB controllers of two MacBook Pro M1's in a row and cost me a small fortune and about 4 months of delays (!). Whose fault is it? Hard to say. You need all 3 conditions to be true to lose the controller. But that was my setup.

When I tried to report this to Benq their CS just ignored me. They also didn't have a firmware update tool for Mac at that time, not that it would help. Well I'm not risking a third MacBook Pro to find out.

I tried to use their windows firmware update tool, freshly installing both windows 10 and 11 but it wouldn’t function and provided no error message or log. See attached photo and video.

Here is a relevant excerpt from the USB Type-C Specification 1.2 (Revision 1.2, August 2016):

"6.2.1.2 Over-Current Protection
All USB Type-C ports and hubs shall provide Over-Current protection (OCP) as specified in this section. The OCP function shall protect against over-current conditions on VCONN and VBUS, including conditions caused by a short circuit. An over-current condition is defined as a current level that exceeds the maximum allowed current for a specified period of time."

Anyway not for M1 machines. If you used an M1 and the passive built in hub check to see if you can still use powered USB devices. I’m sending mine to Louis Rossmann to document how it destroys MacBooks.

Notes:

- Even though an old cellphone can power & send video data to run the monitor with a single cable, older Laptops without video capable USB will require you to connect two cables to your laptop. An HDMI cable for video and USB for power.

I am very pleased with this monitor setup. It's really nice to be able to expand my laptop screen to 3 monitors when I'm working on projects. It makes video editing a dream! Setup was easy - Windows 10 recognized both without any problem and my laptop had 2 video outputs I can use (one USB-C and one HDMI).

One really nice thing I like about the design is that one of the monitors can be flipped around to face backwards on the back of your monitor, so that someone sitting behind the back of your laptop can see what you're working on. This is really handy if you're wanting to go over something with another person while you're both sitting at a table across from one another.

I think this will easily fit most laptops with the way they've built the clips. You stretch out the center piece to the width of your laptop screen and then hang it from steel clips at the top. Toward the bottom there are small plastic clips on the sides that can help to keep the monitor in place. Unfortunately, these are both small and flimsy, I can see them not helping much on some laptops or even breaking. But they may not even be necessary if your open screen is perpendicular to the desk - the monitor should hang OK.

I can see two potential problems with some laptops. First, this screen is kind of heavy, so if your laptop screen hinge doesn't have much resistance it may flop forward or backward with the extra weight. It may also be easy to "knock off" or "knock over" on some models. They do have a "kickstand" you can extend to help support the weight, but it wasn't long enough to help at all with the size of my laptop screen. Second, there's not much you can adjust other than the width, so this method is really expecting to hang from a thin, flat laptop screen.

One thing I'm not thrilled with is that you have to run cables separately to each side for both power and signal to the monitors. It would be nice if they duplicated the ports so that you could run all cables to one side but choose which side you wished to connect to. It really becomes a spaghetti mess of wires everywhere when you connect the monitors, there's no pretty solution.

Finally, the price is a little steep. Given how unique and portable this solution is, however, they can justify it a little. Still, I would prefer to see this listed for around $300 - I think that would be a more fair price. But if you need the capabilities this gives you then price probably isn't going to be a big barrier, it will be worth it.

I should note, they include a nice padded carrying bag to help protect it when you're transporting it. Also included are 2 sets of the cable types you'll need to connect to most laptops - one set for each side.

Overall, I'm quite pleased with this monitor set and will use it frequently. I think they could work on a better solution for securing it to your laptop screen, or even give you a "standalone" option to sit a little behind your screen, and it definitely could use better cable management. But it's wonderful at what it does, so I can definitely recommend it to laptop users looking for a "3 screen" solution.

This monitor is absolutely outstanding. I upgraded from a best-selling ASUS 1080p 23" TN panel that served me well. I am now using that monitor as my secondary, and there's a massive difference in image quality when moving items between the two. The colors on this panel are so crisp and accurate. The higher color bit rate meant that I was able to see shades of colors that my previous monitors simply couldn't show.

What this monitor excels at extends beyond just visual image. The build quality of the monitor including how it looks and feels are tremendously well thought out. It looks its price, and when I adjust its orientation using the easy-to-use stand I'm reminded why it is worth the price of admission.

The only downside I have experienced is with ghosting. It hasn't bothered me, but I've noticed it. I believe the issue has to do with the monitor's higher response time (8ms or 6ms depending on setting) and its IPS technology. It could be an issue in competitive FPS games like Counter-Strike, but I haven't tested.

I paid over $400 for this monitor, and power it using my GTX 970. Surprisingly, the mid-tier card can handle every game on high settings at 1440p. It's truly remarkable.

1. At 1920x1080, The sharpness is horrific. I use $100 hd 60HZ monitors primarily and even they are sharper than this monitor. The letters are slightly blurry and the coloration is awful. It's as if the monitor just isn't capable of displaying good quality. At 2560x1440, it's even WORSE! The coloration quality is even worse and letters aren't even fully displayed. The letters are cut off in several areas and cannot fully appear.

2. This monitor is actually 60HZ. It is not capable of 75HZ.

3. The only way to get this monitor to display at 2560x1440 is by having software like Nvidia control panel or whichever come with your graphics card. Using window's display settings will not allow you to get this thing to show anything past 1920x1080p.

All in all, you get what you pay for. This monitor is dirt cheap at this price point so expect dirt quality. I would prefer saving an extra hundred bucks or two to buy something that will actually be worth your money. I'm returning this thing today and saving for something that isn't a ripoff.

This would be my dream display...if only the resolution were better. It truly opens up a new world from my Macbook Pro, but the text resolution is so far below that of the Macbook or the iMac 5k that the display cannot be used for prolonged reading or writing. If and when Dell (or another company) comes out with this size and shape of monitor in 5k, it will be the best-in-class by far. I would pay double the price if this monitor were 5k.

Pros:
*Screen dimensions are perfect. I have three Chrome windows opened at all times, and that extra width makes it a delight. The OS desktop opens up to a whole new level...you finally understand how well the desktop can be used.
*The curved screen is subtle but makes a big difference in the immersive experience.
*The colors are adequate for a non-professional. I would not think this monitor would be the best for image editing, but it is just fine for scrolling through photos.
*The display has built-in speakers with adequate sound quality. I wanted my physical desktop to be streamlined, and having adequate speakers in the monitor avoids extra clutter on my desk.
*The design is quite nice, especially for Dell, which I have found in the past to be far more utilitarian than design-conscious. The bevel is only a few mm; the stand looks and feels like Apple's trademark aluminum; the control buttons are touch-sensitive and unobtrusive; and the adjustability of the screen is simple and fairly elegant.
*There are numerous ports, but all it took to connect my Macbook Pro was the included DP cable.

Cons:
*The resolution is far inferior to 5k models...and seems to me to be less than many 4k displays I have seen, including the Thunderbolt. I bought the monitor knowing the resolution would be slightly less than 4k, but other reviews highlighted better "aspect ratios" etc. so I took a chance. Bottom line is my eyes begin to hurt reading text on this screen for longer than 5-10 minutes. That's a pretty big con, but I like so many other aspects of this monitor that I will enjoy it for general browsing, photos and videos.

I am what some people might consider a gamer, I play games with people, and sometimes those games require precise planning and it can help to have diagrams or charts of what to do at hand.

I told my group that I would scour the internet for the most cursed screen I could find so they could have a second display for raid plans and what not, and that road led me to try this one out. I went in, honestly, expecting the very worst. You will see shortly how this thing managed to get 4 out of 5 stars, despite ALL of the shortcomings.

I was surpized as well.

First, the bad.
The case/stand is... bad, it tends to fall over and collapse under the weight of the screen.
The color accuracy... is bad.
The motion rendering... is bad.
The brightness... is bad.
The pixel response time... is bad.
The viewing angles are loathsome.
The OSD navigation is among the most abhorent I have ever experienced.

I hooked this thing up to my 4080 and tried to play some Cyberpunk 2077 on max whack, and while the quality of the display appropreatre for the game I guess... the experience was far from what one would call ideal.

But now the good.
It is actually capable of 1080p. Something I was absolutely not expecting.
It has the option to output 1920 x 1080p, and it switches to it, and it is... actual 1080p.

It has built in speakers, they are not very loud... nor are they good at some sounds.
Vocals are... clear, very clear. Music and non vocal tones do get distorted. The notification sounds that came through were especially crusty. But for say a podcast, perfectly servicable.

It is Small.
You could toss this in a small back or backpack and not even know it is there.
This thing is smaller than my iPad.

It comes with some pretty nice cables.
Even one with a 90 degree plug that makes cable management less all over the place.

This Sips power.
Just to see how much power it used, I powered it with my battery bank and in 30 mins of gameplay
The bank still showed 100% charge, even with the screen on max brightness.

It has A FULL SIZED HDMI PORT!
On this tiny monitor.
Even my 17 inch external monitor that cost the more than 4 of these combined doesn't have one.

And finally.
It is ONLY 60 bucks.

While gaming and media consumption is well off the table for this panel, what it would excell at is... honestly... the use I intended this for.
Charts or documents, or a chat window.
Heck, even a sensor panel for your PC.

Would I use this as a last resort for anything serious, no.
Would I use this in place of any of the other monitors I have, no.
But, for what it is, this is actually impressive in terms of what it offers for the cost.
I do wish the case was more stable, or just removale so I could glue on a stand.

Will this work for my joke to bestow a bare minimum monitor on my friends so they can have the charts they need to not make the same mistakes a thousand times in a row? Absolutely 100%.

In terms of pracricality, this would get the most razor close B- possible.
But for the funny factor, and because it has won a spot in my idiot heart, it is a B+
