This is a well designed monitor for the price. A lot of monitors in this price range lack features that this monitor has. Here are things I like about it:

- 2 HDMI ports, so I can plug one into my computer and one into my game console

- Monitor wakes up quickly from standby (compared to other monitors I've used)

- No DVI ports (why bother, since DVI doesn't carry audio. Some monitors in this price range have 1 DVI and 1 HDMI, but I think 2 HDMI is better)

- Built in speakers (the sound quality isn't amazing but it is ok given the price)

- Line in port (In case you want to transfer audio from your computer to the monitor via 3.5mm jack)

- Line out port (In case you don't like the built in speakers or want to use headphones)

- Intuitive OSD (on screen display)

- Volume control is easily accessible from the OSD

- Changing picture mode is quite easy from the OSD

- Has VESA mounting holes in case you want to mount it somewhere

- The bezel is quite thin

- Uses a standard power cable without needing any power brick

The only minor issue I have with it is that the standby LED is a bit bright, so you may want to power it off if you are trying to sleep near it. Instead I added a piece of electrical tape to the front of the LED. (See the attached picture) This blocks most of the light. There will still be some indirect light cast downwards but it does not bother me.

This monitor is amazingly good! The frame is so thin and the touch button for adjusting brightness is really responsive and easy to pick up. The display color is tuned so that it has the same display effect as my macbook pro retina screen.

For people who worries about the compatibility of this monitor with macbook, my experience is that it just works when plugging the DP->miniDP cable, which the monitor comes with, to my 15'' Macbook pro retina.

The reason I only give 4/5 rating is that I have just used this monitor for three days. Probably after half year or something, I will update my comment with a new (probably higher) rating.
No dead pixels on the one I received, no noticeable light bleed either.  Happy with it for the price.

Let me start by saying this is the easiest monitor I have ever assembled! When I removed it from the box, I started looking for screws and a screwdriver, if included. But I couldn't find either, this had me a little worried, I was wondering how I was gonna put it together without any hardware. But I soon noticed that on the bottom of the stand itself there was a tabbed thumbscrew. So hey at least I'll get the base together right? Once I got that together I noticed that there was no screw holes on the VESA mount of the base, and that there were tabs on it instead. This stand literally goes together and mounts to the backside of the monitor with no tools at all, just clip it in and go.

So before you plug this in and use it, you quickly notice how great this monitor looks. It is all black with crimson red highlights. It also has a super thin bezel(More on this later). For connections you have a DP, 2 HDMI, and a power socket. Moving to the right you have a multi directional Joystick/power button combo(best thing to have for a monitor).

Pushing the joystick in to turn it on, you're greeted with a green Acer logo, which I find kind of odd, because Acer implements a lot of red in a lot of their products. I digress. First thing you notice if you're a stickler like me, is that even though this monitor as an extremely small physical bezel. There is an actual bezel on the screen, about .25" left, right, and top. Though it's not very noticeable when you are doing things, I do feel it's a shady tactic to claim a thin bezel.

Getting to the menu, there is a lot of options here. You have 8 different modes, 4 of them customizable, 4 of them are predetermined settings. You have 3 gaming and an user mode, those are customizable. Then you have Standard, ECO, Graphics, and HDR, these are predetermined for you. Other settings in the menu are your typical Audio/Visual settings, you have a special gaming menu where you can enable overdrive, freesync, and AimPoint(Puts a reticule in the middle of you screen, useful in first person shooters like CSgo, CoD, Fortnite and others.). In your system menu, you have your input settings, your PiP(Picture in picture), and your Hot key assignments(Lets you change what is in the main menu when you pull it up).

Now, the good stuff and details!

165hz - This is the highest refresh rate I have ever had in a monitor, 144hz was what the monitor had that this replaced. Now as awesome as 165hz sounds, it takes a lot of horsepower to push. Unless you are using a 1080ti or better, you're going to have to choose between refresh rate and quality of graphics. I have a 2700x and a 1070ti, both overclocked and to get close to persistent 165hz I have to play on medium or worse graphics in most AAA games. Which I don't mind because when playing shooters I prefer smooth to pretty.

Response time - Well this is a small short coming of this monitor, at 4ms it's not the best and using the UFO test, there is a bit of ghosting.

Freesync- A feature that enables a sort of buffer for your frame rate, keeping stutters and screen tearing to a minimum or non existence.

PiP(Picture in Picture) - This is a great feature, I'm pretty sure this is mainly for streamers who use a separate stream machine and with this they can put it up on the same monitor. I myself use it to watch Netflix and Youtube while I do these reviews, lol. Not a typical feature found on many monitors these days.

PbP(Picture by Picture) - This feature allows you to split your monitor into different screens, popular for ultrawides but I don't feel that this monitor is wide enough to correctly implement this. Who knows you might find a use for it.

AimPoint - I love to play shooters, doesn't mean I am good at them, and this feature certainly helps. It gives me a large reticule in the middle of my screen allowing me to be aim aware(keeps me from popping around a corner pointed at the ground...). It's about the size of a 50 cent piece in the middle of the screens and through the custom modes can be disabled(enable it in one of the gaming modes and then switch back to one of the predetermined modes when done playing).

HDR 400 - I myself am not a big fan of HDR but in some scenes it does seem to enhance things. This is equipped with a 400 rating which means it's at least 400 nits (about 25% brighter than typical monitors), I find this to be bright enough, and I actually have the brightness turned down.

Height, tilt, and swivel - You can adjust the monitor up and down from 3.5" to 8" high (measured from the bottom of the monitor), the height adjustment is a balanced weight feature that allows you to raise and lower without any buttons or levers, just push it up or down. For tilt front to back, about 12 degrees forward and about 24 degrees back, and finally it swivels at the base, a full 360 if you can get the cords to cooperate.

Built in Speakers - It has 2x 3 watt speakers. Now you're not going to throw a party with these things but they sound great for anything casual, youtube, netflix, maybe some movies depending on how into them you want to get. At 100% volume, monitor and pc, there is no distortion at all.

Joystick/Power button - This is probably my least favorite thing about the monitor. Though the joystick method is preferred over other methods, this joystick is a little loose in its movements and is too hard to click in my opinion. Not a deal breaker but something that would be nice to be updated in the next model.

Overall at around $300, this monitor is a beast. Just feel sorry for my previous monitor, it was great, but this is better. If you're looking to take your gaming to another level, I highly recommend this monitor.

Thank you for taking the time to read this review. I always do my best to leave honest and detailed reviews. If this review helped you, feel free to click that helpful button, it goes a long ways to help us reviewers bring you reviews of the things you want to see.

If you are able to get this monitor for around $330, and you have a CPU and graphics card that can push above 144 frames in most of the game you play, I would wholeheartedly recommend this model to anyone.

ELMB really is the killer app of this monitor, as even in games where you aren't getting much above 100 frames you can instantly tell the difference as if your frame rate is higher than it really is, it really is like it totally eliminates motion blur! I recommend turning this setting off when you aren't playing video games that can push over 75 frames though as I (YMMV) perceive a strobing effect in anything I am not focusing my eyes on.

One thing to look out for is the finish is extremely easy to nick, I was replacing my keyboards the day I bought it and put a very small nick in the finish of the display. I have tapped my monitors with a large number of different foreign objects and have never damaged a monitor before, so I assume it has something to do with the finish on the display that makes it prone to cosmetic damage.

I got a E1659Fwu for use with my Sony VAIO (running Win 8 64-bit)while traveling. I am glad to have a light monitor like this with low cabling needs. I had a couple of problems with the display that made it impractical to use (see below) and, more importantly, I got zero response from the vendor (AOC)about the problems. Ultimately, I returned the AOC in favor of a similar product from Toshiba, which is working flawlessly with my configuration.

Two problems
1) THe unit seems to work properly only when plugged into the USB 2.0 port. When I plug it into the USB 3.0 port (even with the extra USB power connector plugged in) the VAIO screen and the AOC screen flash a lot then settle down, but flash again when I move the mouse. So, it works for me on USB 2.0 but not USB 3.0. Still, odd that a device that would benefir from USB 3.0 won't work under 3.0? I have asked DisplayLink and AOC about this; so far no response.

2) The native resolution on the VAIO is 1920X1080. The native res on the AOC display is 1366X768. When I try to reduce the VAIO res to 1366X768 (with the AOC display attached) , the size of the desktop image on the VAIO shrinks with a large black border all around (not a desired result) . When I remove (unplug) the AOC display, and change the VAIO res to 1366X768, the VAIO display res changes and the image fits the entire screen. This is a problem because when I try to use the AOC as an extension, the image sizes don't match and this makes using the AOC for things like looking at a wide spreadsheet (spread across both the AOC and the VIAO screens) unwieldy. Sounds like a DisplayLink software limitation. I have asked DisplayLink and AOC about this; so far no response.

My old Sony 19" G520 CRT monitor finally went and it was time to either upgrade the computer or buy a new flat screen and hope it works with my older video card running Windows 2000. (I don't like to throw anything away if it works or can be fixed!) I went down to the local Costco and compared the two Samsung's 23" and 27" and decided on this one (27"). I am generally very pleased with my decision.

During the install, the system was able to automatically find the monitor driver using Windows Update. I also went ahead and installed the MagicTune software that was included on the DVD. Everything worked out the box. Afterwards I searched the web and updated the video card driver, monitor driver and the included MagicTune software which lets you control your monitor from your desktop including what's on the menu control buttons. You may need to update your video card driver to access all the features of MagicTune.

If you just slowly drag your finger across the touch sensitive buttons it seems to work quite well. Standby mode is controlled by Windows.

The Good:
1. Light and easy to set up.
2. Works well with my system. I'm running 1920x1080@60HZ and love the extra screen space.
3. The price is good. You should be able to get it for under $300.
4. The display is vibrant with good colors and brightness. Online videos show well.
5. I appreciate the low energy use of the monitor.

The OK:
1. Fonts do show pixelation. Mine is on my large desk so I'm not right up against the monitor so it's not a problem. If I bought the 23" I think fonts would show better but I like the larger size for videos and graphics (resolution is the same).

The Bad:
1. Documentation/MagicTune messages are poor. Going into the help menu's for some of the more advanced features is worthless. It's clear that it was written hurriedly by someone for which English is a second language.

Let me start out by stating what everyone else seems to be saying is wrong with this monitor. It is just too bright when you first get it and the colors look faded. I purchased this monitor so I could run a dual monitor setup with it being my primary. My first task was to make the blacks, black, the colors look real, and the light colors not look white. NOTE: Do not install magictune from Samsung website! If your video card is not supported it will not run and when you uninstall windows will not boot. My best advice is start at your nvidia control panel or catalysis and correct it until you think it looks pretty good. Then use the monitors own setup to make it look better. Then head back into control panel and finish it all up. Took me about 3 days to really get this thing looking good.

However, once you get past the initial setup this monitor really shines! It looks amazing. I get better color depth and response time than I did with my old LCD screen. I had to adjust to playing fps without the ghosting as I was so used to seeing triple image. Blu-ray movies look wonderful on this screen and I have found myself abandoning the 61" in my Living Room just to watch it in better color depth on my PC. Also, text is very easy to read on this monitor. I had considered a 27" but passed because I had seen a few reviews complaining about reading text. Actually I was at a LAN party last week and noticed this first hand. The guy beside me had a 27" and kept having to zoom to read.

So in summary, a great buy for the price. You WILL be adjusting this screen when you first get it, but the work bears rewards.

This is an "older" new monitor, being sold since 2016 and still on the market. It's got a 4k UHD display and comes in at a rather low price point for a 28" monitor with these specs. So you would expect some compromises.

However, I didn't find a lot of them. The display is clear, crisp, and bright .... so much so that I had to turn the brightness down to 18%. I had read other complaints about backlight leakage and the blacks not being deep enough, but I haven't seen anything of the kind. No calibration was necessary on my sample; it was plug and play, make a couple of minor adjustments, and that was all. There are no noticeable artifacts and the colors are vibrant and realistic.

UPDATE: After a little while I'm noticing, at least at my settings, that the display is darker at the top and gradually increases to brighter at the bottom. You don't notice this so much except on a screen with a black background. I do a lot of full-screen text work with green text on a pure black background, and I can see this effect clearly; the green grows in brightness as you go down the screen. This could be my settings or it could be the monitor (more likely the monitor). If I learn more I'll report back.

I'm using it with an Nvidia 760 GTX video card, not especially high end, with HDMI output (although the monitor accepts DP, I don't have that available). The monitor is good enough to make me consider upgrading my video card by a couple of notches.

I'm not a gamer, but movies look terrific (with good source material, of course). And of course with 3840x2160 resolution there's a lot you can get on the screen if you're doing work, especially graphics. (I find that resolution a little too much for my aging eyes but it's fabulous.)

The compromises? Mostly in physical design. You can't adjust the monitor height; there's one position on the stand and that's all, although you can tilt and rotate the monitor, at least somewhat. The plastic casing just doesn't feel durable to me, although you should be fine with reasonable care (i.e. don't drop the monitor or hit the casing).

I don't see any way to hang the monitor, either, although that may be possible by some means I didn't discover.

Still there's a lot here for the relatively modest price. I honestly think the display comes close to the higher priced units. I give four stars, with one star off for the design limitations mentioned above.

The Dell S2522HG is a good, affordable 240 Hz gaming monitor. I got two of these on sale for playing fast paced shooters like CS:GO, Left4Dead 2, Doom Eternal, Rainbow 6 Siege, etc. I am very sensitive to motion blur and stuttering, and the 240 Hz refresh makes for a very smooth gaming experience if you have the graphics horsepower to push a lot of frames per second. I can tell the difference between 240 Hz and 165 Hz, and I don't know if 240 Hz makes me a better gamer, but it certainly makes motion much smoother for me and I can play for longer without getting headaches now. I would not recommend this for productivity applications, only for fast paced gaming.

As long as one has realistic expectations for this budget gaming monitor, I think most folks will be happy with it for playing games. I have better, more expensive monitors, but none with a true 240 Hz refresh rate that can produce such smooth motion. So, with everything in mind, I can recommend this monitor for those wanting to play games at really high frame rates without breaking the bank.

Pros:
+ 240 Hz refresh rate and 1 ms GTG pixel response time
+ Exceptional video and motion clarity
+ IPS panel with good colors and viewing angles
+ Reasonable panel uniformity for this class
+ Solid stand with rotate, tilt, pivot, and z-height adjustment
+ AMD FreeSync/nVidia Gsync compatible
+ Performance per dollar

Cons:
+ Only 1920x1080 and 24.5" screen (my 27" QHD 2560x1440 S2721DGF has a better picture)
+ Pixel pitch is a bit high, so some text doesn't look very good/clear
+ Contrast ratio is probably only about 900:1
+ Peak brightness may be shy of 400 cd/m^2
+ Matte anti-glare screen dulls images and mutes colors
Love this product

The white flashing is the monitor doing all the work to prevent screen tearing. So use your card for this feature instead. Picture is amazing and colors look true and clear. So far I have only been using the monitor for a day however I can say it's about what I was expecting. I purchased this monitor originally because I will be heading off to university soon and as someone who always needs more screen real estate I wanted a lightweight monitor that I can throw in my backpack. To my knowlegde, the display does appear to be more QHD panel (although the image appears to look best at 2048x1152). The build quality of the monitor is pretty good and it doesn't feel cheap although I'm a little uncomfortable with the stand that comes with the monitor. To make up for it though the monitor has Vesa mounting holes (and includes screws) so a cheap portable Vesa mount is a nice future addon you can pick up for some extra peace and mind.

One of the first things I noticed when using the monitor was that while the pixels themselves appear to be tiny and sharp the image looks blury. After some playing around with the settings I found that to fix this you have to change the sharpness setting in the monitor from 2 to 4 which leaves you with a much sharper image.

The monitor claims to be HDR however windows doesn't identify it as an HDR display and it appears that they use some trickery in the monitors settings to make it "HDR". I don't believe that this monitor is actually a true HDR display although it is possible that I am wrong. That being said I don't recommend using the monitor in HDR mode, the picture looks fine as is and this monitors attempt at "HDR" ruins it. I'm sure spending enough time fiddling through the settings and adjusting things might make for a slightly better "HDR" experience, but to me their "HDR" appears to just be a pre-configured Contrast setting. That being said the "HDR" that comes with this monitor makes me question if this monitor supports freesync like it says in the settings.

One feature that I absolutely love about this monitor is display and power over USB-C, as long as your computer supports it you only need one cable to use the monitor. USB-C is enough to transfer display information as well as power to power the display. Mixing this with its small size this monitor is great acting as a tablet being able to have your laptop off to the side while you have the monitor plugged in with headphones attached to the monitor makes this thing great for watching movies and youtube videos in bed. This brings me to the only real bummer I have about this display, originally I had no interest in wanting touch-screen capabilites however given how nice the image is and how great it feels to use this monitor almost as a tablet in bed makes me wish it had a touchscreen so I don't have to reach over to the laptop to change things.

TLDR:
-Great monitor for content consumption
-Great monitor for working on the go
-Acceptable monitor for gaming (as long as it's casual)
-So/So for graphic designers, fine as a secondary display where color accuracy isn't vital
-Highly recommended for a College student dorming (especially considering its versatility)

Pros:
-cost
-size
-versatility
-resolution
-usb c power and display
-build quality
-VESA mount hardware

cons:
-sketchy included mount
-"HDR" isn't really HDR
-doesn't come with carrying case

Conclusion: Great portable monitor given some time playing with the settings. If you buy this monitor I recommend a cheap portable Vesa Mount and a carrying case. Highly recommended given the cost.

If you are a normal computer user, or even gamer, this is easily 5 stars based on the value for the price. Sure there are models that look a little better, but you pay substantially for it. Despite the price, this is an impressive looking monitor. I purchased it for use on my workbench (software and hardware testing) For that use, it has 2 major flaws.

First, it is designed to "auto" sense which input to select. But it does this by cable connection, not by actual input. So if you have DVI, VGA, and HDMI cables connected to the monitor itself, even if only one of them is actually connected to something, the monitor can't figure out which to use. It isn't a big deal, as pressing the "enter" button cycles through them all manually.

The second, and more pressing issue is low resolution. The monitor looks great at 1920x1080, and is usable at several lower resolutions. But at 800x600 or less (most BIOS screens), the screen is almost impossible to read. Navigating bios screens would be impossible if you didn't already know what the text was. I knocked off 1 star for this issue, but since playing in the bios isn't something most people would do, this is easily a 5 star product. (at this price)

I was looking for an IPS monitor to use when editing photos and video. I originally considered a wide-gamut monitor but was unable to find any that didn't seem to have color issues. Most wide-gamut monitors in my price range had repeated reviews where users had to return the monitor because of color imbalance, banding, or tinting. I stumbled upon this monitor at amazon and the reviews were favorable. There wasn't a whole lot of information or reviews available as a whole because the monitor was so new. I ordered it knowing that amazon's favorable return policy would be used if the monitor was below par. So far I am happy. Obviously the 23-inch size makes it a little smaller than the 24-inch monitors offered by the other manufacturers. It seems to be built well and I've had no issues. I calibrated it with a Spyder 3 Express. For what it's worth, I calibrated three different monitors: my MB Pro, an older Samsung, and the NEC. The Samsung needed the largest correction. The MB Pro had a pretty significant correction as well. The NEC was the best of the three right out of the box. In fact, the before and after photos were not drastically different (as the other two monitors were). So, even if you do not calibrate the monitor, it shouldn't be too far off right out of the box. The only cable that is not included is the newer display port used on the new macbooks. Everything else was included. This monitor fills the needs I had, which was non-professional photo and video editing. Eventually I would like to pick up a wide-gamut monitor but I am waiting until the kinks are worked out of the affordable versions before I buy.

#1 the monitor is cheap and a good value. I bought 4 of these for a wall mount system. They perform as expected, nothing more, nothing less.
#2 Viewing angle is important. With my wall mount office setup, the top monitors are canted quite a lot just so they are viewable. Not really a big deal, but with a multi-panel display, viewing angle is important.
#3 Whoever designed the power adaptor needs to be hung. You cannot plug 2 into a wall socket, as they interfere with each other. In fact, I have to use two long power strips just to plug in 4 of these. It's an absolutely stupid design.
#4 I wanted to flush mount these on the wall, however there is a bracket on the back for the supplied monitor stand that seems impossible to remove. I googled it, watched videos, and nearly destroyed one monitor trying to remove it. Again, poor design on this part of the monitor.
#5 The HDMI port projects perpendicular to the monitor plane, so if you are flush wall mounting, you will need to buy a 90 degree HDMI adaptor or you will have to mount the monitors a few inches from the wall surface. Again, a little thought in the design process would have been wonderful.

I like the design but I'm disappointed at the screen performance. The IPS panel choice was not a good idea here. The purpose of use is intended for direct viewing. IPS panels have bad contrast compared to VA panels which are better than TN. Acer should have went with the VA panel to improve the single viewer experience vs the wider angle viewing. Now, IPS color performance is best but not so much that it trumps the contrast issue for gamers like me. This is after all a gamers monitor, so designers could go a different route for an IPS had this been VA. But since it is IPS let me just say that you will notice pixel glare on each side during dark scenes and content which can steal from immersion. This monitor with its curve and wide view ratio is intended for immersion and so -1 star there. My other gripe is that my unit has light bleed in the upper corners and a Display Port that drops the signal due to some internal loose connection. Now come on Acer, a $1300 monitor is like buying a Bugatti of PC displays, there should be no issues...I mean none... all units should be thoroughly QC tested and only passing units should ship, so for this -1 star. Now lastly, and I don't know if this is just my unit with the issue, the monitor will not OC beyond 75hz. I've tried over and over to get it to anything over 75 and all I get is a blank screen. So for that Acer.... -1 star there.
Games I did play like Project Cars and Elite Dangerous where silky smooth at 75hz and the color production was on point out of the box. But I just cant warrant keeping this thing when I dropped $1.3k and having this many issues.

[[VIDEOID:3e5df2a04b05cc37e4c1ce20a12fc051]] Let’s start with the main thing: the display. The display itself is amazing. I’m using it as a secondary monitor for work purposes connected to my MacBook Pro M1 and it just handles it no problem straight out of the box. Because the MacBook has thunderbolt and usb-c ports, I can use just a single cable to both display and power the portable monitor. The monitor resolution as well as text scaling can be easily adjusted in MacOS as this monitor just behaves as any normal external monitor. The colors are vibrant and the screen brightness is plenty good for indoor use. I can turn the brightness to maximum (did not test for nits) but easily bright enough to see on a sunny day indoors. Your mileage may vary if you’re directly outdoors. Note that if you connect this device via USB-C via phone, the phone’s output most likely will NOT have enough wattage to set the brightness at levels past 20%, so if this is your use-case, I’d recommend connecting an external power source to the monitor.

Since the display panel itself uses IPS, the colors are great and the biggest bonus is that the viewing angles are fantastic, it’s something like 178 degrees so you can definitely view your content from almost any angle without any color degradation.

The touchscreen was plug and play via USB-C to my Windows device. Touchscreen works as you’d think, it is a 10 point multitouch. All of this is handled by Windows drivers anyway so any problems with that are on the driver manufacturer and not UPERFECT. I did not try the touchscreen function with MacOS as that requires downloading an additional driver, but interestingly straight out the box (without the additional drivers) the monitor seems to work as a trackpad where touching the screen will control the mouse on the main MacOS monitor.

The monitor came with a ton of accessories, UPERFECT includes all the necessary cables and power supplies to get the monitor hooked up to a variety of sources, so kudos to them for doing that. The only downside to this is that the cables themselves are kinda short, but that’s mostly fine because your devices will likely be close to the monitor anyway.

Talking about the build quality, the quality is great. It is mostly plastic, with rubber at the pull out feet for keeping the monitor stable. Though it's plastic, it still feels very solid. The front of the monitor is one piece glass, and the bezels are great for the monitor, with the side and top bezels being small while the chin has the largest bezel.

The stand is interesting because this monitor being touchscreen allows you to have it either in portrait or landscape. In landscape mode, the hinge is super strong and you can use it in a few angles. If you want to make it portrait, you just set it via software and then you can turn the entire hinge 90 degrees. There is a push button latch that releases an extending leg that allows you to prop up the monitor. I only tested the touchscreen briefly and generally speaking the monitor is decently stable, although it depends on how hard you’re pressing the screen and where on the screen you’re pressing (top half vs bottom half). Also it depends on the type of surface the monitor is resting on.

Overall I’m very happy with the monitor so far and have used it every single day without any issues! Would highly recommend. The only way it could be even better is if it came with a VESA mount so I can mount it vertically next to my workstation!

I bought this monitor 2 months ago (2-Jun-2006) and here is my experience so far:

Pros:

* Excellent reactivity. Absolutely no problems with even the "fast" games such as Call of Duty 2, Need for Speed Underground 2, Far Cry... This MVA monitor is much faster in practice then PVA models such as 6ms-declared Samsung 970p.

* Excellent ergonomics: tilt, swivel, pivot, height. DVI and VGA input. USB hub.

* Good colors, although not the best I've ever seen (Samsung 970p has better colors, but is much slower and with lower resolution).

* Well differentiated near-black shades - much better then any CRT. Good for Doom 3 and such.

* Effective and easy to use naked-eye color calibration software (PerfectSuite). I was able to make colors visibly more natural and they seem much "deeper" to me now. The real hardware calibrator would yield better results still, but I wonder by how much...

* Good picture quality even in low brightness / low contrast conditions. If you are professional, you won't burn you eyes on over-bright picture.

* Excellent scaling. I have no problem playing games at non-native resolutions.

* Very good analog VGA picture quality (though I recommend DVI if possible).

* I personally like its "no frills, down to business" OSD.

* Non-intrusive resolution change notification.

* Good video quality, though this monitor is so precise you will easily be able to see compression artifacts on DVDs.

Cons:

* Grainy anti-reflexive surface - white is not completely "smooth", but appears as if screen surface is slightly dirty (as if covered with dust). This can be quite distracting in every-day work such as office applications and programming.

* Ideal viewing angles are very narrow. Even slight movement (maybe 15 degrees) away of ideal "view axis" will cause visible deterioration of color quality. With a relatively large monitor like this one, a net effect is that you'll have slightly dimmed colors in the corners even if center of the screen is dead ahead. It is still much better then TN, but visibly worse then IPS (such as older ViewSonic VP201b).

* Black, although pretty good, is not completely black and aforementioned viewing angle effect is especially visible in black corners.

* No bad pixels on arrival, but one always-off sub-pixel developed during 2 months of usage. Not noticeable unless you look for it though!

* Ergonomics: Screen cannot be lowered all the way to its stand - bad if you working desk has heightened platform for monitor.

* PerfectSuite software sometimes fails to establish communication with the monitor - this turns off color calibration until you "restart" (turn-off then turn-on) the monitor and restart the PerfectSuite.

* Price (700 EUR in Serbia).

serious connectivity limitation due to the lack of HDMI input.
While the screen quality is excellent, this monitor represents a real challenge if you want to connect two computers at the same time: inputs available are VGA / DVI / Displayport. With Displayport being bulky and fading out, most now-a-day computers use HDMI so there's no way to avoid a conversion here: HDMI-to-DVI is cheap and widely available but HDMI-to-Displayport requires an active signal conversion and adapter are limited in availability and are not cheap (~$30).
If you buy this monitor, be prepared to spend at least $30 for a signal converter to utilize the Displayport-input

I recently bought this monitor due to the amount of positive reviews and I was surprised just how bad this monitor is. When it arrived, the first thing I noticed was one of the HDMI ports was DOA. I could have easily gotten a replacement monitor with a live port if I liked the monitor at all, however there was much more wrong with the monitor.

The next thing I noticed was there was no DVI port as advertised:
"Inputs of D-Sub, DVI, 2xHDMI and DisplayPort and Built In 2W X 2 Speaker"
It had D-Sub, 2xHDMI, and DisplayPort, but no DVI. I wanted to use the DVI so that was another large negative for me.

Next, the color of the monitor was terrible out of the box. I spent 30 minutes messing with the horrible controls and menus and couldn't even get the color close to as good as my 7 year old Hanns-G monitor.

Finally, the monitor does not auto-detect signal inputs. So if I am using the monitor on HDMI 1, I turn off the appliance connected to HDMI 1, and then turn on the appliance connected to HDMI 2, the monitor will not auto-switch to the new signal. You will have to manually switch the input. I can't remember the last time I used a monitor that could not auto-detect signals. Furthermore, how do you put so many ports on the monitor if the monitor can't tell which inputs are on or off.

All and all, a pretty poor monitor that is very overrated. I returned it and ended up spending $30 less to get an Acer G277HL Abid (https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B00VX3KAAS/ref=oh_aui_detailpage_o02_s00) and I'm very happy with it.

To start off, I did not buy this from Amazon. As it happened, the local retailer had an after Christmas sale that was actually ten bucks cheaper than the Amazon price, and Amazon had a waiting period before shipping, so I bought locally. Therefore, I can't speak to shipping or service here on line. I decided to write a review anyway because I spent a lot of time on Amazon researching; this is my way of paying back all the folks who reviewed those monitors I looked at. I decided on this model because it was inexpensive, had a 2ms speed rating, looked good in the store, and has both DVI and HDMI. I will use the HDMI on my Dell convertible laptop, while the DVI will be useful when I connect to my desktop. Many of the budget models (even from LG) have only VGA or HDMI (or both).

The monitor is very good right out of the box. My unit has no bad pixels at all. The default settings are warm, but there are huge amounts of settings available. The product does not have multiple buttons under the right side as shown in the product pictures; it uses a single joystick below the center "LG" logo. This actually wasn't that hard to use. I found that reader 2 mode is the only one of the special presets that worked for me, but I initially went with the default settings, which has well balanced colors. I did eventually decide to turn the brightness down to about 60, but made no other picture adjustments. There are enough settings here that you will have no problem getting it just right for your viewing preferences. The monitor does tilt, although it takes a deliberate effort. There are a couple of options to draw less power while in use, which I thought was nice (I set mine to low and had no issues). Another thing I liked (because it is a pet peeve) is that they have an option to turn the illuminated power button on or off (defaults to off). I hate having a bright button staring at me all the time.

Videos look very good on this monitor; several people were watching with me, and they had no problems seeing it from about 60 degrees to the side. You should note that there are no speakers, even when using HDMI (which carries sound). I discovered that when using HDMI, it also shuts off my internal PC speakers in Windows 10, so I had to go out and get a cheap pair of speakers. I'm testing this on a Dell XPS12, with Intel Graphics card that goes to a DisplayPort, then via a converter cable to HDMI on the monitor. I suspect it would look even better with a higher end card.

I have not noticed any of ghosting, although I'm not a gamer. But Microsoft applications look great. They supply software that allows you to split your screen into many different segments (it isn't built into the monitor controls). The 2 sided balanced setting works great for having two Office applications side by side. You can divide it into a lot more splits, but I don't see that as being very useful on a 24 inch monitor.

There are a couple of things I'd prefer to change. The power brick is fairly small, but the cord is very thin and only six feet. That sounds like a lot, but I ended up buying a short extension cord. The power cord is three pronged, which seems excessive to me for a monitor.

Summary: A very good monitor at a nice price. Highly recommended.

As a monitor, I love it. Nice, sharp picture, good true color, crisp text - no problem reading even with my aging eyes. I'm an online seller and do a lot of photo editing, and it's great. Haven't had time to game with it yet, but not anticipating any problems there.

The reason it doesn't get 5 stars is that when we first tried to set it up with the included HDMI cable, neither the computer nor the monitor recognized that there was a device at the other end of the cable. We wasted a lot of time trying to find a workaround, and eventually husband had to go out and buy a display port cable, which works great - since cables aren't expensive and computers vary, why not include both types of cables with the monitor?

There's also the jog button, which I'll admit is a little bit easier to adjust monitor settings than the way my old HP monitor handled it... except for the power switch. Having to reach around to the back for the power switch is inconvenient. Power switches should always be easily accessible and separate from other controls. To do otherwise is the sort of design thinking that prioritizes appearance over usability. And while you can turn the monitor OFF using the jog button, it doesn't seem to work to turn it back ON.

The setup instructions weren't terribly clear either. Tiny pictures and type that requires a magnifying glass to read. I don't really care for the graphic-only type of instruction; like most older people, I prefer words. But at least SOME vendors make their graphic instructions large and clear enough to actually see! Please remember that not all your customers are millenials.

So it's really only the quality of the display that gets this monitor its fourth star: otherwise the inconveniences would only rate it 3.

I purchased this item with some hesitation- I was weary of the Westinghouse brand compared to more established brands such as Sony and Panasonic. However, given that this model was almost half the price of the competition, I decided to give it a try. I have since compared this unit to more expensive models, and while there is a difference between the Westinghouse and a Sony Bravia, I don't personally feel the slight difference in picture quality justifies the large difference in price.

If you want the finest LCD on the market and money is no object, then this is not the LCD for you. If you want a very good LCD at a reasonable price then this is worth a look. I would also add that the connectivity on this unit is outstanding, superior to every other LCD currently on the market- DVI, HDCP, VGA, S-Video, and Component connections abound. I gave my purchase 5 stars primarily on the value factor- there are better LCDs on the market, but not at anywhere near the price. Basically it's a very good LCD at an excellent price.

To some the lack of a tuner and a cheap remote control is a problem, and I'm sure in some cases it would be. However, in the setup I have I do not use the remote nor need an integrated tuner, so these factors did not affect my decision. In my setup I have a media center PC hooked into the DVI input, and never change the source, so a lack of discreet remote codes is not an issue. I also have no need for a tuner as the media center PC handles all tuner duties, so the lack of a tuner is likewise not a problem. Consider how your entertainment system is configured before making a decision on the purchase of this item- it is after all a monitor, not a TV. I expect the majority of A/V installations today have little need for an integrated tuner, as most people have direct tv or cable.

The only negative aspect to this LCD is the somewhat limited picture adjustments through the OSD menu. It takes some work to bring the screen up to its potential, but it is well worth the time to do so. I spent approximately 30-45 minutes fine-tuning the video source on the PC and the video controls on the TV and the picture quality is noticeably better. Unfortunately there are limits to what you can adjust on this model, so ultimately there will be some compromises.

If you have standard definition TV, be warned that the high resolution of this screen will show you just how bad SDTV signals are. Some are worse than others, but overall SDTV simply doesn't do this set justice. Even many digital or even "HDTV" signals at 720i don't have the resolution to highlight this screen's capabilities. You'll run into this problem with any HDTV, but the 1080p resolution shows it more than a 720p set. I still recommend buying 1080p; once you see a full 1080p movie on this LCD you will never want to watch anything else again.

Finally, the PC input is outstanding on this unit. If you have a PC hooked into your entertainment system, you will be pleased with this item. The resolution is amazing, but be sure your video card can drive it at full 1080p for maximum effect! I do not have a dedicated gaming system such as an xBox or PS3 hooked up to it, but from what I have seen of PC based games on my system I would say this is an excellent gaming screen. The high resolution and large screen size bring the games alive. The screen is large enough to serve as a computer monitor from across the room with a wireless keyboard and mouse. If you use Windows Media Center, I highly recommend this unit.

I bought two of these monitors to have a large dual montor setup on a single computer. I've since bought a new PC, so I now run each monitor on their own workstation. I paid $200 each for two of these (and a bit more for a third because I was in a hurry and missed the sale price) and I'm very pleased at that price point. It's a struggle to find good 1440p (2560x1440) IPS with good pixel density and refresh rates, decent color gamut, etc., and DisplayPort/HDMI (in addition to D-Sub and DVI) connectivity, combined with the extra small bezel these displays provide -- my main point of focus for use as text-primary, multiple ancillary displays.

(as an aside, if your video card supports it, and you use third-party tools, you can overdrive these displays all the way to 4K resolutions, and they don't look too terrible, if image density is your goal; but the native mx of 2560x1440 is quite lovely and is compatible with Mac OS HiDPI settings -- if your card supports it. I'm using these on a 2008 Mac Pro with nVidia 960 GTX running at 1440p HiDPI via DisplayPort @60Hz)

The image is bright and crisp, excellent for text, and black reproduction is quite good for general image display and use (I would not rely on this display for image and print work, but its fine for a casual user). My complaint is the usual issue with lower-cost IPS displays, in that there is considerable light leakage around the edges and especially corners when in black/dark/sleep mode, making these a bad choice for color and print work. It's just something that only spending three times as much can mitigate, and generally at the cost of a thick, bulky bezel, which is an issue with multiple displays.

I'd say these are generally "okay" for Web and Netflix, and useable for games, but, again, the typical IPS light leaks at the edges will spoil your deep and true blacks; if you don't have high standards, or are simply on a budget, this will likely not bother you, anyway.

If you are a modern Mac owner, these will look okay next to your Retina-class and 4K/5K displays, with acceptable brightness and clarity, but you will absolutely notice the difference; again, good for ancillary text and general use; not image and color purity.

The on screen controls are annoying, like most displays, but generally make sense. You pretty much have to turn off all the garbage image "enhancements" AOC has on by default, increase the time the display is on screen so you can evaluate changes without starting over, and spend some time (after a few hours of "burn in" time) calibrating them for your environment, but that's true with every display on the market, especially for Mac owners, and most especially for those who give a crap about their eyes. It's tedious, but anyone can do it without paying an expert.

The OSD is also tricky to unlock; do a YouTube search for a quick video explaining how; or just read the manual carefully.

They turn on and off very quickly, and seem to require virtually no warm up time to settle the color profile.

One last niggle, the power on LED is a DISTRACTING BRIGHT BLUE, and the Sleep LED is BRIGHT RED (rather than traditional muted/dim amber), so I had to apply gaffers tape, spoiling the otherwise attractive chin. However, the chin itself is a polished aluminum, and I will be applying black vinyl tape over the whole chin and LED, anyway, to reduce glare and color-muxing as these displays reside over my LG 4K displays used for image work.If you need to see the LED state, you may wish to use a mylar filter to reduce the brightness.

Bottom Line: Don't pay more than $200-$225, use them primarily for text/web, and these are a great display; paying anything near $300 for these is close to criminal.

Bought this to be my 2nd monitor when I work from home. I needed a monitor but didn't want to a "permanent" set up with a traditional monitor since I'm working at our kitchen table. The same table that we use for family meals.

Initially I had some issues with my work laptop not recognizing the USB. Turns out my job disabled our USB ports for security purposes. The type C port is strictly for charging and the ancient laptop didn't have an HDMI port. So I spent a few frustrating days unable to use it for the purpose I bought it for. In the meantime I hooked it up to my phone, tablet and personal laptop with no problems. The plug and play worked mostly seamlessly. I did have a minor issue when I plugged it into my phone Samsung Galaxy S9 phone, as it tried to run some samsung DEX screen mirroring program (or something). Whatever that program was, it made me realize that the monitor isn't a touch screen and I kind of wish it was. Although for less than $200, I really shouldn't complain. I closed that app and it mirrored my phone screen just fine. I finally bought a $10 display port to HDMI adapter and was able to use it with my work laptop. Now that I've been using it consistently for about a week, I have the following pros and cons.
Pros:
-Large screen size (bigger than both my laptops)
- plug and plays with anything (even if u need an adapter to skirt overly secure and antiquated work systems)
- good accurate colors
-great price
-comes with a cover and screen protector
-fits in my laptop bag

Cons
-not a touchscreen (in case like me you hadn't considered that, but again for the price would you really expect it to be?)
- the colors are a little dull, still accurate but not as vibrant as my TV. I don't have an issue with it. If you're gaming or streaming Avatar and want super vibrant colors this might be an issue, but for spreadsheets, and powerpoints I don't really care.
- the sound isn't great, and for some reason my work laptop defaults the sound to it. It's an easy fix, but still annoying, when I forget and suddenly my conference calls are coming out if the monitors tin-y sounding speakers.
- No battery storage, it needs to be plugged in to work. (This is probably my biggest gripe)
- theres allegedly a menu that I can use to adjust the brightness, but I haven't found it. The toggle button on the side doesnt seem to do much. But again for my purposes this isnt a big deal.

Overall I'm pretty happy with this purchase. If you're working from home and need a 2nd monitor that you can easily put away, I would strongly recommend this one.

I can't wait until a Thunderbolt 3 KVM monitor switch/docking station comes out. They only have Thunderbolt 2 KVM at the moment, but soon it will be available for the latest 40gbps spec.

Overall, if you're not using a USB-C docking station, this is a great monitor. Picture quality is good, the bezel is thin, and it does 1920x1080 over HDMI without any issues. I've also used it with HDMI to DisplayPort cables and HDMI to USB-C cables without any issues. Probably not a great gaming monitor but for the price it can't be beat.

One big exception. If you're using any type of USB-C docking station, including Thunderbolt ones, they will randomly have issues with displaying full 1920x1080 resoution. They have a tendency to get stuck at 640x480 resolution due to the transfer of signal across HDMI through a dock and to a laptop via USB-C. Something with the way they aren't fully compatible with DP over USB-C makes them not work well with docking stations. I've used them with HP Elitebook 850 G4 in combo with HP Universal Docking Stations, and with Dell Latitude and Precision laptops with the Dell D6000, Thunderbolt TB16, and W15 docking stations and it has issues with all of them. This is a great monitor for anyone that in need of a second display to work from home, or for any student looking to improve their productivity while doing homework. I bought this monitor for those exact purposes; so far, I have found this monitor to have excellent build quality, superb display capabilities, and connectivity that is very user friendly.

Build Quality

This monitor has excellent build quality. From the stand to the monitor itself, it looks and feels like high quality materials were very important to the team that designed it. Assembly required no hardware, and consisted of putting the (two piece) stand together and attaching that to the display. The stand is not height adjustable, but that isn’t really a problem because it already puts the monitor at a comfortable viewing height. Even though the stand design is very simplistic you have no need to worry about the display falling or tipping over. This monitor was definitely built to last.

Display

As stated in the product overview this monitor has a 1920x1080 resolution, which is by no means stunning, but it is still capable of displaying pictures and video in HD. What is really cool about this monitor is what HP calls, IPS technology. Basically, the technology enables the display to have stunning 178° viewing angle while still being able to view high quality colors. That way you can show a group of people what you’re doing on your screen, in high quality, without them having to breathe down your neck. One thing missing from this monitor are integrated speakers, but at this price point you aren’t going to find many, if any at all, that do. Plus, if you are buying this monitor for the same purpose I did you should already have integrated speakers in your laptop.

Connectivity

Connectivity and power for this monitor are very simple and versatile. The power cord is standard to that of most electronic devices, and plugs into the back of the monitor. This monitor has the capability to connect to other displays with either VGA or HDMI. It comes with a VGA cable, but my laptop only has HDMI capabilities. Luckily you can pick up and HDMI Cable for about five to ten dollars at a place like Walmart or Target. Plus, if you’re like me now you have an extra VGA cable to carry around if the time ever comes that you may need one.
Conclusion

For the price, this monitor has to be one of the best deals you can find out there. With easy assembly, excellent build quality, a cutting-edge display, and versatile connectivity, there really isn’t much more you can ask for. I hope you found this review helpful in making your decision for purchasing a new monitor.

I found that the "cool" setting works best for keeping things looking natural, but the colors arent as deep as youd think. On the other modes, blacks have sephia tones to them. I'll have to tweak with the settings more, but the defaults really are odd.

The stand of the monitor is plastic which i've seen complaints of, but its actually very strong and sturdy, and you can tell theres metal inside of it. The stand is more than strong enough for this monitor.

Over all, this is a "good" monitor. It isn't anything special, but it does have a nice design, it's fairly cheap, and it looks alright for day to day things.

It being a 2021 screen I didn't think it was bad. So I took it apart 4 screws on bottom then took a pocket knife and worked it around the edge to pop the back panel off. There is a ribbon connected to buttons on bottom that is also sticky taped down careful at first then peel it off is what I did to give it extra slack. Next I peeled the tape of the main ribbon to  unhook it. Then there is 4 black screw holding the power supply, HDMI  ,VGA housing on. Careful when flipping it over. Then two screws holding HDMI,VGA card down. Then on side there is nuts holding VGA. I used a adjustable wrench they wasn't that tight. Then when flipping that over I noticed the other side of the main ribbon slightly pulled out there is a black wedge peice holding it in with no tape like everything else was. So I pushed it back in and super glued it. Waited on glue then threw it back together. And it's got it's brightness back that person said it used to have. I'm guessing the tilt feature is to tight and the panel has some flex or when person on Monday installed the ribbon they had a bad day that day.

Received my Monoprice 30 inch IPS CCFL backlit monitor today.
This thing is a monster. Must weigh 30lbs or so. The case of the monitor is approx 2" deep, I suppose to be able to house the CCFL's.
The unit (from Amazon) was packed in a box that measures 36x24x13. Inside that box was the packing box for the actual monitor and inside that box was the box that contained the monitor. The monitor itself was well packed inside the inner box and I found no damage at all upon opening each container.
Included with the monitor was the power brick, dual DVI cable (too short for my placement of the monitor), the base (which by the way is not all that bad), a bag of screws and a phillips screwdriver that reminds me of an old time bottle opener and some barely useful instructions.
The stand has swivel right and left of about 30º each direction and raise and lower about 4.5" as well as tilt forward and back. The stand is fairly sturdy considering it is holding a 30 lb monster.
Set up was fairly straight forward with the exception that my video card has two DVI outputs of which only one is dual and it isnt labeled as such. My other monitor was also connected to the video card so I thought it would be easy to just disconnect the 27" Samsung and reconnect the new Monoprice 30"......when I first connected the monitor all I could get on it was some funky 1080 x 880 or something like that. I started to panic a little bit because I tried to connect to the other output and had some issue that kept me from using it. ???? It turned out to be operator error. Once I connected to the other output the monitor turned on into the 2560 x 1600.
The picture on the monitor is a bit grainy but I suppose that is the aggressive anti-glare. I was also not impressed with what I saw on the desktop. It too was kind of grainy and I guess I was expecting the IPS screen to just jump out at me as a OMG, WoW kinda thing. It didnt really. I started up Diablo 3 and set the game to display in 2560x1600 and I was a bit more impressed but not that Wow! kinda thing. I then watched a couple 4K youtube videos at 1440p and now I was seeing some difference. Only reason for four stars and not 5.
As far as I can tell there are no dead pixels. I'm a little disappointed with the controls. Only on/off, high and low brightness.
I'll use for a while and see if its a keeper or not.

This is a really nice display - it's a 5 star based on the quality of the display. But, it's a 3 star when taking into account how much the side-by-side is hyped - as it just does not work very well.  Why? The aspect ratios are all off. The sending computer detects the wide display and then either try to send the super wide resolution to 1/2 the screen or does something totally funky in making the picture too narrow. I'm using Mac computers - maybe it is best on windows - I don't know - but the side by side is useless.  So, the next option is to just switch between inputs - which is what I'm doing. However, there are issues with this as well. For some reason the display has trouble recognizing which inputs are active. And, you have to press about four toggles of the monitor control just to switch inputs. There is software that can allow you to use the same keyboard and mouse to control two computers. It's a great idea but again, doesn't work very smoothly and seems to take up huge CPU. So, do what I did and just buy bluetooth keyboard and mouse that each can support two computers (by flicking a switch on each). So, really good display but really bad features to take advantage of supporting two computers.

The resolution that these monitors provide is absolutely perfect for a 25" screen. I work with data, databases, scripts, and some programing all day and the amount of information I can fit on each screen is a huge asset to my job. I also love the adjustable blue light filter settings, when looking at data all day I have found it does make a difference on eye fatigue.

I also have my Xbox One plugged in to one of the monitors HDMI ports and that also works fantastic. I do wish that you could lock a screen into an input though, so that if for example the xbox reboots it does not search out another signal on the display port forcing you to change the input back to the HDMI port. The volume controls should also be more accessible.

The screens to not display to the edge as others have pointed out, but I have not found that this bothers me at all. I think the monitors look great as they are.

Bottom line, I love these monitors and highly recommend them. They look great and are worth the money. If you are on the fence, just buy them.

I have put this monitor through some extensive testing with various movie formats and PC games, and it generally passes with flying colors. It does take some time to adjust the desktop and video playback settings, in order to get it the way you want it, but that was the only real complaint I'd have.

When my Acer 24" 241W monitor went dead (it had been going downhill for some time), I wanted a brighter, clearer monitor that offered more vibrant colors and better movie playback in particular. After doing research and reading reviews, I settled on this Samsung 23" slim LED backlit monitor. As others state, blacks are "black" and rarely looked washed out, but that is also dependent on the quality of the media you are viewing. Streaming HD video from Netflix or Hulu looks fantastic, as do regular DVD's, in particular if they fill up the entire screen in 16:9 format instead of letter boxing on the sides (which I cannot stand).

It's important to note that I have a Nvidia 285 GTX 1024MB video card, which is still rather high end at the time of this review, and Directx 11c. I also installed the trial version of PureVideo from Nvidia, and it "seems" to have somewhat dramatically improved video playback, but it's hard to tell if that is from my tweaking or the program's enhancements. In any case, I had the best luck tinkering with the video color and playback settings in the Nvidia control panel, pushing up the sharpness and noise reduction to about 20% and tweaking the brightness and contrast to my liking.

Games simply look great out of the box, colors are bright and vivid, accurate and the 2ms response time serves games well. The monitor's built-in menu is easy enough to navigate and only takes a little practice, but I have let my video card's settings take over for the most part, as noted above.

It was very hard to find an LCD/LED monitor that had across-the-board high reviews, most of them are still lacking with multimedia. This Samsung model is probably the best, and paying an extra $50 for the quality seems to be worthwhile thus far.

The monitor is sleek and attractive looking, lightweight and slim. It was a bit more of a size reduction than I anticipated from my Acer 24", but you get used to it fairly quickly, and is due more to the slim profile of the entire monitor, than the screen size.

As HD video becomes more available and popular online, along with the great enhancements in Windows 7, this monitor stands above most of the others, and is again very much worth the extra $, you get what you pay for, and I've learned that lesson time and time again!

Okay, let’s start with the price point. It’s a 144htz 1ms response time 24” curved monitor. You are getting a ton for what you pay. I’ve had dual monitors for 2 months and absolutely love these. The stand is great. Goes up and down and all it takes is one hand. Tilts forward/back and also goes left to right. You won’t be disappointed. If you plan to get these then jump on them as soon as they are in stock. When I got mine, they went so fast they were sold out in about 30-45 minutes.

+ Form factor: Slim with a slightly thicker base, nicely integrated with the cover/stand.
- Portable use only: No vesa holes for use with a custom mount. Only the magnetic cover/stand.
+ The cover can be used at a steeper more vertical angle (viewed across) or a flatter angle (viewed downward). There are rubber feet on the cover and the monitor itself to ensure a stable stand.
+ Matte screen finish reduces reflections
+ The monitor’s construction and bezel feels metallic
+/- Like most portable monitors, uses micro hdmi even though full size hdmi can also fit in a slim monitor like this
+ Volume control is a joystick like dial, but the volume only moves at one speed. The center of the dial is the options/confirm button, while the power button doubles as source select and back depending on context. You might need to reference the manual for first use as the buttons aren’t labelled, but is overall quite intuitive.
+ Power draw from USB: 11.1W max, 4.9W min, as measured by a power meter. It is good that this monitor has adjustable backlight, as some portable monitors do not have adjustable backlight, where the brightness option only affects color and looks washed out at max brightness.
+ 60hz is a normal frame rate. 1080p is a good resolution for this size. The monitor was also able to downsize higher resolution input from my laptop.
+/- HDR is not a advertised feature. There is an HDR option in the menu but the monitor only supports 8 bit with dithering.
+/- In terms of value, I’m seeing full price of $199.99 with a “prime price” of $159.99 as of this review. For the feature set the price of $160 is fair, but $200 would be a bit steep considering at that price point you can add touch screen or wireless video features.

Original Review:

Odyssey Ark has finally arrive (despite being a day late). It’s really massive and despite the warnings to set up with two people, I was able to do it myself (not recommended at all) First impressions were initially mixed but after a week of using this thing I've grown quite fond of it despite its quirks.

Pros-
The 1000R screen I can easily see everything going on despite the screen being larger than the 48” it replaced and with 165hz refresh rate at 4k it’s a great gaming experience. I don’t notice any noticeable color shift in the panel so it’s very uniform. Backlight bleed exists but if you make sure Local Dimming is on you'll never see it with a black screen (all noticeable on a full black screen with it turned off). It's still a VA panel after all. And the 165hz at 10-bit color looks amazing and games play fast and smooth with no ghosting or flicker. GSync works great and VRR is detected when enabled with no issues. And as a TV the picture look fabulous out of game mode in Netflix and on the web browser.

The stand is heavy and solid, and is very easy to move the monitor into the different positions. Moving from landscape to vertical is easy; just lift up the screen and turn. Never had an issue.

The sound is absolutely amazing for computer speakers. I’m going to use this for night gaming instead of headphones. I'm using this with speaker setup with optical in and I've had no issues.

Ark Dial is fun and seemed easy to use (and despite what you may have read there is volume control on the dial). It's very satisfying to use and works very well. Changing the screen ratio was fast and easy and worked as intended. Switching the aspect ratios with the dial made the game much more manageable for playing FPS games.

After fiddling around with picture quality, I was able to finally get it to my standards. Having previously used an OLED, that contrast ratio is hard to beat, but this does come close. I do plan on getting an HDR calibration tool to make adjustments in the near future.

Also this is a monitor, and one of the drawbacks to using an OLED TV is burn-in risk, and we don't have to worry about that here.

Cons -
For starters, it is an LED panel. Previously using an LG C1 OLED contrast made the picture richer. I couldn't duplicate the quality not matter how hard I tried in the settings. This is very important to me because soon curved OLED monitors are coming and you might get a better experience with those (expect the price tag to be about the same despite C2's being cheaper). And forget using the Samsung Calibration software - it doesn't work. I tried to get it to work on an iPhone 14 Pro Max and it just won't come up.

Switching sources is a little laggy. Switching between sRGB and HDR is laggy (and you get a blue screen for a second). Multiple sources at once are bad. As one of the main selling features, I noticed considerable lag playing in this mode and it was unusable playing an FPS, or since it didn't change the resolution even text wasn't legible. Hopefully this gets resolved in an updated. Again, maybe I need to play around with the settings more but I planned on using this frequently and as it stands it’s unusable. I tried using it as "two" monitors on windows - one as HDMI and one as a wireless, and I was somewhat successful in getting it working. Launched correctly, could use multiple programs on both monitors no problem, but when I tried to play a game on HDMI the whole thing crashed, and I had to restart the monitor. For getting use out of vertical mode, I had better luck with Windows latest update and snap windows and the hot key switching it to vertical - works much better since you cannot use more than 1 HDMI port at at time (which would be amazing). So because of the latest Windows 11 update, I found it more useful (Samsung also has a software on their Ark page to download but it's useless with the new update since it does the same thing). I also programmed my Razer Stream Deck to have a Vertical/Horizontal key so I don't have to mess with the Ark (which isn't hard to use at all, but this makes it even faster to swap).

RGB colors are mixed. Yeah, they work and the settings aren't difficult to set up, but the colors don't quite match what was on screen, and having my PC mainly be a white colored setup, I couldn't get white to show up (would look blue-ish). I noticed this with a few other colors as well - colors were just not accurate. Maybe this is just my panel, but I don't ever plan to use it since it's quite bright.

The price tag is hefty. Samsung/Amazon dropped the price by $200 and gave out $100 credit. I contacted Amazon support and after a little escalation they gave me the price adjustment and the credit. Good on Amazon support for that call. But for the price you can get smaller monitors or OLED TV's and it can be hard to justify unless you want to pay for the experience.

Verdict -
Overall, it's a great concept of a monitor/tv, but at times it still feels like a concept instead of a polished product. I hope with some future firmware updates Samsung can really make this panel shine more. Gaming with that 55" curve is amazing, the response of the panel is incredible for 4k/165hz/10-bit color (you need a beefy graphics card to hit even close to this - my 3090 Ti still struggles). The sound is quite simply the best TV/Monitor sound I've ever heard. And the picture quality the most immersive experience and is one of the best I've seen in years outside of OLED TVs. For $3200 after discounts, this is a great monitor if you want to have a whole new experience on your PC... abet with the early adoption fee that comes along with trying something new. It's a much better value at the discounted price.

I use this with my 2014 Mac Book Pro (MBP) with some other monitors, but the setup took some sacrifice, not due to the monitor, but due to the inability to run this and all my other monitors as desired. Some insight into my exploits and working setup below:

I had a late 2012 MBP that was previously natively running:
1) Dell 30" 2560x1600 @ 60 Hz over mini display port to display port
2) Dell 24" 1920x1200 @ 60 HZ over mini display port to display port (or mini display port to HDMI)
3) Dell 24" 1920x1200 @ 60 HZ over HDMI

So I wanted to get a 4k Monitor and run:
1) 28" 4k @ 60Hz
2) 30" 2560x1600 @ 60Hz
3) 24" 1920x1200 @ 60Hz

The above wasn't possible, because the Mac turns off one display port when you are running MST to use 4k @ 60Hz due to required bandwidth, thus I had to sacrifice for the setup below.

1) Asus 4k MST 3840×2160 @ 60 Hz over mini-display port to display port
2) Dell 30" 2560x1600 @ 30 Hz over HDMI (Using software called SiwtchResX to hard code the ability to do other than 1080p/1920x1200)
3) Dell 24" 1920x1200 @ 60 HZ over DisplayLink USB to DVI

So additional costs:
A) new MBP ~$3k (in order to get 1.2 thunderbolt/display port slots, up from the existing 1.1)
B) displaylink usb ~$60
C) SwitchResX ~$20

I guess, if you want to run the most ideal monitor setup, you'll need a desktop in order to have the required video cards, but then you won't be as portable.

UPDATE:
I have stopped using the 3rd monitor via USB DisplayLink because it would cause video lag when I was using video and such, and decided to use my MBP as my third monitor.

Connecting the mini display port on the mac to the DisplayPort on the monitor (via a mini to regular display port cable) only gives you 1080p resolution.

Connecting the 2012 MacBook Pro with an HDMI cable gives you the full resolution (as well as some lower resolutions that look fine) but only at 30htz

BEWARE OF ACER RECERTIFIED!!!!!

While the price of this product is a great deal until you discover something is wrong with it. The monitor worked great for a couple weeks then started experiencing severe pixel flashing over the entire right side. So I started the RMA process and discovered that I have to pay the shipping costs to have it repaired. I figured no big deal until I found out how much it is to ship a 27” monitor in the box it came in. So what was initially a great deal turned into essentially paying full retail price for a re-certified product. If I had paid full price and bought new I would have a longer warranty and wouldn’t have to pay extra to have a defective product repaired.

I guess I missed the small print somewhere. I called Acer Recertified back to explain to them the high cost to send their defective product back and in the hopes I could just take it to an authorized repair center, instead I just got a sarcastic employee who told me that they only have one service center in the entire country. They also sarcastically explained that, “well if you bought from Best Buy they would require you to bring it back to them.” I guess he missed the my point and doesn’t realize that retail stores don’t charge you $100 to repair products under warranty. I can’t remember ever buying anything on Amazon where I incurred additional cost to have something under warranty repaired or replaced.

DO NOT BUY ACER RE-CERTIFIED, that is unless you are willing to take a gamble that it may go bad even while it’s still under warranty.

I know this monitor has great reviews and I have to say that it is an awesome monitor, that is when it is working. I guess if a company makes something cost prohibitive to return, it will never get sent back. And unfortunately most people just don’t bother writing reviews.

At this point I don’t know if I am just better off living with half the screen flashing. Can’t tell you how disappointed I am with Acer as I generally have had a good experience with their products. I can tell you I will never spend a single penny on another Acer product ever.

Update (July 13, 2019):

So I bit the bullet and paid for the return shipping to have the monitor repaired, giving Acer a chance. Of course after paying for the shipping to return their defective product for repair I have essentially paid the price of a new monitor. After about two weeks waiting for my monitor I got it back on July 12, 2019. During that time I tried several times to check the progress of the repair through the Acer site but my monitor never showed so I had to email Acer for updates. To Acer's credit they did replay fairly quickly.

At the first opportunity I had, I unpacked the monitor to hook it up and found the repair paperwork which claimed they replaced the LCD. So I plugged the monitor in and it appeared repaired for about 30 seconds, then it immediately began the screen flashing again as if it was never repaired. In fact, the monitor flashes completely black and takes several minutes before it will actually show. When it does finally come on the screen flashing now appears worse than before.

I am really questioning was this thing ever repaired. I mean whats to stop them from just sending it back without doing anything knowing most people will not want to pony up the additional shipping costs to send it back to them to get repaired a second time. I attempted to email Acer Recertified immediately upon discovering that the monitor was not working and my emails got kicked back with the message that the Service@acerrecertified does not exist. Of course they are only open during business hours Monday Thru Friday so calling wasn't an option. I really don't know what to do at this point because if they require me to pay the shipping costs to send it back for a second repair I will have paid more than the cost of a brand new monitor. This is a hard way to learn, so I am hoping I can keep at least one person from having to go through this. Right now I am so angry I am actually considering small claims court but I have little hope for that process.

I am going to attempt to appeal this to Amazon because any time I have dealt with Amazon customer service for issues they have been excellent. Again, I hope people are reading these reviews because, like I said before, if I can prevent one other person from going through this it will be worth the time spent writing these up. I have never had such a bad customer service experience in my life, this is horrible. I know in the grand scheme of things I'm not out a life damaging amount of money but I think it's fair to expect to at least get what you pay for.

I needed a monitor for my new HP desktop, and wanted a high definition 20" widescreen. I chose this one, mainly because of cost and brand. Initially I wasn't too impressed by the sharpness of the picture, or the general look of the colors, but after I set the monitor at the optimum resolution of 1600 x 900, and color calibrated it, things got better. Be sure to check the HP website to make sure you have the latest drivers.

The built-in speakers are a nice feature, but the output level is very low. For any serious listening you will need to get some external speakers. You can adjust the viewing angle, but not the height of the monitor. The setting adjustment buttons are located along the bottom of the front edge of the monitor, hidden from view. This works esthetically, but a clunky menu design makes it a bit of an adventure adjusting the settings.

Widescreen movies in particular, look more impressive on a wider screen, although the image quality and resolution of this monitor is like most other computer monitors, vastly inferior to that of a typical television for viewing DVD's. Working on documents on the widescreen is very nice, especially if you are nearsighted or presbyopic, and have trouble making out text. Just crank the zoom up. Many webpages display better on a widescreen, you still have to pan around, but not as much. You have more room to work in applications like Photoshop, where saving a few keystrokes here and there, can add up and be significant over time.

Even with a few shortcomings, this is a very nice unit, with very good performance. Competitively priced, I have no reservations in recommending it.
