The image is bit of too bright to my liking, the black level is not dark enough, and color banding is bad in gradient..so, I toned it myself, I'm no professional and don't have a calibration device, I have just been eye-balling it with reference pictures for hours. . Follow me if you like.
I have a Nvidia gtx 1660ti graphic card, I don't know how amd works..
First, to enable 75hz refresh rate, right click an empty space on desktop and go display setting, advanced display settings, display adapter property, Monitor tab, click drop down menu and select 75hz, apply and ok.
Now let's do the picture. Select quick start from the monitor menu, backlight 50, brightness 50, contrast 56, preset User.
Go down to Color, temperature User, red 41, green 38, blue 43, in advanced settings, Cyan 53, everything else leaves at 50. Gamma 2.0
(Now the yellowish pink feel of a TN panel is gone, the color temperature is either too warm nor too cool)
Now, right click an empty space and click Nvidia control panel. Go Change resolution under Display, make sure Sceptre e20, 1600x900 native is selected, in number 3, select Use Nvidia color settings, select Highest 32bits in desktop color depth, Ycbcr444 in output color format. Click apply. (Color banding problem is much improved in my case)
Still under Display, in Adjust desktop color settings, tick Use Nvidia settings, brightness 40%, contrast 27%, gamma0.83
Apply and close Nvidia control panel. (Now I have good deep black with good contrast, good white saturation)
Ok, hope all of my long writing helps someone. My monitor and personal preference can be different from yours. If everything fails and you can always do a reset.

I just received this monitor this morning. Right off the bat I have to say I love how light and thin it is. My old 20" HP LCD monitor is at least twice the weight. Weight isn't much of an issue since it's hanging on the wall, but piece of mind that it won't come crashing down is nice to have. Because I have it wall-mounted (VESA mounts) I can't comment on the stand.

After hooking it up to my computer, I immediately calibrated the monitor using Windows' color calibration program (W7: Control Panel - Display - Calibrate Color). This provided the neutral whites and grays, contrast, brightness and color I need as a professional photographer.

So after that was completed, I began running my computer through everyday tasks - internet viewing, reading text, editing photographs, etc. What I noticed, which is incredibly annoying and difficult to focus with the eyes, is text shadows. There would be bright contrast lines beside the text which can give me a headache. I read that this can be solved by separating the the VGA cord away from the power cord. It has something to do with electrical interference or something. Well, I did that, but it didn't solve the issue. Then I looked to see my computer has an HDMI output, and lo-and-behold it does! I swapped out the VGA cord for HDMI and that completely solved that issue. (The monitor comes with an HDMI cable but not VGA. I initially used the VGA from my old monitor.)

I always prefer a glossy monitor surface to the matte screens because it produces richer colors and sharper images. I work in a dark office with no window light, so there's never an issue with glare. As far as the visual quality of this monitor, I have to say it's superb. The screen is viewable from every angle and the brightness is nearly consistent across the entire screen. My eye level is about 1/4 from the top, and there's a slight gradient in brightness toward the bottom, but it's not too bothersome.

The sound quality is very flat and treble-y. I don't use them since my system is run through Bose speakers. But for anyone needing these as their only source for sound, it's pretty tinny.

PROS:
Size is great. The 23.8" diagonal size is just right sitting 30" from my head.
Colors are rich and deep.
Sharpness and detail are superb.
Minimalist design. The bezel is very thin (1/4" sides and top).
Very light weight.

CONS:
Text with a VGA hook-up is hard on the eyes (definitely use HDMI).
Speakers sound like listening through a tin can.

For how I use this monitor and it's setup (wall-mounted, HDMI connection, speakers off) I'm grading it 5 stars.

This is probably one of THE most popular gaming monitors right now, as it has been referenced time and time again on the PC forums. This is the monitor to go with if you are after certain expectations, with little (fixable) drawbacks that you need to worry with.

BUY IF:
If you want to step up your gaming beyond 60 FPS
Your needs support 1080p vs 1440p vs 4k
Your space supports 24" > monitor size only
You want to dabble with 3D Gaming now or later

Skip IF:
Your GPU Can not processes > 60 FPS
Your wanting to go into 1440P or 4k gaming soon
You NEVER have the intentions of using Nvidia 3D
You use AMD GPUs.

Currently, I am running three of these monitors with Nvidia surround technology loaded, as well as Nvidia 3D running on certain games. By itself, this monitor is simply superb, I even attached my xbox one to it and found it more enjoyable to game on versus my 47" or 60" TV's (personal preference perhaps). The trick about this monitor that you must accept is, if you plan to use it at 144hz, you need to know that your GPU can handle the frame rates required. It takes a lot of horse power to get up in frame rates on some of the newer AAA titles like Call of Duty Black ops III (horrible optimization at the moment), Witcher 3, Just Cause 3, SW Battlefront, etc.

If your GPU is running an Nvidia 970 or < you will be fine. If you are planning to push 120fps with a 960, or anything lower than this, you will have to turn down a bit of settings to make it happen (no AA, dynamic shadows, god rays, etc).

My setup -
i7 4790k Devil's Canyon (OC'd 4.7ghz 24/7) (Liquid cooled)
SLI (dual) GTX 970 (OC'd to 1550 / 8100 @ 1.275V)
Asus Hero VII Maximus MOBO

Results in all games maxed out with greater than 120 fps on a single display.

In Surround. . .
Black Ops III ~ 110 fps with all settings on Medium to high
Witcher 3 ~ 60 fps on High preset (extremely demanding)
Battlefield 4 ~ 120 fps locked all settings ultra
Diablo 3 ~ 120 FPS locked max settings

The downsides mentioned. . .

Thick bezels. but you know this going in - there are plenty of thinner options out there, but none at this price point with this feature set.
Horrid color calibration out of the box - you'll need a new color profile ASAP. I used the x-rite ColorMunki calibration tool and sunk all 3 monitors in order to match profiles. AFTER this, the monitors look EXCELLENT and are able to match printed colors.

The splendid mode is awful. Only the theatre mode is worth setting it to, and even then you must change the "Skin tone" to natural instead a the red preset. Other than having to fix the colors it's a great monitor and I would suggest it to anyone.

I got a few of these for the office, they are more flexible, lower cost, and better looking than any of the other monitors. I’ll keep getting those for any new employees, I have about 6 now.

For context, in my picture, the 4k monitor is the one on the bottom right. OS is scaled up to 200%, and you can still see how big the variance is compared to the 1080p settings of the other monitors.

This monitor is pretty spectacular. With the setup I have, I'm running some absolutely gorgeous scenes with godrays, RTX, and steady frames. Everything is crisp and fluid. The monitor has picture quality like nothing I've had before. I know IPS isn't the end-all-be-all, and PC HDR isn't that good apparently, but this is worlds above my previous displays. It picks up on G-Sync. Windows loves it. All around a pretty great experience!

But, there is a small problem. This persistent flicker.

It's purely video signal. The monitor never loses power. All of the systems are cabled up properly, and I've swapped my current cables and ports a few times, to no success.

Sometimes it'll go hours and hour without a single flicker. Other times it'll start strobing at me to the point I have to turn it off - which usually calms it down for awhile.

I have done all of the driver updates, removals, and reinstalls half a dozen times. I've removea nd readded my display adapters to try and get things running. Even run a recovery on the operating system, which I was reluctant to do.

Did you see that typo I left up in the previous paragraph? The monitor flickered on me just now, and I kept typing through it. So, again, not a huge problem. One I'm adapting to, but kind of something you don't want.

I imagine I *will* solve it, in time. I'm going to invest in some highly spec-ed cables for the entire setup. I'm going to test my success with different ports, and really run it through the gamut of testing. To narrow down exactly what's causing it, and fix it for good - or maybe RMA it if absolutely necessary. Probably not though. This thing is huge, cumbersome, and likely pretty fragile. So if I can avoid that, I want to.

Now, I do this for a living. So I'm fine with this level of effort. A normal user? Probably not. And a gamer who might be competing in ranked and throw an entire round over a screen blacking out for headshots? Yeah, this would probably be an instantaneous refund. (That did actually happen to me once. Was sad, but not completely rage inducing.)

So, consider your use case. Look at other reviews. If I'm the odd one out, that means this monitor is super reliable, and you should see if it fits your needs. If you see reviews like mine, and you need ultra-reliability, I'd consider maybe finding something a little less hi-fidelity that really rocks the house.

4K is a luxury. We still have issues maxing games on 4K Raytracing with the best components money can buy. Don't feel the need to rush.

I have a 27 inch monitor but I felt like I needed an extra one to increase my productivity. Some of the features that this monitor offered really made me want to test it out.

What is unique about this monitor is that it is created from ocean-bound plastic which is something that not many devices can claim. The material seems durable and of pretty high quality. The stand allows for easy adjustment and the back of the monitor offers a variety of different ports that come in useful. GAMING:
Something important to note about this monitor is that it only offers a 60 hertz refresh rate which might be something you need to take into account if you plan on doing high FPS gaming. CONS:

I picked this up from a local big box retailer as a replacement for my 19" widescreen. The pieces snapped together easily making assembly a cinch. The monitor looks great. I really dig the bezel and the fact that it's not completely plain.

For the most part, that's where my compliments stop.

The monitor was very washed out right out of the box. That's understandable, so I started tweaking. There's a Black Level setting that was defaulted to 1. Changing it to 2 made the black levels darker and helped a little with the brightness/contrast. That's what I adjusted next. I forget which setting, but I want to say I had either the brightness or contrast set really low, something in the teens, just to get the monitor to somewhat acceptable levels.

I utilized the screen calibration tool that's available in Win10 (also available in Win7) and got things tweaked even further. Even with that tool, I couldn't get the screen's black deep enough to hide the black X in the tool. After I was done, compared to my primary monitor, the blacks were just need deep enough...more of a dark gray than anywhere close to black.

The stand is pretty bad too. The screen wobbles quite a bit, especially when you're hitting the buttons to adjust settings. I jokingly described it as a bobblehead monitor because of how bad it was. Once you stop fiddling with it, the screen stays in place well enough. I imagine putting it on an arm would further alleviate this issue.

Overall, given the fact that this cost me less than $100, it's an OK purchase. It's not a good display for gaming or movie/TV streaming, as the colors aren't vibrant enough and the black levels just aren't deep enough. However, you can't really expect that for $100. I've had better monitors for around the same price, so I'd give it 3.5 stars, but I can't give it that score and rating it at 4 stars would be too high.

THE GOOD:
--------------
* They provide you with both external and internal accessories to connect a raspberry pi.
* Once up and running, 800x400 pixels is very adequate for a 5 inch screen.
* 10-point capacitive touch.
* Built in dual speakers.
* Monitor has onboard control as well with display menu.
* I tested it with a PI running RasPlex. It ran with no issues.

THE BAD:
------------
* The bezel for this 5 inch is bigger than others on the market, and that's due to having the internal speakers. This is not necessarily a bad.
* No fan. You might want to buy a fan for the cpu.
* Manual only shows you the easy set-up, which will have you running cables all over the place, and it looks messy.
* They do include accessories that will eliminate the external HDMI cable, eliminate touch usb cable, as well as eliminate the need for the monitor to power itself. But they don't explain or teach you how to do it. I didn't make a video for this review, but you can watch my review for the 7 inch version for an idea.
* However, for some reason, the internal touch cable did not work for me in this package. The external micro usb version worked fine. But every time I hooked up the internal cable, it killed the display, it just won't turn on. But once I unhook it, and I use the external cable, the display is back.
* Lastly, not a pro or con, just a FYI.. the packaging and instructions are from USPARKLE.

I'll go 4-Stars overall.

2) There is a known (?) issue with using DVI (which is required for G-Sync...) on Windows machines where using the sleep function works effectively the same as physically disconnecting the monitors (which means messing up things like which monitor your applications are on and often the sizes of your application windows). This is really frustrating and in spite of the myriad potential workaround I found online none of them worked for me for this particular model.

3) The default visual settings are bland and washed out, meaning that they need to be tweaked manually. For a single monitor this is probably fine, but it's tedious across three monitors, especially if one of them unexpectedly dumps its settings.

4) Six months after I bought these one of them has had an entire vertical line of pixels go bad. I'm going to work with Amazon support to see if anything can be done but... this is not something that should happen on such a new monitor. Well, after reading hundreds of reviews I bit the bullet and ordered this monitor because I wanted a 43 inch gamin monitor for a large new desk that I built for myself.  I had seen all the bad reviews about ghosting, blurry text, etc, but took a chance.  It was worth it.  With a little tweaking you can fix the blurry text issue using windows settings.  On a dark screen you can see some cursor ghosting but it is not visible in games and for ordinary web viewing you don't notice it.  You have to make it happen to actually see it.  Colors, brightness, and response are all excellent.  The only issue I had was a weird one.  In Civilization VI I would get some weird screen flickering around edges of screen.  This never happened in other games.  I tried a way too expensive DP 1.4 cable and still had it.  Finally I lowered the target refresh rate to 115fps vice 120.  (Did not even try 144 refresh as even my RTX 3080 cannot run 4K with all the eye candy at that refresh.  I sit about three feet from monitor and it fills my vision with it glorious color and resolution.  Very satisfied with my purchase.  For the naysayers, I just say spend some time getting to know the monitor and tweak your settings and all is fine.

The monitor is absolutely amazing. I bought the GW2765 27inch IPS 1440p.I was going back and forth between this and 144hz 1080p for weeks, IPS and TN. Finally settled here and I couldn't be happier. As an avid PC gamer, it was time for an upgrade. If you are not at the Esports level or an extremely competitive streamer trying to reach the tops then I would recommend going 1440p 60hz. The graphics are crisp and beautiful colors are superb. I will say there is a difference between 1080p 60hz and 144hz a major one at that especially in FPS, but in my honest opinion, the amount of graphical change from 1080p to 1440p is far more substantial. For example, I play a lot of Rust, on 1080p I couldn't tell what guns people had from a distance and the armor they were wearing most of the time, with 1440p as it makes the characters on the screen bigger and more detailed that is no longer an issue. Of course, as you know the difference between IPS and TN is night and day for colors and viewing angles.

I am running this monitor and a 1080p 24 inch as a secondary with an overclocked Evga Geforce 1060 6gb FTW+ addition GPU. Researched a lot before buying and being able to power this monitor on ultra settings with this GPU was a huge question mark some said yes (maybe), many no's, some said 1070 would be far better and while that may be true I have had zero issues what so ever powering every game I play on ultra and getting 80 to 100 fps sometimes more depending on the game. I will say that this card is a beast came that way out of the box and with me overclocking it, the thing might just be as strong as 1070. GPU clock is running around 2100hz and my memory clock is running around 4200hz.

I tried to cover every major issue as it took me weeks to research everything till I was comfortable making a purchase. If you are like me and take every little detail into consideration then I hope this review helped you on your journey. The monitor is beautiful the stand is superb A+ all the way, in closing if you are not a pro gamer trying to reach the top then yes, id go with 1440p at 60hz over 1080p 144hz the graphics increase is just gorgeous and if you are a graphics +++++ like me you will not be disappointed.

For just web browsing use and/or gaming, a 23 to 24 inch monitor is usually a better choice because it's not as overwhelmingly bright for the many pages with a white background. However, if you know some free plugins to use, you can color all the white backgrounds grey, depending on which browser you use.

But I bought this 27 inch because I wanted the screen space for applications which are much easier to do with lots of screen space. It is also nice for movies, if you usually don't watch them on a big TV. I'm not going to be going 4K for several years, and since I'm happy with the laptop I use this as a display monitor for, and my laptop doesn't have a 4K-capable graphics card, this was a good choice.

Like another commenter, I found the blackness was blacker at "low". It comes with a VGA cable, but if you hook it up with HDMI and the picture is dark and terrible, try the VGA cable. Then search the web for the Windows display setting you'll need to use to make it work with HDMI.

A lot of people are confused by the monitor configuration buttons, since you have to use the buttons. I mean they don't work with a mouse. You can't just click on "exit" from that menu and exit. But those settings are important and can make big differences in how it looks. The button labeled "EXIT, to the left of the on/off button, is one you can press to make the monitor configuration menu disappear. Instead of everything being accessible from the MENU button, some things are configurable only through the FUNC. button.

The monitor is thin, and the texture in the plastic screen border is good for glare. It was easy to put together and completely plug & play on Windows 10. In other words, you don't have to use the disk to get the driver. The included disk has the manual on it. I like that it has no speakers. Be aware that it only has VGA and HDMI though -- no DVI-D.

Lastly, look around for a much lower price. I got this new from a major online auction retailer (four letter name begins with e and ends with y), as a "buy now" item who was selling them for $10 cheaper than they do on their own website, and $67 cheaper than the price showing today here. So, I paid 140.

Once I got it connected, a second challenge was that the monitor reflected my light fixture in the middle of the screen so I couldn't really use it since the built-in stand tips it back.  It took another week to find a stand that I could use that would keep the monitor vertical. I found a heavy one so when I use touchscreen it doesn't move.  Glad I didn't give up - a second touch screen makes it so easier to maneuver between two screens and makes me more effective.  Ten times better than a regular monitor and worth the extra money.

I travel a lot for work and ended up getting a bunch of my coworkers to buy this monitor too. Needed two new monitors for a new PC in the office. Went with this because it was the best value in terms of size vs price. I'd also purchased these for clients in the past and heard they were happy with them. After seeing the complaints about the colors being washed out, I gave my monitors away and decided if there were any problems with these I'd either live with it or fix it.

Out of the box the monitors were fine. The only complaint I can come up with is that the VESA mounts are lower on the back due to the slim design. After looking at settings, I determined that for me as an IT manager I didn't need to recalibrate the screens at all. Granted, I spend most of my time looking at text documents, configuration pages for network equipment, and the accounting software my company uses. I couldn't speak to color calibration for individuals doing video or photo production.

One thought on the others experiencing washed out colors - HDMI out on a computer kind of expects a TV. Televisions and Monitors will sometimes have different ranges for black levels, which can cause the screen to look washed out. If you're experiencing this, check the control panel for your graphics driver. You should be able to adjust black levels and fix the issue. This has been a driving factor in my preference for just about anything over HDMI. (Display Port is king, for what it's worth.)

I'm currently using this monitor as a second screen for my MacBook Pro, and it works exactly as designed. My MacBook's USB port provides enough power for this to operate without a separate external source, so this has been perfect. One thing I will say is that the knob on the side to access the OSD controls is a little finicky when you depress it, but I can't take away a full star for it just because of that. This has provided everything that I want in a sleek, portable monitor.

Bought two, both failed shortly after the return period. Just getting the 'touch' features to work at all was a big experiment. If you use only a single USB C cable, then this might work as expected, but all other supposed working configurations are almost guaranteed to not work...well you may get video and audio, but touch? Forget it. I finally found a custom cable that did work (DP & USB A on one end, USB C on the other end for video and touch, with a separate USB C via the provided power block to actually power the monitor), but both monitors died within a couple months (they won't power on, they just making a clicking sound, which the seller verified was a failure state - and this brand is so uninterested in providing products that work that they don't even make these anymore, so there's no way to get a replacement - the company now offers a cheaper version they charge the same amount for - want to guess how useless those probably are?).

I've tried several of these portable, touchscreen monitors (from various sellers and manufacturers) and they're all junk. Don't waste your money on this crap - just suck it up and buy a REAL NAME BRAND product since those companies actually have a reputation to protect and their products actually DO work consistently.

The Viewsonic XG350R-C is a really nice monitor, HOWEVER, the RGB portion of the monitor DOES NOT work with Thermaltake RGB Plus software, nor does it work with RAZER CHROMA as advertised.
I spent a couple of weeks with Viewsonic Support via email trying to get this monitor to work with ThermalTake RGB Plus software. and then Chorma Synapse. It is NEVER recognized by the software, even though Viewsonic says it is compatible. We tried TT RGB PLUS versions 1.2.7 thru 1.2.9 and the monitor is never recognized. Additionally, only one side of the RGB LEDS seem to work with RAZER Visulizer. They will change color once in awhile, but the monitors RGB leds are not syncing with CHROMA at all. After a couple of weeks of trying to get this to work, ViewSonic deemed there was a problem with the monitor, so, they Advanced Replaced the monitor. When I rec'd the replacement XG350R-C, I connected up to my PC and the exact same issues remain. About the only thing the RMA accomplished is me having to lay out $90 to ship my original monitor back to VIewsonic. They don't cover the return shipping. So, it cost me 90 bucks for ABSOLUTELY NOTHING. In the meantime, had i just returned the monitor when I first contacted Viewsonic, I could have just swapped it out for a new one or got my money back...however, by the time the trouble shooting was finished, I could not return it. - The RGB section of this monitor does not work as advertised and they know it. I sent them pictures of my hardware config, videos, you name it. Everything else, from the Thermaltake Riing Trio RGB fans to the Bitspower Reservoir RGB leds i have installed in my new rig are all recognized and working as designed. The only thing that isn't working is the XG350R-C monitor. As long as you don't care if the RGB portion of this monitor works correctly, it's a really nice monitor, especially for gaming. If you do care about RGB, PASS!

1 star for an ASUS Professional Monitor? With great color, crisp details and 3.0 USB ports (plus the convenience of nearly every possible cord/cable and adaptor combination) what could possibly be so bad? What makes this monitor a RIP OFF at even 1/2 the price is the smarmy, devious, unhelpful and ill-informed Support staff. I must have bought the bargain-basement version of this monitor because I was only able to contact them by email. They offered no phone support. The problem I had is listed in numerous reviews....most of which I read prior to making my purchase. I use a monitor extensively for my work so I tried to choose carefully balancing cost with the specifications that would work best for me. I did not read in any reviews that I would only be able to communicate with ASUS by email. THE SUPPORT SITE IS A NIGHTMARE OF CONFUSING MISINFORMATION. I spent over an hour being spit from one form to a FAQ page to the form to General Monitor information and never once finding an answer to my problem. I finally sent the Help request successfully but took more than 48 hours for them to respond. They chose their 2014 Best Agent for Destroying Customer Confidence, Manny, to answer my question. And as rude, condescending and wrong as his email to me was, I NEVER ACTUALLY GOT AN ANSWER ABOUT MY PROBLEM. I was so confused by the site's hellish redirection (from checking the warranty status one is redirected back to the home page where one is encouraged to register the product that is already registered. When I read the FAQ's for this monitor, I began to believe that I had a knock off. None of the specs matched. I found illustrations for buttons and settings that are not on the monitor I have. I wrote ASUS about this confusion and told them to tell me why the FAQ's illustrations showed completely different settings than the monitor I had. I asked them to first verify I had an authentic monitor before advising me how to fix it.
It was Manny, the confidence-killer that hacked out the rudest and stupidest answer I've ever received for any product from ANY company and that includes the company that made the defective fake barf I ordered from the back of a comic book when I was 11.
Manny confirmed that I had the monitor I ordered. He quoted me chapter and verse from the User Manual.
Wait a minute! The User Manual is not available for viewing on the ASUS site, only for PDF download. Additionally, I had specifically referenced the FAQ's as the source of the information that conflicted with my actual monitor. The smirky tone of Manny's letter implied clearly that ANY IDIOT could find what he did. After telling me how wrong I was, he cheerfully signed off without ever mentioning my other concerns listed in the email.
I was stunned. Days had passed and I still had no answer to my problem. All I had was Manny's condescending joke of a response. I started to reply to ASUS. 3 days later I had 11 screen shots laid out with numerous notes on each pointing out where I found the misinformation and why I needed help. It suddenly dawned on me that ASUS would never look at any of this.
They kept sending me requests to rate their service. Having received none, I couldn't answer. I sent them the 11 screen shots and told them not to contact me again. That is the one request they have honored completely.
I see on these pages NO MEANINGFUL RESPONSES FROM ASUS about problems reported repeatedly (and easily found reported on other sites). I should have listened to the one and two star reviews which may cite different technical problems but are UNANIMOUS in their disdain for the Support at ASUS. It would be faster, easier and much less frustrating if, when you clicked the ASUS Technical Support heading on the site, you saw the following message: TECHNICAL SUPPORT IS NOT AVAILABLE FOR YOUR PRODUCT. Because after all the struggle to get help, the truth is there is NO Technical Support for my product. I have attached a screen shot of the 11 annotated screen shots I was compiling to show ASUS what I had encountered. I am so relieved I purchased a separate warranty,

First thing first, this is an amazing screen, perfect for indoor use, I do not believe a better screen currently exists, so I am content with ownership. That said, this device has some GLARING issues which I hope they address in time.

1- The power button is overly complicated, it should just power the unit on and off! Instead it opens an on-screen menu, with the off option not even highlighted, so you find the DOWN button to go to it, then you find the ENTER button to select it! WHY??? More often than not I just tug out the power cable to turn the poorly designed thing off.
2. When connecting via USB-C (with Display Port Alt) it will only display with 50% brightness! This is true regardless if I have it plugged in with a separate power connector or not! Nowhere in the manual or on the website is this noted, in fact it annoying me more and more as I recall how much of a let down this is. So to get full brightness I now need a mini-HDMI to HDMI adapter, an HDMI cable, and a USB-C to HDMI adapter, AND a USB-C cable for power, with the power brick, and the USB to USB-C adapter it ships with because they for some reason ship with the wrong cable. It is absurd how messy that is for a supposedly "portable" monitor that should just be able to connect with a single DP Alt USB-C thunderbolt cable..
3- The cover/stand is complicated does not work outside of a stationary desk. Save your money and get a monitor with a built in stand (thank me later). I purchased a third party stand that works well enough.

This is a good overall monitor. The picture is clear, it's lightweight, and the colors are nice. This leads me to my issue with it.. the colors are nice, but the ViewSonic company doesn't have installation software that is compatible with OSX Lion (I emailed the company, and they told me in their reply). This makes it hard to get the proper colors on the monitor. Even after messing with the Mac advanced calibration settings for a long time, I can't get something that looks right (the colors on mine have a warm hue, but it's the best I could do). If it wasn't for this compatibility issue, it would be a 5-star product.

Bought an NEC Multisync LCD 1770VX monitor a few days ago on Ebay for $140.00 new--or open box, more likely. Shortly after the auction, I came apon the few reviews to be found for this monitor which say it's overall design is fine but that its image quality is mediocre--specifically, it has less dynamic range than competitiors, the lighter grey tones falling off into white (clipped highlights) and the darker greys falling off into black too quickly. I didn't want to hear this after buying the monitor for judging digital photos. but there was nothing to do but see for myself when it arrived.

The monitor arrived in its product box and I was easily able to hook it up to an old Apple G4 mirror door Power Mac with the monitor's enclosed cable. Turning it on and judging initial results with some familiar digital photos, the images showed much too much contrast and exhibited characteristics that matched the reviews. But the images improved considerably with adjustments, both through the monitor controls and the Apple monitor programs for selecting and modifying profiles. The monitor looked best when its brightness and contrast levels were set well below 50% by the monitor's controls. Images could also be individually adjusted in Photoshop to look just fine. The highlight clipping is minimal and seems to be most obvious in JPG images that were over-exposed to begin with. There is software not in the box available from NEC that permits adjustments to the monitor by your keyboard--but it only works in Microsoft (and possibly Apple's newer products).

Since I bought the monitor to just peruse Iphoto images and the results are really quiet good, the monitor is a keeper. Images on the monitor do display considerable change in value or saturation when viewing the screen from different angles, something not unique to this monitor. If an image fills the entire field of view, the top will be darker than the bottom of the scene--but then, this isn't an Apple monitor.

All in all, this is a better monitor than the reviews give it credit. It is very compact and doesn't clutter the desk. Full retail price seems steep and is steep compared to current Ebay prices.

I purposely wait for a while to write this review, so I can gain more experience with the product.

The packaging and shipping of the product is good, there is no unnecessary packing materials to threw away, and shipment is on time as promised.

I installed this monitor initially with the old DVI cable that I already tied down with my old monitor (this is a replacement), when I turn this monitor on, the image flickering started, it appeared that the screen was pulsing with portion of the screen flickering on and off, I almost want to pack it up and ship back to Amazon.

After second thought, I decided to see if I can figure out what was the problem and where it might be coming from. I tried the HDMI cable came with the shipment, the flickering went away, then I decided to see if I could use the DVI cable came with the monitor, once I replaced the cable, the flickering stopped.

I think this monitor is very sensitive to the cable you use, it may be the shielding problem with the cable, or the monitor itself, but I took one star off the overall product rating.

Now, the good stuff:

1. The color is vibrant and accurate, I can see immediate difference between this IPS monitor with another IPS monitor I am replacing, the color accuracy is obvious.
2. Brightness is a little bit too bright for me, I turn it down to 65%, which is good enough for my daily use.
3. Default setting is good enough, I have tried a few changes, but came back to the original default, I think the factory calibration of this monitor is just right.

Price performance wise, this is a good choice, I am a little bit put off by the initial cable issue, but overall I am satisfied.

I bought two of these for my office. Nice monitors. Good brightness and vibrant colors. The DP cords they ship with are frustratingly short, but luckily I had some longer ones on hand.

The resolution when connected to DP is great. Clear picture, good response times, etc.

However, there are two absurd issues that are most likely software-related:

1. 4:3 aspect ration doesn’t work at all. It is greyed out. It’s the only option greyed out, and is required for console play as all of the other aspect ratios stretch the image.

2. When in PBP mode, all adjustments are disabled and some kind of default color/contrast/brightness scheme is enabled. This results in the image when gaming on console to be dim and almost completely without color. PBP mode could be a workaround for issue number one if this wasn’t also defective.

Here’s the worst part, though, and something that will absolutely sink this company in the US market: the customer support is terrible.

I called their support line and wasn’t greeted. Nothing formal was said to indicate that I had reached a support department. Just something along the lines of “yes, hello?”

While explaining my issue, I was interrupted rudely by the agent who stated “our monitors (are) not for game consoles.” I explained that the 4:3 ratio wasn’t available on my PC either, and he responded that I should try another cable.

I tried two different DisplayPort cables to no avail, then two different HDMI cables to no avail. These cables work with other high resolution monitors just fine, including a 34” ultra wide and a 28” 4K.

He said at this point there was nothing he could do to help. I responded that, per the manual, the 4:3 aspect ratio should be an option. I asked what could cause the monitor disable that option. He responded that he doesn’t know.

I asked for my case to be escalated to a more technical department, and he said they don’t do that. I asked to speak with a supervisor, and he said there is no supervisor, that he’s the only person in support in the United States.

That’s it. That was the whole conversation. I’ve worked in and managed support teams, and this is the worst support experience I’ve been exposed to.

I’ve did the next logical thing; I described my experience on twitter and tagged AOC. They responded that they’d like to help and that I should DM them my contact details. I did so and didn’t hear back for a day. I messaged them again asking if they received my info and when I should expect to be contacted. They said they needed more time. I waited another day and received no contact.

I’ve returned this monitor to Amazon as of today.

Regardless of the stats and features of their hardware, I’ll never buy another AOC product simply because of this customer support experience. I would have been willing to work with them and await a firmware update to resolve my issues, but this type of treatment is not worth it, not acceptable.

I would guess the main cause of most of the problems is the manufacturer skimped on the controller chip.
These problems are:
1) No working "auto input" mode. Although in the list of inputs you can choose from there is one labeled as "auto", choosing it just results in: no input detected going to sleep mode.
2) If the input you have selected has no active video connected you have about 1-2 seconds to invoke the menu / select change input / scroll up or down the list of the seven possible selections / and select the one that is active before the monitor turns off. If you have not completed all of these steps before the monitor turned off you must turn the monitor on again and start over. This leads to a lot of frustration.
3) More minor is that the monitor seems slow in both powering on and in detecting a valid video signal. Requiring about 5 seconds to switch from a valid signal on input A to a valid signal on input B.
4) A last minor issue is that the VESA wall mount is a 100mmX100mm. That seems very small for a monitor of this size. I have had to tape a bit of weight to one side to make it hang straight. Otherwise it leans to the right, probably due to the power supply or other heavy electronics being located on that side. I also worry that a minor bump could lead to damage to the monitor.

Besides IPS glow, which is minimal on this monitor and inherent to the technology, this is a flawless monitor. A very light AG coating makes a huge difference in clarity and makes colors pop (although glossy would be preferred for best image quality) vs. some other high end gaming panels that use a medium AG coating, which can result in duller colors, crosshatching, and loss of detail. The stand is excellent, although I quickly  removed it and mounted the monitor on the AmazonBasics mounting arm. The OSD works well enough; no real complaints there. Default color accuracy is a tiny warm, but toning down the green a little brings the whites into balance. No significant backlight bleeding to be seen, and no dead pixels. Response time is SUPER fast for an IPS display, and it shows in game. Coming from a 1080p 120hz TN Samsung monitor, the 4ms response time results in a little bit more motion blur to my eyes, but it is outweighed by the vastly improved colors, Freesync support, and 1440p resolution. Highly recommended monitor if you're looking to upgrade to 1440p, IPS and Freesync.

I purchased 2 of these Boifun monitors.  They work great and are worth the money, but the instructions are not as clear as they can be.  I had a hard time getting the monitors hooked up to my laptop but after some investigation at Best Buy, it turned out to be quite easy.  If you have a laptop with an old USB type "C" port (NOT thunderbolt), this monitor won't work on that type "C" port.  But the solution is simple.  Get yourself a "USB 3.0 to dual HDMI Multi-Monitor Adapter" from 'j5create".  This adapter turns 1 regular USB port (type A or B, which all laptops have) into 2 HDMI ports.  Then connect each Boifun to each of the HDMI ports (and connect each Boifun to power using the power cable provided) and they'll work great for video and sound. I could not get this information from Boifun itself, but got it from the local Best Buy.

I done a lot of research on monitors before making this purchase. I was looking at the Acer Predator and the Asus 27" Gsync IPS panels. At the time of ordering, the Asus wasn't on sale on Amazon because of the bad reviews and complaints of black light bleed. I wasn't too much impressed with the weird looking bezel and red feet of the Acer Predator. The Acer XB270HU had quite a few negative reviews and had the glossy bezel which didn't impress me. I read articles on TFT central and forums and looked at charts and graphs before making my decision. The XB270HU bezel and stand is very standard looking and didn't have all the red highlights so I went with a more minimalist approach with my decision. I was very nervous about spending $700+ on monitor and shipping it outside of the US. I had to really play the lottery game and take a chance. To put a long story short, I am very happy about my purchase.

I got the XB270HU and the box was a bit beat up. However, the monitor itself was untouched. I have 0 dead or stuck pixels. There is slight bleed about 1 inch wide and .5 long on the very top of the panel in the center. I had to really go on a hunt to find the yellow bleed everyone was talking about. It's so small it's not even worth talking about. I am very impressed with the quality control of Acer. The panel works great for games and it was very easy to enable Gsync and 144hz. After fiddling around with the OSD controls, which are quite quirky by the way, I realized I could overclock the monitor to 165hz. I run the monitor on 165hz and it is noticeably smoother than running the monitor on 144hz especially when playing Rocket League. Acer needs to add the 165hz to the specifications for the monitor.

The colors on the monitor are bright and vibrant. White is White! I see details in images and games that I've never seen before. The higher refresh rate also makes the panel more responsive. Even all of my older games like UT2004 look incredible on this monitor. Moving from 1920x1200 to 2560x1440 is a major difference and brings out more clarity and detail. I am so impressed with the monitor, I am thinking about buying a second for dual displays. My current displays Doublesight DS-263n 25.5 IPS panels look like they have aged compared to the sleek and vibrant XB270HU. Once you experience 144hz+, you will never go back to 60hz.

EDIT June 6,2016 - I replaced the displayport cable with one from Cable Matters/Amazon and somehow I was able to fix the stuttering with Rocket League that I was experiencing. Now it's buttery smooth. The bezel on the monitor is a semi-gloss and doesn't reflect hardly anything. It's darker than matte and does a good job in front accent lighting. The ULMB is fantastic with Battlefront and Black Ops 3.
Using it with a Zephyrus G14 laptop. Excellent picture quality. Turns out I can connect it with just the USB C cable (no HDMI needed for me). Very happy with this purchase!

I recently turned 50 and decided to redo my entire office/gamming setup as a present to myself. $4000 including standing desk, office chair, other furniture, gaming computer, and, obviously, a monitor. I wanted a curved ultrawide, but I didn’t think I could get a good one for less than $800-$1000. Everyone knows you should buy a 12900KS, a 3090 TI, and an Odyssey G9, but now you’re talking about $8000. So, I started searching for “best video card for the money” (the 3060 TI, BTW), or “best CPU for the money” (Ryzen 5 5600X), etc. When I searched for best gaming monitor for the money, I ran across this monitor. I had never heard of VIOTEK; it seemed a little suspicious. But the cost to features ratio was nuts. This monitor should cost twice as much as it did. And, I’ve got to say, yeah, it’s awesome and a steal. I have zero complaints.

I will point out a couple of things. I got the GNV34DBE2, not the GNV34DBE. The 2 at the end means you’re getting the second revision. It seams a lot of these reviews are the GNV34DBE2 and the GNV34DBE grouped together, so make sure you’re getting the second revision with the 2 at the end. There were a lot of complaints that were completely fixed with the 2nd revision. The other thing is that the provided stand does completely suck. I purchased an Ergotron monitor arm. I was worried that the size and weight meant I had to pay more for the “oversized” Ergotron monitor arm, but I just purchased the normal one and it works perfectly.

But, yeah, man, this thing is everything I ever wanted from an ultrawide monitor. Couldn’t be happier.

I am confused by the pricing on this. The Samsung models are very simple, S60, S70, S80 etc.  The higher the number the better the quality.  UHD has more pixel density than WQHD, for example.  So why is this monitor, the S60 at just over 100 PPI, priced higher than the S80 with about 160 PPI. Does it make a difference?  Yes! It really does. This is a good monitor for what it is, but the price should start with a 2 and not a 3.  Go for this monitor if you are not a stickler for image quality and you need a nice budget monitor. If you need any level of image quality, skip this and go for the S80 UHD 4k. No matter which monitor you go with, Samsung monitors come very dim out of the box. To fix this, you need to adjust TWO settings in the monitor system setting menu.  Eco and Eco plus need to be turned off.  Also download a secondary app for your computer so your keyboard or screen shortcut can adjust the monitor brightness without digging into the monitors menu tree.

This was a fantastic monitor while it worked! Unfortunately, after less than 3 months of owning it the screen began to have significant display issues. Despite the issues I had with the product, I've included some good and bad points of this product.

The Good:

- The Monitor is pretty crisp for the price range. If you're looking for something to watch videos on or utilize as a mobile gaming monitor, I would suggest spending a bit more to get something designed for videos or gaming. This monitor is great for working on Excel or Word documents for work.
- The case seems very nice. I only used it once to transport the monitor from home to work, but have left it at work as an extra monitor.
- The fact that it does not require a power source other than a desktop computer or laptop USB or SS port is fantastic. You only have one cord to deal with and it's extremely easy to disconnect your laptop and move to meetings and reconnect when you get back to your desk.

The Bad:

- After 3 months the screen started to have random color glitches. Fairly soon afterwards, half of the screen had significant color issues. Now I am missing half my screen, which makes the monitor completely unusable.
- Other reviewers have mentioned the cord connection having issues. I had similar problems with the cord. It connects and disconnects randomly and had become even more frequent. Before the screen became unusable, I would have to disconnect the monitor and reconnect it, otherwise it would blink on and off (with the disconnect and reconnect sound that Windows makes) about every 3 seconds. I have plugged other USB items into that same port and have not had that issue with anything else. Even when utilizing the SS port, it has the same problems.
- Customer service is so-so. To their credit, their response time is very quick. However, don't be surprised if you receive, "This is not a product defect and is thus not returnable or covered under warranty." Despite sending half a dozen other reviews citing the same issues, I was still told it was a "user issue". I'm not an engineer by any stretch, so I took the monitor to the technology support at my company. Within 30 seconds of looking at the screen the support team was able to tell me it was a product defect. Sadly, when I handed over the monitor the first technician started laughing immediately and said, "You bought an AOC product?" Long story short (somewhat), apparently my company used to furnish some company desks with AOC monitors, but after numerous issues they have moved to utilizing Asus monitors. I asked nicely (begged) and was given an Asus monitor to use at my desk. I've enjoyed the Asus monitor so far, but will need to wait out some time to see if it has a similar issue. Perhaps it's just a mobile monitor issue.

Overall, not a horrible product (for the 3 months that I was able to use it). But if you're looking for a monitor to use long term, I would not recommend this one. Either spend a little bit more for a sturdier monitor or spend a bit less and get a little less image quality. If you're using it mostly for Word, Excel, PowerPoint, etc. then a lower image quality should not be of significant concern. You may be able to roll the dice and get a monitor that works, but it seems that AOC has quality control issues. At $100+ that isn't a risk I am willing to take again. Check out an Asus product or other quality brand that doesn't have the high brand visibility and associated costs that a Samsung or an LG would have (Side Note - with that being said - Samsung customer service was some of the best I have ever worked with. Less than 2 days to get a replacement for a TV that my girlfriend purchased and was broken upon arrival.)

I travel frequently from Virginia Beach to Florida to visit and spend time with my 92 year-old Grandfather. Even though I'm getting older (43), I still love PC gaming. The problem with gaming on a laptop (aside from not being as powerful as a good gaming PC) is that they're only created with a single monitor and traveling with traditional monitors isn't very practical. This allows me to have a dual monitor setup on my laptop without having to carry a "real" monitor around.

The display is similar to many tablets on the market today because it's using similar screen technology. The color isn't perfect and the blacks are a tiny bit dull and not very deep. That's to be expected, though, as you sacrifice some quality for convenience of portability and for a lower price. The color can be adjusted and while not entirely intuitive, it's easy enough.

The display comes with a clam shell styled cover that can fold to double as a stand, just like the covers for iPads. For a monitor display that's intended to sit next to my laptop, I didn't like this. That's a personal preference of course and does not detract from the overall score of the display. I did find an aftermarket tablet stand (included in the picture and link provided at the end) that works very well and folds up to be just as portable as the display. One thing that I do suggest is that if you go this route, buy a few Velcro strips to secure the display to the stand. We just received 2 Dell UltraSharp U2412M monitors at my company (Rev A01). One has the yellow background tint problem that many reviewers have referred to and the other does not. These were purchased through a corporate supply chain contract and not through Amazon. Thus, the yellow background tint problem is not specific to Dell Monitors supplied by Amazon. We have done some side by side comparisons of older monitors versus newer ones. The older monitors that we purchased prior to mid 2013 are sufficiently bright when set at 67-77 depending on the external lighting. The newer monitors that we have recently received need to be set at 95-100 in order for the brightness to be sufficient. This is with or without the yellow background tint problem. Dell clearly changed the design specifications on this model by designing-out components and reducing the brightness in the process. The yellow background tint is most likely an unintended consequence of lowering those specifications. A relatively easy way to save money if you're a design engineer at Dell but not even close to the monitor it once was if YOU ARE A REPEAT CUSTOMER and can make side by side comparisons of old versus new. Or should I say WAS A REPEAT CUSTOMER. We are returning both of these monitors and will not be ordering any more of them. We order dozens of monitors each year and so I hope Dell takes notice. You can reduce costs thus impacting image quality. Maybe for those who don't have any of the older monitors in front of them it's not apparent. But in the case of our company we have many of the older monitors and when placed side by side with the newer ones it is very obvious that this is not even close to the monitor it once was.

NEC MultiSync EA244WMi: If you can afford an extra $100 the NEC MultiSync EA244WMi is the monitor to get. You can get it for around $365. It's got excellent contrast and instead of a matte finish like the Dell the screen has a semi-gloss finish. The semi gloss finish is more than enough to eliminate screen glare while there's less grainyness and thus a sharper picture than the Dell. You will especially notice the difference if you read a lot of text. Read the reviews on B&H photo in addition to those on Amazon. PCMag rates it the top 24" monitor for 2014. It's superior to the Dell. Additionally it's got a 3 year warranty and NEC stands behind their warranty. Many reviews have had problems getting warranty service for Dell products purchased through Amazon. You won't have that problem with NEC. Their warranty is legit and they have a good reputation for honoring it.

The ASUS PA248Q is a fine monitor if text sharpness is not as important to you. However, if you will be reading a lot of text you will find that the text on the ASUS is a bit fuzzy compared to the Dell. As previously mentioned, the best of both worlds is the NEC MultiSync EA244WMi: Sharper text, better colors, web pages are sharper. Just all around the NEC is the one to get if you don't mind paying the extra $100.

This monitor is beautiful. The colors are vibrant, clear. The bevel is a perfect subtle matte finish. You can turn off the annoying power indicator light (honestly why do monitors even have that anymore).

But it has a crippling problem recognizing DisplayPort. Google "asus PB278Q displayport detection issue" and you will find hundreds of unresolved threads with people complaining about their Asus giving them the dreaded "no signal" while using DisplayPort. This usually happens to me when switching between my TV connected by HDMI and this monitor. If I switch the display mode in windows to output to only the second monitor (my TV), windows will forget the monitor, and the next time I boot up, it will fail to send its specifications to Windows, and Windows will be unable to output the right signal. It only happens sometimes; It's very temperamental, like all of the most annoying tech problems. The only way to fix it is to disconnect the power from the monitor, reconnect and turn it back on. I can prevent this from happening by choosing "duplicate" and simply turning off the monitor.

I can't really say it's a dealbreaker, though. This monitor is the most beautiful one I've ever owned. It's incredibly bright; I actually have it running at 45% brightness in my basement room, even with light coming in through the windows. 100% is ridiculously bright for the setting in which I use it. I have seen no ghosting or bleeding, and I have noticed no lag, even while playing first person shooter games. I'm a fan of simplistic, minimalistic design, and I hate fancy over-designed bevels, so the design of the monitor is perfect for me. It's all rectangles with very subtle curves on the corners, much like IKEA furniture design, so it looks great on my desk and doesn't distract me from what is on the screen. It makes me want to upgrade my speakers to something to better match the simplistic, angular, all-black motif.

This monitor is a fantastic buy, but don't expect it to be completely frustration-free.

What's the best thing about going into an Apple store? The hordes of black-shirted dudes with gleaming iPods round their necks? The Genius Bar answering any and every question that might come up in the Apple universe? Well, in my book it's the rows of Macs with those killer large TFT screens. Even if a lowly Mac Mini is out on display, it will be connected to a gleaming Apple Cinema Display, and even the iMac G5s seem to favour the 20" models. As for the Power Macs, a pair of 24" or 30" screens seems to be the only way to truly show off Final Cut Pro or Logic.

Cinema Displays are killer cool, with their gleaming aluminium cases and fabulous industrial design. They are priced as a premium product, and they look like they are worth every penny but that doesn't make it any easier to afford one. I'd always thought that I would never be able to aspire to such a fine window to OS X. However, I have recently discovered that this is not the case. This technology is now available to us mere mortals, for little more than half the price that Apple charges.

Come Over to the Dark Side

You see, there is only one supplier of 20" 1680x1050 resolution panels that manufactures in volume - LG.Philips. Accordingly, Dell's 20" 2005FPW panel uses exactly the same LCD panel as Apple's 20" Cinema Display. Dell prices for monitors start out somewhat less expensive than Apple, but are frequently discounted by massive percentages, something Apple is not known for.

I needed to replace the 17" Acer panel I was using on my Power Mac G5. It showed poor colour registration and some fuzziness, principally because it only had a captive analogue connector, and no DVI input. It was playing havoc with my digital photography work, and was quite uncomfortable to use for long periods. I decided to get a DVI display, and when I got wind that Dell had a sale on their widescreen displays, I got my credit card revved. Three days later, a courier delivered my new screen.

Now, with Dell you get good value, a three-year warranty and industrial design that is firmly placed in the mid-nineties. The 2005FPW is dressed in a plastic shell in standard Dell dark grey, with the occasional silver highlight. However, build quality is very good, and the monitor ships with a robust grey and silver stand with cable management and height adjustment. The stand also allows the display to be rotated through ninety degrees to display a portrait image, though I have not tried this myself as I do not have enough vertical room on my desk. I believe this functionality will work without additional software on any Mac that is running OS X 10.4 Tiger and has an ATI graphics card.

As well as this feature, the monitor is stuffed with other goodies as well. There are four inputs (DVI, VGA, RGB and Composite) as well as a plethora of display modes, including a very funky picture-in-picture mode, which allows TV monitoring or multiple PCs to be used in you have them cabled correctly. The monitor has four USB ports, and acts as a USB hub - two are on the back and two are on the side. Dell also throws in power, DVI and VGA cables in the box but as you might expect on a higher-end product, none of the cables are captive to the monitor.

The only thing that is missing are speakers. I personally see this as no great loss, as monitor speakers are rarely of good quality. Dell will send a media bar  with speakers as an add on to the monitor if required,

Yada, Yada, Yada - What Does It Look Like?

In a word & superb! Connected via DVI, a bright and clear image of my Tiger desktop is displayed, and running through the calibration screens shows that no changes are necessary. Backlighting is strong and even across the display, and contrast looks fine. DVD and video playback shows little smearing to my jaded eyes, though I have to admit I am not a big movie watcher while I sit in front of my computer. Of course, DVDs play back in the 16:9 aspect ratio they were designed for, and the excellent contrast ratio means that blacks look black and a 'washed-out' image is not seen.

I do play the occasional game, though, so I fired up Halo to see how the Dell performed. After adjusting the monitor to display the image in the centre of the screen rather than stretching it to fill the display, I was pleased to see that here too the image was bright and showed little ghosting.

What I was really pleased with was the way this display affects my workflow. In Photoshop, you can push the took palettes to the edge of the screen while working on a large representation of your image, browsing images is a much nicer experience as well, and iPhoto really works best on a widescreen display. The Finder takes on a new ease of use as it is easier to drag and drop between two windows that you can actually see, and applications like Mail, iTunes or NetNewsWire really benefit from the extra real estate.

The Bottom Line

Of course, it makes sense that the extra screen real estate that a 1680x1050 display gives you will improve your computing experience, and it s a no-brainer if the price is right. Here in the UK, you can pick up a decent, brand name 17" 1280x1024 4:3 ratio panel for around £170, or US$300. A 19" 4:3 panel will give you the same resolution for around £240, or US$425. This panel lists with Dell at £507 (US$900), which is £40 less than the price for a 20" Apple Cinema Display. However, I got it from Dell at £325 (US$575), substantially less than the Apple product. I am lead to believe from some of the posts I see online that in the US, rebates can bring the price below the $400 mark!

I can remember just a few years ago that 17" CRTs or 15" TFT flat panels were priced at just over £300. The fact that for a little more you can today get such a fantastic display amazes me. As you spend all of your computing time looking at your monitor, and such a device should last longer than your current Macintosh, I feel that these displays represent excellent value for money.

MyMac Rating:

At this sort of price, I have no hesitation at all at giving the Dell UltraSharp 2005FPW 20" Widescreen Display a mark of five out five.

5 out of 5

The Asus MB16AMT display touchscreen will not be accurate if you have more than your primary (e.g., laptop) and the Asus displays. I use 3 screens and with the Asus, it would 4. My primary purpose of purchasing this product was to be able to easily annotate 'shared' desktop presentations during online meeting and web conference calls e.g., skype, zoom, webex, etc. With 2 screens, the accuracy of the touchscreen with the underlying image/slide/application is correct but with 3 or more, then the Asus touchscreen features do not work. It the touchscreen capability reverts to the primary screen. So, if you have PowerPoint Presenters display on your primary, the presentation screen being the Asus and Outlook at the 3rd, the touchscreen capability will be mouse-like -- nothing consistent -- on the primary. therefore not usable. Again, if you will be using the Asus as the second screen of a two-screen configuration -- all is good.

The picture quality is great, sound through the display not so good via USB-C. The menu operation via a single 4-way button is cumbersome but manageable. A better option (though not perfect) is downloading the desktop application called Display Widget. while the application tries to replicate the menu button operation via graphical UI, some of the commands do not seem to be actually work e.g., changing orientation. if you turn off "auto" via the display menu button (which i could not get to detect rotations), and change rotations via the application, this will work. i could not get the application to turn off "auto" configuration and allow for manual. I first had to turn it off via the menu button. If you use the Technical Support Application selection, the Portrait Displays web page is launched via browser. The are the OEM. Keep in mind, the application is downloadable from the Asus Support page, as is the Windows driver for USB, and the user guide manual -- not included in the product box.

I am a photographer, and I replaced a dying, elderly and vastly more expensive monitor with a larger color space with this product, due to budgeting constraints and wanting to see my output as others do. It doesn't do me any good to spend thousands on a monitor so that I can edit my Adobe color space photos but lose everything but sRGB as soon as it leaves my office.

So, as I'm used to that larger color space, sRGB just looks a bit dull. But, it's as good as any non-professional monitor will do and far better than any printed version will ever be. And, the software that comes with the monitor is happy to switch between multiple presets automatically when switching between programs, so I can punch up the colors for web surfing and very occasional gaming, but have the monitor automatically switch to an accurate setting when I start Photoshop.

If this monitor lasts, and I have no indication that it won't, it'll have been an excellent investment.

UPDATE: Buy the Acer Nitro XZ342CK monitor instead. There are fewer issues with the Acer than with this Nixeus. After deciding to return the Nixeus, I ordered the Acer as a replacement. I'm glad I did, since the Acer is $50 cheaper, and is far better in almost every way. HDR looks more realistic with the Acer, the color accuracy is better, text smears less, and the stand that comes with the Acer has height and swivel adjustments. The Nixeus has red lights on the back. Ultimately, it's your choice as to which monitor to get. However, the Acer monitor knocks the Nixeus monitor out of the park in every way I can think of.
https://www.amazon.com/Acer-XZ342CK-Pbmiiphx-Certified-DisplayHDR400/dp/B08BW3XS12

I’ll start off with the good stuff: This is a 1440p ultra wide 144hz monitor for under $600. This monitor is a good value, considering many other monitors with similar specs cost far more. However, the Acer Nitro ultra wide is $50 cheaper as of this review, and might be a better value. Since I don’t own one currently, I can’t tell you which one to get. The particular Nixeus monitor I got had great contrast, and great color accuracy. However, these panels are unique so your mileage may vary.

I’m returning this monitor to get the Acer instead for a couple of reasons: firstly, ghosting, text smearing, and whiteness uniformity are all issues with this monitor. The monitor I got has a noticeably darker patch in the center when viewing a uniform white screen. Pixel response times aren’t great, and things tend to smear when doing something quickly. Additionally, my monitor had a bright spot on the bottom center of the monitor. It’s a green stuck pixel. Nixeus has a horrible policy with stuck/dead pixels (you need 7 to qualify for any warranty service). Luckily, Amazon has great customer service.

In short, this monitor offers decent value for the money, but be aware that Nixeus will NOT help you with any dead or stuck pixels. I'm returning this monitor because of its issues.

With multiple computers on my desk being used for different purposes, it really helps to have a decent size and good quality monitor for each. Up until now, I have only been using the laptop screen on my Mac while my PC’s have reasonably large monitors. I ordered this 22-inch Computer Monitor by Real HD specifically for use with my MacBook. This monitor just fits onto my desk with 2 other monitors (whew) although it sits notably lower than my other monitors and there is no vertical adjustment. The image quality using HDMI is quite good as seen in the last photo below which is one of my recent underwater photographs. This monitor is super lightweight, especially compared to my others. This device was truly plug and play, I hooked it up, powered it on, and it immediately mirrored the desktop display on my Mac. Next I closed the laptop lid since I do not want to use the small laptop display when connected to this much larger monitor and bingo, my Mac display stayed visible on this new monitor and my mouse and keyboard continued to function so this monitor has achieved my goal. Besides the low vertical height, there is another minor annoyance with this monitor and that is the manual controls for settings including the On/Off button, brightness, contrast, blue light level, etcetera. The control buttons are underneath the screen and there are absolutely no labels, not even in the very minimal user manual so it takes some trial and error to figure out which buttons do what actions. For the price (at the time of this review) of $75.99, this is a monitor worth considering.

I bought 3 of these in a row within a month apart. All 3 came with 1 dead pixels in different locations of the screen. Manufacturers will tell you that up to 3-5 dead pixels is normal but this is just their way of being lazy. It just means that the manufacturer does not want pay to increase their quality control and filter out the bad monitors which would cost them $$$ and that any monitor that has under 3-5 pixels is considered new condition. However, ask any consumer if they would accept a monitor with a dead pixel in it on a brand new monitor and 100% of them would say no and return it. It is not acceptable to pay for a brand new item and it comes with a problem. It's like paying for a brand new windshield for your car with a small crack already in it. No one would accept that. I am exchanging all 3 monitors. If I receive the new monitors with dead pixels in it again I am reporting this problem to the BBB and will never buy a ViewSonic product again.

Panel is 1440p IPS , 60 hz, 10 bit , LED blacklit, VESA mount

A positive is that the AC/DC inverter is built into the back so no ugly brick AND uses standard 3 prong / IEC C13 plug . No barrel plug if that matters

OSD /settings is average , fine.

This is compared to LG GL850 / GL83A panel which cost nearly double at 400-500$ , a IPS 1440p 144hz monitor

I still don't know what the difference between these connections is, but I do know now that I can't use the digital aspects of my new monitor. ****** IMPORTANT ****** Before I start - As everyone has said on these reviews before, the colors look very pale and washed out once you get the monitor. I highly recommend that you Google or go on YouTube and search for this specific video "ASUS VG248QE Color Settings / ICC Profile (and discussing lightboost)". The uploader's name is Static Sleet and the video was uploaded in October 2015. There are other websites that teach you how and some Reddit threads that do the same, but this video is extremely straightforward on what you should do as soon as you get your monitor out of the box.

Now, I was very hesitant in getting this monitor because I'm a pretty cautious consumer and read all the one star reviews. I was so afraid of being one of the dead / stuck pixel receivers, but whew! Everything is perfect. I just upgraded from a 20 inch, 60hz, and not a 1ms response time monitor and the difference is staggering. I have this new one and my old one set up side by side and it's like night and day. This monitor is much more bright, vibrant, and crisp than the blurry and dull image I see on the old monitor. The box was well packaged and the monitor very secure, and there are no scratches or any parts bent / out of place.

Sure, it's not one of the more expensive 1440p models that boast G-Sync and yada yada, but if you're on a budget constraint that allows you only to get monitors around 300-400$, this is a steal. So, if you're like me, stop looking at the $600 - $1000+ monitors and narrow them down to two models - this ASUS VG248QE or its 27" counterpart, or the BenQ Zowie XL2411Z that is probably the other one you've been looking at. The only thing that you should do now is go on Google / YouTube again and search "BenQ XL2411Z vs ASUS VG248QE 144 hz Monitor Comparison - Which is best for YOU?" by Gus Tech. As you can see through the comments and other sites that pit these two versus each other, ASUS blows BenQ out of the park when it comes to picture QUALITY (color, vibrance, etc). The BenQ is claimed to be more efficient however in games because it stays true and strong in those parts of FPS shooters like CS:GO where you're turning around corners and shooting people very quickly. As a FPS fanatic, I can tell you that it is not noticeable at all. It was a no brainer for me to choose the ASUS model after looking at the video I just mentioned because a little "efficiency" is not worth sacrificing an amazing picture for. Unless you're some world champ shooter that somehow notices this little difference when you're turning around corners, you're not going to notice any difference in those terms at all. The BenQ colors are just terrible. I wouldn't want to play video games to be better if I'm not going to like what I'm looking at, and you probably don't want that either.

Also, one thing I was debating on before getting this model was whether or not I should get the 27". My 20" is so tiny and I was so sick of it, so I thought - the bigger the better. However, I looked up "Is a 27 monitor and 1080p" worth it and the results weren't that great. Many said that you would notice a loss of quality almost immediately unless your computer is extremely far away from you and to the back of the desk, but that wasn't me because my monitor is sitting only an arm's length away. I also read on Reddit from people with 27" models that it was simply too big for them and they actually got worse at high speed video games because their eyes couldn't keep up with everything that was going on in the corners and sides of the screen. So, following that advice, I got the 24" because the quality would seemingly be at 'perfect' balance and not stretched out onto a 27" screen and because I didn't want the problem of not being able to see everything happening if I'm playing a fast-paced video game. I'm very glad that I listened to them! I'm actually having trouble sometimes keeping up with this screen too because it's also very big when an arm's length away. If I had a 27", it would be great for watching some movies far away but.. not at all for games unless you have additional eyes beside your ears. An added bonus with this is that you'll be saving 50$ too (by not getting the bigger model)!

Overall, I think you should get this VG248QE if your budget falls around here and you're looking for an entry-level 144hz, 1ms, 1080p monitor. Yes, there are lots of one star reviews because of misplaced pixels, but I can tell you after hours of searching for monitors before I got this one that there is only ONE monitor I found with only 4-5 star reviews - and it cost 2300$ and only like two or three people actually reviewed it.. Every monitor you come by will have problems from consumers such as dead pixels, power failures, the monitor simply dying, buttons falling off, etc. The VG248QE will have a lot more of these simply because of the sheer amount of people that have bought this over the years. When you're producing a lot of these monitors, obviously some are bound to fail. but this model, as you can tell, is always holding strong near 80% five star reviews - which is a lot higher than other monitors that I have seen on Amazon. Just pray that you're not in the 20% and buy it. Don't press your back button and go to the BenQ. Just get it!!

Oh, and a lot of people seem to not be getting DVI-D cables with their orders so I read those reviews and got the gold plated, ferrite, DVI-D cable that is under the "products frequently bought with this" because I thought I wouldn't be getting one. Well, I did get one with the monitor. So, take that with a grain of salt; you may or may not get one.

I'm actually driving it through an IOGear 4-port HDMI KVM switch (GCS1794)...two Mac Minis, an ASUS Zenbook laptop and a home-built desktop with an NVIDIA GTX650 graphics card (this is my main machine, the one that I use for photo processing, and the reason I bought this monitor).
