What a disappointment! If I could have tried this in a physical store I'd never have bought it! Only the Displayport connectors work correctly. The USB-C and HDMI connectors do not work correctly at all. As a "Displayport-only" monitor it's superb, but if you're looking for USB-C to have a single connection to power your laptop, or HDMI, look elsewhere.

The first problem with this monitor is the lousy stand. It's clearly designed by an artist with no concept of how the monitor needs to fit on the desk. Unlike all of my other monitors' included stands, that allow the monitor to sit on the edge of the desk, this monitor's stand is designed in such a way that it wastes about 4-6 inches behind the monitor. The bottom of the stand is curved instead of flat, so you can't place things over the stand.

The USB-C and HDMI inputs only support 1080p instead of 1440p, leading to a soft image. When I plugged the HDMI input into my older Mac, something must have been wrong with the metadata because the Mac made the text unusually large.

When I use USB-C to power my laptop, the monitor flickers. Clearly the internal power supply isn't able to drive both the monitor and the laptop at the same time. In the 10 or so hours that I powered my laptop with USB-C, the connection unexpectedly dropped.

I was able to get full resolution (5120x1440) via two different techniques. The first was to use both the USB-C and HDMI connectors on my laptop, and run the monitor in a mode where it acts as two monitors side-by-side. This was unsatisfactory, as the color tone was different for half of the screen, and placing a window in the middle acted strange. (I also encountered the flickering and disconnects from the poor USB-C support.) The other way was to use the Displayport connector. This really disappointed me, as I wanted to get rid of my USB-C docking station. I haven't gotten USB to run over Displayport, but I don't know if it's a limitation of my docking station or the monitor.

There are only three USB ports, not enough for a monitor.

When you do find a third party VESA mount, you'll find the power socket and DVI port on the back of the monitor are incredibly inconvenient and placed so that it is almost impossible to install cabling without extreme measures. I replaced two aging Acer 1920x1080 23" monitors with this screen, and I love it. The total resolution is less -- 2560 vs 3840 -- but it's much more useable. With two monitors, I usually found myself actually working on one screen, and only occasionally dragging something over to the other one or only using part of it. With the LG, there's no bezel in the middle and the amount of space is exactly right. I'm in my mid-40s and my eyesight isn't what it used to be, so I am happy for the larger vertical size. I had considered a 3440 x 1440 screen like the 34UM95 but I didn't want to get a bigger monitor just to end up squinting at it again. So, for me, this size is perfect.

Setup was easy. I purchased a 10' DisplayPort cable along with the monitor, and my 780TI immediately recognized it and set its resolution accordingly. The image is shockingly better than the old Acers. The darks are darker and not washed out, and the brights are brighter without being overbearing. The panel quality seems excellent; I have not noticed any uniformity issues or light bleed.

It's perfect for games, too. The resolution gives you an expanded field of view, but unlike 1440p it isn't so high that it strains your graphic card. Response time is excellent as well.

The screen has not dead nor stuck pixels as defined in wiki. It has not bleeding at all. But there are thousands of green pixels that fire up incorrectly and flashing (not static). Specially annoying with photos and videos.

Most of the screen is perfect. The problem mentioned happens mainly in a vertical band at the left. Starting at 7.5 cm from the left screen edge and with a width of 7.5 cm also (height is from top to bottom).

I tried each combination of resolutions and cables (VGA, DVI and HDMI) with the same results. I do it with Windows and Linux, and with two PC and two laptops.

The problem is moderated with white text windows. And it is intolerable with movies or pictures in that area of the screen. When moving a photo with green tones into this area, many green pixels become completely saturated up to the maximum bright.

Better find another brand.

It works, the chassis looks great and most importantly it was received with no dead pixels which is why I gave it 2 stars but in terms of picture quality it should be skipped in favor of a different model or even technology. IPS panels aren't a zillion bucks anymore and you can get a similarly sized S-IPS panel (like the 21.5" LG) for less than 50 bucks more than this dismal performer and be infinitely more satisfied.

With things like this I always recommend going to a local computer or electronics store and see the monitor in person, THEN come to Amazon to get the great price.

I got this monitor for $1600 at microcenter almost a year ago now. I have zero issues with it. Yes there is a fan on the monitor and it will continue to run even after you turn it off(until the internals cool off enough then it does shut off) but i don't hear it over my computer fans anyway so it's a total non issue unless you want absolute and complete silence. The colors are unmatched(maybe some 10k+ reference monitors are better) but when you combine it with the high brightness and high refresh there just is no other monitor on the market like it.

There is something people don't talk about though, but it's not just this monitor. Local dimming can improve the image in certain scenarios but it also creates "halo" affects. if you have a black background with white text (or mouse cursor) it will have a bloom glow from the backlighting zone making the black around it be much brighter. Now our eyeballs will perceive this bloom to some extent anyway but be aware it is noticeable. may not bother some people, but others won't be able to tolerate it. you can turn local dimming off, but if you do that the blacks become gray not really black anymore.

Until micro led monitors become a reality or the burn in with oled gets fixed(which it realistically won't just like plasma back in the day) this is as good as you'll get. At least until they come out with new display standards where more frames can be pushed at 4k but that's no less than 3+ years out.

There is a lot to like about this monitor. We moved and my new WFH office desk is smaller (but nicer), and I was resetting my office equipment setup. I didn't have room for a 27" monitor, so I searched for a 24" high quality monitor with a nice Blue Light Filter, the highest resolution possible (for the size), and USB-C connectivity. The Asus PA248CNV checked all the boxes perfectly. Being able to pivot the monitor 90 degrees to work on "tall" spreadsheet models is a bonus (I like having the 1920 resolution on the portrait mode.)

I purchased this after a failed effort getting a Dell S2422HZ monitor to work. The Dell didn't like connecting to the MB Air, and despite almost two hours of debugging (inc. driver update attempts, reworking cable options to make sure I had PD compatible cables, config changes to my MB), I decided to just return the Dell and go with the Asus.

The Asus was a bit more money ($60 more with tax), but here are the benefits of spending a few more bucks:
- The single cable connectivity for power and video to my MB Air was plug and play from the get-go. Makes my desktop setup clean with a single cable to my Mac, and 100% easy peasy worked from the get go.
- The 1920 x 1200 resolution is a higher than the 1920 x 1080 on the Dell. working with emails, spreadsheets, etc. is nicer with the extra 11% of video real estate is nice to have, and functionally it works more like 15-20%+ more real estate since some things are fixed like menu bars, etc.
- The color perfectly matches my gray MB Air
- The extra USB ports on the monitor work as a seamless hub, and even includes a higher power USB A port to charge my iPhone faster (1.5 amps / 7.5 watts). The other three USB A ports work great for connecting my audio Dac, high res video conference camera, and wireless mouse. Again, all the cables and connectors are hidden away making everything sleek and clean.
- This monitor has a real physical on/off switch. We travel a lot, and being able to just use the off switch to shut everything down when we leave (not wasting power) is a wonderful thing. Wish more companies would do this.
- The monitor arrived pre-calibrated with documentation showing its color settings. The monitor is bright, with incredible color accuracy.
- The menus for the monitor are super easy to access via the front buttons.
- The physical setup and attaching the stand didn't require any tools, and was able to be completed in literally 15 seconds.

Things I wish the monitor had:
- Another 1 or 2 USB-C ports (instead of USB-A). The world is moving towards USB-C, and it would be nice to have that sort of future proof setup. But, most everything I'm attaching are USB-A, so not a current issue.
- A smaller footprint on the stand - others monitors are able to get by with a 6.75 - 7 inch deep stand, and this one is more like 8 inches.

You interact with your monitor more than anything on your computer - show some self care and invest in one that gives you clarity, crispness, and great ergonomics. The Asus is does this, plus makes my office look of so snap!

I bought this monitor because I disliked having to switch browsing windows every second to get scattered bits of information. Having two windows comfortably fit on the same screen saves me significant time so I am extremely happy with my purchase.

And yes, I do use shortcuts to switch between windows, but it's still far more convenient to fit two active windows on one screen. I'm always searching information, or referencing something or other while working so it's great to have essentially two monitors fit onto one screen.

The trade off is that using one window, say Chrome, is a complete waste. Most sites aren't fitted for 21:9 aspect ratio so you'll be staring at the left side of your monitor more often than not. For browsers it's essential that you are comfortable with the wasted space, or that you're happy making use of that space for two windows.

A word of caution:
If you are buying this to acquire more space to do color sensitive work then beware that there is significant back-light bleeding at the four corners. The colors themselves are actually quite good, but the back-light bleeding will probably annoy you. I only notice this when watching dark movies so it isn't a critical issue.

TLDR: If you have a Mac and are struggling with the aspect ratio being correct, check out the BetterDisplay application that lets you add custom resolutions.

I originally gave this a pretty poor review and I'm updating now as I realize it may be a Mac issue and not the display. I have a 2020 MacBook Air M1, and by default, the only resolution that actually looks like the aspect ratio is correct is 2560x1600, but everything is a bit too small to read. 2560x1600 is a 16:10 aspect ratio. All of the available resolutions were not 16:10, so you get stretching when choosing them. By default, this display looks great at 1680x1050, but that wasn't an option to choose in Mac's settings. I found a program called BetterDisplay that allowed me to configure additional resolutions and add more 16:10 aspect ratio options. Once I did that, everything looks great.

The speakers are awful, but I didn't expect to use them anyway so I don't really care.

This is an initial review, I will update later with a more thorough review of the picture quality. I'm an engineer, so I'm technologically savvy and very picky, but not well versed in monitor picture quality metrics, so I will run some tests on a later date. I think it's important to state that I am planning to use this monitor for a multiscreen solution, so a thin bezel was a factor in my purchase. If you plan to line up more than one of these side by side, you would have noticed this model and perhaps the ASUS VN247H-P for their relatively thinner bezels. There isn't a whole lot of information out for these two monitors, because they both came out just this year about a month ago, so maybe you can take advantage of the research I did. When I bought this monitor the ASUS model had free shipping and a discount making it $20 cheaper, but at the time of writing that discount is gone and the monitor is actually $10 more compared to this one. I'm guessing because that monitor is very popular as well.

This monitor is amazingly sleek, imagine a 1.5 cm thick slice of glass and metal, and all the connectors barely protrude out the back. The packaging it came in was two slabs of styrofoam with seemingly not much space in between, where the monitor was sandwiched. I was surprised because this monitor is actually frameless, and that was not stated in the description, which is obviously great. A comparable model with a frameless design is the ASUS MX239H, also out recently, but that one retails for $230. I really think Acer outdid itself here, but their marketing is just not as ostentatious about their product as ASUS. Why do I say that? Besides the design of this monitor matching the $230 counterpart of ASUS, this monitor is IPS! while the earlier ASUS model I mentioned (now selling for $190) is only TN! AND the stand of that monitor has received complaints to be flimsy. Not here, this stand is solid and it is METAL, not plastic! A $700 monitor I bought a few years back from Samsung didn't even have this! I doubt the ASUS counterpart does either. This is in every way a $230+ product if I use ASUS as a benchmark, which of course is legitimate as ASUS is revered as the more reliable and quality brand. This is a steal.

Part of why I leaned towards the Acer because there is another reviewer, much better informed than myself regarding picture quality that reviewed about half a dozen of these screens, and gave ASUS a fairly low rating in general and said the one bright spot was another Acer he got. Regarding the IPS, I can have this screen dead perpendicular to my face and still make out the picture, it's that ridiculous. Not that you ever need to watch movies that way but if that's your thing, your time has come. IPS sells at a premium to TN so this is a great feature over the ASUS. On arrival, there are no stuck or dead pixels that I can see yet, I will check again more throughly later. One word of caution, although frameless, the screen is not borderless. Like the $230 ASUS model, the border just sits under the glass, I will update with the exact width later, but I'm guessing it's 1.5 cm. I think the fact that Acer is sort of an underdog to ASUS and has less marketing forces it to price its products more aggressively so this is definitely great value for a beautiful piece of hardware.

Another thing regarding Amazon, their customer service is phenomenal. The monitor was shipped to an old address of mine. My orders default to that address from 5 years ago, and presumably I did not click my new address or it did not register. Either way, when I called, Erin H from customer service did not give me any hassle, did not charge extra shipping, and actually shipped me another unit immediately! She even put in 2-day shipping! This is unreal. So they basically took the risk of losing or breaking that other monitor and just gave me a brand new different unit at no charge with faster shipping. The unit came the next day. I honestly cannot think of a way their service can be improved. I hope Erin gets a raise.

All in all, I'm glad I went through Amazon. My alternative was newegg, and nothing against them, I'm just saying Amazon is great. The monitor is spectacular. I will update later with some picture tests and metrics. I have not tweaked anything not downloaded any color drivers/enhancers as some ASUS monitor reviewers suggest as crucial for their models.

ASUS has always had a reputation for great products and this monitor is no exception. The display is bright, vivid, and clear. Get your input cable (VGA, DVI, or HDMI) and power cable, plug them in, and it's on. It has multiple display modes, all of which you can modify to your tastes (something I haven't done as I'm lazy and lean to having the most versatile settings for any sort of work or gaming I'm doing). When I purchased this particular monitor, it was selling for around $140 USD, and I read some reviews about how it is only decent (on a scale of horrible/lame/decent/good/great/excellent) for doing visual-based editing/creation work (meaning videos/movies, 2D/3D modeling, photo work, and whatever else I left out). After doing some light research, I believe I concur with them, but only if it's regarding any professional level work. Any hobbyist or student will be pleased enough with it.

Now there are a few annoyances with it (hence my 4 stars instead of 5), which mainly concern the options/side-features rather than the point of having a monitor (it's a display, which it does fantastically). It has an almost-fixed volume (it has built-in speakers, which produce sound like it is coming from behind the LCD screen itself). There are two buttons on the front to adjust the volume, which are horrible for adjusting it from blasting to quiet or vice versa. You can barely make out the adjustment buttons on the bottom right corner of the monitor in the dark, so you need an additional light source other than the monitor to see them enough. The volume is on a scale of 0-100 (like most), and the auto-adjustment (holding down up/down) is slower than just rapidly pressing them over and over again. I should be slightly more clear on this: the numbers change, but the volume itself seems to be reluctant to change with the numbers (if it was at 75, turning it down isn't noticeable until 50, at least significantly, and vice versa). A dial would have been much better. Now the sound quality is good, but some $30-50 USD pair of 2.1 channel speakers (or higher) would do much better. Another disappointing side-feature was that there wasn't a built-in USB hub. Not required, but a good majority of LCD monitors have them as a common feature. The last one is that the screen is a matte finish, not a glossy finish. Depending on who you are, this is a good or a bad thing. I personally find matte finishes annoying, as they're slightly harder to clean without leaving streaks (a bit of a neat freak).

All in all, it's a great monitor for most people. For those who want extras in addition to a display, go digging around a little more in Amazon (or wherever else) for one. There are a bunch that will have what you are after around $140 USD. Lastly, for that little niche of people, save your money and buy one that's $250 USD or more for your really clear and exemplary display.

I was planning to use this to replace an older monitor with a scratched screen, but it's not going to work form my setup. The negatives out weigh the positives in my case.

Pros: IPS and easy on the eyes. Includes the HDMI cable. Thinner bezels. Easy setup. 100Hz is better than my current 60Hz. Fast response time.

Cons: only two inputs - HDMI and VGA (no Display Port). No audio - there's no speaker. Not VESA compliant so you're stuck using the included mount. Included mount is not adjustable for height - you can tilt somewhat, but that's it. Wobbly on the mount. Connections are facing the bottom so cords stick out at the bottom.

The monitor has a nice thin body for the most part with the bottom portion being much more "chunky" so you can attach the mount (which my monitor constantly rocks slightly side to side while I type). As mentioned, it's not VESA compatible which means I cannot replace the monitor I wanted to because that's on a dual monitor mount on my desk - which does not wobble!

On the back bottom section you have three ports. One for power supply connection, one for VGA and the last one for HDMI. Very minimal which wouldn't be too bad if it were better positioned but as they are now, your connection and cords are going to be sticking out from the bottom of the monitor.

There are a few buttons located on the bottom as well for the power and then OSD menu and options. You can select a few preset themes - movies, standard, ECO, Graphics, Dark Room, etc. You can also manually set it (custom).

There was no audio (speaker) with this monitor but apparently some do have audio as my Windows 11 PC detected the monitor and then set it as the default audio output source which caused me to do some research and figure out how to have my second monitor which has a speaker set as my output. Another option would be to connect speakers and if your into gaming you probably already have that type of setup so no worries. Of course a lot of gamers have more than one monitor setups so keep in mind the mount setup for this one as well as the two connection types for input.

Overall it is an okay monitor and if it was VESA compliant I'd use it. As it is, this will probably be used with our second PC the boys used or as a separate screen for a game console using the HDMI input.

I'll start by saying that I'm writing this review since I have to purchase another unit since mine broke under normal usage conditions and AOC assumes no resposibility. I'm trying to not be too mad about, and writing a pretty objective review. I've used it happily during more than a year just about daily, so I do like it. Now the problem is that there aren't really too many alternatives. This AOC screen has lower resolution and is much thicker than technically possible nowadays, so I really don't get why those advances aren't applied to these screens. Also, the driver for the screen is pretty bad and doesn't allow for color calibration, so color sensitive work (photoshop) is out of the question. I do prefer Asus's execution of the USB screen (MB168B), but they really messed up with the stand. The folding case stand takes way too much surface to be practical in a library booth or anywhere where space is a concern. If not I would much rather their thinner and lighter product. But since they don't, the AOC screen is king, albeit due to lack of better options.

So, when the monitor works ok, the concept is so good that it's downsides don't really matter because there isn't anything better. It works, it's reliable, and it's small and light enough to always be with you in a backpack. Now since this is a travel monitor basically, I would really appreciate a combination of Asus's thin profile, and AOC's small stand solution. Whether or not touchscreen is a techincal posibility nowadays I don't know, but considering that we pay less for tablets than a simple screen, and that the former are independant computers, it's obvious that USB monitors are lacking due to demand. It would also be nice to have a resistant screen with a glass surface. In my case, my monitor screen failed after developing a pressure point on the screen, under light, normal use. It has always been kept flat with the monitor screen towards a laptop, so it's imposible for the screen to take an impact without destroying the laptop first. But since this item is a need and not a want, and their aren't better options, I have no other choice than to buy another unit. All in all, it's ok and functional, but it really wouldn't be your first choice should there be better options.

As a summary, what they should include in the future is:
-Thinner screen (If Asus can, why can't you)
-1080p resolution (It doesn't make sense for your phone to have better resolution than your monitor)
-Touchscreen capability as an option (After all, scrolling documents via touch does make life easier, and most of all, touchscreen is affordable enough for it to not add significantly to the price to just have it there in case you need or want it)
-Please maintain the small folding stand!
-Glass screen surface

If they improved that, I would be more than willing to pay 200$ for a monitor, but as it stands, I'm just purchasing another unit because I need one. Thanks for reading, and I hope it was helpfull!

I bought this to reward myself for getting a scholarship, (which is rather counter-intuitive, but hey you need to celebrate.) and I was pleasantly surprised. This was not before swearing at the computer for about 30 minutes trying to install all the drivers the right way, but once it worked, the 3d effects were quite amazing.

I was disappointed to find that nothing really jumped OUT at you, but instead it felt like the monitor was a window to a smaller world. Nevertheless, it was very impressive. It didn't put as much of a press on my computer as all the reviews said it would, I was still getting very acceptable framerates playing rather demanding games using a GTX 275.

The glasses are pretty cool looking, and as a small bonus, they come charged enough to work out of the box. The monitor is really fantastic too, beautiful 1080p resolution, bright colors, and you can adjust it a lot of different ways for the best angle.

I originally got this for the games, and I was really shocked at how well all of them worked, I've yet to reach a game that doesn't support it.

My beef with this is the price. I would have waited if I wasn't so impulsive. The price was just a bit too high for what you get. It's cool and everything, but you are definitely paying a premium for new technology. It is cool enough that I'm keeping it though - I'm not regretting it THAT much.

And since this is a new technology, the amount of media for this is pretty small for everything but the games. There are a couple picture sets on nvidia website, but aside from that, you are pretty much stuck playing games for the 3d effect.

tl;dr: If you have the money, and PLAY GAMES, I'd recommend this. Otherwise, wait it out.

This has been a real lifesaver. I wanted to take a short break from being stuck inside constantly, and so was planning to go rent a remote airbnb for a couple weeks for myself. However, i rely heavily on having two monitors at home, and so I was pretty sure i'd be annoyed if i only had my laptop screen.

This solved that problem! Setup is easy (I have a laptop with USB-C, which is best as it removes the need for separate power to the display, but you can also use HDMI, and with USB-C you just plug in the included cord), and it worked right away without needing drivers or anything.

Quality of the monitor itself is pretty good--metal construction that feels solid, it comes with a screen protector you can apply if you want, and a nice leather-y cover that folds back to become a stand (has two angle/height options). It's slim when all packed up, so very easy to carry alongside a laptop (I just keep it in my backpack in front of the laptop sleeve) and to set up on a flat surface. You can also detach the cover (it's held by magnets) if you want to use without.

The monitor has speakers which if you need alert sounds are sufficient, but nothing to write home about. The mechanism for adjusting settings is a bit finicky (it's like a two way dial with a press, hard to describe) but works fine and I got there.

In terms of image quality, the white balance is kind of off (some very light colours would be difficult to see on the monitor, but if shifted back to my laptop screen then i could read them fine) but it only seemed to matter for the windows of one application. You could probably use this for more intensive applications if you wanted, but I wouldn't for anything like photo editing for that reason. For work stuff like word/excel/email/etc. it's perfect, though.

Purchased for home office use and watching media (youtube, Netflix etc.). I used to have dual 22-inch 60hz monitors, but replaced them with this one. I use the MS Windows "Snap dual monitor" capability to mimic a physical dual monitor set-up. It look a little getting used to, but now I enjoy only having to worry about one monitor and one set of cables versus two w/ a docking station.

The monitor feels sturdy/weighty, I can adjust the height and the screen stays where I put it (some reviewers said this didn't work when they tried). I spend much time typing for work and the sharpness of the typed text online, or in MS Word documents etc. is crisp. My laptop's graphics card can handle 100MHZ refresh, so I use that setting with the monitor. I don't use the monitor for gaming so I cannot attest to the monitor's performance with that.

The light at the bottom of the monitor can be changed to cycle through various colors. I set mine to change every 5 minutes. Nothing special, but it's a neat future to have for fun.

I really like how the power button and buttons to adjust the monitor are on the right side. I like being able to gently push the power button and everything is ready to go versus how some monitors have their buttons on the underside. the other not so popular feature I like is having the audio jack on the bottom left side of the monitor, it's helpful for headphone users.

This monitor has a beautiful picture when it works but it's made in China so expect it to die in just a couple years. I picked up one at Goodwill for $15 that looked to be in good shape but it wouldn't turn on and the power button blinked all the time like it was in power saving mode. Upon taking it apart I found that all the capacitors in the power supply were bad which is something that also happens with motherboards, PC power supplies and other devices made in China. The capacitors are easy to replace though and it's working great again now. For some interesting reading Google "China capacitor problems" and check out badcaps dot net for photos of what they look like.

What a bargin and what a monitor! Clear, crisp, bright with easy adjustments and certainly is a lot easier on these tired, old eyes. There were two things they could do to make it better: (1) Add a couple of USB ports like Dell and a few others have and (2) Create a mode that shuts the monitor down when the computer goes off. The current one goes into an economy mode but from I can see it never shuts off.

Both are minor inconveniences considering how much monitor I got for my money!

Okay, what's lacking - there is no HDMI input. Does that matter? Not really, I discovered. I had an Amazon basics HDMI to DVI adapter cable - and that's all it took - and since there are no speakers in the Monitor, an HDMI input would be misleading.

I absolutely love having the extra 120 pixels vertical the 1920x1200 offers over the usual 1920x1080 of an HD screen. I can slide show HD pictures without losing the menus at top or bottom - same for movies. I can put two pages side by side on MSWord, and the whole text of both shows up.

The anti-glare surface is like the other Dell AG screens I've seen - and to my eyes, works as it should - I don't have to dim the lights to see what I want to see. The easy access menu allows me quick changes for brightness and contrast if I'm in bright room.

Did I mention the "premium" screen standard? Mine had no pixel problems whatsoever - but if it did, I think that even a single bright pixel qualifies for a replacement with the Dell Premium Screen policy. (Of course, you should double check to see if that's still in effect.)

The stand - that is another premium feature missing from many other units - it slides up, down, pivots left and right, and will reposition to vertical as well as horizontal - all without having to release any catches or buttons. You just move it, and it goes. There's actual metal in the stand, too! (Not just plastic.) Of course, the extra pixels and sweet stand wouldn't mean much if the image weren't so gorgeous - but it is. I can see colors and details never before visible on my 17.3" laptop screen - and from angles that make it possible for others to see them as well.

It even comes with VGA and DVI cables bundled. But, as I said, I use my HDMI to DVI adapter (which I did have to get separately), and it works without a hitch. I plug it into my laptop HDMI output, and the laptop has learned to turn off the built-in screen and automatically switch to the Dell.

This is a model that has been tried and tested - and nothing is lacking, unless you want speakers.

ASUS advertises this as providing "outstanding color reproduction" but I can only get 64% of sRGB, and an independent reviewer got 63.7% as well, so it's by design! If you want high quality color reproduction, avoid this monitor. If you just need something to work with and don't mind dull colors, then this is fine.

Overall the screen quality is better than a couple other portable monitors I've seen.

Important: There are multiple versions of this monitor being sold on this amazon page over time (I hate that.) The one I just purchased does not have the kickstand and comes with a magnetic case that it stands on, and that also protects it when traveling. My version DOES have four VESA holes in the back. Based on other reviews, the kickstand version did not have VESA holes.

Also important: If your monitor settings are re-setting every time you reboot your laptop, you need an extra USB- C cable plugged in to where it says "PD USB-C" so it maintains power to keep the settings when you power down. I run mine to the included external wall plug so it always has power even if the laptop USB ports lose power when shut down.

With it powered externally like that, when I shut down my laptop the portable monitor gives a "Power Saving ZZZZ" message for a second and then goes black. When I reboot the settings stay the same.

As soon as this display unit arrived, I swapped it out for my old Dell HD Monitor, and I haven’t been disappointed. I need a large screen that accommodates several windows, with accurate graphical representation when I’m operating a client’s computer via a remote control program. But I’m a solo user and this monitor may be more suited for home use than for business settings where a mounting plate would give mobility options for the display.

The screen is bright and sharp, with "Quick View" controls to change the color balance. I chose "Low Blue Light," which gives the screen a warmer cast. It has two video sockets, HDMI and the good old blue VGA socket. It comes with an HDMI cable. Missing is the larger white DVI socket, but because HDMI is essentially DVI plus sound, if needed you could plug your HDMI cable into a computer's DVI interface with an inexpensive passive adapter.

Hidden underneath the right bottom edge of the monitor are four unlabeled control buttons. I found the lack of labeling annoying at first, but if you push any one of them, an on-screen label block pops up right above the buttons, rather than activating any other function. Once the screen labels are visible, you can push the buttons again to navigate through the menus, such as "QV" for the Quick View menu mentioned above.

No user guide was included, but an envelope the same size as the screen was taped to it, including one large folded sheet with a couple of tiny paragraphs translated into a few dozen languages, listing the website for the user guide, drivers, and other software. The other side is a pictorial “Quick Setup” guide with a mere 6 pictures – sufficient to swap out a monitor, but less useful for an initial setup from scratch.

Pros:
• very thin and lightweight
• blue light adjustment
• HDMI cable included
• built-in Audio

Cons:
• external power brick
• support foot and leg are not height adjustable
• scant documentation included
• four recessed control buttons, black on black

Despite the annoyance of the unlabeled control buttons, I’m impressed with this display. The resolution is high, images display clearly, and the cost is reasonable.

I am working on simulators of point of sale devices using Arduino Uno and ATmega 2560. This application would be fine with this touch screen display as what I need is some way of simple data entry to change settings, display settings to the user, and provide a way to trigger some events.

Unfortunately when using this display with an Arduino Uno and the original KeDei TFT library, there is very little memory available for storing data due to the small amount of memory on the Arduino and the large character font array in the KeDei library in file KeDei_font.cpp. However the touch interface seems to work with the Arduino.

The ATmega 2560 has much more memory and I was planning to use an ATmega 2560 instead of an Arduino Uno however I have run into a problem with the touch interface providing incorrect touch points when using the TFT display with the ATmega 2560. I have set the jumpers on the back of the display to 2560 however I'm still seeing incorrect touch points returned.

I have cloned the Osoyoo KeDei TFT library into my GitHub repository and have been working with the library. I have made a change to the 16x16 bitmap font table const unsigned char font16_B[95][16] in file KeDei_font.cpp of the KeDei TFT library to use an 8x8 bitmap font and to also eliminate lower case letters. This change has made a significant improvement by reducing the amount of memory consumed by the KeDei TFT library.

I have since found a font with smaller, 5x7 bitmaps, so the font table is now const unsigned char font_table[59][5] which requires me to use upper case only but really improves the amount of memory I have available. I've also modified the library to do scaling of the font to double wide or high or to triple wide or high.

I've had this monitor for about a year. Ever since, I've been wondering how I managed to deal with the dual monitor and monitor stand setup I had for so longer before that. I need 2 screens and really 3 would be better. Even a nice monitor stand is just clunky to attach to the backs of 2 monitors and position comfortably. I absolutely LOVE that this monitor is just affixed to a sturdy, but lightweight stand that easily slides up or down to adjust the height. The monitor is lightweight and so easy to move if needed. For the past several months, I had breakdown my computer setup and transport it to work elsewhere every couple of weeks. All I kept thinking was how thankful I was to have this monitor and not my dual monitors on the heavy, clunky stand. So easy to set back up now.

Another bonus is the built-in 'OnScreen Control' program. Makes it super easy to split the screens selecting from a variety of configurations. I've used several of them, but tend to just stick with the equal split screen most often. I also like that there are more settings to easily adjust the color of the screen depending on use. To cut down on the brightness, I keep it on the 'reader' setting and this works great for me. Helps my eyes not get so tired staring at my monitor all day long.

The only downside is that even though the monitor seems very large, I've lost just a tad bit of space on the split screen views versus the amount of display space I had on my 2 separate monitors. It's not really that big of a deal, but the more advanced I'm getting with my monitor setup, the more picky I'm becoming. :)

I plan to see if there is a larger one I can buy. Then I'll just this one as a 2nd setup in another room or as a TV in a spare room. I have not had any issues with bleeding, color quality or anything of that nature. I'm not a graphic artist so probably not the best person to comment on that. It's a high cost for a monitor but the benefits definitely outweigh the cost.

This is a great monitor from a brand I have never heard of!

Pros 
(1) There is very little ghosting (and it has overdrive settings).
(2) great sharpness (its 1440p).
(3) It is actually 170 HZ, not 165, so that is a small bonus of 5 HZ, making for a great gaming display and really smooth.
(4) Good colors, but not as good as a Dell 1080p 144hz monitor that I have, but better than an AOPEN monitor that I have.
(5) I love the curve; it's not that noticeable, but it is nice.
(6) The OS is really good, and you control everything via a single joystick on the back (great job on that, btw, KTC), and I like the OS it's easy to use and you can do a lot.

cons

(1) The marketing is weird for this monitor; it says that it is a HVA panel type, but what is that? There is like no info online that I can find about it. What I can tell you is that it is virtually like a VA panel type. It acts the same, like the viewing angles are not as good as my other two IPS panels but way better than a TN for example, and it does not bother me at all, as well as having black things on the screen fading when moving quickly and such, but it is not very noticeable unless you are nitpicking or trying to see it. I wish that they had said it was a VA panel or given more information about the panel type.

(2) This is really just nitpicking at this point, but the borders around the whole display are a tad bit thicker than my other monitors.

(3) The colors are great but not fantastic, so if you are working with color-sensitive stuff, maybe skip this one.

Over all, I really like this monitor, and I highly recommend it, coming from someone who loves gaming and is a programmer. Great job, KTC!

This review is just after unboxing and ~1 hour initial usage:

Unboxing the lepow portable monitor is a treat. The packaging is among the nicest I've ever received computer hardware or accessories in.

The process for operation is: power up, plug in input source, you're already using it.
The process is /not/: power up, plug in input source, select input source, scroll to input source, wait for monitor to switch to signal.

The stand is quite nice and works like an old Microsoft Surface Tablet magnetic, foldable stand/cover I used to use. The instruction book does warn that the magnets in the cover/stand are strong enough to destroy a computer, so don't stack your portable monitor on your laptop while it's operating, and to shut down and separate these if you're putting them in the same bag.

I'm really satisfied with the product, it seems to be great value for the purchase price.

The stand is still a little weird but works, and the customer service helped with the power cord issue. They did it very quickly too. Updating my review from 2 to 4 stars as this now works at the airport much better with the longer cable.

The power cable is probably less than four feet long and one of my work laptops doesn't provide power through the connection so it has to be plugged in, do you know how hard it is to try to get a plug in less than four feet of you at an airport with a place to rest the monitor. (4 feet is generous, it's probably closer to three) I paid for this and it's been sitting on the self while I use my old Asus monitor that doesn't require to be plugged into a wall outlet.

I was looking for a portable monitor to use with my Chromebook, and I came across this one. It was very affordable with a price range of less than $100, and it had a lot of features that I was looking for, such as two USB-C ports, a magnetic foldable cover, and a sharp display. I was also impressed with the packaging box. It was well-made and sturdy, and it protected the monitor during shipping.

When I first opened the box, I was surprised by how good the quality of the monitor was. It only weighs slightly less than 2 pounds, so it is very easy to carry around. I also liked the magnetic foldable cover. It protects the screen when the monitor is not in use, and it also makes it easy to store and transport the monitor.

The monitor has two USB-C ports, which allow you to connect it to your laptop, tablet, or smartphone. It also comes with a variety of cables, including a USB-C to USB-C cable, a USB-C to USB-A cable, and a HDMI to a mini HDMI cable. There is also an adapter for power supply that works well with the provided USB-A to USB-C cable.

I connected the monitor to my Chromebook using one of the USB-C ports, and it worked perfectly. It has a 15.6-inch display with a resolution of 1920 x 1080. The display was sharp and clear, and the colors were accurate. The display is also IPS, which means it has wide viewing angles. I was able to use the monitor to watch videos, browse the web, and work on documents. I was also able to use it to play games.

Overall, I am very happy with this portable monitor. It is affordable, well-made, and has a lot of features. I would definitely recommend it to anyone looking for an affordable and portable monitor.

This is a superb monitor with many of the features of $200+ monitors. The viewing angles are phenomenal, and the colors are brilliant and sharper than 90% of other monitors I have seen. It is a very thin, elegant monitor that should fit well into any household setting. I used to play Counter Strike on a bad CRT, but even the bad CRT let me play 640x480 @ 120 hz or 800x600 @ 100 hz. Eventually, I got rid of it in favor of an LCD monitor due to the crisp image possible with DVI and the thinner profile. But I had not realized practically all LCD screens were 60 hz. It has been years since I switched over to an LCD screen and for all of those years, playing Counter Strike was not the same, and it was precisely because of the refresh rate difference.

To all of the naysayers out there, they probably don't play games at a competitive enough level to know how much of a difference it makes. It is NIGHT and DAY the difference between 60 hz and 120 hz, even 75 hz to 120 hz. The moment I hooked up this monitor and turned it on, I could tell the difference. Even just moving the mouse around on the desktop feels way better (aka - smoother). It is the most glorious purchase I've ever made with regards to computer hardware.

With this monitor, it is like playing on a CRT without all of the CRT baggage. 1-deags have never felt so good... there were entire situations that I simply wouldn't be able to handle very well on my previous LCD screen which was 60 hz, 75 hz on certain resolutions. Anything that required very fast movement was much more difficult to do at 60 hz because of the jittery feel. Twitch reaction shots like 1-deags are much easier to perform at 120 hz.

This monitor is an absolute must have for those who can afford it, play first person shooters, and have a computer capable of running said games at 120 FPS or higher. This monitor isn't really necessary in my opinion if you play RTS games or other games where you're not in direct control of your camera. Don't get me wrong, 120 hz is a very noticeable difference in many aspects that isn't unique to first person shooters, but it's hard to justify the premium price of this monitor if you do nothing but play StarCraft, World of Warcraft, Diablo, The Sims, or other non shooter games.

Also, you should know beforehand if you really can notice the difference between 120 hz and 60 hz. For me, it is absolutely clear the difference between 120 hz and 60 hz. Just show me the mouse moving around the screen and I can tell. My roommates, however, none of them can tell the difference. They simply don't know what to look for. They don't play games competitively. They don't push boundaries of player capabilities. If you're not the kind of player I am, you won't find much value in these 120 hz monitors.

Anyways, this monitor isn't just great for the 120 hz. It's also miles ahead of other monitors in regards to ergonomics. The stand the monitor comes with is pretty amazing. These days, almost all LCD monitors come with a silly fixed stand that will rotate if you're lucky. This thing has the full setup when it comes to the stand. I personally don't need it that much, but it sure is nice when I do to have all of that adjustability (height, tilt, swivel...).

The on screen display controls are also pretty slick. There are many adjustable options. One of my favorites is the variety of scaling options provided. This monitor is 1920x1080, but say your computer can't run your game at that resolution at the performance you need. So the simple thing is to drop down your graphics settings and drop down the resolution, right? On my old LCD, I HATED doing that. The scaling never worked right (well, it just wasn't in the monitor). Your 4:3 resolution was stretched to fill the whole 16:9 screen. Gross. I played Counter Strike like that for years just to get 75 hz. Not so on the XL2420T. The monitor will properly scale the image to a variety of presets, or to your own custom scale! When this scaling occurs, it will retain the 120 hz refresh rate, so you can play at whatever settings you wish at the correct aspect ratio.

My one main gripe with the screen is just how long it takes to switch inputs and the fact that the on screen display controls are programmable touch buttons without a "Switch Input" option. Why is this an issue? If you hook up both your computer and your gaming console (in my case, a PS3), you might often be running both. Sometimes, I switch to my PS3 and it takes a little while (not too long, maybe 2 or 3 seconds) to switch inputs. Not so bad. The problem comes when you turn a device OFF before switching inputs. Say I switch to the PS3 and then turn off the PS3. The monitor takes a few seconds to detect that the input has lost signal. During this time, the on screen display controls DO NOT RESPOND. You have either beat the momentary pause before the monitor realizes the input signal is lost and race to switch the inputs or you have to wait out the 2-3 seconds for it to detect the lost signal, then you can switch inputs. But then you need to wait another 2-3 seconds when you switch inputs, since that's how long it takes. What does this have to do with the programmable touch buttons? The touch buttons don't respond during the signal loss/input switch. Even if they did, you can't see what you're selecting, so it's hopeless there.

The main issue is the input switch times. If the input switching were instant, then this would be a non issue.

A few other items worth mentioning, since this is an LCD screen: viewing angles and ghosting. The viewing angles, as with all TN monitors, leaves something to be desired. This screen is not ideal for group use. You should be sitting squarely in front of the screen to see all the colors without distortion. It isn't like an atrocity compared to other TN monitors, but when you compare it to IPS screens, the viewing angles really are horrendous. With regard to ghosting, I personally don't notice ghosting while I play Counter Strike. I can, however, notice the halo effect when watching text scroll up and down when I browse web pages. This is a side effect of overdrive on low response time LCD screens, which is GOOD for gaming. But if you do a ton of text or image work and no gaming, you don't need a 120 hz TN panel.

Still, this monitor, is hands down the best gaming monitor I've ever used. Nothing else comes close (but I haven't used other 120 hz LCD screens). Even CRTs, I wouldn't want again. Who would want that kind of bulk and blurry VGA images? I want my crisp digital signals and my 1-deags.

This is a pretty sexy monitor.

## Highlights

HDR: looks great
Blur reduction: not the best I've seen, but it works and I appreciate it.
Freesync 2: my first time using VRR, and it works great with some caveats I will explain below.
Colors: I bought a colorometer recently and apparently this monitor has great color accuracy.
Contrast ratio: not bad for not being OLED.
Profile buttons: very useful.
OSD: fully functional, but if I could corner a samsung developer at a party I would rant about some bugs and UX issues.
Overall: I think the features outweigh the flaws. 144Hz 2K panel with accurate colors, good contrast ratio, HDR, blur reduction, Freesync2. All work. All nice. If you want a perfect panel, you probably will have to settle for a smaller sized 1080p at this point in time.

## Issues

Yes, there is some ghosting. There is also a small amount of crosstalk with blur reduction.
Ghosting is a result of less-than-perfect pixel response times. Crosstalk is the result of blur reduction tech being out of sync with the panel. This monitor has a bit of both. I do not believe they are significant enough to be a deal breaker.

Samsung calls its blur reduction tech "response time: fastest". Turn this off and the crosstalk issues will vanish.

The profile buttons are a cool idea, so the fact that they're half baked, inconsistent and a bit buggy is disappointing. Profiles don't always restore brightness on the first try, some settings are not profileable (power saving mode, eye saver mode). Still, I appreciate that the feature is here at all. I have a profile for blur reduction mode, a profile for freesync, and a low-brightness profile for night time.

The corners of the monitor have a small amount of light bleed, but it's not noticeable in real world conditions.

Under a very specific condition, the display driver / board / whatever it's called seems to have a small barely noticeable rendering flaw. I've had access to two of these monitors, and both had the exact same flaw. I have accepted this flaw and will not be returning the monitor over it.

## Freesync details

Freesync "standard engine" has a very narrow range of operation: 120Hz-144Hz. You are covered for framerates of either 60-72 fps OR 120-144 fps. Nothing in between, and probably nothing below 60 (this is true for DisplayPort, the range is offset a bit lower for HDMI). However, the brightness will not flicker in this mode.

Freesync "ultimate engine" has a range of 48Hz-144Hz. You should be covered for all framerates between 24-144 fps. HOWEVER! You will experience brightness flickering if your game is hovering around 50fps. This is not a defect afaik, it is simply a rather complicated quick of LCDs. The tl;dr is: different Hz have different brightnesses. You won't notice this unless there is a massive change in Hz. Unfortunately, this is exactly what happens when "Low Framerate Compensation" kicks in. The monitor can't go lower than 48Hz, so a dip from 50fps to 45fps causes the monitor to go from 50Hz to 90Hz (2*45=90). In practice, this sudden doubling of Hz can happen with framerates below 55fps.
For the same reason (sudden massive Hz change), frametime spikes will also result in a noticeable flicker.

Neither of these freesync modes are friendly to weak PCs. You need to be pushing at least 60fps to even use standard engine, and you need to push at least 55fps to avoid flickering in ultimate engine. This might be generally true of freesync monitors, I wouldn't know.
However if you do have a weak PC, you might avoid flickering if you keep the game below 48fps.

## Misc

The monitor HUD will tell you when HDR is active, which is very useful. You can also check this from the OSD menu- which will also tell you when Freesync is active, and what your current Hz is.

I actually had two of this monitor in my possession for a short time, and both had exactly the same imperfections. I conclude that these are merely the realities of this monitor, and not something that a replacement would fix. Maybe I'm lucky, but seems like quality control is doing their job as of mid 2019.

I read somewhere that "low input lag" is samsung's way of saying "turn off HDR tonemapping". The assumption is that your GPU should do the tonemapping while freesync is active, so it is not necessary for the monitor to do additional tonemapping. But HDR is a mess on Windows right now, I have no idea if things are working as intended. Sometimes this setting gets "greyed out" when it shouldn't be, and I have to turn the monitor off and back on to change it.

## Misleading reviews

One of the highest rated 1 star reviews is misleading. I have copy pasted my reply here: Both the review and the first reply are not entirely accurate.

The response time setting "Fastest" is about strobing/blur reduction, NOT a "1ms mode" (where did the reviewer get that idea?) which inherently reduces brightness and thus kicks you out of "real HDR" range while enabled.

However, this is not a negative aspect of the monitor. The reviewer is simply uninformed. Blur reduction reduces brightness by definition and there is nothing anyone could do to change that. This monitor has both blur reduction and HDR. It is physically impossible to use one while the other is active. That's just the way it has to be. Having both is great- the monitor's inability to break the laws of physics does not warrant an angry 1star review.

Good news is most amazon reviewers seem to not know what blur reduction even is, so they probably won't miss it if they leave it off.

I bought two of these in October of 2021, and one of them had the DisplayPort fail within three months. HDMI still works, and I can still get up to 144 Hz refresh rates, but that isn't the 165 Hz that was promised. The DisplayPort on the other one failed today, and as my graphics card only has one HDMI output, I either have to roll the dice on getting a good DP-to-HDMI adapter or replace the monitor.

One thing's for sure, I'm not buying another Sceptre monitor. I should have taken a hint and returned them when I realized the silver logo on the front is literally just a bunch of stickers that will come off if you remove the protective plastic too quickly. Furthermore, the reason for the backlight bleed is that the top 70% of the monitor consists of the panel and a thin sheet of plastic holding it up, with no rigidity whatsoever. That means any tilt adjust has to be done from the bottom unless you want to risk cracking the panel! I'm half expecting the HDMI to fail in another year and force me to throw out the monitors entirely. This level of quality is unacceptable in a time when there is so much concern about sustainability.

If you really must have a cheap high-refresh rate monitor, even if this is $20 cheaper than the next option, go with something else. And if this is the only option in your budget, be prepared to use it with only HDMI, and put up with the heavy backlight bleed.

The biggest challenge I faced was getting the resolution to scale properly on my 2015 Macbook Pro. It wasn't giving me HiDPI options through my Mac's display settings, so on that computer the best resolution I could achieve out of the box was 1080p. I solved this problem by installing a third party display settings software called SwitchResX, which gave me a ton of additional resolution options. Now everything looks crisp and beautiful!

The video input is versatile -- I love that it has both standard HDMI and USB C. The speakers aren't good but it's a very slim unit, so they're right in line with my expectations.

I love this monitor!

First, I will list the cons then get to the good stuff

Cons -

1) menu navigation is a royal pain if using the buttons on the back of the display, however a remote control is included which makes menu navigation a breeze! Just don’t lose the remote!

2) very slight bit of backlight clouding is visible on a black screen. Given the price point of this monitor and it’s otherwise great picture, this is acceptable to me.

3) the build quality (case, etc) is not as polished as some other name brand monitors. Cheap plastic, but why do you expect for a monitor with these specs at this price?

4) speakers suck, but who cares?

5) stand it comes with offers no height adjustment or swivel to portrait mode

Now the PROs!

1) outstanding picture quality and it has HDR / this wasn’t clearly defined in any of the literature I read before buying, so this was a nice surprise to me. The quality of this VA panel seems better to me than the IPS panel of a Samsung that I tried out before returning it for excessive backlight bleed

2) VESA compatible, uses 100mm Mount. This is a huge positive when compared to the Massdrop VAST 35” display. The Massdrop is not VESA compatible.

3) several different mixture mode presets available by using the remote

4) the remote! Allows tweaking the monitor picture, adjusting input, aspect ratio, picture in picture mode, etc without having to reach behind the monitor

5) refresh rate - comes stock at 100hz out of the box, but I was able to easily run it at 120hz using the NVIDIA control panel (I can’t comment on AMD compatability, I don’t have an AMD video card but I was pleasantly surprised it’s running at 120hz no problem with my NVIDIA 1060 6Gb graphics on my Acer Predator 17 laptop)

6) it’s 35”! Just a tad bit larger than the 34” monitors - I measured and the display is exactly 1/2” wider and 1/2” taller than the Samsung I previously used.

All in all, I love this monitor! I was hesitant for awhile to give this one a shot because at the time I couldn’t find any YouTube video reviews and there were very few reviews found on the web. Some of the reviews I saw said there were problems with the Freesync and stuttering, but I don’t use Freesync so that didn’t concern me.

I would absolutely, positively buy this monitor again. Save hundreds of dollars over a name brand, comparable monitor.

Having moved to this monitor from several pairs of old HP L2035 and L2335 displays, I can attest that the "usable" viewing angles on these are somewhat below 178 degrees.

Unlike the older IPS displays with older backlight technology, if you're using a white background or image editing, you'll notice image brightness falloff stars at about 30 degrees outside the optimum view angle. While the image is visible, brightness start to diminish almost immediately when you move your head out of the sweet spot. This is clearly visible when you have a multi display setup. With two monitors you wind up literally on the edge of the sweet spot most of the time. Slide left or right a foot or stand or dip down a foot and falloff starts.

That said - I paid about $1000 per monitor over a decade ago for top quality L2335s. The VH240a costs 1/10th of the price, weighs 1/5th as much, draws 1/6th as much power and is 1/6th as thick. Like the L2335s, they rotate, however these also come off their stands easier and have muuuuch thinner bezels.

For the price, I'll learn to tolerate the brightness falloff as my head moves around. They’re a “decent” value. However I can’t recommend them for anyone expecting a wide viewing angle with consistent brightness across the viewing angle. The manufacturer is misleading consumers a bit by using the term “178 degree viewing angle.” It should read something like “178 degree visible / 140 degree usable / 80 degree optimal.”

I've owned several LG TVs and computer monitors, so I was eager to try this dual-monitor setup, and so far, it's definitely exceeded my expectations.

Physically, the dual 27" monitor setup is large but fairly compact given the amount of screen real estate it includes. The overall system measures about 48" x 24" x 24", and it tips the scales at about 38 pounds.

With 2560x1440 resolution and a 75Hz refresh rate, these monitors are capable of very high picture quality. I'd argue that this is all the resolution you can use on a 27" screen - you wouldn't be able to visually tell any difference if LG bumped the resolution higher.

Colors are also spot-on...LG claims 99% sRGB compliance - a very high score for a monitor like this. With a contrast ratio of a claimed 1000:1, the black levels are also quite good - though not as good as I see on my best LG OLED large monitor. At under 5ms, lag is very low too if you're into gaming. Plus, with both monitors connected, the image quality is quite consistent across both units.

I do a bit of photography with my computer, so color accuracy is important to me. The LG is easy to calibrate, and my Datacolor SpyderX calibration tool had no trouble with the LG - in fact, it was nearly perfect right out of the box.

As with other LG monitors I've owned, the picture is bright and sharp, and you can adjust it to look amazing in your environment. AMD's FreeSync is supported, as is HDR10. The viewing angle is wide, text is sharp and easy to read, colors are accurate and vivid, and the screen is relatively glare-free, even if you work in a brightly-lit space.

As good as the image quality is, one of the crown jewels of this product is LG's Ergo stand. It's simple to reposition either monitor in nearly any configuration you can imagine - stacked, side by side, one landscape/one portrait, etc. Unlike most monitors I've had in the past, the Ergo doesn't have a large base taking up space on your desk...instead, the monitor clamps to your desktop, requiring only a small footprint of desk space. I found the whole setup easy to install and quite secure once properly bolted down.

As for connectors, while USB-C (Thunderbolt) seems to offer the best performance, the monitors have a range of inputs, from USB-C to HDMI and DisplayPort (DP). My main use is with a 2021 MacBook Pro M1 system, and a single Thunderbolt cable is all I need between my computer and both monitors. Conveniently, you can connect the monitors in daisy-chain mode, and then a single USB cable gets you both monitors with a single cable (you'll also need a DisplayPort to USB-C cable to connect the two monitors together). In addition to my MacBook, I tried a Dell Windows computer and a Linux computer - all worked exactly as expected with no issues.

As with most modern monitors, there's a built-in app that lets you customize and configure the display. If you've worked with prior LG monitors, this one is no surprise.

The USB-C port supports charging at 65W...in my case, this means I don't even need to plug in my MacBook's power adapter - I can let it charge directly from the monitor (although it charges more slowly than the higher-power charger).

One small note is that there's no speaker built into these monitors, so if sound is important to you, you'll need outboard speakers or a headset. There is a standard headphone connector port, so the monitors can send sound to an external device - there's just no speaker included inside the monitor itself.

Overall, my only complaint is that LG doesn't currently offer exactly this same product but with their 34" monitors instead of the 27". Otherwise, this is about the perfect monitor for most of us, with the ideal mix of features, rugged/high-quality construction and overall performance no matter what you use your computer for.

You would be surprised how difficult it is to check those 4 specification above without paying an obscene amount. The monitor market at the moment is quite expensive with the release of the 30 series Nvidia cards. 4k 120hz is now a reality. This monitor is $700 MSRP but you will be hard press to find it at that price new. I have gone from 1440p 144hz(TN Panel) to this monitor and the difference is night and day. Once you go 4k, you don't go back. TN panels have good response times but you can achieve similar response times with IPS panels and have drastically improved color accuracy and image quality. There are 3 different variants for the Acer Predator XB273K: Pbmiphzx, Sbmiprzx, and Gpbmiipprzx. Pbmiphzx is the original with an actual G-Sync module. Sbmiprzx is a refresh of the Pbmiphzx also with a G-Sync module. Gpbmiipprzx(this monitor) is a cheaper variant with the only difference being it lacks a G-Sync module but uses freesync technology to be G-Sync compatible.

At the moment, this monitor is about $800 compared to its counterpart model Sbmiprzx which is about $1200 on Amazon. The difference? This monitor is G-Sync compatible while the Sbmiprzx model has an actual G-Sync module. G-Sync is a Nvidia branded technology that uses a variable refresh rate to prevent tearing in your image. Do a bit of research on G-Sync and you will experience a tear-free experience with a G-Sync module or G-Sync compatible monitor. With my current settings, I haven't experienced a single tear with a wide variety of games on this monitor. If G-Sync's primary goal is to get rid of screen tearing, well you can achieve that with this cheaper model no problem. There are no major discernible differences between the two models besides that. If a dedicated G-Sync module is worth $400 more to you, be my guest.

I spent countless hours researching monitors because I spend all day looking at excel sheets and my eyes (and back!) could no longer tolerate working on my 13” laptop. After reading a bunch of reviews here and elsewhere, I kept coming back to this Sceptre monitor. The only other ones I found that were as highly rated are all curved and I wanted a flat monitor so I could mount it above my desk and not have it taking up any desktop space. This is perfect for what I was wanting. The display is beautiful. Certainly too good for my work, but I’m sure I could also use it for 4K Netflix videos or something if I wanted to see all of its glory. I have it connected to both a 2019 MacBook Pro and a 2011 MacBook Pro. It works great with both.

As for negatives, I only have 2 and consider them minor. The sound is not great, but it’s acceptable. The song I use to judge speakers was skipping and stuttering when played on the 2019 MacBook Pro, but I’m thinking that might not be the monitor’s fault because the same song played smoothly when played on the 2011 MacBook Pro. It’s no Bose, but it’s fine. It also stuttered with a YouTube video so I might need to adjust the sound settings or something. Also, I wish it had a built in webcam or at least USB port. I do a lot of video conferencing and will have to use the actual computer instead of the monitor for those since there’s nothing built in and the external one I bought requires a USB connection. Maybe I’ll be able to daisychain it to my wired keyboard? That’s TBD, but at any rate I wish there was a built in camera or USB port. I’m still giving it 5 stars though.

Finally, if you’re worried about the light on the back, don’t be. I can’t even see it unless I’m working at night with no lights on. It looks cool from the side, but that’s all. It is not bright enough to be disruptive (except possibly in complete darkness so if you want this for movies, it may or may not be an issue).

I haven't even changed the settings, haven't had a need to. Using the default factory preset still and the picture is great. I viewed the other settings and went with the default. No problem running Bioshock2 at 1920x1080. Previous build uses 2 19" Samsung that are 4x3 with 1280x1024 max which look great but these are beautiful. I give this monitor 5 stars.

Considerations: None of these were really a problem for me but some things you may want to consider.
The vertical viewing angle. Also, they sit low. I had to put mine on stands to raise them up a few inches. I knew there was no height adjustment going in but was not aware of how low they sit. Getting out my tape and visualizing isn't the same. I found some aluminum 9" round straight-sided cake pans at a local box store and inverted them to raise the monitors 3" for me. I am 5'10", desk is 30" tall. They are a perfect fit, people think they came with the monitors. They are strong enough for me to stand on. I painted mine black to match the monitor bezel. I will be buying third one when the GPU dust settles on both sides of the fence and the cost goes down on a card that will support three monitors. This is a new build and I moved the 9800GTX+ from the old box over to this one and it still works just fine. Pros: HDMI 2.0 (HDCP2.2) which makes this better than the BenQXR3501 or even its sibling, Z35 Predator. It also has "Daisy-Chain" with Displayport (Displayport Out/In).

I definitely like the fact that it's VESA compliant unlike the BenQXR3501 which you have to pay extra money by paying a VESA adapter to mount it on an ARM. Speakers are decent and can be used as a makeshift speakers until you buy monitor speakers unlike the BenQXR3501 which doesn't have any speakers at all.

It also has an USB 3.0 hub so you can do firmware updates and other things unlike the BenQXR3501 which doesn't have any USB inputs.

It has better Contrast ratio (3000:1) than the BenQXR3501 (2000:1) for deeper blacks.

And the factory's stand has height adjustment unlike the BenQXR3501.

On Provantage, it's around the same price of the BenQXR3501 or cheaper so it's a no-brainer between the two.

Cons: I didn't appreciate the lack of information. I kinda had to figure things out for myself instead of being informed by some official guide. For example, Freesync can now be done via HDMI so I was wondering does this monitor have that capability? Chances are, it doesn't given that it's Adaptive Sync which is only for Displayport interface. Another lack of information is the Adaptive Sync's range on this? I been having this monitor for over 2 months now and I still don't know what is the Freesync's range of this monitor. I already checked Acer's official website/forum to no avail.

Other Thoughts: I bought this on Provantage for $915 (included shipping). You can see pictures of my monitor on a VESA ARM on Overclock.net at the display/monitor section and also on Ama zon for verification.

-- the monitor sits far too low on its pedestal and it's not adjustable. The fix is eithering lowering my chair or raising the monitor with a book (not ideal). While marketed as a computer monitor and the aspect ratio works nicely with Windows 7, it's a severe drawback that there's no adjustment.

-- Second the monitor moves as I type due to the nimble base. I have a very sturdy steel and glass desk, but the vibrations from my typing make the monitor wobble in its pedestal. This should have been corrected - an extremely sloppy and disappointing design move
