Quick Summary: The Cocopar 17.3" `1080p monitor might be one of the best purchases I have made in the last five years. The build quality is excellent, the accessories you need are actually included, and the support you get is unparalleled. Oh, and I can use it to game while my wife is watching her TV shows. I rarely ever write reviews, but I can honestly say this is one worth writing a review for.

So with the summary out of the way, let's break down each of the aspects, good and bad!

- The Real Estate: Let's be honest. When you're buying a portable monitor, you are usually buying it to supplement an existing monitor or a laptop screen. In my case, I am using it as a primary and only monitor for a gaming desktop because when you combine 17.3" with a 1920x1080 resolution, you get the perfect balance of real estate and pixel density. It's In other words, while you can absolutely use this to supplement an existing screen as a secondary monitor, this works perfectly well as a primary viewing screen, too.
But don't let the benefits of a secondary monitor to be lost. In my case, I took the monitor to work today to connect to my Surface dock. And guess what? I ended up with three screens -- one on my desk, one on my Surface itself, and this monitor. All work fantastically. Here's a tip that you can use if you have a Surface -- lay the Microsoft Surface down vertically and use it as a note-taking mechanism -- this way, way, you can actually see the stuff on the Cocopar monitor while using your tablet's capabilities of handwriting notes.

- The Picture Quality: Right out of the box, I was a bit disappointed with the colors. Then I realized I just needed to calibrate the panel using the buttons on the left-hand side. I tend to love blue-ish colors (sorry, not a graphic designer), and so my settings are tailored to a way that I love. Additionally, I have tested to make sure that the settings get saved. Another panel I had before this one was fine when you calibrated it. However, after a power-cycle, the settings would reset to default. That's not something you want in a portable monitor where you will be unplugging it frequently -- so, very glad that my settings travel with me. So much to say, if you feel the screen contrast, color, etc. are not up to your standard, modify them until they are! One last thing: this is not an IPS panel. If I had to guess, it is TN. This means that this is not glossy -- rather, the display is matte. You can use the included screen protector to add the gloss, if you prefer, but I'm not about that. TN panels get a bad rap usually, but ever tried working on an IPS monitor that is slightly angled? It'll make you think you forgot your primary colors. TN panels are great for angled viewing at the cost of that glossy pop that renders your screen useless in overhead lighting situations. In this case, it is a portable monitor so being able to view it at an angle is essential. And Cocopar delivers.

- Portability: This is where life gets really exciting. Sure, you can buy a traditional monitor and it works. But if you're like me and travel a bit, this thing gives me a reason to continue living. Seriously light; extremely portable. Like I mentioned, I took it to work in my backpack and brought it back home. It took less than 30 seconds of setup each time. Can you do that with your traditional monitor? Absolutely not...at least not as quickly. The other night my wife wanted to watch NCIS while I wanted to play RDR2. You know what I did? Hooked this bad boy to my Raspberry PI with Steam-Link and off I went...playing a lawless cowboy while she watched Gibbs take down yet another bad guy. Last thing here: get you a laptop tray and you can use this in bed, too -- not like that you pervert! I mean you can actually use it to get stuff done while never leaving the bed. So in the last few days this monitor has been my (1) my primary monitor for my home gaming; (2) my secondary monitor at work, allowing me to use my Surface as a note taking tablet; (3) my primary monitor for gaming on the couch; and (4) my in-bed companion for firing off late night emails. Can your monitor do that? Probably not. I imagine this is even more helpful for people who work in web-design or graphic design. Instead of going through a million iterations of the same dang logo with a client, plop this monitor in front of them as you both drink coffee...you can make modifications on your laptop while the client sees them as you make them.

- Connections: This is where things get truly outstanding. If you are lucky enough to have a computer with Thunderbolt through USB-C, you can use a single cable for both power and display. Now, I am not that lucky yet -- I am sticking with my Surface Pro 6 for now which means that I have to connect a power cable to the monitor along with HDMI. But get this...this monitor has PD passthrough on the USB-C. That is absolutely bonkers. You know what that means? I can take one 90watt USB adapter and plug that into my screen. The second USB-c cable then goes to my Surface connect port to power the Surface. When I'm not using the Surface, I can use that second USB-c port to power my Pixel 3a. What does that mean? When I'm traveling next time, all I need to carry is one power brick and a couple of USB-c and Surface Connect cables. No more carrying a charger for my phone and laptop like a muggle. The mini-DisplayPort is a nice touch because you can connect to most pre-2020 laptops using a mini-DP to mini-DP connection -- no adapter necessary. Before i go, THIS PORTABLE THING HAS A FULL SIZE HDMI PORT. I REPEAT. A FULL SIZE HDMI PORT. So in addition to USB-C Thunderbolt, an extra USB-C port, and the mini DIsplayPort, you have a full sized HDMI input. That is absolutely bonkers. All ports work well. The only port I have not tested is the micro-USB on the side. Haven't had to use it yet but I assume if you've got them lying around, you cna always power the monitor using that instead of USB-c.

- Support: I am usually a jerk when it comes to screens. Look, in 2003 I bought my first laptop and it had dead-pixels. I spent hours on the phone with Dell explaining that no, a $2,000 machine should not have a dead-pixel. Since then, every time I buy a monitor, I run it through a full color cycle to see if there are any dead or bright spots so that I can catch them during the return window. Thankfully, this display had no such defects. But I wanted to test the support structure anyway before leaving this review. I asked them that since this monitor has USB-c data connection, could I use my computer to communicate with the monitor so that I can setup color profiles, etc. and use the software on my PC to drive the profiles instead of the physical hardware buttons. Within minutes, Cocopar reached out and asked how we can chat further. They asked if I prefer email, Facebook, Twitter, etc. I went with Twitter and had a direct message from the seller within a few minutes. We ended up chatting for a solid day just about ideas -- how you can use USB-c for data, how you can mount this guy using VESA adapters, how I would prefer for the ports to be on the left side instead of right (since i am right handed, I don't want anything impeding my range of motion). Now, if a seller will talk to me for hours about my technology fantasies and weird quirks that maybe 10 people in the world people get joy out of, you can safely assume this product is backed by good people. That is the real reason I was tempted to write the review in the first place. The seller had no reason to communicate with me -- it wasn't like I was returning a product or complaining about any missing features. The monitor is absolutely the best and life-changing for me. And yet, the seller continued to talk to me about the potential of getting me the firmware code for my color profile request at some point in the future, the potential of moving the ports to the left side for the next release, etc. This is huge. It tells you two things: 1. You're working with a good company that listens to feedback; and 2. You're working with engineers who built this thing from ground up -- not just a reseller. These things are important. If you can't talk to an engineer during the troubleshooting session, your only option is to send the product back during the limited return window. What happens if something breaks after and you're dealing with a reseller? Can they / will they help you troubleshoot?

- Longevity: I have not had this screen for nearly long enough to comment on this. While all the parts feel premium, the case is solid, etc. you just can't tell these days how long something will last. I will say this though: I would be both shocked and disappointed if this thing didn't last 2 years at least. If it does last 2 years, I am happy -- do the math: if you pay $250 for this monitor and it lasts you two years, you're basically paying 35 cents a day to tremendously improve your productivity.

- Accessories: The monitor was brand new, and came with 2 screen protectors, USB-c cables, power block, HDMI cable, etc. Everything you might need to get started.

- Where is the cheese? Of course, here I am with all these platitudes. But where is the cheese? What are you giving up because of course it sounds too good to be true? You’re giving up ~$250 when you factor in the coupon. That is a lot of money. So on the cheap, good, fast triangle of the two things you can get, this falls in the good and fast category. Cost is a sacrifice you make. But I feel like you get your money’s worth as long as you have a use case for a portable monitor. So…”expensive, but good” is how I could’ve said that in like 3 words.

In conclusion, I love this Cocopar monitor. 17.3" is the perfect sweet spot for me. If any issues arise and I am not satisfied with any areas of the monitor, I will update the review. But for now, this thing worth every single of the 35 cents I spent on it today.

I photograph portraits and weddings and I have a very vibrant style. Because of that I needed to make sure the color was pretty spot-on. So as soon as I get the monitor set up I pulled up images on both my iMac and the new BenQ screen and they looked very close! We actually noticed that my iMac whites are a little warmer so I pulled out a bunch of prints I have had made and started comparing and the BenQ seemed to be even More accurate to what my prints looked like!

For a $800 TN panel monitor I expect near perfection with everything except for the obvious TN flaws. Unfortunately this monitor has all the negatives of a TN + other problems as well.

Lets start with the positives:

The build quality of this monitor is exceptional. The stand feels extremely sturdy, rotating the screen, pivoting it, everything feels exactly how I would expect a $800 product to feel. The face of the monitor itself is absolutely stunning as the screen stretches nearly the entire length with minimal bezel. The back is elegantly designed while maximizing airflow across the components inside. I've read that ASUS overclocked the G-Sync module inside and fitted it with a larger then standard heatsink. Regardless the monitor doesn't really get hot after long gaming sessions so their cooling design is great.

The picture quality when not in motion is also extremely good. I calibrated the screen with a Spyder3 + DispCal and honestly the end result isn't much different then how it came from the factory. The colors are excellent, far better then the BenQ 2420T I came from. Contrast isn't as good as a high end IPS but it's far better than all my previous TN panels. Horizontal viewing angles are good, vertical are meh. I expected this going into it knowing it's a TN panel though, so I wasn't surprised. 1440p @ 27" is absolutely incredible, not only does it improve the usability of Windows and apps with more space to work, but it definitely provides a greater level of detail in games.

G-Sync is also very good. Initially I had a problem getting it working. My setup is a GTX690 + 750Ti, when the 750Ti is enabled though, G-Sync caps framerates and causes severe stuttering. After disabling the 750Ti G-Sync worked beautifully. Playing Crysis 3 at a mere 40fps felt as smooth as playing other games at 100fps. Screen tearing is a thing of the past. Pretty much any game between 30-60 fps with G-Sync on turns it from a laggy mess to a fluid dream. Nvidia is constantly improving the experience via driver updates and supposedly the next driver will fix the 750Ti issue (I think this also effects other cards, not just the 750Ti but any dual nvidia card setup that isn't SLI).

ULMB is also a great feature. CSGO is one of my favorite games and on my 2420T lightboost simply dimmed the screen too much for my liking. While ULMB also dims the screen, it's no where near what it was on the 2420T. The image instantly becomes clearer, especially during quick motions like snapping to different positions. ULMB caps the refresh rate at 120 (the option is actually greyed out when the monitor is running at 144) but honestly the difference between 120 and 144 doesn't really effect fluidity in my experience.

Now for the negatives..

For starters lots (when I say lots I mean more than I've seen with any other high profile monitor release) of people have received their monitor with dead pixels. I personally have zero on my screen but it's definitely alarming reading the reports of some people having as many as 2-3. Now I understand that on a panel this new there are going to be defects but to me the number of people that are experiencing issues is too high for a $800 monitor.

The second and probably the biggest issue I have found is something called "Pixel Inversion" others have referred it it as a couple other names. Essentially the jist of the problem is that brighter colored objects (light blues and light greens especially) become distorted when in motion. It almost looks like a checkboard pattern and can be seen here courtesy of TFT Central:

PG278Q: http://www.tftcentral.co.uk/images/pixperan/asus_rog_swift_pg278q.jpg
VG278HE: http://www.tftcentral.co.uk/images/pixperan/asus_vg278he.jpg

In the first image on the right hand side you can see the vertical line pattern of the blurred images behind the actual rendering. This artifact occurs in nearly every game I have tested. It shows up for brief periods of time on brighter objects that are in motion. Things like muzzle flashes, explosions, or even the green names that hover above characters in MMO's (World of Warcraft to be specific). The effect at first wasn't very noticeable to me but once I started seeing it in more games it's nearly impossible to unsee. In Dota 2 for example the textures on the ground in the "radiant" base show this effect slightly during any motion of the camera at all. I've also seen the effect in other games like Witcher 2 and Warframe. Now if I was the only one I'd simply just chalk it up to bad panel but there is a thread on ASUS Rog's forums with a number of people experiencing the issue. I also think more people will report the issue once they've spent some time with the monitor.

http://rog.asus.com/forum/showthread.php?50004-PG278Q-vertical-stripes-esp.-in-3D-mode.

Third, apparently some of our European friends who have had access to this monitor for some time report that initially the monitor was excellent but started displaying distorted text and "halos" around objects several weeks after purchase. Again this isn't one person but several reporting it.

Overall I think this can be an excellent monitor if ASUS sorts out the QA issues. When you pay $800 for a monitor you expect that the product you receive will be reasonably tested for things like dead pixels, inversion and longevity issues, especially when this is a monitor that was initially supposed to be released in March. When you couple the awkward NA launch of this monitor with the QA issues it definitely leaves a poor taste in people's mouths. I'm not even sure if I should RMA mine because the pixel inversion problem seems to be an intrinsic property of the panel and not simply a one off thing. I might just return it all together and wait for ASUS to sort out the issues.

I had an older 24-inch monitor that I'm not impressed with, and thought this might be a nice upgrade. I've never had one with IPS before, and I like the features of this monitor. After using this monitor for a little while now, I do think it is a nice upgrade. I read that this monitor is calibrated nicely by default, and I found that to be true.

The first one I received had a stuck pixel. It showed up most easily as a bright bluish-white spot when viewing a black image and was a bit distracting. I returned it and they sent a replacement that also had a stuck pixel. It was in a different place, but otherwise the symptoms were the same. That one also had a gap in the top bezel where the front and the back of the monitor weren't fixed together properly. I asked to return that one as well, which raised a flag for Amazon support and they asked me to verify that the two I had received both had the same problem (stuck pixel) and to describe it more completely. I explained that I had tested with multiple cables just to make sure it wasn't the cable, and asked them to send me one more. That third one (which they overnighted--thanks!) had zero stuck pixels (bright spots). Hurray!

Now you may be thinking that I'm picky, sending a monitor back for a single stuck pixel. Well, yeah, you may have a point. But customers and the manufacturers themselves are holding themselves to a higher quality standard these days. The ASUS warranty for this "Professional" line of monitors says that their threshold for allowable bad pixels is up to 5 dead pixels, but 0 (zero) stuck pixels (what they call "bright spots"). So their own warranty acknowledges that any stuck (always on) pixels are unacceptable. This is a fairly major purchase for me, and I don't like to accept defects when I don't have to.

One thing that is a minor annoyance is the power button. It's quite small, and it's on the right-side bezel, near the bottom, but not all the way at the bottom, so you have to feel for it (especially in the dark), and since it is flush with the bezel, there is nothing really for your finger to feel. I end up just kind of guessing where the power button is and pushing there to eventually find it. That really could have been designed better.

After reading quite a few unfavorable reviews about the U3014, I initially ordered the older U3011. The U3011 is a nice monitor, and after a few days' usage I can say that I liked everything about it EXCEPT the heavy anti-glare coating. Maybe my U3011 was defective or something, but the heavy and uneven AG coating made white backgrounds look "dirty" or "grainy". So along with wanting the U3014's newer design with LED backlight and USB3, I decided to ignore the bad reviews and give the 3014 a try since it reportedly has an improved AG coating.

I ordered one "sold and shipped by" Amazon on Jul-31-2013 with overnight shipping, and the next day received a revision A02 unit made in April 2013.

I compared the U3011 and U3014 side-by-side. In my opinion, the U3014 beats the U3011 in every way. I have NONE of the problems mentioned in earlier reviews. The color balance and lighting uniformity are better than the U3011. The touch panel works perfectly. There are NOT any of the "horizontal lines in lower left corner" mentioned in earlier reviews. I *suspect* a lot of the problems in earlier reviews reflected A01 units, and improvements have been made in the now-shipping A02 units.

The U3014's LED backlight runs very cool and uses about 1/2 the power compared to the U3011. Using a kill-a-watt meter, I measured my U3014 at 41watts, compared to the 3011's 83 watts (both set to my preferred brightness level).

But by far, the BIGGEST improvement is the anti-glare coating. I found the AG coating on the U3011 to actually be distracting. It wasn't real noticable on darker backgrounds, but on light backgrounds (including web pages with black text on white background) the U3011 had a horrible "sparkle" that actually made text difficult to read. Moving a windows with a white background on the U3011, the heavy AG coating made it appear that the display was dirty or the backlighting was uneven. I'm happy to say that the U3014 coating is almost perfect. It is more uniform and lighter, enough to cut reflections from surrounding objects, but has almost none of the "graininess" I hated on the U3011.

Regarding the warranty, this unit carries the full 3 year "next business day replacement" warranty when purchased from Amazon (not sure about other sellers operating through Amazon). I went to my Dell account, entered the monitor's service tag, and it was immediately added to my list of devices with slighly more than 3 years warranty remaining.

Beware that this is a "day one" review, and some of the past reviews indicated failures after a couple weeks or a couple months. I will be using this several hours per day, so if there are any problems you can be assured I will be back to update this review. But so far, I am VERY happy with the U3014, and will be returning the U3011.

Okay. So I had A LOT of trouble installing this kit. And there's adhesive all over the back of my brand new 500$ g-sync monitor to show for it.

The instructions are not neatly done. They do one line in english, then another line in spanish, and another line in another language and so on. This means you're unfolding a newspaper of instructions that make you search all over the huge paper to find your next step, or look back at the spot you lost. PLEASE. FOR THE LOVE OF CTHULU - Group up instructions by language. One full A-Z set of instructions in English. One for A-Z set of instructions for the next language. Do a booklet. Show BETTER pictures. Show more details about the strips/plugs.

The next thing.. The adhesive. Omg.. this stuff is unforgiving. I dunno about other people. But I have a lot of issues keeping my hands steady. I have an injury to my spine. So placing the strips was difficult. Made worse by the fact that once you attach them... they're on... and the only way they are coming off is by leaving an UGLY adhesive mark. And thanks to the bad instructions... i had to replace every.single.strip. because it wasn't explicitly clear on which way to direct the strips. At least not to someone like me - who it was his first time installing such a thing.

I would really like to see the adhesive velcro set up. Like what is used for the Hue 2 Box that's included with the kit. That way, if i needed to switch around my strips, I could have flipped them easy. and there wouldn't be this crap adhesive all over the back of my monitor. and the adhering quality of the strips wouldn't be reduced since i had to rip them up. For an 80$ kit - yea, NZXT, you shoulda gone with the same idea you had for attaching your Hue 2 Box.

The other thing is the extreme LACK OF INFORMATION on the ambient setting. I'm only just now finding out that I should have attached more strips/L-corners so i could have enabled the ambient function. That should be in it's own "BEFORE YOU START" warning disclaimer at the beginning of the instructions.

I'm really interested to see if i can remove, then replace the adhesive somehow, then re-connect everything so I can have that ambient function, and have the back of my monitor cleaned up.

So - Newbies beware. The instructions are not for the initiated.

IF your box lights up, and the CAM software is recognizing the strips - but they aren't lighting up - you have the cord plugged into the strip backwards. As in, the cords that come from the Hue 2 Box. The arrow on the end of the cord that plugs into your strips - THAT ARROW should line up with the +5v marking on the RGB strip. They will light up once you get that plugged in correctly.

I have finally received a good monitor. After ordering and Asus monitor and receiving 3 with defects, I decided to go with BenQ.

First one is flawless having no stuck pixels, backlight bleed, or bad osd input. It has very good colors for a TN panel, and I can barely tell the difference from my acer ips panel which is awesome. The colors were a little off out of the box, but with a switch to the standard mode and some rgb adjustments, it looks perfect. The freesync, which has a range of 40 to 144hz, makes gameplay buttery smooth even with large framerate drops in some of these new unoptimized console ports. Another feature i like is the osd controller which connects by usb to your monitor and allows easy osd adjustments when needed. It has a scroll wheel which is used to browse the menu and select settings, a back button, and 3 hotkeys for different monitor profiles you save. I managed to get this monitor on sale, but the price is normally a little steep.

Pros: Excellent colors for TN, smooth gameplay with freesync and good overdrive settings, good freesync range, fewer quality control issues than acer and asus for this panel type, osd controls on a handy disk

Cons: TN if you consider that a con, price a little high, bad colors out of box (fixable, see below)

Best settings for my monitor (may have to tweak for your specific one, but good place to start): Standard preset, Brightness 47, AMA high (premium causes negative ghosting), Gamma 3, Contrast 50, Sharpness 5, Red 94 Green 98 Blue 99, Black Equalizer 0, Blur Reduction off, Color Vibrance 10 (preference anything higher than 14 makes things looks awful), Dynamic Contrast 0, other settings at default.

The combination of this monitor, the NVidia driver and Win 10 Pro worked perfectly, recognizing the second monitor, and extending the desktop properly. Compared to recent experiences with other configurations on other machines, this setup required no reboot.

The stand isn't the most flexible I've seen, but it does tilt up slightly and is adjustable to some degree. Since it was perfect out of the box, I can't attest to its merits in more demanding physical arrangements.

I was ready to go in minutes after receipt.

Do you need 32" screens for a computer?

Do you have the desk real estate for it in a dual monitor setup? Not if you're working in a cubicle or little college dorm. This will have you spreading out like you own the room.

Do you need 32" screens for a computer?

Probably not, but do we want to disappoint the rest of the world who sees us as wasteful, gluttonous consumers?

Note: Haven't had it long, so can't address long term reliability. You won't have room for the box in Manhattan apartments.

My Viewsonic VX2250WM monitor arrived yesterday and I promptly set it up. The picture is excellent and I was pleased to note that the stand is essentially free of wobble. I was also pleased to find that it has more tilt (backward), than my previous monitor, which requires a pencil under the base to achieve a proper viewing angle.

A lot of people have bought this model, Amazon's reviews number 617! Quite a few people have commented on the sound quality. A few have even characterized it as good. Don't you believe it for a second. The sound quality is God awful rotten. It sounds exactly, and I do mean exactly, like a $5 pocket-sized transister radio. Seriously, that's the character of the sound. Wretched bad. The sound quality is not even suitable for spoken services like YouTube narrations. It's that bad. Have you ever heard the speaker-phone function of a typical cellphone? That's the sound quality. If you want this monitor and don't intend to use sound with it, or conversely have a seperate set of desktop speakers, fine. But, if you are relying on the internal speakers for anything but the most mediocre sound, think again.

Also dismal, was the included software. After loading the CD-ROM disc, it hung and I was forced to restart my computer. The software tries to load tedious splash screen stuff and "commercials" for ViewSonic products. You've seen this sort of thing before. I finally got the User Guide (in PDF format), to load. It was close to useless as well.

Lastly, I want to help out anyone who may be as baffled by the OSD (On Screen Display), as I was. I eventually figured out some of the functions, and I may be able to offer some useful tips. Here goes:

There are four buttons labeled (1) (2) (Up Arrow) (Down Arrow). I tried to push the buttons but they didn't seem to move. I thought they might be stiff but I eventually found that they are "touch sensitive" and probably involve some kind of optical scanning, kind of like the bottom side of an optical mouse. I found that stroking the (1) button makes a menu appear. Use the arrow buttons to travel up and down the lists, and then press the (2) button to make your selection. The (2) button works a lot like the ENTER key for computer menu selections.

The monitor comes with four cables: Power, DVI, VGA, and a miniature phone plug for audio. This is color-coded in the industry standard Green...for line level.

I will conclude by saying that I like the monitor overall, but am dismayed by the hideous sound quality and rotten software.
Would I recommend this monitor to others? Perhaps, for picture quality, a fairly steady base, and an adequate tilt adjustment. But, I suspect there are better and cheaper monitors out there. One things sure, if you want this monitor and need decent sound, you MUST have external speakers.

I purchased this display to use with my 2011 MacBook Pro. I use it mainly for coding, basic Photoshop editing, Word and Excel, and an occasional movie or video game. The only adjustment I made was to crank down the brightness so it didn't blind me when the lights in my office were dimmed. The stand is very functional. In addition to easy tilt, swivel, and height adjustments, the display can be rotated from landscape to portrait orientation.

Minor complaints: the Display Port cable carries sound to the mini jack port on the back of the display but there is no system level (keyboard) volume control if you plug speakers in that way. Also, there are no USB ports on the display.

Big complaint: sometimes the display will not wake up from sleep when used with a MacBook Pro. After about two weeks this happened to mine and I ended up exchanging it for the same model. Amazon is absolutely the best when it comes to returns and exchanges. After a few weeks the same problem happened with my replacement monitor. This time I Googled a little deeper and found a solution. When the display will not wake from sleep you have to unplug the power cord to reset it. Turning it off with the power switch does not reset the sleep circuitry. Crawling under the desk to turn my display back on sucks. My solution was to set Display Sleep to never in the Energy Saver settings and then use a black screen saver. I shut down and turn everything off when I leave for the day so this works for me.

Overall, this is a great display. 2560 x 1440 resolution for $400! How great is that? And the color is good, too. I took off one star for the sleep problem. That may be a deal killer for some people but not for me. I plan to get another one for my wife's MacBook Air.

The right one has a mediocre contrast, but the backlight bleed is not significant as well.
The left one has much better contrast, but also a much worse contrast.
If you are considering your first monitor for gaming, you can expect your monitor has a similar backlight as the right one. So it depends on you whether to accept the backlight bleeding.

To be clear, the backlight bleeding is noticeable when there are dark scenes, like moves, or some games(cyberpunk 2077). In most other cases, it disappears.

And I have replaced one monitor, this is the 2nd one. The 1st was worse since the backlight bleeding is in the center of the bottom, which is very annoying when playing games. Now it's in the corner. Much better.

Original:
The Samsung G7 32" is an overall great monitor so far. It looks nice, has a great picture and didn't completely bust the bank. These are working out great in a 3 monitor side-by-side setup.

Installation was a breeze. It takes maybe 5 minutes to put the stand together and have it on the desk. Packaging was easy to deal with and you can attach the arm to the panel without removing the panel from the box. I used the DP cable that was included for one monitor and supplied my own for the other two because of needed length. The trend these days is apparently to package the power supply in a brick, which is a pain in keeping wires and clutter from around your desk. I'd assume this eases their manufacturing process and does at least lighten the monitor a bit if you're going to mount them.

I'm using the stands that came with the monitor right now, although I'll transition to a set of Amazon arms I was using previously after I get a feel for them. The monitor itself weighs in less than 15lbs, which allows you a fairly decent arm selection. The stock stands are actually pretty nice. They are spread out enough that I still have some desk room, even with 3 of these puppies side-by-side.

The curve of the monitor worried me a bit reading and watching reviews. It turns out they are great. With 3 of them, it is an extremely immersive experience. Work and gaming all took on a new meaning. Spanning them with Nvidia is an incredible sight in games that don't distort the extended viewing. Far Cry 5 for example, looked absolutely amazing.

Picture quality is great. I generally keep the room dark, so these VA panels do a better job than IPS in general. They've been very easy on the eyes and the local diming is working extremely well. Text has been very clear - much better than other 32" I've owned. I'm just blown away at how great everything looks.

There's a bunch of little features you can read about in the specs - USB, some lighting and blah blah. Nothing really special here over the competition. No issues with any of the extras.

No for the bad part. First, a nit pick about the power plug. About anyone that cares about their hardware these days plugs into a UPS. I've been using a UPS (sometimes multiple) for 20 years. Unless there's some new trend on the orientation of the plugs these days, I can't figure out why they decided to put the plugs side ways. So instead of coming straight out or even at an angle to both prongs, it is angled to the side of a single prong. This makes plugging into a UPS horrible because it takes over two plugs. I see a lot of Samsung TVs doing this, so I'm guessing they didn't realize that TVs and monitors may be used differently. Depending on whether you're using a power strip, this might work out well. The easiest solution here is to make a standard plug where the cable comes straight now.

Second issues I've got and this one is a pain and took a star off. When these monitors enter sleep, they actually disconnect from Windows 10. That of course causes Windows to freak out and do all it's connect/disconnect sounds and notifications. The really bad part is that they don't always wake from sleep. Not even the power button with turn them back on. I'll randomly have one of the 3, sometimes 2, just not come back from the dead. In those cases, all I can do is unplug the power and back in. I'm assuming there's a bug in the firmware causing this. For now, I've turned off monitor sleep in Windows, which of course could have its own set of issues down the road. If I can't find a solution to this in the next 30 days, these might have to find their way back home.

Note that I've updated the firmware to the latest as of this writing, factory reset the monitors, read dozens of pages online from people with similar issues, tried various combinations of wake, sleep, eco, etc. and nothing has resolved the power off problem.

Assuming I figure out some solution I can live with on the power off, I love these monitors. They are expensive. My next in line choice was Gigabyte's 32" that costs about half this. The best one was flat panel though and I wanted a curved, so these were the best for the money and quality I could find.

I'm quite aware of the trouble a lot of people had trying to calibrate the colors of this monitor, and I already bought it thinking I would need to do the same. But to my surprise, my VG248QE came with great colors straight out of the box. Probably newer batches are coming already calibrated, or I just got lucky, I don't know. Fact is I plugged it to my PC, turned it on and BLAM!, awesome colors for a TN panel's standards, even at 144hz! Comparing this monitor to an IPS one is a futile exercise, but among the TN panel monitors this is clearly one of the best I've ever seen through these years.

If you're looking to buy a 144hz monitor for your PC, I believe you already know some important facts, but anyway, these are the things you must know about the VG248QE:

- The color palette is very likely to be horrible out of the box for most people. But calibration is easy and you can do it here: http://www.lagom.nl/lcd-test/
- It's a 3D monitor, but you still need to buy the nVidia 3D Vision set to use this feature.
- To play your games at 120 or 144hz, you'll need to keep the framerate at steady 120 or 144fps, otherwise you'll get the same annoying screen tearing and stuttering you usually find playing some heavier games at 60hz when the framerate decreases to 59 or less. SO, you need a REALLY powerful gaming PC to achieve this at most games from today and fully enjoy the buttery smoothness of this monitor.
- You don't need to buy a DisplayPort cable, unless you really want one. The DVI cable bundled with the monitor is a Dual-Link DVI, it'll stream 144hz from your video card without any problem.

When I say a powerful PC, I mean it. My videocard is a GTX 780Ti paired with an i5 3570k at 4ghz and I'm still unable to run a lot of games at 120hz steady. But 100hz/fps is quite achievable even in Battlefield 4 @1080p, this monitor supports refresh rates of 60/85/100/120 and 144hz.

One thing is sure: play some games at 144hz and you'll never want to go back to 60hz. It's like listening some music with $300 Sennheiser headphones and having to return to the Apple earbuds right after.

2560x1080 is a very underrated resolution IMO, still giving you that ultrawide FOV without the performance drawback of an incredibly high pixel count such as the more popular 1440p ultrawides. Expect gaming performance to be somewhere between 1080p and 1440p. At 30", the pixel density is comparable to a standard 24" 1080p monitor, so it is still very visually pleasing and much better than many of the 35" models of this resolution. The 200 Hz refresh rate makes for very fluid gameplay in lesser-demanding games like CS:GO and Apex Legends, plus the wide aspect ratio is an absolute treat for AAA titles. Colors were good out of the box, as far as my untrained eyes could tell, with only a slight yellow-green tint. FreeSync works like a charm. Overall an incredible value for the feature-set at around $250. I would recommend this to any PC gamer on a budget looking for that ultrawide experience. Very glad the monitor market has diversified so much over the past few years; I must thank the factory workers for their hard work.

I purchased this sight unseen, and solely to give my eyes a break from the much smaller laptop monitor I had been using. Since others have gone into the technical details (which are also amply described on this page), I'll stick to what I love about it and how I am now using it.

The main thing was price. At less than a hundred fifty bucks I took a chance. Hooking it up was super simple: a power cord, a VGA connection to my laptop (using the included cable), and screwing in the VESA adapter from my [[ASIN:B005C2914C Mount-It! MI-705, 3-Way Adjustable Tilting Desk Mount Bracket]] took literally ten minutes. This comes with a pedestal base that is more than adequate, but I wanted the mount I mentioned to free up desk real estate as well as to make this monitor a grab-and-go solution for a second use, which I'll get into below.

If there is one thing that I do not care for, it's the cramped area for connecting cables. The cable adapters for VGA, HDMI and power are inserted vertically, and the VGA and HDMI connections are closer together than necessary. Since I am connecting and disconnecting cables often this is a peeve, but if you plan to install it and leave it in one spot it's not even an issue.

The speakers are not the loudest when you are running sound via the HDMI port, but are more than adequate when you directly connect to the audio in port. This monitor comes with a 3.5mm cable for that purpose.

For my laptop (an aging ThinkPad) it is literally plug and play. I have finally migrated from XP to Windows 7 and this monitor's model was immediately recognized.

My second use is to press this into service as a large sheet music display. I am mirroring the screen using an [[ASIN:B00ZWOU5R2 EZcast 5GHz Wireless HDMI Streaming Media Player]] to display [[ASIN:B00WLIDHA4 MobileSheetsPro Music Reader]] mirrored from a tablet screen. Since this monitor weighs less than ten pounds it is fairly easy to set up at a gig. As a side note, if you do intend to use this on a gig or as a karaoke display this is an excellent mount: [[ASIN:B001TMCVDW Audio2000'S AST420Y Portable Flat Panel TV/Monitor Stand with Foldable Tripod Legs]].

Do note that the HDMI port will not power devices. For the EZcast dongle I have to use either a battery backup or a small wall wart (the same kind I charge my phone and tablet with), but that is not an issue either. I figured that for the price some corners had to be cut.

Although this is designed to be a desktop monitor I do wish there was a remote option for it. When I am using it to extend my laptop display it's perfect. For the screen mirroring use I mentioned a remote would be helpful.

The bottom line is I am thoroughly pleased with this monitor and may, in fact, purchase a second one so I am not swapping my laptop monitor out to take on gigs.

Overall, this is a solid monitor that compares really well with ASUS IPS monitors. For Desktop production use, this will be a good workhorse.

First of all, I did not purchase this item from Amazon... rather this review is for Viewsonic support. I purchased a Viewsonic Factory Refurbished VX2835WM 28" LCD Widescreeen monitor that was delivered to me on August 20th. 48 Hours later (yes, a mere two days folks!) the monitor was dead. It would not power on, nor display any sign of life. Power cords and outlets checked out okay. Nothing. As this was a refurbished unit the vendor was unable to replace it as they were out of stock, but I was told to contact Viewsonic. My first call to them resulted in an clueless rep telling me there was nothing they could do. I called back and spoke to someone else who told me the refurb unit carried a 30 day warranty. I snidely asked him 30 days from the time it left their factory or 30 days from date of purchase? Suprisingly, they issued an RMA but I had to ship it back to their factory in Walnut CA at my expense ($50. I was told it would be approx 14 days before I got it back. I shipped it on Aug 22, 2008 and it arrived on their dock on Aug 27, 2008. I checked the RMA status online a couple days later and there was no record of receipt showing. I called viewsonic and was told it sometimes takes up to 72 hours for an item to be checked in. (Mine took 120 -- or 5 days) Today, September 10, 2008 I called to check status, as surely one would think it would be repaired by now... I was then (politely) informed that the 14 days are 14 BUSINESS DAYS.. and they commence from the date the item is checked in.. meaning I will have to wait until at least September 23, 2008 before I can expect any update. If at that time the monitor could not be repaired, I will be offered a comparable model replacement if identical model is not available...
If this is any indication of the level of support you receive when you purchase a Viewsonic product, you should keep my experience in mind...

I got this on a deal and really liked the way it looked. Its sleek and build quality is great. I also own a HP x2301 which has served me well and I am still using it. I was very excited to upgrade my screen from 23 inch to 27inch but unfortunately the lack of research did me in. I hooked it up to my Macbook Pro and immediately noticed blurriness in text. It does have sharpness adjustment and that helped (set it around 3-4 if you are using it for Mac on 1920 X 1080 resolution). The problem is , and I agree with other reviewers here, 1920 X 1080 is not a good enough resolution for a 27 inch monitor. If you are a casual browser then this might work you. If you use your machine for development where you have to spend lot of time editing text on white background, be prepared for a headache. The graininess gets to you eventually. I used it for a day and couldn't get adjusted. For browsing news items, it was OK but my wife had issues with that too. So I guess YMMV. I really can't blame HP or monitor here because I tested other 27 inch monitors with 1920 X 1080 resolution and they had similar issues. So as long as there are people who are OK with this resolution for this size companies will continue to manufacture such monitors. I am not docking any stars for this issue. My recommendation for 27inch would be 2560 X 1440. I got a 4k monitor 28" monitor and that was a mistake as well (another story!). Also if you are using Macbook Pro make sure you use HDMI to DVI adapter and use DVI cable to connect 2560 resolution monitors. Over HDMI you will get 1920 X 1080 (evidently).

Regarding colors, again I have to agree with other reviewers. The colors are off. I tried to adjust everything, RGB, gamma, etc. but couldn't get it to work. It was a deal breaker. My wife takes lot of pictures and does photo editing. I wanted to take her Asus 23inch monitor and use it as a second screen while she would use this for her work. But lack of true colors turned to be a bummer. I had to return it. I felt pretty bad because it does look great. This is a second Pavilion monitor I returned (bought a 23 inch long time ago) because of color issues. My x2301 worked well right out of the box. So it could be IPS vs TN issue but general agreement is IPS is better when it comes to colors. My wife's Asus is little too bright for my taste but it works lot better than this one. Asus monitor is IPS too so it must be something that HP is not getting it right.

I can't comment much on the 7ms response time. I am not a gamer and for all practical purposes this response time should be fine. I really wanted to like this monitor but I think I am done with HP. I wish I had bought two of x2301 . 27Xi fell disappointingly short of my expectations. I am still giving it 4 stars because I think it might work for others just not good for developers.

Dell, this is why you can't catch up to Samsung!

Pro

Con

Other thoughts

1080P is decent enough for a 27 inch monitor, 1080P on the Samsung was sharp on this monitor its smooth and it makes me feel like 1440P would have been better but this is OK.

UPDATE: It's almost been a year now and I mainly use this as a secondary monitor. It would be fine if not for two problems.
First and foremost, its malfunctioning and causing my cursor to get stuck in the middle of my monitor. Secondly, you can't disable touch functionality at the tablet (an oversight I hadn't considered in my initial review) and have to do it in device manager. Except for some reason when I turn on/off or restart my computer, or disconnect and reconnect the tablet it will re-enable the touchscreen despite still being disabled or uninstalled in device manager. This has made my work from home experience troublesome every day.
Will be attempting a warranty repair or exchange. Will update again with customer service experience.

Pros
- Screen is bright, sharp and displays pretty well. Although if you're using it as a laptop extension like me and have it mounted like a monitor, my recommendation is to go into your display settings and up the display scale to 125%. I got 20/20 vision and looking at text in 1080P from about 30" away isn't easy.
- Plug and play. My laptop's USB-C does not support display-out so I need the USB-C (power) + HDMI (video/audio) but once both are plugged in it worked without configuration.
- Touch screen is responsive and works in all areas. Passes the paint.net test for 10 point contact. All 10 fingers on screen draw at the same time. However, they have to be added 1 after the other. Doesn't work if all 10 are pressed at same time, but that may be a software issue, not a screen issue.
- Input/output options aren't bad. HDMI for audio/visual input. 2 USB-C connections means you still keep 1 when using the other for power/visuals. The micro-USB also provides an extra connection for game controls or other devices. Finally the 3.5mm audio jack rounds it out if you want to use headphones, speakers, or a bluetooth transmitter.
- VESA mounts on the back, mount it virtually anywhere.
- Magnetic back for the protective cover (pretty standard) but it means I don't need to latch the screen to my stand at the top. It magnetically attaches itself!

Cons
-The speakers are just awful. Only use them in the most dire of circumstances. I'm no audiophile, but man are they disappointing. Even a standalone speaker that can power itself off the 3.5mm audio jack is going to do better. The only upside is that there's 2, so you get double the awful in stereo. Would have been better to just get the 1 awful token speaker and save us a few $$$ at checkout.
- I don't like that the controls for volume and brightness are shared on the "wheel." To change volume you roll up first, then you roll up/down to change the volume. For brightness you roll down first. For me, I feel like I won't be changing the brightness that much. Would've preferred to access it through the power button, which opens an input menu on a short press.

Overall, I would recommend this product to anyone given their expectations don't rely on my cons listed above.

I got the GD235HZ to use with my NVidia 3D Vision setup on my main computer. I use the monitor with the dual DVI input, I haven't tried the HDMI or other inputs. I use it for both 3D, and as a second display (my primary is a 30" monitor).

The monitor does the main thing I got it for convincingly: the 3D display is reasonably bright while using it in 3D mode with the NVidea 3D Vision. It's also decent in non-3D mode, and the resolution is reasonable (full HD, 1920 x 1080).

For the price, I had hoped this would be a great monitor. Instead, I find it only OK for several reasons.

1) As other reviewers have mentioned, the monitor stand doesn't have adjustable height. It sits pretty low on the desk, well below the ergo recommendation of having the top edge even with my eyeline. This is unacceptable IMO.
2) The buttons to configure teh mointor seem to be designed to be unobtrusive, but I find they're a little hard to hit, and the menus themselves a bit unintuitive.
3) Occasionally the monitor gets confused and shows the left half or the right half of the screen completely black. The video card things the pixels are still there, they just don't render. Very weird! Powering down the monitor and powering it back up fixes this problem.

There's not a lot of 3D-Vision compatible monitors out there, and especially not at full HD resolution. If I need a replacement for this, though, I will absolutely look for other options, and only grudgingly buy another one of these.

Pro:
- full HD 3D. That's nice!
- not a bad looking monitor, the orange highlings on the base are metallic and actually look kind of cool

Con:
- stand isn't height-adjustable
- menu buttons and menus aren't as friendly as they could be
- sometimes blanks out the left or right half of the monitor (once every few months)

Great monitor, it does what it's supposed to do. Images look sharp and great. It runs at 2560x1440 resolution, exactly matching the maximum possible resolution of dual-link DVI. That's what I use, and it looks beautiful. I standardized on DVI because that's what all of my devices can produce natively (including my trusty early-2008 MacBook Pro, which maxes out at precisely this resolution).

There's a major usability quirk, though. Whenever your video signal is interrupted for any reason, even if it's for a small fraction of a second, the monitor automatically switches to a different video input!

This is frustrating, because I use a DVI switchbox, and every time I switch sources, the monitor flipped to VGA! The only workaround is to standardize on only one input source. Don't ever plug more than one input into this monitor at the same time.

I eventually used a second monitor for VGA, to work around this problem. I used Asus tech support and they said there was no way to turn off this "feature", unfortunately.

This monitor also forces you to wait through an ASUS "splash screen" every time you turn the monitor on. That gets rather old. I don't need to sit through an "Inspiring Innovation - Persistent Perfection" mini-commercial every time! It gets annoying having to wait. There's also no way to turn off this "feature".

If the video input is interrupted for a slightly longer period of time (assuming you have only one video connection plugged in), the monitor will turn itself off. Guess what, no way to turn this "feature" off either! How frustrating. Whenever my computer goes into low-power mode from being left unattended for a while, I have to manually turn the monitor on again, by pressing the power button, it won't just come back on again when the video signal is resumed. And then there's the mini-commercial again!

I wish the thing would just sit on the video source that I had selected, permanently, and never change, and never turn itself off. Would it be too hard for Asus to do this? Evidently so.

The monitor does have a really good stand, with just enough friction and weight to hold it nicely into place, while still allowing it to be set to a variety of angles and rotations to suit your needs. I can rotate it 90 degrees, in order to use it in vertical mode, which is great for coding. Unfortunately, it only rotates in one direction, to the right. It's exactly the wrong direction from what would be needed in order to lift the video cables to a nice easy angle for access! I wish they had chosen to make the monitor rotate in the opposite direction instead, or better yet, allow it to rotate 180 degrees to allow choice of direction.

Although this monitor is nice and big, the cables connect at a surprisingly tight angle. It would have been nice if Asus would have positioned the connectors at a more friendly angle, perhaps with a shield at the back to cover them up. Instead, Asus tries to hide the cables from sight by making them cram in at an awkward 90 degree angle behind another bend. This is not the greatest design, and it's probably bad for the cables over time, because they have to be bent in order to fit.

Asus does include cables, though. In addition to the power cord, you get 1 video cord of each type: VGA, DVI, HDMI, and DisplayPort. The included DVI cable is dual-link, so it will work at the maximum resolution of this monitor.

Also, the description said that it came with speakers built-in. I don't see any holes in the monitor where the speakers would be. Maybe they're sealed up entirely in the plastic? I don't use any video cables with audio (I use only DVI and VGA video), so can't test this.

I wouldn't use this monitor for sound. There's no convenient volume control, you have to go through the menus just to access the volume control, so using it every day for sound would be a pain. There's no headphone jack on the front, either, which would have been nice to have.

Another nice thing would have been a built-in USB hub in the monitor, as many monitors have. Considering Asus is known for throwing in all sorts of silly features and gimmicks, in order to distinguish themselves, this is a rather surprising omission.

Don't let the negative tone of this review get you the wrong idea. I'm still rather happy with this monitor, it's a great step up from what I used to have. I'm just pointing out its quirks, in hopes that somebody at Asus will read this and make the next monitor even better.

Josh

I considered buying this monitor for a long time and I have to say that I am not disappointed. Firstly, to all those people out there who say that "the human eye can't even detect refresh rates beyond 60fps" or what not, I will say, 100%, with no doubt, hook me up to a lie detector, give me a blinded test that you are WRONG. The difference between a standard 60 vs the 144 which I use on this is extremely obvious. It is actually a little strange at first because it feels a little psychedelic like everything on the screen just got really, well, smooth! Whether or not this is somethign that matter to you is another matter. At this point, I prefer it, but I didn't have a problem at 60.

Game wise, its response time and all that is nice, but I never played many twitch games so it never really mattered to me.

The brightness is good. I work in a pretty dark environment, so the brightness doesn't matter to me as much, but compared to any other monitor I've ever had, I would say this is just as good if not better than most.

As far as 3D is concerned, I bought this initially because I thought "3D gaming...Awesome!!". So I bought the NVIDIA 3Dvision 2 kit and all that jazz and two days later, returned it all. Just wasn't as good as a wanted and there weren't enough instantly compatible games to really make it worth it. I have the full 3D setup from my projector (Epson 5030U which I also reviewed-excellent projector) and truthfully, I decided that if I want to play 3D games, that big screen vs close screen didn't really change much for me.

Size wise, I always would say to go bigger but realistically the monitor is perfect for me to work and mess around on. I sit about 2-2.5 feet away from my monitor and its size is just right. Also, I can see from A to AC on an excel spreadsheet in full screen standard view and that is plenty for the majority of my work. That's a weird way to describe the size, but if it is relevant to you, then I did a good job :-)

Overall, I was between this and a BenQ model that was similar and I'm not thinking that I should have gone with the other one. I have nothing negative to say about the similar BenQ, I never used it. I don't know if I made the right choice, but I'm not thinking about it anymore, so I feel like that means that I am pretty happy. I recommend.

The monitor itself is easy to assemble and hook up. It has a variety of inputs, and a very nice physical appearance. However, straight out of the box the colors are very washed out in appearance. It might be hard to tell this unless you have another monitor hooked up for comparison but with my setup the difference was striking.

My current monitor is a "WASABI MANGO UHD400 REAL 4K HDMI 2.0 3840X2160 UHD Samsung PVA Panel 40-Inch Display Port 10Bit Monitor" that I also got from Amazon. Unfortunately they aren't available any more. It was almost the same price as the AOC.

Adjusting the color with the joystick button on the back of the AOC monitor did very little to improve color quality. Finally I found that you can effectively adjust colors using the NVidia control panel, and I managed to get the desktop background to display almost the same on both monitors. The vibrance setting is really necessary to get some deep colors. Even with the enhanced vibrance setting, the AOC still isn't nearly as beautiful as the Wasabi. In particular, white is noticeably more of a grey/yellow hue on the AOC display. Again, this would be hard to notice without another display to use for comparison.

Edit: I found that for a whiter white, you need to set the color temperature setting to "cool" using the joystick control on the rear right bottom of the AOC monitor.

I purchased this display to use as a second monitor (wall mount) above my existing primary monitor (desk). So, while gaming, I could have maps, websites, or other resources available for reference, etc. Like you, I was concerned with an OLED screen and potential image retention (burn-in) but, after doing a ton of research, found the issues were present but not horrible. As long as I used good judgment and didn't abuse a static image on that second screen (deploy Windows screen saver after a reasonable amount of time), then it seemed like a good choice. I was wrong but not how you think.

Precisely two days into ownership, I'd mounted the monitor without issue, and was using it as that second monitor resource previously mentioned (in this case, a reference map). After a few minutes the screen dimmed, then dimmed again, then turned on an internal screensaver and began to slowly bounce the word "AORUS" around the screen. I'd have to alt-tab out of the game, mouse into the AORUS monitor to reactivate the display, then alt-tab back. It seemed tedious but I was sure I could adjust that particular setting. I could not.

This is hard-coded into the display and there is no user adjustment of that value. The system is called ASC (AORUS Static Control), an automatic function with the display intended to prevent potential burn-in. And I quote from the user manual, page 21, "If the monitor has not been in use for a period of time, the ASC starts to reduce the brightness of the screen gradually or active the screen saver... After 5 minutes of inactivity, the screen brightness will be reduced by 30%. After 10 minutes of inactivity, the screen brightness will be reduced by 50%. After 15 minutes of inactivity, the screen saver is activated automatically. You will see the word 'AORUS' appearing randomly on the dark screen."

I checked with professor google and found a number of complaints about that issue. There is an accompanying piece of software called AORUS Sidekick which is supposed to be able to update the firmware and *potentially* adjust that setting. I could not make that work; Sidekick never recognized the monitor and kept telling me to connect the USB-to-USB cable. I read there's a hidden technician menu which *may* allow adjustment but I did not pursue this option.

TLDR; Without the ability to adjust an auto screen saver function which turns on after 15 minutes, this isn't usable for me as a second, less used monitor. Needless to say, I did not experience burn-in after 7 hours of use. 😉

This is in incredible monitor at a very good price. Once you experience 144hz at 1440p, you just can’t go back. No dead pixels, no color or backlight bleed issues, it’s a solid display.

HOWEVER, there are two huge drawbacks for me.

First - it overheats. The monitor itself. After a few hours of gaming, I will suddenly drop to what seems like 20-40 FPS. The FPS counter will still show 144 FPS, GPU temps are well within range, but the monitor stutters so bad that you literally cannot continue gameplay. This behavior continues even after exiting a game and just browsing the internet. The back of the monitor is hot to the touch. I’ve found turning the monitor off and walking away for about 10 minutes lets it cool off enough to do 144 FPS again. I am going to try putting some USB-powered fans on the vent holes to increase airflow and see if that helps.

Second - the stand. I’m honestly at a loss on who designed such an awful stand. It actually points DOWNWARDS about 10 degrees with the factory stand installed. I had to buy a generic VESA monitor stand just to have this monitor sit upright without pointing down at my chair. Having to spend an additional $40-$60 for a mounting solution really takes away from the price point of the monitor.

UPDATE:
So about a year later, I’d say I’m still pretty pleased with this monitor for the price. I use a USB fan on my desk, pointed straight up on the bottom right side to keep the monitor cool. During hot temperatures this summer, the monitor would start turning off and on due to overheating, and was shockingly hot to the touch in the lower right corner. The fan keeps it cool to the touch, no overheats.

It does still drop to 40fps/40hz inexplicably. Usually about once a day. The quickest fix I’ve found is to “switch user” in Windows. Start to finish, this takes about 10 seconds, and doesn’t interfere if I’m active in a game. No idea why this happens but it’s easy enough to fix.

For $130, I wish I had gotten a different USB monitor.

Pros:
* Instant connection and recognition (most times). I didn't need to install the accompanying software -- the machine seemed to be able to detect and install its own driver -- expect for one time when my machine failed to access the driver;

Cons:
* brightness. This is my biggest grip with the product. You can make the screen brighter while adjusting the contrast, but these are the only 2 attributes you can adjust and you end up losing screen definition. For example, if you max your screen brightness -- which *almost* matches a conventional monitor brightness -- you can no longer see standard MS Excel cell boundaries. That is a critical thing for what I do at the office. You can adjust the
* bulky stand. The case is the stand, and it has to lay flat to support the monitor. This takes up a bunch of additional space on your desk vs. a simple arm stand;
* flimsy stand design. The "tab" that allows your case to lock into a support stand is just a piece of plastic that locks with gravity on the back side and terrible velcro on the front side. It hasn't failed me yet, but the velcro constantly comes undone on the front -- annoying;
* driver issue. The monitor usually connects on USB plug. But I had one experience where the monitor did detect on plug, but the screen was black. I ended up having to remove the driver from Device Manager (under "USB Display Adapters"). This allowed the device driver to reset, install, and connect properly. It was a hassle to figure that out.

Bottom line -- I would get a different monitor that has better screen definition.

There are a lot of good things already being said about this monitor, so I'll cut straight to the bad:

* I've had some technical problems with the USB ports just not working until I've rebooted and powered cycled the monitor several times over days, then **poof** it starts working again. Support was no help, and we exhausted all their options before they told me to return it. Another of the 3 I bought is now having the same issue.
* When my monitors kick back on after sleep, something funky is happening and causing Windows to see some monitors before others, causing my windows to get rearranged. This didn't happen with my old set of monitors, so it's something specific to these. It's annoying and erodes my faith in the product quality.
* Lastly, but maybe most importantly, I can't seem to find a happy medium in the settings. There are too many options that interfere with each other. You turn on power savings and suddenly most of your picture options are greyed out. You enable eye-saver and the same thing happens. Every single mode seems to have a serious downside.
* Finally, as others have said, the colors, while vibrant, feel over-saturated and overblown. It might be nice for movies and games, but for any other use it's just not great. Also, given just how incredibly bad the off-angle viewing is, it would be a really bad choice for any use like photo editor or graphics work, since being anything but perfectly-dead-center, both horiz. and vert., causes major color shift.

I own all kinds of Samsung appliances and electronics in my house, and frankly, this is the only one that's ever been less than a 4-star experience. I have more than once wondered if this is some kind of knock-off that somehow made it onto Amazon. Yeah, it's that bad!

The monitor works like it should.  I watch netfix and surf the web with it and it is good for the money.  The dark seens in movies are hard to see but I still like it for the price and would suggest it be considered by a friend.

GAMING

First off let me tell you that this has been a great monitor to have for it's price (504$ at the time of the sale). Even though it had 25ms, I ran games like UT 2004, ET, and Doom 3 and saw that there wasn't any ghosting at the native resolution 1280x1024. If you run it at any other resolution in games such as Diablo 2 (800x600 max) you'll see artifacts and pixelation. This is thru the DVI connection on an ATI 9500 pro.

BRIGHTNESS

Another good point of the monitor is it's extremely bright that I had to turn down the brightness on the monitor to 0. Along with adjustments to the video card brightness in the Display options. To help alleviate the headaches caused by the bright screen, additional back lighting in the room is needed (because it will sting your eyes if the only light source is coming from this monitor).

DVD PLAYBACK

DVD movies, DIVX, and others ran fine, popped in the Matrix and Reloaded and saw it handle the fast action sequences just fine and crisp as well.

LOOKS/AESTHETICS

The looks of this monitor is extremely sleek with it's thin bezel and black charcoal color. When I first took it out of the box, I marveled at the size of the panel (previous monitor was a 19" crt).

GHOSTING ON WEB PAGES

But as the previous reviewer mentioned, there is ghosting while scrolling, especially with dark background web pages with white text(or a light color). It's extremely evident. And it's a problem associated with LCD's. Because of this I get headaches while browsing through web forums or pages because the ghosting is really evident and my eyes have been trained to detect it now.

THE UNFLEXIBLE STAND

Plus the stand that comes with this monitor does not let you turn the monitor left<->right (you have to adjust the stand for that). It was also a pain to install the DVI connection since the monitor comes attached to the stand, you have to tilt the panel backward so that you can reach in there blindly to attach the DVI/power connectors.

I'm sorry to say that I am going to have to return this monitor for a dell 2001fp and see if the higher response rate will help (even though I was told the problem occurs with 16ms response rates as well).

Hope this helps you.

I do a lot of photo editing (Photoshop, Lightroom), Graphic Design (InDesign) and occasional video editing (Premiere). For years my setup was dual monitor with 22 (Dell) and 24 inch (HP) monitors. Now, end of 2015, my system is rather dated (4+ years) so I was not sure if my video card would even support the resolution of 2560 x 1440.
After initial installation (I replaced my 24 inch monitor) the size seemed rather overwhelming, but very quickly I got used to this new size and 2 months later I can't imagine I would go back to smaller resolution. The biggest advantages are in Lightroom where the space for image view is big, and in InDesign where pages can be viewed at considerably higher size (or several pages at once). Similarly in Adobe Premiere the large size offers a lot of real estate for timeline, resources, preview windows and many other panels.
The colors were good out of the box though I did some calibration later on.
The monitor has a light sensor that dims or brightens the monitor based on the light condition in the room. It can be helpful during regular use, though for photo editing I always turn it off.
Interesting feature that I tested, but not used much, is PBP - Picture by Picture. You can connect two computers to the monitor (for example a desktop and a laptop) and have the screen be divided in half, each half displaying the screen from different source.

Though there are monitors intended specifically for photo editing (like the 30 inch NEC for over $2200) I find this monitor perfect for my photography, graphic design and video work.

You are gamers like me. You are going to use it in full 4k and expect 144fps like me. Your only option is DisplayPort and that will not work... OBVIOUSLY.

First, UPDATE THE FIRMWARE. It's ages old and has a ton of problems.
Second, CONNECT THE USB. It's used for some driver control or something. Even thou it's supposed to be only a passthrough. The armory crate use it and it help with the problems.
Third, LEARN NEW CURSE WORDS. Yes, no way around it.

The big problem: Displayport HPD .
What? It's a bug/feature that detects when the cable is connected and turns off your monitor. As if you didn't just connect it yourself and expect the thing to show an image. NOOOO it will just go to sleep.
There is no solution and ASUS will not move a finger for it.

Expect to waste around 5 minutes every time you start your rig. The PC will not detect a monitor, so you manually turn the monitor on, then the PC is already not sending signal so you manually enable it, but the monitor is already asleep because of HPD and everything all over again. Until you get lucky and PC and monitor agree to work together and let you play.

What could asus do? disable HPD !!! or give it longer detection period, like, more than the 1 frikin' second that it has now.
( Note, some genius is selling a hardware emulator that keeps the HPD alive and fixes the universe... it costs more than the monitor... )

I've had two of these monitors. The first one didn't last long through my own fault. I tripped while carrying it and it broke. However, it's worth noting that I did have this one for reasons I will get to.

This monitor is very light and portable, which is nice. The cover which converts into a stand is a bonus, although I really like the hole you can stick a pen through to use as a stand. I can't speak to its battery life, as I always used it plugged into my laptop via USB, but the screen quality is perfectly fine for a portable screen, and the brightness is acceptable although nothing special.

However, there are issues. I mentioned that I had one for a short time before I accidentally broke it, and this is important because it lets me speak to varied build quality. The first monitor, the buttons functioned perfectly, and had a nice click to them. The second one, the combined D-pad/button is mushy, and requires some concerning force to register a press. However, as this monitor really only requires setup once (and I hate dealing with returns), I was prepared to accept this.

But that isn't the end of the problems. On multiple occasions, the monitor has failed to go to sleep with my laptop, and becomes stuck on a black, unresponsive screen. Unplugging it and plugging it back in has no effect. No settings on the laptop have any effect. The buttons on the monitor do nothing. The only solution I have found is to unplug the monitor from power and allow the battery to die. When this happened the first time, I was happy to find the solution online and figured it wouldn't happen again. The second time, I was frustrated. This is the third time, and I'm now outside of my return window.

I would reconsider this monitor. This problem has been noted in several forums without resolution. At the very least, I would get one without a battery, so unplugging it would instantly depower it.

When I first heard about this monitor, it was almost too good to be true, an under $200 g-sync compatible, 144hz monitor?! I mean come on who are you kidding? My curiosity got the best of me and I had to check it out. At first glance it seemed pretty good, but after reading other reviews I was skeptical because I feared I was going to regret buying it. People had said that they had monitors with dead pixels and washed out colors, or simply a black/white screen. However, the huge amount of positive reviews just made me want it even more. After receiving this monitor and testing it out, it did not disappoint at all. Initially I had it hooked up to my laptop through HDMI (supports 48-120hz), but I realized that I wasn't using the monitor to it's full potential and so I went ahead and purchased a USB type-c to DP cable and "viola!" I hit that beautiful 144hz and I can tell you this, once you reach 144hz, you're definitely not going to want to drop back down to 60hz. I paired it with my older Acer KG221Q monitor to get a nice dual monitor setup and it looks wonderful. Anyways I have nothing but praises for this monitor.

Pros:
- 144hz (so smooth!)
- 24" this is the ideal size for gaming and even work related tasks
- three unique ports for display (HDMIx1, DPx1, DVIx1)
- brightness (it's literally the brightest screen ever, I think like 316 nits or something)
- color accuracy is wonderful (around 96% out of the box, you can see this in the picture I took compared to my older monitor
- The ergonomic features is really the cherry on top with the swivel, tilt, and height flexibility

Cons:
- This may be an issue to some but for me it's not that big: pixel density/ppi (pixels-per-inch), the pixel density is as big as I would like it to be, coming in at about 92ppi at 1080p vs the typical 100ppi+ sweetspots at 1080p. It is a 24" so it makes sense that the pixels will be stretched slightly, vs say a 24" 1440p 60hz monitor. Again, this isn't a big problem for me as I'm not sitting like two inches away from my screen and it's not that noticeable when doing normal tasks whether it be gaming, work, etc. [REMEMBER 24" 1080p 144hz > 24" 1440p 60hz]

Side note: make sure you go to settings in order to change the refresh rate to 144hz, otherwise it'll most likely be set by default to 60hz out of the box. Also YOU NEED USE THE DISPLAY PORT TO GET FULL 144hz, HDMI will only get you up to 120hz, and that's if you have an HDMI cable that supports that bandwidth.
