UPDATE 7/17/17: I saw that Pixio released yet another firmware on April 18, 2017. The release notes say "Bug Fixed" so I figure they must have finally gotten their house in order. To my absolute bewilderment, now it's even worse! The FreeSync range is STILL 50-144Hz which is not supported by this panel, and now the maximum refresh rate is only 134Hz! I honestly have no clue what Pixio is doing over there. I was one of the first people that bought this monitor and I shared with Pixio EXACTLY what needs to get done to fix their firmware. This was almost a year ago! For some reason, they STILL have not fixed it, and I doubt they ever will. Shame on you Pixio. I'm lowering my rating to 1 star due to absolute incompetence of the manufacturer.

UPDATE: I finally had some time to test out the new firmware, and I have no choice but to lower my initial rating from 3 stars to 2 stars. The new firmware still doesn't work! I just wasted another several hours of my life figuring that out and fixing it. The new firmware sets the FreeSync range to 50-144Hz. This is STILL out of the panel's spec, and it causes flickering and other problems. I used CRU to correct it which seems to have fixed it again. I just can't recommend this monitor for all of the grief it has caused me, and the total lack of support from Pixio. I have better things to do with my life than fix things that don't work right out of the box, and the 10-15 hours I have wasted on this monitor is absolutely not worth the $100 or so I saved compared to similar alternatives.

I want to share my experience with this monitor because there aren't many reviews yet. I also want to share how I got FreeSync working on this monitor, because it does NOT work out of the box. I'll update this review as I spend more time with it.

Pros:
-144Hz is awesome! I read a bunch of reviews and everyone was right - you really can't explain it, but it's totally worth it. Even without FreeSync, the fast refresh rate makes tearing harder to notice without v-sync for a much better experience. It makes gaming and general computing much more enjoyable. I'll never buy a 60Hz monitor again.
-OSD is full featured and easy to use once you figure it out. "Menu" selects and "source" goes back.
-Included DP cable is a welcome addition.
-My panel has very little to no backlight bleed. I do have a small clump of grey pixels near the center of the screen, but they are only really noticeable when the background is white, like during web browsing. I'll take this over backlight bleed any day, and overall I am pleased with the panel quality.

Cons:
-Stand is trash. Normally this wouldn't be such a problem (I use $20 cantilever wall mounts), except that you can't "officially" remove it. You have to pry the entire bezel off and unhook the panel in order to remove the stand. I'd rather it just come without a stand.
-Bezel is thick and ugly as everyone says, but honestly I don't mind it nearly as much as I thought I would.
-The literature is lacking. It would be nice if they at least explained what all the OSD settings do.
-No monitor driver, possible firmware bug. Unfortunately this is preventing me from giving it more than 3 stars at this point (see FreeSync section below)
-I personally don't care for the matte coating. I'd much prefer glossy.

Other thoughts:
-Colors are awful out of the box, but it can be fixed to look pretty good. I'm using 100 brightness, 95 contrast, 2.4 gamma, 80 red/green/blue but you may find a different setting is better.
-Pixio customer service has been very responsive and friendly. They even called me past working hours on a Friday to make sure I could play games on my new monitor over the weekend. They couldn't help me, but that's another story (see FreeSync section below).
-One of the cons I mentioned is the lack of literature. I have no idea what "dynamic frame control" is, but "response time" in the OSD refers to overdrive mode (blur reduction). It definitely works on the desktop. It causes some weird color effects to appear in some games at its default setting, usually in the sky. You can enable it at the same time as FreeSync, but I couldn't tell you if that means it's working.

FreeSync:
To all the other reviews claiming that FreeSync on this monitor is awesome - you either got a different monitor than I did, or FreeSync still isn't working for you and you're just observing how much better 144Hz is. When FreeSync is working, you can see the refresh rate constantly changing in the OSD. After many hours of troubleshooting, going back and forth with Pixio customer service, and posting on the AMD forums, I think I found a solution:

-First you must enable DP FreeSync in the OSD. This is NOT explained in any Pixio literature.
-Go into the Windows display adapter properties and change the refresh rate to 144Hz.
-Now you can enable FreeSync in AMD Crimson display settings, but this FreeSync still would not work in any applications for me. The refresh rate remained constant at 144Hz.
-What you need to do is install custom resolution utility (CRU). Open it up, select the monitor, and click the top edit button. At the bottom where it says "range limits", change the V rate from 30-143Hz to 55-144Hz. Click OK twice and restart your computer.
-What I THINK is happening is the monitor firmware/driver is incorrect, and is reporting a FreeSync range that the monitor cannot handle. This results in either FreeSync not working at all, or it causing horrible corruption and flickering because the monitor is trying to do things it isn't capable of. This is inexcusable and needs to be fixed by Pixio ASAP.
-I made a post about it on the AMD forums that you can search for if you want more information.

I hope my experience helps someone else avoid all this trouble. I also hope Pixio responds and fixes the problem. I'll update this review once I have more to report. I'm just excited to finally be able to use all the features I paid for!

After some initial frustration using an "active stylus" and it not really working, I tried the Microsoft Pen and it worked really well (relative to other options). I needed to play around a bit to get it to work right, but I found if you consistent use the "ink to shape" option in OneNote, the inking works consistently. Without figuring that out, I had some odd behavior where it would default to eraser mode and not switch to drawing. An added bonus is that there is virtually no parallax effect since you are drawing directly on the screen.

The screen is really responsive otherwise and I am actually just using one USB-C cable to power and control it. This is a lot better than the drawing tablets which required 3 cables to get them to work and makes it far more portable.

There are definitely things that could be improved and I hope that driver updates make the inking more consistent but for the price and quality I am happy.

Dell "U" series monitors have a service tag that is permanently registered to Dell. This service tag is on the back of this monitor, I just went onto Dell's website and there is a place to type in your service tag number to see your warranty. I bought this monitor to replace my Samsung 4K, which was a couple years old and had gotten a few dead pixels. Not for that reason alone, of course, but I digress. The point of mentioning the 4K is that many who look at this are possibly also thinking of buying a standard 3840x2160 panel, since it takes about as much GPU juice to drive 4K 60fps as it does to drive 3440x1440 at 100fps. If you're looking for advice, buy the Acer X34. The difference in resolution is noticeable, though perhaps only because I've spent the last 2 years at 4K, but the immersiveness of the 21:9 aspect ratio combined with the buttery smoothness of high (80+) frame rates with G-Sync more than makes up for it. And, to be honest, it isn't THAT noticeable in games. I notice the difference more on my desktop, since the icons are automatically larger and spaced further apart. I've used this for only a few days, and can already tell that not only will this be my monitor for the foreseeable future, but I won't go back to 2160p until they make a 21:9 model that is 1) an IPS panel, 2) >80Hz refresh rate, 3) with G-Sync.

Arrived in a huge package on time. Shipped in the product box, which I always hate, but have learned to live with. Comes in the box fully assembled, just plug it in, remove the saran wrap from the back and foam padding from the base, and you're ready to go.

The monitor's very sturdily constructed, doesn't shake much when I hit my desk or at all when I type. The stand has a fairly large footprint, so it takes up a lot of desk space. Back panel is black glossy plastic, which should never be used on anything, ever. Very slim bezels around the panel itself, no reflection in a dark room.

My first unit had no dead pixels, though as with any monitor your mileage may vary. It is an IPS panel, so there's the normal IPS glow, and a noticeable backlight bleed. At this point, I've never seen an IPS panel that didn't have at least a little backlight bleed, and the bleed on this monitor is within what I would call tolerable levels: only in two corners, only really apparent on a black or near-black screen.

After searching for dead pixels and backlight bleed, I went into the OSD to attempt to overclock (which is a bit of a pain to navigate), and this is where I encountered my first real problem with the monitor. It's advertised as overclockable to 100Hz, but what you find out when you read the manual and go to Acer for support is that they don't guarantee each panel can overclock to that refresh rate. Your mileage may vary. I overclocked to 100Hz in the panel's OSD, and then opened NVIDIA Control Panel to change the active refresh rate, but after doing that the panel wouldn't refresh at 100Hz. In the end, I had to settle for a 95Hz overclock: not enough for me to justify an RMA in my head, but a slight disappointment since, after all, it's marketed as 100Hz capable.

It seems a bit silly to quibble over 5 frames, though, because everything else about this monitor is excellent. The greatest downside to it has nothing to do with the monitor itself, but is the fact that not all games support the 21:9 aspect ratio. Even if they do run as delivered in 3440x1440, I've had numerous issues in several games that require third party fixes. GTA V, for example, flickers to black fairly constantly and the HUD is still displayed in 16:9. The Mass Effect series has no native 21:9 support at all, and the same goes for Fallout, Elder Scrolls, and others. It's something you'll encounter relatively frequently. Luckily, there are third party fixes like Flawless Widescreen and .ini edit tricks that can help you customize your experience and do the job that game developers often seem too lazy to do.

Finally, the feature I've said nothing about: the curve. The curvature of the monitor is fairly subtle, almost imperceptible when you're staring at it head on. I'm not certain that it adds anything, but it certainly doesn't detract from the experience. The monitor's so wide that I could see glare becoming an issue if it wasn't slightly curved, but the matte finish on the screen would also probably prevent most of that. Not sure if the curve of the panel knocked the price up a little further, but to be honest I probably would have preferred a lower price on a flat panel with identical features.

So, to dumb it down into a brief pro/con list...

Pros:
- Overclockable up to 100Hz
- G-Sync
- IPS panel delivers excellent color reproduction
- 21:9 aspect ratio delivers multi-monitor immersion in a single panel
- Sturdy build
- Arrived with no dead pixels, no damage from shipping, and tolerable levels of backlight bleed. YMMV.
- Matte finish prevents screen reflection

Cons:
- Acer does not guarantee 100Hz overclock, your panel may top out lower
- Glossy black plastic case
- OSD difficult to navigate
- Price

Pro 1: 3 inputs - DVI, VGA, HDMI. HDMI added about $50 to the cost of this monitor but it definitely came in handy when I was able to hookup the HDMI output from a cheapo graphics card on a new computer. So I was running three computers using one monitor - really great. When I got rid of the old computer, I kept the new one on HDMI - picture looks fantastic.

Pro 2. After a few days, I found I did not need the so called fast response time or maximum brightness. I have set it using the supplied software to "Internet" and find the monitor is sufficiently bright in my room. Sometimes, I bump it up to a custom user configuration when watching video. BTW, this monitor can be dazzling bright if you so need and can be adjusted via software depending on the time of day and room light levels.

Pro 3. People complain about the touch buttons but once you understand them they work fine. Touch once to activate (labels light up so you can see where the touch buttons are) before using. After the initial settings, you rarely need to touch them except to switch inputs - you can control the monitor from software, i.e. Magictune.

Con 1: Magictune x64 software for Windows 7 64-bit is rather flaky and will sometimes disable itself requiring me to kill it and restart. I often get 3 icons in the system tray in addition to a LCD gamma icon. Update: one suggested solution is to use the 32 bit version of Magictune instead of the 64 bit version - so far so good.

Con 2: Magictune does not save more than one user configuration. Big win if Samsung would add this.

Con 3: Slightly wobbly stand, not bad but not great like a Dell stand.

Con 4: No height adjustment or swivel on the stand.

Con 5: Limited viewing angle. I understand that this monitor has pretty good viewing angles given the technology used (TN).

Becoming acclimated to the many options (color, warmth, brightness, contrast, response time) and finding the right combination of those options takes time. The screen is small, although larger than my 13" laptop screen and affords me good screen extension when I travel. I am still very pleased with its ease of connection with a single USB 3 cable and fold-flat stand. It packs well in my carry-on and has held up well under travel stress in a neoprene sleeve.

Original Review (December, 2014)

I waited to review this until I had used it enough to be sure how I liked it.

This is the second AOC 16" portable LCD monitor I have purchased. While I like them both, this model is my favorite. It has a slim profile, has a sturdy stand, and a nice wide viewing screen (my other one is the same size but seems to have a narrower screen). I'm using it with a Samsung Series 5 Ultrabook, 8 GB of RAM on Windows 7 Home Premium 64 bit.

The best part: it runs on USB 3.0, so it only takes up one USB port (3.0, of course) on my computer, rather than two.

Runner up best part: the monitor came with its very own neoprene case.

I gave it 4 stars instead of 5 because it sometimes gets lines all over the screen and is unreadable. Others have commented about this annoying problem. For me, this seems to happen when (a) I am running several devices on a portable USB 3.0 adapter without its own power cord (the adapter, that is), (b) I am asking my laptop to do some heavy-duty work, and (c) I don't have my laptop plugged in. My guess: the laptop doesn't have enough power to run everything I'm asking it to run at once. I don't have trouble when my laptop is plugged in. While this has been frustrating at times, attaching my laptop's power cable has been an easy fix.
This is a beautiful display. You get what you pay for and this is exactly that.

To get the elephant of the room out of the way, yes this is 144hz. I forgot to screenshot it in Windows but I was pleasantly surprised to find it, since typically these portable monitors are 60hz only. This one is otherwise very typical, you have the type-C input and the mini-HDMI+type-C power input. The speakers are nothing exceptional but at least it's there if you don't have any, and the image quality is fine, typical of these monitors. Overall a great experience and the novelty of it being 144hz makes this pretty special if not a bit more expensive compared to other portable monitors out there.

I guess a good use case in addition to what's typical of portable monitors is using it to game. Say you have a portable workstation or a laptop where there's a powerful GPU but the display is either really high resolution 60hz or just otherwise not as good for gaming, you can connect this. Alternatively for gaming laptops w/out MUX switches but has one of its output connected to its dGPU, you might be able to connect this and get that dGPU direct FPS boost.

I love this monitor, but as the headline says, it has the flaw of not being compatible with Comcast's standard remote. If you want it to be a multi-purpose TV/Monitor, using standard cable, you'll have to use the HP Remote for power and volume.

Now, that said? In all other respects, its a great monitor, with good if not spectacular sound. The visual quality is quite sharp, as expected, with the exception of when you're plugging in a phone or tablet, at which point it adapts to the maximum resolution that the device offers. (No fault of the monitor). Note that you'll need to get an adapter to use Apple devices, but Androids are apparently plug and play.

It is easy to set up, and ~very~ solidly constructed, with the option to either wall mount or put on desk or table top. Bottom line? If you're using this as a monitor for a Desktop computer, it's just great. If you want to use it to stream video, or use with standard cable, you're probably going to wind up using two remotes, at least until the Cable companies accommodate this type of monitor.

I did almost no real research on anything but price when I first started looking for a new monitor and ended up with a LG 32" inch 16:9 with Freesync . While it was a decent monitor I didn't know what Freesync and G-sync were and hadn't thought about refresh rate. I have a Nvidia 2080 so I was pretty bummed when I realized it wasn't compatible with my Nvidia cards G-sync and had a max 60hz. After sending the LG back I really started delving into the tech and this monitor rose to the top pretty quickly. I was initially a little concerned whether I would miss the extra few inches of vertical viewing with a widescreen but that is not an issue at all, I actually like it more.
The curvature is perfect on this monitor, the fit and finish is amazing and it's built like a tank. There have been reviews concerning light bleed in the corners and that was the first thing I looked for when I booted it up. There is very minor bleed on the two lower corners but that is it. It in no way effects any image at all, you can't even see it on the desktop screen with a all black background. There are no dead pixels and the brightness is just fine even with sunlight in the room. The refresh rate is fast and the picture is clear.
This monitor is really sweet, I catch myself just staring at the back of it sometimes with it's colorful pulsing glory and Alien logo. I can't overstate how pleased I am, get it and don't look back.
This is good for travel and needing another monitor in a temporary/compact work space.

I am driving this monitor from an MBP 16in. The key aspects are as follows:

* The software and manuals are on a CD/DVD. One might wish that at least the packing would include a physical printout of the manual.

* Using the USB-C cable to connect USB-C to USB-C does not drive the monitor with all of its possible options available.

* Trying to drive the monitor through a modestly-budgeted (Anker) USB-C to HDMI adaptor does not provide all of the possible options and indeed seems to give issues tracking the color calibration.

* The only option that worked was to use a trusted USB-C (thunderbolt) to HDMI adaptor (I use one from iVANKY). This drives the monitor with its fullest options exposed in the menu settings.

* The menu toggle is conveniently located at the center bottom. You can adjust the sound volume with left-right action. You can select the full menu options with a press.

* The speakers are somewhat tinny sounding. When sound is being driven through the HDMI, you cannot control volume form the MBP, you must use the monitor hardware button (as noted above).

Now that this is set up, I appreciate the 4k next to the Retina on the MBP (sitting on the right of the monitor). I appreciate the extra screen real-estate. I can have a split-view of two different apps running nicely on this monitor. Finally, I appreciate being able to toggle between different pre-sets in monitor output, such a Vivid, Custom, or Cinema. I use this to run Vivid in daylight, a lower-brightness Custom set in dimmer evening light, and Cinema for video movie streaming.

Overall, this is a very good to excellent option as a second monitor to a MBP 16in.

After a few weeks with this monitor, I feel compelled to give my impressions...

The screen is exactly what it advertises itself as, that being a quality 22" widescreen monitor. Shipping was unexpectedly fast (the time from placing the order to setup was less than 50 hours for me) from Antonline. Setup was a breeze, taking no more than a few moments.

Using this screen with my 2.0 GHz Macbook running Mac OS X Leopard, all I had to do was connect one end of the DVI cord to the monitor, then connect the other end to the Mini DVI to DVI adapter I had purchased. After plugging in the power cord, the notebook automatically recognized the monitor, optimizing the resolution to 1680 x 1050 instantly. And I have to remark what a breath of fresh air it is to finally enjoy the kind of screen space I've sorely missed since ditching desktops 3 years ago (no offense to notebooks, but 13.3" inches of screen space just doesn't always cut it). A very easy and hassle free set-up experience, although this is clearly my specific account of proceedings.

The images are very crisp. No dead pixels, no light leakage, no noticeable problems whatsoever. The included 3 year warranty definitely goes a long way toward my feeling secure that I'll have a functioning monitor for the foreseeable future. And should also add that Acer includes both analog (VGA) and digital (DVI) output. Many comparable monitors sometimes exclude the digital option, so to see both ends of the spectrum covered is in this instance a pleasant surprise.

There are a few shortcomings worth mentioning, the most notable of which is the omission of any sort of speaker system. I bought it as a monitor, so this was not a major problem for me, although I suppose it would be convenient to consolidate the various PC peripherals in the name of conserving precious desk real estate.

However, at the end of the day there is very little to complain about here. The monitor offers a quality picture at a price point that is virtually unbeatable for a unit of this size. This definitely a product worth considering for anyone interested in upgrading to a unit with a little more screen space, or for anyone looking for an affordable second monitor.

However... I installed the VDisplay software (which lets you control some monitor settings from your computer) and noticed that the monitor had a firmware update available. In addition to the impressive display and advanced technology, I also love the versatility of this monitor. I have my Dell XPS 15 docked using a TB16 docking station, and I also have my gaming computer hooked up to it as well. It looks amazing while gaming and has definitely improved my gaming experience. The wide screen has also been a huge help while working, allowing me to be more productive and efficient. I am very happy with my decision to purchase this monitor and would definitely recommend it to others.

I got this LCD despite what some reviewers have said about its colors being washed out -- I like Viewsonic a lot, wanted a huge 1920x1080 monitor, and hate glossy screens.  This is it.  There's no frills with this one: it's got DVI and VGA, no usb hub or cheap speakers.  Set it to its native res, hook up a DVI, and go.  The stand isn't ridiculously large, no dead or stuck pixels, nice basic controls (I like the 3 minute auto-shutoff) -- simple, perfect.  I must admit, it was a little less bright and contrasty out of the box than most LCDs, but I actually like that.  In my GPU settings I turned the brightness down and the contrast up (just a little) and now the picture is absolutely flawless.  I am a big-time online FPS (TF2) player, and this display is great for that -- if I set it a couple of feet from my eyes it literally takes up my entire forward field of vision, this makes gameplay a lot more immersive.  It's great for movies, desktop use, everything.  I upgraded from a 20" Optiquest (Viewsonic's red-headed stepdaughter brand) at 1600x1050, and WOW what a difference.  Not only is the resolution higher, but the image is larger -- best of both worlds.  If you're on the fence, consider this review a shove to the "buy it" side.

Lenovo is a prestigious hardware company, known for their utility and consistent build quality among their computers and computer components. This monitor met those exact expectations.

After opening the very well packaged box, I was instantly pleased by the aesthetics this monitor offers. A narrow bezel goes very far in terms of looks, and the squared off design with tight corners emanates practicality and utility.

After mounting it to a VESA desk mount, I plugged everything in while examining the I/O panel interface. One has quite a bit of options in terms of display interfacing, such as HDMI, DVI, and USB-C. It comes equipped with two USB ports, I believe these act as a hub for the peripherals of whatever is attached to the display via USB-C. I have not had the ability to take advantage of the USB-C interface, as my computer displays are currently routed via GPU - DisplayPort and HDMI. However, I imagine this would still be useful in many applications.

In regards to performance and quality of display, it is simply working as intended. I have meticulously examined nearly every line of pixels with the help of "display-testing software", and I have yet to be confronted by a dead pixel. It's relatively "matte" display panel is non-intrusive to the eyes and perfect for reading extensive amounts of text. The back-lighting is consistent across the entire display and the color uniformity is exquisite. I am perfectly content with it's performance and I have yet to even take advantage of the full 75 HZ due to the fact that I am only using an HDMI, which inherently caps at 60 HZ - the DVI port would be needed to make use of the full 75 HZ, USB-C may work as well.

This is my third monitor in an array of 27" monitors, and it adds the much needed space for my daily work. I currently have it mounted and configured in an inverted portrait orientation. This is due to it's use case; reading and writing long standard format text documents and PDF's. I occasionally use it to scroll through my emails, as it's current orientation allows for a "big picture" perspective.

In conclusion, this is the ideal monitor for the architect, the IT professional, or the casual office worker. It is built to work well and to keep on working. I'd give it some serious consideration if you are in the market.

The BenQ XL2411Z is a great 144hz monitor. If you are someone who games a lot then this is definitely a good monitor to look at. If you are not someone who games a lot then I can't really recommend 120hz+ monitors because they do not offer as good of color reproduction as regular 60hz IPS monitors. I play PC games a lot, especially CS GO, so this monitor changed the way I play. It is a night and day difference to go from 60hz to 120hz+. Everything is extremely fluid and there is no more screen tearing as you look around quickly. However, you need to have a high end graphic card because in order to get higher than 60hz on a monitor you need to be getting over 60 fps in a game. For instance, in CS GO I get around 260 fps so I can easily achieve 144hz with this monitor. If you can't push over 120 fps in a game then there's no point in getting a 120hz+ monitor.

The construction of this monitor is very sturdy and solid. It definitely feels premium and I like that it is not flashy. It is very sleek and fits into my desk setup nicely. Also, the fact that the base has a nice little tray in it is a very nice little feature. I place a lot of little things in there so they're not taking up desk space. You can tilt this monitor so that it is portrait mode, which is nice if you code a lot. You can also raise it up and down so it is more level with your eyes when you are sitting. When it is pushed all the way down it locks into place and there is a nice notch on the back you can grab to carry it.

The visuals on this monitor are OK. Again, 120hz+ monitors are not known for there color reproduction. Out of the box you are definitely going to want to mess with the picture settings because it will look awful right out of the box. I settled on standard picture mode with about 40% brightness and 80% contrast. It is a great looking monitor but don't expect it to compete with high end IPS panels.

I have always wanted to try a portable monitor, so I was really excited when this came to my door. Right away, I was really impressed by the number of cables included with the monitor - you get a USB C cable, USB A to C cable, and a mini HDMI cable. My 16 inch MacBook Pro's USB C ports are powerful enough to use the monitor without the included power adapter, but I am glad they include one in the box! I have only tried this monitor with my MacBook, but I am sure it would work well with a game console or any other HDMI capable source you wanted to display on it. I think the touch screen is more of a novelty than it is practical - it is nice to have in a pinch but it is not something I personally use often. I am glad they included it though! As for the actual panel, I was a little disappointed by the resolution. I was hoping for 1080p rather than 768p. It is more than useable for basic tasks but I do notice the pixels on the screen from time to time. Because the monitor is only 11.6 inches, pixelation is less noticeable than on a larger screen with the same resolution, but it still annoys me a little. Lastly, the monitor is not very bright, so I am having to use it on 96 brightness (out of 100), which makes it work fairly well for my needs. I do think this is a good portable monitor for the price and the included stand + ability to VESA mount it is great. I can't give it five stars due to the lower resolution/low standard brightness but I think it deserves four!

I bought this Pen Display after using Wacom drawing tablets, such as Intuous. This product is amazing. The pressure sensitivity works in ALL of my programs that I use (Adobe Flash, Photoshop, Illustrator CC) when using the correct drivers** (see below). The viewing angles aren't great by any means, however they rarely get in my way. I do sometimes double-check my color palates (on second screen), however they turn out to be correct 99.9% of the time.

** As for my driver issues. The Monoprice Drivers aren't the greatest, lets be honest. The best they have so far, is 5-point calibration, which doesn't quite correct for parallax as well as I need it to.

The dumbest thing is, if you are using Windows 8.1, and setup this Pen Display without installing Monoprice's driver, Windows will install their own driver. In Windows' driver settings, you can use their 16-point calibration, and it is AMAZING at correcting for the parallax. However, if you use Windows' driver, you will not have Pressure Sensitivity in anything other than Photoshop (in my experience).

Luckily, this monitor's hardware is literally identical to the Huion GT190 (as-in basically just rebadged). Huion's driver, is exactly what you need to make this monitor work in a more professional way, with pressure sensitivity, and a much better 9-point calibration (9-point so far, but they say even better is coming, and I believe them). Find Huion's driver here:
https://docs.google.com/uc?id=0B5LrxEbDolynWERVOEZhQ3NVYzA&export=download (This Driver link was emailed to me directly from Huion's Support Team, it's their most up to date driver as of 9/17/2014)

So if you're worrying, like I was, over the bad reviews found here and the Monoprice website, and are hovering over the 'Buy Now' button, please do it. All these nitpickers who are used to overpriced Wacom products and then try to compare it with this, are ridiculous. If you need one of these and this happens to be in your budget, take the chance like I did and be pleasantly surprised!

Let me start off by addressing D. Morris' review "Let Down". It sounds to me that you just got a bad product. It looks like a refurbish or a reboxed return. I've ordered from Tiger Direct in the past and they do not have the greatest service, and I have heard similar complaints. I have no fuzzyness in my display. It is very clear.

As for the Analog only mode, I have had similar problems. Occassionally on bootup it would use DVI-D mode and other times it would use DVI-A mode. It seemed almost random and it was beginning to frustrate me. This was with both a GeForce4 and GeForce7 graphics card on 2 seperate computers. I have recently found on nVidia forums other similar complaints regarding this monitor and it seems to be a monitor related problem(or some weird nVidia issue with ONLY this monitor). The only workaround I have been able to come up with is to go into the OSD controls and for the "Prefered input" instead of using "Auto Select" choose "DVI-D". This has been able to force it to use DVI-D on my system, however, I cannot view anything until Windows has fully booted, since DVI-A is required to view the BIOS Post. Perhaps if complaints are loud enough Viewsonic may fix the problem in a firmware update.

I love this monitor as my first LCD. The response time is very quick and I use it for normal use, Gaming (UT2004), and watching movies and everything seems very smooth. I love the native res 1600x1200 on this monitor. As with most LCDs you will not get very good quality running it at any other resolution than it's native res. This monitor does not suffer from the Horrible backlight bleeding X across the screen like many of the 930bs. On a full black screen I do notice very light "smuges" in the corners from some backlight bleeding, but it is not noticeable on anything other than an all black screen and even then it is not bothersome to me. The colors are vivid and this monitor is very bright! I use it with my brightness and contrast set at around 70 each. Ocassionally even lower for the brightness. It is that bright! My monitor came with zero dead pixels.

The only complaint I have is the DVI-D and DVI-A mode selection issue I explained above and the large footprint of this monitor. I really wish Viewsonic had given this monitor the more modern and smaller base of some of their other LCDs.

All in all I'm very satisfied and I would give this 4 1/2 stars.

EDIT (1 Jan 2017): Nearly 10 year life, and it finally died on me. It's getting power and the green light indicates signal to the monitor but the screen is black. I suspect the backlight circuitry died. I've already taken the thing apart and will attempt to replace a few suspect capacitors and see if I can't resurrect this monitor. It's been a great companion through all the recent changes to monitor technology and has held up fairly well. Hopefully I can add a few more years to its life.

1st: Purchased this for my son to play his Nintendo Switch games;
2nd: Not expecting high-level quality graphics
3rd: Purchased to mount on wall.

Overall, my son and I are very satisfied with this monitor. It does what I needed it to do and was easy to mount and hook up.

Hookup to Nintendo Switch through HDMI cable with no problem. The monitor also doesn't have any speakers.

Wall Mounting kit is not included, but it is VESA ready for standard 100x100 mounting kits. I used Mounting Dream Full Motion wall mount kit which was perfect for this size of monitor.

The only issue I had was the power cord: by mounting the monitor on the wall, I placed it about 4 feet high. However the power cord cable is too short. While the cable will reach from surge protector on the floor to the monitor fine, the small cable from the power block is shorter and the weight of the power block pulls the power cable out very easily.

If you wall mount this, please take note of the length from power block to the monitor when measuring. We have a temporary mini-shelf set up just for the power block. Even if the monitor were closer to the outlet, this power block issue would not be resolved.

-OSD tends to have false positive conflict detections. Can only be fixed by unplugging the monitor and plugging it back in. (The monitor will detect a feature is on that is not and will tend to not allow me to use settings that I should be able to)
-I had to contact ASUS so they could turn on their support web page for this monitor, this shows how not paying attention they are to this monitor (Yes asus, that was me)
-Tends to have the right side of the screen show complete garbage on input switching.
-Cannot TURN OFF AUTO DISPLAY DETECT (Big off putter for me since I am a techie and am usually needing the input to NOT CHANGE when turning on and off devices. I would get an EDID emulator but idk if those will support 3840x1200p120hz)

All in all, this monitor REALLLY needs a firmware upgrade that its never going to get. I would avoid this cheaper Super Ultrawide and maybe look elsewhere.

I must confess...after I ordered this, I wondered if I should have just invested in a less expensive CRT, especially since I play a lot of computer games, and LCD's traditionally haven't had the best reputation for gaming, due mostly to technical limitations causing "ghosting" - sort of a temporary after-image. Once this thing arrived and I plugged it in, however, any such notion vanished from my mind.
Bear in mind that I have not had a lot of experience with LCD monitors, as I'm sure most people haven't. I did a bit of research on various sites, including this one. I had it whittled down to a few different choices and I still wasn't entirely sure which monitor I wanted to get. The swing vote came when I stumbled across CNET's recent pick (January) as this as their number one buy for a 15 inch LCD.
The image is bright, it's crisp, and I can see textures that I never even knew were there on my old CRT. I have not one problem gaming with this. I have been playing Warcraft 3, Thief 2, and other games with this thing and they are running smoothly and better than I have ever seen them before. Actually, I should say that at fist, I thought I noticed a slight "ghosting" in Warcraft 3, but I see no such thing now, so make of that what you will; perhaps it was a case of seeing what you expect to see or something. Anyway, I have absolutely no problem playing Serious Sam: The Second Encounter, a somewhat obscure but good first-person shooter, and I notice no ghosting or anything of the sort. You can actually see extremely well in Thief 2, which is saying something since the game is quite dark by design. (I actually had problems with this darkness on my old Compaq CRT.)
Some people have said that text does not show up right or something to that effect. My best guess is that they do not have either the monitor or their computer's display settings set to the native resolution of 1024 x 768, because the text on mine is clearer than I would have ever hoped for, and causes less eye strain than on just about any CRT in my experience. There is also software included to help get the color settings just right.
You do need to have it adjusted to the proper angle in order to have uniform brightness from the top to the bottom of the screen, but I don't see why this should be a problem. Unless you are getting a monitor to watch DVD's on from your couch, you probably do not need a bigger LCD than this. I would even say that any bigger might be too much, unless you are planning on mounting it on a wall or something. Keep in mind that the 15 inches is the actual (diagonal) size of the viewing area, whereas when sizes are used to describe CRT's, the viewing area is less than the stated size...don't ask me why; that's just the way it is. So, if you want to know how big the screen is, get out a tape measurer and measure out 15". That is the distance from the top-left corner of the display area to the bottom-right. That is just the display area - NOT the whole monitor, which is actually slightly bigger. It's probably bigger than you would have thought.
It even comes with a 3 year warranty on parts and labor. Some other companies' warranties, such as NEC's (according to some of the reviews I have read), allow for so many "dead pixels". To the best of my knowledge, Samsung has no such "gotcha" policy.
All in all, I would say this is a great buy if you are in the market for a new monitor and want to jump into the twenty-first century. CRT's are still cheaper though, so it really depends on how much you want to spend.

Moreover, it's so short there's a good possibility that you'll need and extension cord in order to reach a power source. This becomes a certainty if you're mounting it to a desk arm (e.g.. in a multi-monitor configuration) as the power cord will barely reach the base of the arm.

I was almost ready to buy a GTX 460, a Nvidia 3D vision kit, and the Asus 23.6" 120HZ 3d monitor. This would have totaled to $640, ($490 if you have the proper card already). The Asus wasn't even back-lit LED.

Instead at the last minute I saw this, strangely without any reviews. I looked more into it, and apparently it was a new release, and some review on Newegg said the 3d looks impressive, so I took the risk and went ahead. Total cost $300 (the price seems to fluctuating up and down). The biggest con is there just isn't so much information about it online. The funny thing is, I still don't know some important details, which I will go into.

PROS:

Hands down, the cheapest way to get into 3d gaming, 3dwise the value doesn't get any better than this

The 3D Looks GOOD, no flickering

LED Backlit saves energy

1080p resolution, cinema aspect ratio (i.e. no bars on Hulu)

Comes with TRI-Def (3d conversion software for games and viewing photos/videos)

Cheap Polarized Lenses, and NOT tied to Nvidia Graphics Card to keep 3d

CONS:

2d wise, it is a typical run of the mill screen, blacks are disappointing, there is minor backlight bleeding, definitely doesn't have that WOW feel some displays have, did I mention the blacks were disappointing?

I can't find too much information about it. The 3d solution is TriDEF, (The same software AMD 3D uses) BUT, AMD does not list this as a compatible display. If you go to the downloads section on
Tri-Def, the LG is a separate download. I installed the "generic" TriDEF software and it did not work with the display. How much modification does TriDef do for the LG display? Is this a "standardized" 3d display that will continue to have updates? It's a mystery to me.

I do know that the Tri-Def software included is modified to the point that upon start-up it checks every time to make sure that the specific LG monitor is connected. If the monitor is not connected it will prompt you to enter a license key Not the biggest deal, though know that the included software ONLY works with the specific monitor.

The Tri-Def software does say that it does not support 3d Blu-Ray movies. If you hook up a 3d blu-ray player to this monitor, will it work in 3d? Third party sources say yes, though it can't be confirmed from LG's website. (Update- other people are confirming that it does work with 3d blue ray players)

Text is difficult to read in 3d mode (and in 2d mode with glasses on)

The Display is VERY bright (for 3d), though it's annoying to push it down every time you take off the glasses. (UPDATE- It is easier with Forte Manager, see bellow)

It's not REALLY 120hz, from my light understanding "tru-motion 120hz" is a technology that helps control motion blur you see on quick moving pictures.

Just like my old Samsung 226BW, it has a bright blinking blue light, fine for an office, but this is supposed to be a "cinema monitor". Why the distracting blue light? And when it's sleeping it starts blinking, so you have to turn it off if it's in your bedroom. (UPDATE- Install "Forte-Manager" [...] , you can actually turn off the power light in the settings (Note forte manager does not work via a VGA cable) Well played LG, (though if you turn it off, it will still blink when the monitor is off)

Conclusion:

I didn't really elebaroate in the Pros, but the 3d effect is pretty cool, and it does work well. This has the advantage over active technology of giving you two simultaneous images, (so no flicker effect), though it has the Con that in 3d mode you really aren't seeing 100% 1080p. I've only really tried it in WoW, and I have to say it's pretty cool. It does take some getting used to, and probably makes the game a bit harder, though as most describe it, the added depth you see into the screen is impressive.

Overall I'd say I'm quite happy of my purchase, though am admittedly a little concerned over future support. I really hope that AMD eventually lists this as a "compatible display". Just like the series of "Lost" I'm sure this review gave you more questions than it answered, though I'm sure in the next 30 days we will figure out the answers to these. You don't hear this very often, but shame on LG for lousy marketing. (Update- I've been told that it is a compatible display on AMD's website now)

First off, I would HIGHLY recommend this monitor. The thing performs wonderfully, and 4k at 60hz without having to use scaling is such a lovely experience. I was surprised just how deep the blacks were and how good the colors were on the VA panel. Among the best backlights I've seen on a monitor - nears OLED level blacks. If you are displaying all black on it, you wont be able to tell if it's on or not during the day. Even at night, it's a barely noticeable glow that you have to look for.

Now on to why it's only 4/5 stars:

1.) The extreme size of this monitor (make no mistake, it's enormous) means that at the horizontal edges you're going to experience some hue/gamma shift due to the extreme viewing angles. The reality is that if you're using a monitor this size, it really needs to be curved. This is a first world problem, but it's a problem nonetheless.

2.) No monitor adjustments. The monitor is heavy, if you need to move it around on your desk, it's not a trivial thing. It does have vesa mounts, which is nice, but the base stand doesn't include any way to move things around.

3.) Factory calibration is a bit off. This isn't huge because I calibrate all my monitors when I get them, but it *needs* to be calibrated. If you want to use the thing right out of the box, you'll be met with a blue tinted high temp monitor. Once calibrated, it's excellent.

4.) It's hard to use other monitors after using this thing. Seriously, it spoils you. It'll make every other monitor out there disappointing.

Overall though it's a fantastic monitor. If you're thinking about getting it, do it.

I ordered 2 of this primarily due to the glowing Amazon reviews. I also have 2 newly purchased BenQ EW2420's, which was convenient for a side by side comparison.

First off, what most of the reviewers are saying about this monitor is generally true. The stock stand is pretty awesome for any monitor. It raises, tilts, and rotates. Has pretty good construction quality, and the nicest thing is that it uses standard VESA mounts, unlike most stock stands. This means A. the monitor actually has VESA mounts (which is getting increasingly rare), and B. You could use these stands on any monitor with VESA mounts.

As far as the general picture quality of this monitor, it's really good... for a TN LED panel. I point that out because they were head to head with my new Ben Q's, which are VA panels. (my first experience with VA). Backlight uniformity was generally good on the Viewsonics, but just like all TN LCD's, they weren't quite up to snuff in black levels, which translates to better color reproduction. I calibrated both the BenQ and Viewsonic using a Spyder 3. I was a little upset that this monitor didn't have more manual controls, but I was able to calibrate both to the point where the colors looked almost identical. I opened up a bunch of RAW photo files, and even did some work in Photoshop. It's interesting to see how the Viewsonic shows banding in subtle gradients whereas the Ben Q smooths it out noticably better.

On a different note, I happened to get these a week after Battlefield 3 released, so I was able to test them out with fast paced gaming. I was pretty horrified to see really pronounced ghosting/ screen tearing on the Viewsonic. Not going to pick on it too much for this since a super fast response time and good gaming performance is not it's deal, but it's worth noting. Not a very good gaming monitor at all.

Very odd as well, to have a monitor nowadays with speakers, but no HDMI. Thought that was a little weird.

Overall, it's a really good monitor for what it is. I can totally see why it gets so many glowin reviews, but I think it's overpriced. After actually using it person, I can confidently say that it's a 200 dollar monitor with a 70 dollar stand. That's what you're paying for. I'm not really sure this offers all that much more then a solid 180 dollar monitor, like a cheap TN LED Asus.

I was a little hesitant buying this monitor coming from a 7 year old LG IPS Ultrawide, but I needed higher refresh rates and 1440p at a decent price, and that led me to this monitor.

So far, it's very good for the price.

Things I'm most impressed with:

-Black uniformity (Very good on my unit, especially for a curved VA panel)
-Grey uniformity (Okay on my unit, above average for sure)
-White uniformity (Absolutely perfect on my unit, 0 flaws)
-Lack of backlight bleed (My unit has the slightest bleed in the top left and bottom right corners, but hardly noticeable.)
-Zero dead pixels
-Fast Pixel Response (It is much faster than the LG, even being VA, VA has come a long way! There is some blur, but nothing that bothers me as it's much better than my previous monitor. My unit passes the blur busters test with ease to the naked eye)
-Color accuracy (I haven't officially tested it, it out of the box stock settings seem to be pretty accurate to my eye, I changed the settings though as I like the oversaturated look)
-Overdrive settings (Previous reviews from Tech Channels when this product first came out always mentioned improperly calibrated Overdrive settings. Gigabyte seems to have fixed this on the newest models, I have noticed no issues using the "balanced" overdrive mode @165Hz)
-Overall Brightness (This thing punches well above 250 nits, other reviewers on their units have cited 320 nits, mine feels even higher, potentially pushing 350+ nits peak brightness. It can hurt my eyes in the dark)
-Useful extras included (Gigabyte includes 3 separate power cables (for different countries), a DP cable, and an HDMI cable. Decent quality cables as well, nice bonus.)
-Great packaging, assembly was super quick and straightforward. Only thing that got me stuck was releasing the spring tension from the monitor arm. There is a small piece of black plastic keeping the spring tension, I first tried to remove it without releasing the spring tension, that was a no go. Once you push up on the stand and release the tension, the plastic falls out with ease and the monitor arm snaps back how it's supposed to, and you can install the stand. It's ready to go after that.

The not so good:

-G-Sync flicker. (It flickers bad, and I'm still trying to diagnose it. Some games do, and others don't, so it might not be the monitor. To my understanding, ALL G-Sync monitors flicker, some less than others. So far, playing around with a few settings has helped reduce it, I will update if I find a way to completely mitigate the flicker. I also have not tried a different DP cable, that might fix it as well. I'm not putting this issue against the monitor just yet as many other trusted reviewers mentioned G-Sync working fine on their units.)
-Annoying port placement (Ports and IO is on the bottom of the monitor, serious design oversight. Very difficult to plug things in, you have to reach under the monitor and do it blind.)
-That's it that I can find so far, if any issues arise in the future I will update the review accordingly.

Something worth mentioning:

-The seller (Amazon in my case) is supposed to stamp the warranty card with the purchase date. If they do not (Of course they didn't, even though the box arrived opened, indicating that they did!!!!) your warranty begins from date of manufacture, not date of purchase. This is super annoying as I just lost a year of warranty coverage thanks to Amazon refusing to stamp the warranty card.

Final thoughts: Overall, fantastic budget 1440p 27" curved VA high refresh rate monitor. For the price I paid ($270 + tax), it is 110% worth it. Great upgrade coming from an older (2014-2015) LG IPS Ultrawide panel.

This should have been the monitor of the future. I was so excited that _finally_, someone made a large display that wasn't just a rebadged television that would let me replace my multiple monitor setup with a glorious Minority Report-style single display. I was so giddy when I placed the order to review it, and that glee lasted right up until it arrived. It even persisted through the fiasco of getting it assembled and installed.

But when I saw the actual experience of using it, I was crestfallen and quickly fell out of love. The image quality is mediocre to poor, and the One Connect box powered by Tizen software can't handle more than a single HDMI input at a time. Turns out this is just... a large display that's a rebadged television.

I waited for a while hoping that some of what I experienced was launch growing pains and that the monitor would get better with software updates, but no dice - so it's time to document my experiences with all their warts, I think.

Setup for the Odyssey Ark begins with finding somewhere to put it and getting it assembled. It took a full SUV trunk's max capacity to move, and two people and quite a bit of muscle to get it set up. The actual process is easy, it's just the nerves of lifting over three thousand dollars worth of LCD weighing a hundred pounds into position from the floor to your table. Ours was going in a tight corner, and it took us a few misfires before we finally were able to get it into position successfully. ("I've got it! NO I DON'T, SET IT BACK DOWN!"). The weight itself isn't too terrible, but the whole thing is extraordinarily awkward to get a grip on: you need to team lift it by the central pillar, and that requires moving bodies into places where desk usually would be. It's not an easy task.

The monitor curvature, which has been much talked about in advance reviews, is actually pretty great in person for me. This monitor needed the curve to make sense, and it fits well in my space only because the curve exists. It's exactly the right amount for portrait and landscape mode. This is the maximum size you'd ever want a monitor to be, but it's about perfectly sized to be an expansive piece of glass that covers your entire field of view.

Once it's in place, enjoy your last giddy smiles before the reality of your ill-advised purchase sets in. The monitor is definitely stunning when you first turn it on, with an extremely vibrant display and colors. But then you get mired up in setup for the OneConnect box, and it's a lot less straightforward than you'd hope: I encountered several errors and had to set it up manually because the smartphone flow didn't work, and then it went downhill from there.

The OneConnect box's OS looks exactly like the experience on Samsung's high end TVs, and that's really the root of all the flaws of this product: at the end of the day, it's just a Samsung TV with a different label on the tin, at a much higher price. A lot of clicks are needed to change sources or connect to streaming apps, everything is two layers more complicated than it needs to be, and promotional content is strewn throughout.

Let's talk about the much-vaunted multiview features, because they're so limited as to be effectively useless. The OneConnect box has four different HDMI inputs, and you can switch between them - but you can only display one at a time onscreen. Yes, this really is a real limitation, and there's nothing you can do about it.
You can only multitask with the web browser, casting and one or two other apps - it doesn't even work correctly for me with cloud game streaming or many video streaming services. Worse, the inputs are also stretched to fit the window you place them in, so pixels don't snap correctly even if you set the source resolution to 1080P. The whole thing is a muddy, overcomplicated mess. What could have been the world's best-integrated KVM switch and a must have for developers and day traders ends up being a feature that's essentially useless in most scenarios.

So, OK, you're going to just end up connecting your computer directly instead and using it fullscreen, and maybe that's okay if you rely on features like Snap to rearrange windows. But let's talk about image quality... even for a single HDMI input, the crispness is not good, at all. The resolution of this monitor is only 4k, meaning the effective pixel density is equivalent to about a 28" 1080P monitor... about as low as an average television.

Worse, the pixels don't render cleanly at all, because the OneConnect box applies a ton of image processing that's intended for video feeds to the input before displaying it. It's like everything goes through an intentional blur filter and is slightly misaligned from pixel boundaries. The pixels don't snap at all correctly, resulting in what should be a crisp one pixel black line appearing as a blurry line spanning multiple pixels. The whole thing is enough to give you a bad headache, and you can't resolve it with any of the onscreen settings or input mode changes. The problem compounds because computer font smoothing isn't really designed for the type of pixel layout used on the Ark, so you see lots of colored artifacts even after running the tuning wizard. I've included several photos showing just how bad it is, even after spending hours trying to tweak settings.

Portrait mode was also a miss. I thought I'd use it a lot in this mode, but it turns out I leave it in landscape 100% of the time. Most software isn't designed for vertical use, and when I tried to open large documents and read them that way, I ended up craning my neck upwards too much. Vertical might be useful for opening apps and a video screen, but as mentioned, the MultiView feature isn't flexible enough to do that, so it's kind of pointless. It's a shame, because physically the stand is really well built and it rotates smoothly - a real mechanical engineering feat.

In the glimmers of moments when I could push past the image quality, I really did legitimately enjoy working with a large, curved panel. This window goes here! This one goes here! Wow, I can see this entire drawing all at once without scrolling! But inevitably I'd end up squinting at missing detail, and the illusion of riding the wave of the future finally broke and rolled back to frustration.

So this is a total miss for productivity. What about gaming? Well, it's pretty okay! I hooked up my Xbox Series X and it was a much better story, with the bright HDR environments of Forza Horizon 5 being a particular standout. I also tried Xbox Game Streaming through the built-in app by connecting my controller to the One Connect base and that worked well too, so that could be an option if your console isn't located near your new monitor but you still want to play an occasional game. For all of this, you'll want the monitor in horizontal mode - portrait is really only useful with the useless Multi View feature. Basically, this acts like a high end TV when you connect a game console, and the video smoothing anti-features become less of a liability.

Was the game experience life-changing? No, it was in an uncanny valley between a really good gaming monitor and an immersive headset like an Oculus Quest 2. The screen is large enough where you'll occasionally want to turn your head, and that can be fun, but it's not enough to cover your entire peripheral field of vision. I think it would be great for a Flight Simulator cockpit or racing games, but less so for real time strategy or first person shooters.

Wrapping up... I hoped for two months some of the image quality issues would get resolved through software updates to the OneConnect hub, because so many of my complaints originate from that. The native UI in the OneConnect box looks fine, so maybe it can be fixed with a 1:1 pixel-to-pixel "no processing" setting? But so far, I haven't seen any updates to the software, and I'd operate on the assumption they aren't coming.

Fundamentally, this product was a miss for me in its current form. The basic concept of "a large, single surface monitor" is sound, and I think many of us will work this way someday. And the mechanical design of this is awesome! But the Odyssey Ark is not there yet. The three star rating is generous; realistically, this product needs three changes to make sense:

- It needs 5K or 8K resolution. 4K is not enough pixels to replace a classic multimonitor setup in a 55" diagonal form factor for anything other than gaming, even part time. It's rare somebody would settle for the pixel density a 1080p 28" monitor, and that's effectively what you get here.
- The One Connect box needs to be simplified, focused on handling multiple inputs correctly. Having four inputs and an elaborate multi-view feature that doesn't let you use any of the inputs is absolutely crazy, and I can't believe this thing shipped this way.
- The monitor needs to treat inputs like a _monitor_, rendering the pixels provided by the computer faithfully without blur or sharpness filtering.

So in 2023, who is this for? There's really one audience, and to be fair, that's who Samsung is marketing it to: hard core competitive gamers. But it's more specific than that: it's only for gamers who never do general computing tasks on their rig, and for whom image and prestige matters. I think this is, fundamentally, a Twitch streamer monitor. If that's you, it might be exactly what you need to draw attention to your feed and impress your subscribers while having a stunning backdrop for your streams. But if you're a normal human being who likes games but occasionally does other stuff too, a multimonitor setup of more reasonably sized displays is your best bet for the immediate future. And even if you do need something like this, you could just buy a cheaper 4K TV and skip the unnecessary rotating stand and overcomplicated OneConnect box.

On a past trip to South Korea, I visited Samsung's flagship showroom in Gangnam and was blown away by the beautiful displays I could only dream of owning. Now that I have one in my office, I'm afraid I've come to the conclusion that this should have stayed in the showroom... at least until they can offer a higher pixel density, a better solution for managing inputs than the heavily flawed OneConnect box, or at least crisp rendering of input video streams. I've now got an albatross that will stay put only until I can round up enough friends to relocate it so I can restore my multimon setup that actually works.

How many of us are more than peeved with the LCD industry for not disclosing dead pixels. They then have the nerve to claim that a dead pixel is not a defect; Of course it is.

This monitor (HG216D) has excellent build quality with an 8 bit LCD panel that gives accurate color and deep blacks. However, it also has a dead bright green sub-pixel in the center of the screen. It appears that this is a common problem with this monitor. The most likely reason is that a percentage of these panels are rejects from a high quality panel manufacturer. That's why the monitor is inexpensive. Now, there is nothing wrong with that, provided you TELL the customer what he or she is getting!

This monitor has been on the market at least since the summer of 2007. Monitors with dead pixels tend to be returned by customers, so the supply of bad monitors will accumulate over time. It is anyone's guess just how many of these are left, but odds are a lot of them are returns.

For general web browsing and document reading, the dead bright pixel is essentially invisible, but forget it when it comes to viewing videos. You can see the dead pixel from a mile away. If you want a monitor to display video, beware that you likely have a higher chance of getting a dead bright pixel with this model, but if you only want occasional video viewing, then this is a very good choice at this price.

The front buttons under the unit that control volume, brightness, menu, input, and power functions are a little hard to use. You have to feel the bumps as if you are reading Braille. The icons on the face of the monitor that locate the buttons are useless in a dark room, or for those of us with old eyes. I taped a paper key to the monitor to help me locate the buttons. I use the VGA input, and have not tried the HDMI input. Auto-sync of the VGA signal works flawlessly.

Some technical notes. What little light shows through fully black areas has a slight bluish tint. The dead bright sub-pixel reveals that the brightness control does vary the back-light intensity preserving gray scale and saving power (i.e. The dead pixel changes in intensity on a totally dark screen when adjusting brightness). Also, according to the energy star website, the monitor consumes 0.82W in standby - and no way to turn the monitor truly off. Good grief, what is a monitor doing that requires power when off? That extra power costs an additional 10 bucks over the life of the product. But all "modern" monitors probably do this. I connect the power cord to a power strip with a switch to get the proper 0 watts off. Works fine this way. Sound is poor (of course), but serviceable.

My rating reflects just how much I value honesty. It reflects more on the LCD industry rather than Hanns-G. We consumers would appreciate it if the LCD industry "got religion" and published panel quality ratings with all LCD panels. class 1, no dead pixels; class 2 , some dead pixels; etc. But then none of us would want to buy class 2 panels. Perhaps this is something that should be legislated...

After about five years, the screen went dead.  I checked the 140W power supply and found it was dead.  I replaced it. The new LG supply worked, but there was still no picture.  I opened the case, removed the connectors and found power to the board.  No obvious signs of damage, but I did find the PCB was warped with a bulge in the center.  When I opened the inner shield, I found an extra spacer/heatsink under the main board.  This must have led to a crack/open circuit over time which in turn gave it a premature death.  Bummer. I Bought this as a replacement for an ailing secondary monitor, with the intention of using it as a primary display next to my existing 32" VA panel. Overall, I'd call it solid but not great. The monitor looks decent generally, and the Freesync feature, once enabled, plays well with G-Sync on my NVidia GPU. However, there is an unfortunate amount of off-axis brightness shift in this display, to the point that there is noticeable vignetting at the edges of the panel when viewing a uniform image from a typical desktop distance. Next to my older BenQ VA panel, the difference is pretty stark. It's bad enough that I actually double-checked the specs to be sure I didn't order a TN-panel monitor by mistake.

Gaming performance is solid. 75Hz is a nice bump over 60 Hz (though not as dramatic as a pricier 144Hz monitor would be), and Freesync/G-Sync works well in the range (though it only goes down to 48Hz by default. For the price and size, it's a good choice.

That off-axis shift is a real bummer, though. My day job requires a fair bit of graphics work, and while I don't have to have perfect color accuracy I need better than this monitor will give me. Added to that, LG has combined picture adjustment settings in the on-screen menu in counter intuitive ways. White balance shifts with contrast for some reason, gamma is renamed "Black Stabilizer" and hidden away under "Game Adjust" setting. (ETA, 9-25-20 -- Changing picture mode to "Custom" alleviates a lot of these issues and unlocks more granular color and gamma adjustments. I will maintain that the other picture mode presets are terribad and should be avoided.) I'm struggling to find a happy medium where this display matches the other one. Was IPS always this bad and I just never had a monitor big enough to notice?

Regardless, it's a solid mix of features for the price, and if you aren't editing photos it'll more than get the job done.

As a monitor this is quite good-big, bright, no light leaks-but it has  some annoying lapses. The controls for brightness, contrast, position, etc. are of the onscreen software variety, and their triggers are invisible, located on the underside of the bezel. I looked through the CD-ROM PDF manual for some guide to the row of control buttons, such as which one does what...and there isn't a guide. You're on your own. The "User Controls" section in the PDF is pretty cryptic and un-organized, so it's going to be trial and error on how to make changes in the display. The other problem I had was with the included base. The image of the monitor in the Amazon page is accurate. Mount the screen on its base and it will sit roughly 4" off the surface, and there is no provision for raising it or lowering it. What you see is what you get. Luckily, I had a couple of adjustable monitor pedestals from previous, long-dead occupants of my desk, and, although the screen is supplied with VESA mounting holes in back, the threaded holes are metric, and the manufacturer didn't think it necessary to include the correct screws. So if you get one of these and need more height, get 4 M4X0.7 machine screws, maybe 13mm long to attach it to whatever VESA pedestal  or arm you want to use.

I'm pretty please with my purchase. I was wanting a cheap monitor to keep at home to use for my side computer repair business.

The blacks could be darker and other color more vibrant, but that's not really of any significance for me considering how I am using it. Also, I was able to adjust the monitor settings using the side buttons and my picture is much crisper and looks much cleaner than when I first plugged it in after unboxing it. I found the menu button system on the monitor to be better than most and easy to navigate.

Although it has an HDMI connection and although there are audio jacks, this monitor does not have speakers built-in. I'm guessing the audio jacks are so you can plug in some external speakers. Perhaps the sound is carried from the PC via the HDMI and then out to your external speaker that are connected to the audio jack? Just a hunch. I haven't tested it yet. This would make it a little more convenient to plug-in external speakers since you wouldn't have to crawl around on the floor to do so if you have a tower case.

One other reviewer mentioned that the metal making up the bezel around the outside of the screen is not flush all the way around. I noticed this minor issue as well, but for the price I'm not expecting perfection. I did dock it a star on the "material quality" feature for this issue, but I don't feel this should detour you from purchasing this monitor if you need something cheap.

All-in-all, a very solid monitor for the price.

I was amazed by the quality of the picture after I hooked this monitor up to my PC - sharp image, clean, vibrant colors, surpassing even my trusty old IBM CRT in depth and contrast!

Unfortunately, my perception of this monitor was about to change. After about an hour of use, the whole screen flickered, before readjusting itself. I could not believe my eyes, and I though, oh well maybe some driver problem or my video card (ATI Radeon HD 4550) is still readjusting. Then it happened again, the whole screen literally jumped up and down for 0.5 seconds. At this point I was becoming worried, so I googled "PA246Q flickering" - only to find out that I am not alone! It appears that this extremely annoying flaw is inherent to the manufacturing process and a great number of these monitors are shipped with this crippling defect. Definitely not something one would expect to find in a "professional grade" $500 monitor. I returned this monitor to the store I picked it up from (and swallowed a 5% "restocking" fee since they couldn't reproduce the problem on the spot).

Buyer beware - this could have been the best bang for the buck, but the ubiquity of this extremely annoying defect (which may surface up to a month after use, from what I read) may ruin the experience. I remember the time when the name ASUS was synonymous with quality and reliability, these times it seems are long past.

My deskstop includes a pair of Windows PCs shared by three enormous monitors, so switching to my lone-screen laptop for remote work is kind of painful. I've always wanted to get a second monitor for the laptop, but even the smallest I could find weren't portable enough.

This Huyouming monitor fits the bill pretty well. It's lightweight and extremely thin, even when enclosed by its protective folder/stand, and takes up very little space in my laptop case.

Hooking it up as a second Windows monitor is stupid-easy. It's powered by a USB cable (supplied) that fits any standard USB-B or C socket. The monitor powers up immediately when connected; unfortunately, there's no dedicated power switch.

Video is supplied by a separate HDMI cable. I'm using the one supplied with the monitor, with a standard HDMI plug on one end (for my laptop/PC) and a mini-HDMI plug on the other to feed the monitor. The monitor immediately sensed the presence of input, and my laptop's HD screen is mirrored perfectly. I can use Windows utilities to assign the second monitor's position and orientation relative to the laptop's monitor. Everything works without issues.

The monitor also accepts an HDMI signal via USB-C from laptops, phones and tablets that support it. None of my current Android devices can perform this trick, so I can't report on how well it works.

The quality of the 1920 x 1080 IPS display is more than adequate for my purposes.

The monitor's faux-leather folder can configured as a support easel with the aid of a plastic accessory stand. It works, but looks and feels a bit flimsy. However, the back of the monitor is equipped with a set of four VESA holes (matching screws included) which allows it to be mounted onto any VESA-compatible support device. I ordered a desktop-clamping support with a swiveling arm that will let me position the unit freely.

I didn't need to consult the instruction manual. This is a good thing, because the printing is barely legible without a magnifying glass. The teeny diagram showing the location of the connectors and controls is inaccurate. It shows only one mini-HDMI input and one speaker hole. The unit actually has two of each. Both of the speaker holes are located on the left side of the monitor, an odd arrangement not conducive to watching movies with stereo sound. Luckily, there's also a 1/8" stereo headphone jack.

I'm looking forward to experimenting with this monitor. Its compact size and VESA mountability should make for some interesting placement options.
