The SAMSUNG 49" S95UA is a Super Ultra-wide Dual QHD Monitor with USB type-C geared towards business users. Its 32:9 aspect ratio provides plenty of space to work comfortably with multiple windows opened side-by-side.

The monitor has built-in internal stereo speakers, Picture-by-Picture (PBP) to work with two sources and it does support HDR400.
The borders on this SAMSUNG 49" S95UA monitor are thin on three sides, and they do look good.
The curvature of the screen makes it quite thick, so I was expecting better sound from the internals speakers, which have tiny audio and sound quite horrible at 70% volume level and above.

Even though you could game on this monitor, with 120Hz (DP) fast refresh rate and good 4ms response time...the lack of Adaptive-Sync (G-Synch/FreeSync) means that tearing and artifacts will be present.
Adaptive-Sync allows the monitor’s refresh cycle to synced with the computer graphics card, to prevent the monitor from drawing a new frame before the current one has completed rendering.

The stand is well built, solid, and does not take up too much space and it is quite thin considering the size of the monitor. The monitor does wobble a bit, even when just navigating the OSD controls.
The stand height adjustment range is mediocre, and there is very limited swivel range and almost not tilt adjustments. The size of this monitor does not allow rotation to portrait orientation, unless you use a VESA wall mount.
On the bottom there is an array of ports including 2x HDMI ports, 1x DisplayPort, 2x USB Type C ports, 3x USB A ports, 1x Ethernet jack and Audio out.

One of the best features for laptop users, is the USB Type C ports, that besides supporting data and video, could also deliver up to 90W of power to your laptop keeping it juiced up, while the Ethernet could provide Internet access.

The back of the monitor is quite plain and it comes with a ring to cover the stands screws. There is a small foldable hook, near the top of the stand, to hang your headphones. At the bottom of the stand there is small hole for cable management in combination with the included clip.
The monitor could be VESA-mounted, but it requires a special adapter, which is included in the accessories bundle. The bundle includes a USB Type C cable, HDMI cable and power cable.
I would have liked a DisplayPort cable, since that is the only way to get to the advertised 120Hz refresh rate.

The SAMSUNG 49" S95UA picture quality looks great, if you are located dead center of it.
There are no noticeable signs of color bleed and it has good reflection handling. But like most displays with VA panels, it has subpar horizontal viewing angles. Depending of the viewing angle, colors lose accuracy, brightness decreases and the image appears washed out.

The contrast ratio is decent, but the local dimming option improves it just a tiny bit.
Gray uniformity is very good, but black uniformity is not that good, since the left side near the edge remains lit, when most of the middle and the right side are dimmed.
The luminance uniformity could vary between monitors, so this is an observation on the unit I received.

Out of the box, the SAMSUNG 49" S95UA has decent color accuracy and after calibrating the monitor reached excellent color accuracy. Gamma follows the target curve very close.
This monitor has very good color gamut. It has perfect coverage of the sRGB color space, and great coverage of the Adobe RGB color space, which is mostly used for photo editing. I also has a great coverage of the DCI P3 color space, which is used by most HDR movies

The ultrawide 32:9 aspect ratio and 5120x1440 resolution deliver an outstanding amount of screen real estate to work with. This is an outstanding monitor for multitasking and content creation and could had also offered an immersive gaming experience if it supported either FreeSync or G-Sync.
The SAMSUNG 49" S95UA overall build quality is great and solid.

This monitor is great for a $80 monitor. While it's not quite an edge-to-edge screen, it's very close, and its display is much larger than another monitor I have that has the same outside dimensions. It uses the smaller VESA MIS-D 75x75mm standard, and you cannot mount it and have the base on it at the same time, because the two occupy the same property on the back.

We had four major problems with the monitor:
#1: When attempting to remove the base, you're supposed to *gently* press the button over the mount, and it can slide out. This was only explained in the manual - the back just says "push". When I pressed on it at what is a gentle strength for me (and I'm relatively weak), the button instantly broke and fell back inside the monitor, where I can never retrieve it again. Now, I need a screwdriver whenever I have to take it off or put it on.
#2: The screw holes for mounting it to a monitor mount are actually really short. I had to add about a 3/16ths of an inch worth of washers between the monitor and the mount to get a screw to hold it properly.
#3: The monitor had a weird flickering when trying to do video chat using Zoom. It might be that we had the refresh rate wrong, and I haven't been able to duplicate it using other types of streaming video.
#4: We did NOT receive the monitor pictured on Amazon. The back is totally different. It still has HDMI and VGA ports and speakers.

All that said, it plays video games just fine and has reasonable depth of color, so even with the problems mentioned above, I'll be ordering a second one.

The Samsung 2343BWX has a 23-inch TN panel, and has the main weakness of TN: degradation when viewed off-center. You need to set this up such that your eyes are aimed directly at the middle of the screeen in your primary viewing position, or you will be disappointed. Viewing by multiple people will mean viewing off-center. You start to see the degradation just moving from one side of the screen to the other. It's worse from top to bottom.

The viewable area of the screen is only 11.3 inches high, and the monitor sits low on the desk. The stand rotates nicely, not too important for a one-person display. It tilts, but not enough to get the panel perpendicular to my eyes. (That would probably turn it into a dust-catcher, anyhow.) What this unit needs most is what it lacks: a height adjustment. People use books or reams of paper to address this, but then the keyboard won't slide back over the base. To address this issue, I looked at [[ASIN:B000BUK7KW Ergotron LX Desk Mount Arm - mounting kit ( 45-179-195 )]] and [[ASIN:B000AMJC2K Ergotron 45-174-300 Neo-Flex LCD Arm-Extension Grommet Desk Mount]], but why should I have to pay for that? Up in the attic, I found an old monitor stand with keyboard drawer I had for an old CRT - I'm now using that without the drawer. [Yes, there is a reason I keep all that old junk!]

Since I don't play action games, I also looked at S-PVA screens - [[ASIN:B0018LJYFC Dell 2408WFP UltraSharp 24-inch Widescreen + High Definition Flat Panel Monitor]] seems best, cheaper from Dell direct - and IPS screens [[ASIN:B001FS1LLI HP LP2475W 24in LCD Monitor 1920X1200]] or [[ASIN:B0013FMLXK Apple LED Cinema Display 24-Inch MB382LL/A]] seems best. Ominously, Amazon has withdrawn the HP from sales, only offering it through a partner, due to inventory, shipping, or description problems [my guess, users noted color shift problems, and started returning them]. The Apple costs almost $900.

Associates have the Dell at work, and it's fine, but not spectacular. I have used a single Dell 2001FP at work for years (with 8 Linux desktops, one dedicated to my Windows VM). My wife has a 2007FP at home for photo work - these 4:3 20-inch displays may be better quality than the 2408WFP.

The 2343BWX replaced a 1280x1024 Princeton from Costco that cost me $600 a number of years ago. I paid a premium at Costco for the new one, because of their 90-day return policy on electronics. I have had this unit 2.5 months. The Dell would definitely be better, but it would cost me about $200 more. I've pretty much talked myself into keeping the 2343BWX, which I rate at 3.5 stars.

The 2048x1152 16:9 aspect ratio on the 2343BWX is better for my use, which is working with many Windows XP windows open at a time. For side-by-side viewing, the extra width is more important than height. The total number of pixels is also slightly higher than a 1920x1200 16:10 resolution (e.g. the Dell and HP units above).

Quite a number of 1920x1080 16:9 units are out there - I cannot see any reason to buy one of them instead of the Samsung 2343BWX.

---- update 12-Feb-2010

Alright, so bear with me for the long review but I think it will help out a lot of folks...

I've been in depths of monitor research for the past couple of weeks. I have a 2021 MacBook Pro M1 13" that I use in clamshell mode with a dock, as well as a Xbox Series X. After many headaches and banging my head on the wall I finally settled on the M32Q. I'd describe myself as the average user for monitor needs (a little work, web browsing, and some gaming on the weekends). I'm coming from a 1080p 34" 60hz curved ultra-wide VA panel monitor which worked just fine for my MacBook Pro M1, but didn't work with my Xbox Series X due to the lack of ultra-wide support. I was using a 4k Vizio TV (obviously 60hz) for the Xbox but wanted to have an "all in one" station.

I like having a large monitor and 32" is the largest you can get with a 16:9 aspect ratio (supported by consoles). In my research, I discovered that the Macs have a hard time with your typical 27" - 32" 4k display due to display scaling issues, so I would have had to purchase a 5k display to get into the ideal 220 PPI range for Macs, which costs $1,000+. Macs also work fine with 120 PPI (or below). Unfortunately that middle range on 4k monitors just isn't supported that well. 1440p has always been the best of both worlds between 4k and 1080p, plus you get a higher refresh rate for a decent price. The M32Q comes in right around 93 PPI, which compared to my 4k 43" Vizio TV I used for gaming wasn't that far off for pixel density...

I received my M32Q which was nicely packaged and well protected. Although I have it on a vesa mount, the stand felt hefty and of good quality. I got everything hooked up and connected my computer and Xbox.

My Mac recognized the display without issue and it is displaying at full 1440p with the 144hz refresh rate, right out of the box. No dead pixels and no bleeding sections or corners! Text looks just fine to me and there is no noticeable red/green outline around the text. Keep in mind, I sit about 2 - 2.5 feet away from the monitor. The brightness is great and everything is adjustable from the easy to use joystick controlled menu. For what I'm doing with my Mac and for work, I couldn't be more pleased!

Then I hooked up my Xbox. I went into display settings and changed it to 1440p at 120hz and everything was recognized without issue. Gaming was a true joy. Everything was so fluid and I wish I had gotten a high refresh rate display sooner!

I'm not really utilizing the KVM features but might in the future...

All in all, this is a really great monitor and I'm incredibly happy with it. You'll see some mixed reviews on here about various quality issues which may be valid (maybe I won the panel lottery). In my opinion, there's a little nitpicking going on too in these reviews. I think if you unbox a monitor and start looking for issues, you will find them. I'm just your average user that appreciates an all around good large display with good colors and a high refresh rate for the weekend Halo gaming sessions. And for that, it works perfect. Plus, you can't beat the price.

Final thoughts... I think this is a good product and I'd recommend it for the average user. The 27" monitor I'm sure looks even better with the larger pixel density, but if you like large monitors, this is a great choice. This has plenty of features and doesn't look obnoxious (no RGB or a crazy plastic back panel like the Samsung Odyssey). Work and play, this monitor does it all, and it does it well.

One thing I have to take issue with is the build of the monitor. Although the screen is nice, the casing is really thin, cheap plastic. Quite frankly it looks and feels like a toy. My guess is that one small blow and this monitor will be toast, as it just look extremely fragile.

I got an AL2416Wd yesterday for $529 plus tax at J&R in New York. It certainly is a bargain for a large monitor. My first color monitor, a 20" Radius CRT I bought many years ago, cost $2500 -- nearly seven times as much in today's dollars.

At lower resolutions at the correct aspect ratio (16:10), such as 1600 x 1000, the screen is blurry and no amount of adjustment of the focus control will fix it. The only way to see things at actual size with reasonable (not great) sharpness is to use the Mac's Universal Access to zoom in.

This is my first LCD monitor, so I don't know if this inability to run satisfactorily at different resolutions is characteristic of all LCD monitors, only this one, or all or some LCD monitors on a Mac. I would appreciate any comments about this.

Nevertheless, I'm enjoying the expanded real estate, a big improvement over my previous 20" CRTs. The brightness is outstanding, the color and sharpness at native resolution are satisfactory, the refresh rate is fast.

For my last two years of college I had the same roommate and used his TV and XBox. When he started getting stingy about the XBox, I got my own, but still used his TV. Then we graduated. He moved out. His TV went with him.

Not wanting to give up my gaming experience, but not being equipped with large amounts of funding, I decided to avoid an HD TV and go for a much cheaper HD monitor. Since I could set it on a table in front of me, I did not need to get something with huge dimensions, but I did want HD capability and an HDMI connection for my XBox 360.

This ASUS monitor, which I purchased after considering the options and reading the reviews, was worth every dollar (which was far fewer dollars than any comparable TV I could have acquired). More versatile than a TV, due to its VGA and DVI connections for computers without HDMI outputs, and delivering every bit as much picture quality, the only thing this monitor can't do is connect to RCA or component devices, though there are converter boxes readily available such as this one: [[ASIN:B003NS0UUQ New 3 RCA Composite S-video R/L Audio to HDMI Converter]]. This would be useful for connecting a Playstation 2, for example (to prevent glitches with the PS3's spotty backward compatibility).

Besides being a good value, this monitor looks sleek, takes up a minimum amount of space, has a rather easy to use OSD (anyone who complains about it just needs to spend 30 seconds figuring it out), comes with a rugged box that makes it easy to safely transport, and generally works in a "smashing" fashion.

An additional detail which may be valuable for people with XBox 360's is the fact that you can plug speakers into the audio output on this monitor, routing sound from your HDMI into better speakers than the obviously tinny ones built into the monitor. Prior to acquiring this, I had purchased a Microsoft audio adapter which allowed me to pull audio separately from my XBox while using the HDMI cable. This monitor eliminates the need for such a device.

I know that some people have complained of flickering issues, but most if not all of those people have later realized that they had a defective unit, and were able to get a replacement under warranty with minimum hassle. I was fortunate in that I got a top notch unit the first time, and I couldn't be happier.

Unless you have a huge home and a lot of money to spend, why buy a large TV and sit miles away when you can set up a monitor on a table in front of you (great for apartments) and get the same visual effect? A 1080p image, fitted to this screen, will display at approximately 100 pixels per inch, which is the typical resolution of most monitors and laptop screens. This resolution is tight and crisp, but the pixels are not so tiny that details are obscured. This monitor makes my gaming a pleasure and will serve me well until such time that I deem a TV a necessary purchase.

After a week of searching and contemplation, I settled on the Gigabyte M27Q as my next gaming monitor, and I have not been disappointed in the slightest so far. Out of the box, the colors look great, but they can be made even better with a bit of tuning. I love the configuration Hardware Unboxed provides in their review video. I switched to this monitor from a 1080p VA panel, which I hated due to copious amounts of unbearable ghosting and low pixel density. I switched to this monitor, and I cannot find any smearing, ghosting, or flickering at any of the modes I've tried, and I have done a lot of tinkering.
As expected, G-Sync works great, without any sort of tearing or flickering. While you should NOT buy this monitor for HDR, the HDR gaming experience was a pleasant surprise. The monitor auto-switched to HDR mode when I first opened Mass Effect Legendary Edition, and I decided to leave it on to test it out. The claims of colors becoming washed out aren't exaggerated, and the lack of any local dimming hurts a lot, but the high peak-brightness of this monitor is quite good, so I did not find the experience to be "bad" by any stretch of the imagination. One of the things that I was worried about when I bought this monitor was IPS Glow/Backlight Bleed, and upon first turning on the monitor, I noticed a hint of it in the top right corner, but unless I'm staring directly at it and the screen is displaying complete black, I don't notice it at all, and I consider myself to be quite finicky with this sort of thing. Another feature of this monitor that I don't see mentioned by anybody is the 10-bit color support. While you do have to turn the refresh rate down to 120hz to get it, and it's NOT true 10-bit, (it's 8-bit+FRC,) I find the experience to be completely worth it, as I do little extremely competitive gaming, and my RTX 2070 really can't hit above 100 fps in games at high settings 1440p anyways. It's also worth mentioning that NO gaming monitors in this price bracket have TRUE 10-bit color, so it's not much of a downside that it is 8-bit+FRC. Another thing you'll hear people mention about this monitor as a negative is the "BGR" subpixel layout compared to the traditional "RGB" layout. After setting up ClearType in windows, I can see absolutely no difference between text on this monitor and text on any other display I use daily. In fact, to my eye, which again, I consider to be astute, text is actually sharper than I expected. Overall, I think that this monitor is unbeatable at the price, and I'd recommend it to just about anybody.
